YouTube Insights Digest

2026-06-06 · 40 episodes · 1567 insights · 18 channels
One-time backlog extraction (≥25-min episodes). Standalone — not wired into the dashboard.
Quotes shown “verbatim” are matched in the source transcript; “≈ … paraphrase” means close-but-not-exact (auto-caption drift). Timestamps were not available (transcripts stored as plain text) — every link opens the episode at the start.

Bankless 4 episode(s) · 176 insights

Privacy Alts Are Pumping While Bitcoin & ETH Are Weakening64 min47 insights2026-05-29
▶ Watch on YouTube
Bankless weekly roundup (last week of May 2026): BTC fell below $73K with the second-largest IBIT outflow since debut while ETH broke below $2,000, yet a narrow set of 'consensus' alts (Hyperliquid/HYPE, Near, Venice/VVV, Zcash, Railgun) hit all-time highs. Hosts debate whether this privacy/up-and-comer rotation can survive a major breakdown (Berniske/Berninski says no), David Hoffman explains why he sold all his ETH, and a privacy-as-the-surviving-use-case thesis runs through Vitalik's EF refocus on ETH-the-asset and Near's confidential-intents adoption.
BTC/ETH weakness and flow signals · 5Rotation into privacy and consensus alts · 5Hyperliquid strength, perps land-grab, ETFs · 5Bitcoin treasuries (Strategy, BitMine) under stress · 3Bitcoin store-of-value thesis under fire · 5David Hoffman's 'why I sold my ETH' thesis · 7Vitalik / EF refocus on ETH-the-asset and privacy · 3Privacy-as-the-surviving-use-case and Near intents · 3DeFi AI-vulnerability risk debate · 2Macro backdrop: Iran, oil, yields, AI equities · 6Stablecoins, treasuries-of-AI, and tail stories · 3
BTC/ETH weakness and flow signals 5
Bitcoin fell ~5% on the week to roughly $73,000 (from $78,000), retracing back toward recent ranging lows and erasing the gains it made during the Iran war.
data_point🗣 David Hoffmanconviction: highBitcoinBTC
So what: BTC giving back its geopolitical-hedge gains signals the safe-haven bid has faded; watch the recent ranging lows as the line that decides whether 'cycles are just cycling.'
“Bitcoin's coming in right at $73,000. It is uh down down from $78,000 on the week.” ▶ 5:58
BlackRock's IBIT saw its second-largest single-day outflow since debut, including a single ~$1.3 billion IBIT sell, and the last 12 days ranked in the bottom 5% of worst inflow/outflow days in Bitcoin ETF history.
data_point🗣 Ryan Sean Adamsconviction: highIBITBlackRockBitcoin ETFMichael Nado (Mike Nato)
So what: Sustained record-bad ETF flows mean the marginal institutional buyer has turned seller; ETF flow data is the cleanest real-time tape for BTC demand.
“the last 12 days have been the bottom 5% of uh worst performing days for inflows outflows” ▶ 6:37
Michael Nado interprets the large IBIT selling as front-running macro worries, casting Bitcoin again as a 'canary in the coal mine' for macro trouble ahead.
causal🗣 Ryan Sean Adams (citing Michael Nado)conviction: mediumBitcoinMichael Nadomacro
So what: If BTC is leading macro stress, weakness here may presage broader risk-off; treat crypto outflows as an early macro indicator rather than a crypto-specific story.
“Michael's take is this is like front running some macro worries right just bitcoin again being kind of canary in the coal mine” ▶ 7:12
ETH fell ~6.2% on the week and broke below $2,000 to ~$1,995, with hosts framing this as the worst Ethereum sentiment since 2019.
data_point🗣 David Hoffmanconviction: highETHEthereum
So what: ETH underperforming BTC into a sub-$2K capitulation with 2019-level sentiment is a classic contrarian setup to watch, but only if a major-driven washout completes.
“ether down 6.2% 2% below $2,000. We're at $1995.” ▶ 7:36
ETH staking has risen to ~31% of supply and the staking queue is up, which David argues is not a clean 'who's left to sell' bottom signal because staked ETH can still be unstaked and sold.
framework🗣 David Hoffman / Ryan Sean Adamsconviction: mediumETHEthereum staking
So what: Don't treat a rising staking ratio as locked-up float; supply can re-enter the market, so it's a weak floor for ETH price.
“look at the staking queue, you know, it's up. There's, you know, 31% ETH staking. Who's left to sell?” ▶ 45:26
Rotation into privacy and consensus alts 5
A narrow 'four horsemen' set of alts (Hyperliquid/HYPE, Near, Venice/VVV, Zcash) plus Railgun hit or neared all-time highs in late May 2026 even as BTC and ETH broke down, an unusual divergence for a bear market.
data_point🗣 David Hoffmanconviction: highHyperliquidHYPENearVeniceVVVZcashRailgun
So what: Money is concentrating into a privacy/up-and-comer basket; these are the momentum names to track, but their fate is tethered to whether the majors hold.
“It's uh Hyperliquid, Near uh Venice, and uh Zcash all hitting basically all-time highs. That doesn't usually happen during a bare market.” ▶ 5:30
After topping out, these leaders have already pulled back sharply: HYPE from $64.40 ATH to ~$57, VVV from just over $20 to $14.88, Near from $2.88 to $2.33, and Zcash from $682 to ~$535.
data_point🗣 David Hoffmanconviction: highHYPEHyperliquidVVVVeniceNearZcash
So what: Double-digit drawdowns from ATHs as BTC slid ~80k to ~72k show the alt strength is fragile and high-beta to Bitcoin; size positions for sharp reversals.
“Hype is now down from 64 to 57. VVV hit an all-time high of just over $20. It's now at 14.88. Near hit $2.88. It's now at $2.33. And then Zcash hit an all-time high of $682. It's currently coming in at 535.” ▶ 19:04
David argues the alt strength is structurally thin: these 'consensus coins' are bid by perhaps only ~50 remaining crypto-Twitter participants, so the rally lacks broad demand and is not sustainable if Bitcoin drops.
contrarian🗣 David Hoffmanconviction: mediumHYPEVVVNearZcashcrypto Twitter
So what: Treat the alt ATHs as low-liquidity, low-float moves vulnerable to a vacuum bid; thin consensus rallies reverse violently when the marginal buyer steps away.
“there are so few people left in crypto that it doesn't matter if these tokens are consensus cuz there's just like 50 people buying these things that are left and that's all of crypto Twitter.” ▶ 20:45
Chris Berniske's bear thesis: there is no durable paradigm where majors are rotated out of and up-and-comers sustain a swim against an outgoing tide; the majors are 'telling us something' and the alt pump will not last if BTC/ETH keep falling.
thesis🗣 Chris Berniske (cited by David Hoffman)conviction: highChris BerniskeBitcoinEthereumaltcoins
So what: The dominant analytical view is that alt outperformance is a head-fake; a BTC breakdown of even ~$10K would drag the whole complex, so the alt rotation is a fade for risk managers.
“I don't see a new paradigm where majors are rotated out of and select up and upandcomers can sustain their swim against an outgoing tide for long.” ▶ 4:41
Mike Nado's framework (developed with David): the market is at a make-or-break level where BTC rising from here would be unprecedented bear-market strength, but a drop confirms 'the cycles are simply cycling.'
framework🗣 David Hoffman (citing Michael Nado)conviction: highBitcoinMichael Nado
So what: Defines a binary technical decision point; a confirmed breakdown validates a normal four-year-cycle top and argues for de-risking the whole complex.
“if Bitcoin goes up for here from here it's pretty unprecedented in terms of like bare market strength but if it goes down from here then the cycles are simply cycling.” ▶ 5:05
Hyperliquid strength, perps land-grab, ETFs 5
Hyperliquid ETFs (chiefly Bitwise BHY and 21Shares TYP) launched ~1-2 weeks ago and have drawn over $101 million of inflows, with Grayscale (GHY) and reportedly VanEck also filing.
data_point🗣 David Hoffmanconviction: highHyperliquidHYPEBitwise BHY21Shares TYPGrayscale GHYVanEck
So what: TradFi is gaining wrapped HYPE exposure via brokerages; strong early inflows make Hyperliquid the standout institutional alt-adoption story to track.
“There's over $101 million of inflows since their launch earlier this month. This is mostly between the Bitwise BHY and the 21 Shares TYP uh ETFs.” ▶ 21:09
A single Hyperliquid trader is profiting by going long S&P 500 and NASDAQ while shorting ~12-13 cryptos (ETH, MEGA, Fartcoin, TRUMP, Athena, LayerZero, zkSync, Bitcoin Cash, UNI, Cardano, Litecoin, XRP), with every long green and every short green simultaneously.
anecdote🗣 David Hoffmanconviction: mediumHyperliquidS&P 500NASDAQETHLayerZerozkSyncCardanoLitecoinBitcoin CashUniswap
So what: The dominant working trade is long equities / short crypto beta; the regime favors equity AI exposure over crypto, and crypto-shorts as a basket are paying.
“just go long equities and short crypto and you can make money” ▶ 8:46
ETH is the single most-targeted crypto short, with the example trader running a $10 million, 25x short on ETH (his largest position) rather than shorting Bitcoin, reflecting a market belief ETH can be shorted 'more safely' than BTC.
data_point🗣 Ryan Sean Adams / David Hoffmanconviction: highETHEthereumBitcoinHyperliquid
So what: Concentrated, leveraged ETH shorts are a positioning signal that ETH is the consensus underperformer, but also sets up squeeze risk if sentiment turns.
“This is a $10 million position on ETH. It's a 25x uh position on ETH” ▶ 9:00
No US-regulated perpetuals license exists yet, so the onshore US perps market is an open 'golden goose'; Polymarket is now building a perps platform (beta live for select users) and Coinbase/Robinhood are positioning, with no clear winner.
thesis🗣 David Hoffman / Ryan Sean Adamsconviction: highPolymarketCoinbaseRobinhoodHyperliquidperpetuals
So what: Whoever first wins a compliant US perps license captures the largest capital pool; this is a major land-grab to watch among Polymarket, Coinbase, Robinhood and Hyperliquid.
“it's really the regulated compliant onshore market that is really the golden goose. I mean United States is still the center of capital.” ▶ 22:53
The community-supported Hyperliquid DAT 'PURR' (Hyperliquid Strategies) is the only one of roughly 30 remaining digital-asset-treasury vehicles trading in the green.
data_point🗣 David Hoffmanconviction: mediumPURRHyperliquid StrategiesDAT
So what: The DAT model is broadly underwater except where the underlying (HYPE) is outperforming; underlying-asset strength, not the treasury wrapper, drives DAT returns.
“it is the only DAT out of all of them like the 30 of them that I think remain that is in the green.” ▶ 16:41
Bitcoin treasuries (Strategy, BitMine) under stress 3
MicroStrategy/Strategy's average BTC cost basis is ~$75,700; it has spent ~$63 billion to acquire BTC now worth ~$61 billion, leaving the position ~$2 billion underwater.
data_point🗣 David Hoffmanconviction: highStrategyMicroStrategyMichael SaylorBitcoin
So what: Saylor's cost basis sitting at ~$75.7K (above spot ~$73K) marks a key level; an underwater Strategy is unusual and worth watching for forced-behavior or sentiment effects.
“the average Bitcoin cost basis for Strategy is $75,700 uh dollar bitcoin. Strategy has spent a total of $63 billion to acquire what is now worth $61 billion of Bitcoin.” ▶ 13:44
Strategy used dry powder to repurchase/clear convertible notes rather than buy Bitcoin this week, which the hosts read as a sign that Strategy's Bitcoin buying is tapering.
causal🗣 Ryan Sean Adams / David Hoffmanconviction: mediumStrategyMicroStrategyconvertible notesBitcoin
So what: If the largest structural BTC buyer is paying down liabilities instead of accumulating, a key demand pillar is weakening; watch Strategy's purchase cadence.
≈ “using some of their dry powder to go short up their balance sheet rather than buy Bitcoin, which gives you the sense that the Bitcoin buys are tapering.” paraphrase ▶ 14:46
Tom Lee's BitMine is down ~$8 billion on an ~$18 billion Ether position, a far deeper drawdown than Strategy's, framing it as either a generational entry or a badly-timed trade given it started under a year ago.
data_point🗣 David Hoffman / Ryan Sean Adamsconviction: highBitMineTom LeeEtherETH
So what: An ETH treasury down ~44% concentrates ETH downside risk in one large, recent holder; BitMine's solvency/conviction is a key ETH overhang to monitor.
“Bit mine down $8 billion on their position on their much smaller position uh of Ether... $8 billion being down $8 billion on $18 billion is a very different story” ▶ 15:17
Bitcoin store-of-value thesis under fire 5
Mark Cuban has sold most of his Bitcoin, saying it 'lost the plot' and failed as a hedge: during the Iran war gold ran to ~$5,000 while Bitcoin dropped, and BTC didn't rise when the dollar fell as a fiat-debasement hedge should.
contrarian🗣 Mark Cuban (clip)conviction: highMark CubanBitcoingoldIran war
So what: A prominent ex-bull abandoning the digital-gold thesis on the basis that BTC failed its first real geopolitical/debasement test is a sentiment marker and a challenge to the macro-hedge narrative.
“gold just blew up and went, you know, to $5,000, Bitcoin dropped. And every time the dollar dropped, Bitcoin should have gone up... and it just didn't do that.” ▶ 12:00
Cuban says crypto is disappointing because it never built 'an application for grandma' (a mainstream consumer killer app analogous to the iPhone app store), and dismisses memecoins/token stuff as 'garbage,' while being less disappointed in Ethereum than Bitcoin.
thesis🗣 Mark Cuban (clip)conviction: highMark CubancryptoEthereumBitcoinmemecoins
So what: The consumer-adoption thesis remains unfulfilled per a sophisticated former bull; absent a mainstream app, the asset case rests on store-of-value/DeFi, not mass utility.
“crypto is disappointed because it hasn't come up with an application for grandma.” ▶ 11:09
David Hoffman's counter-framework: ascribing a fixed model to Bitcoin (e.g., 'it must rally in an Iran war') guarantees disappointment because we don't actually understand how or why Bitcoin trades.
framework🗣 David Hoffmanconviction: mediumBitcoin
So what: Cautions against trading BTC as a deterministic macro hedge; its behavior is regime-dependent and not reliably predictable from a single narrative.
“I I think if you over overcribe a model onto Bitcoin, you're you're destined to be disappointed.” ▶ 13:08
Polymarket odds for 2026 are deeply bearish: ~55% chance BTC hits $55K and ~42% chance $50K, with only ~50% odds of $90K (vs $140K ATH) and ~20% odds of $35K; ETH shows ~60% odds of $1,500, ~21% of $3,500 and only ~5% of $6,500.
data_point🗣 David Hoffman / Ryan Sean Adamsconviction: mediumPolymarketBitcoinETH
So what: Prediction markets are pricing meaningful downside (1-in-5 chance of $35K BTC) and weak upside; a useful sentiment/positioning gauge that confirms bearish skew.
“we got a 55% chance that Bitcoin will hit $55,000... There's almost a 20% chance that we get to 35k Bitcoin at some point this year.” ▶ 17:20
Mark Cuban also quit Bitcoin saying it is 'worse than gold' this week, reinforcing the digital-gold-vs-gold underperformance narrative as a recurring bear theme.
contrarian🗣 Ryan Sean Adamsconviction: highMark CubanBitcoingold
So what: Reinforces that BTC has lost the gold-substitute argument in this regime; relative performance vs gold is the key narrative battleground for BTC's macro case.
“Mark Cuban also quit Bitcoin this week. Uh says it's worse than gold.” ▶ 1:04
David Hoffman's 'why I sold my ETH' thesis 7
David Hoffman sold all of his ETH after being pro-ETH since 2017-2018, arguing the 'strong crypto' environment (cypherpunk alternative financial system, DeFi/NFT/DAO peak of 2021) that made ETH valuable as money hasn't existed since 2021, while 'weak crypto' (a back-end efficiency upgrade for big financial institutions) is now the meta.
thesis🗣 David Hoffmanconviction: highDavid HoffmanETHEthereumDeFiBankless
So what: A founding ETH bull capitulating on the monetary thesis is a major sentiment event; the strong-vs-weak-crypto frame argues ETH's monetary premium depends on a macro/cultural regime that has gone.
“strong crypto has never felt weaker to me now in 2026 because weak crypto, which is just like a back-end efficiency upgrade for the world's biggest financial institutions, seems to be the meta.” ▶ 35:11
David's core L1 valuation framework: no layer-1 will ever have sustainable fees because chains perpetually expand block space and minimize fees, so L1s are 'all money or they're nothing' and ETH must win a head-to-head competition to be the best money.
framework🗣 David Hoffmanconviction: highETHEthereumSolanalayer-1 blockchains
So what: Reframes smart-contract L1 valuation away from revenue/fees toward monetary premium; implies ETH is undervalued only if it wins the money competition, otherwise it's worth little.
“no layer 1 blockchain will actually have sustainable fees into the future. They're all money or they're nothing.” ▶ 38:16
David argues ETH-as-money required Ethereum to maintain ~95% smart-contract dominance to be valued as money; because Solana 'nipped at its heels' and took users/revenue, dominance fell toward ~65%, so the market now values ETH as a (revenue-dependent) smart-contract chain instead.
causal🗣 David Hoffmanconviction: highETHEthereumSolana
So what: Ties ETH's lost monetary premium directly to lost market share; Ethereum's dominance percentage is the key variable determining whether it re-rates as money or as a cash-flow asset.
“If it's 95% dominant, then it's likely that ETH is valued as money. If it's 65% dominant, then it's going to be valued as a smart contract chain.” ▶ 39:26
David frames ETH as having 'skipped a cycle' (barely hitting ATHs last cycle, no overperformance), blaming an 'original sin': the rollup-centric roadmap and a culture that emphasized the Ethereum blockchain over ETH-the-asset and DeFi.
thesis🗣 David Hoffmanconviction: highETHEthereumDeFirollups
So what: Argues ETH's underperformance is self-inflicted via misallocated focus; a culture/roadmap pivot back to ETH-the-asset and DeFi would be the catalyst for any re-rating.
“ETH effectively is skipping a cycle... Part of this had to do with a rollupcentric road map that felt like kind of a side quest in retrospect.” ▶ 42:52
David's near-term ETH view is explicitly not bearish but neutral: he expects ETH to merely track the market with no path to a re-rating in either direction, comparing it to Microsoft ('too big to grow, too existential to die') or silver vs Bitcoin's gold.
thesis🗣 David Hoffmanconviction: highETHEthereumMicrosoftsilverBitcoin
So what: Even the bear case is 'beta with no catalyst,' implying ETH is a market-tracking hold rather than alpha; investors seeking outperformance should look elsewhere unless a re-rating catalyst appears.
“I am and ether is about tracking the market is like kind of my base case. I don't see how it gets rerated in any direction.” ▶ 47:53
Ryan Sean Adams's rebuttal: ETH still scores best on the actual properties of money (censorship resistance, decentralization, hardness of inflation schedule) with nothing close, and the monetary competition plays out over decades, so David's call is 'too early' rather than wrong.
thesis🗣 Ryan Sean Adamsconviction: highETHEthereum
So what: The bull counter is that ETH's monetary fundamentals are intact and only the timeframe is in question; a decades-long currency battle argues for patience, not exit, for long-horizon holders.
“if you're looking for the properties of money, right, which is decentralized money... Ether and Ethereum score really well, and nothing else is really close.” ▶ 39:41
Ryan argues Ethereum's residual bull case rests on ETH-as-money fundamentals still intact (DeFi, scaling, coming quantum resistance, and privacy via app-layer Railgun), making private multisig store-of-value a use case unmatched by Bitcoin or Zcash.
thesis🗣 Ryan Sean Adamsconviction: mediumETHEthereumRailgunDeFiBitcoinZcash
So what: Identifies private, programmable store-of-value as ETH's differentiated edge; if privacy ships at scale, it could be the catalyst that David says is currently missing.
“if you're looking to store your value...multisig with privacy like that's not a bad route to go. I mean show me that on Bitcoin, show me that on Zcash.” ▶ 46:16
Vitalik / EF refocus on ETH-the-asset and privacy 3
Vitalik says the Ethereum Foundation is shrinking and running low on ETH, so it is resizing to focus narrowly on 'crops'—censorship resistance, open source, privacy, and security—while pushing the rest of the ecosystem to handle everything else.
thesis🗣 Ryan Sean Adams (citing Vitalik)conviction: highVitalik ButerinEthereum FoundationETH
So what: EF is concentrating on privacy and credible neutrality as the core deliverables; these become the roadmap items most likely to drive ETH's differentiated value.
“Our focus is going to be crops. And so we're sizing the team and the foundation to support uh censorship resistance, open source, privacy, and security.” ▶ 50:13
Vitalik stated unusually directly that the most financially high-value product of Ethereum is ETH-the-asset (securing ~$250B), that the protocol's properties are 'very good for ETH the asset,' and that ~90% of his net worth is in ETH with the remaining ~$40M of on-chain fiat fully allocated.
data_point🗣 Ryan Sean Adams (citing Vitalik)conviction: highVitalik ButerinETHEthereum
So what: A long-awaited explicit endorsement of ETH-the-asset from Vitalik could begin to repair the 'original sin' of neglecting the asset; a narrative/culture pivot bullish for ETH framing.
“the most high value product of the Ethereum blockchain financially speaking is ETH the asset Ethereum secures 250 billion of ETH” ▶ 50:34
Standard Chartered likens ETH-the-asset to Amazon trading post-dotcom-crash in 2001 and projects ETH to ~$40K by 2030, predicated on a doubling of the ETH/BTC ratio.
prediction🗣 Ryan Sean Adams (citing Standard Chartered)conviction: mediumStandard CharteredETHEthereumAmazonETH/BTC ratio
So what: An institutional sell-side bull case ($40K by 2030 via ratio doubling) gives a long-horizon target; the ETH/BTC ratio is the explicit lever to track for this thesis.
“Uh Standard Charter is saying that Ethereum right now ETH the asset is like uh trading like Amazon during 2001... you get ETH by 2030, 40K.” ▶ 48:45
Privacy-as-the-surviving-use-case and Near intents 3
Near's confidential transactions/intents (TEE-based, not ZK cryptography) have reached production adoption—Venice uses it, it enables private ETH-to-ETH transfers routed through Near, and works as cross-chain privacy infrastructure for any integrator—driving real Near revenue and Near's price strength.
causal🗣 David Hoffmanconviction: highNearNear intentsVeniceEthereumTEE
So what: Adoption-plus-revenue, not just narrative, is what separated Near in this rotation; usage-backed privacy infra is the bull signal, and Near is dominating the intents space despite earlier movers.
“this is one of the reasons why Near is up so much because this has actually seen adoption and has led to a bunch of Near revenue.” ▶ 58:04
Block's Cash App (60M MAUs) added multi-chain USDC support (Ethereum, Polygon, Arbitrum, Solana), and Near private intents can route Cash App funds privately to other chains such as Zcash.
data_point🗣 Ryan Sean Adams / David Hoffmanconviction: mediumCash AppBlockUSDCNearZcashEthereumPolygonArbitrumSolana
So what: Mainstream fintech distribution (60M users) plus privacy-routing rails expands real on-ramp demand for stablecoins and privacy assets like Zcash; watch consumer-app integrations as a demand vector.
“you can go from cash app to near private intents to any other blockchain. Zcash for example” ▶ 59:51
Privacy is the recurring thread tying the winners together: the alts pumping (Zcash, Railgun, Near/Venice via confidential intents) and the EF's refocus all center on privacy, suggesting privacy is the use case the market is rewarding in this regime.
thesis🗣 host synthesisconviction: mediumZcashRailgunNearVeniceEthereum Foundationprivacy
So what: Privacy is emerging as the dominant surviving narrative; a thematic basket of privacy infra/assets is where capital and roadmap focus are concentrating.
DeFi AI-vulnerability risk debate 2
The founder of OpenZeppelin has been privately advising friends and family to exit ALL DeFi positions—including low-risk blue chips like MakerDAO and Compound—citing AI coding agents now superhuman at finding smart-contract vulnerabilities.
contrarian🗣 Ryan Sean Adams (citing OpenZeppelin founder)conviction: highOpenZeppelinDeFiMakerDAOCompoundAI
So what: A premier smart-contract security firm's founder calling for a full DeFi exit on AI-exploit risk is a tail-risk flag specific to Ethereum DeFi; reassess protocol exposure under an AI-attacker threat model.
“I've been privately advising friends and family to exit all DeFi positions, including low-risk blue chips like a maker dowo and compound due to coding agents that are superhuman at finding vulnerabilities.” ▶ 51:42
Leading DeFi founders pushed back hard with data: Stani (Aave) called DeFi infra materially more resilient than prior cycles; Mark Zeller said <10% of past-year DeFi issues were code bugs; Jack Sanford said of ~$630M lost in April 2026, over 96% was unrelated to code security; Hayden Adams (Uniswap) called it a supply-chain/opsec problem, not a DeFi problem.
data_point🗣 Ryan Sean Adams (citing Stani, Zeller, Sanford, Hayden Adams)conviction: highAaveStani KulechovUniswapHayden AdamsDeFiMark ZellerJack Sanford
So what: The counter-data reframes losses as opsec/supply-chain rather than contract-code failures; the real risk vector to harden is keys/dependencies, not necessarily audited contract logic.
“$630 million was lo lost in April 2026. Unfortunately, over 96% of those losses were unrelated to code security.” ▶ 52:39
Macro backdrop: Iran, oil, yields, AI equities 6
An Iran peace deal is reportedly near signing (leaked terms: reopen Strait of Hormuz, US lifts blockade, 60-day ceasefire, Hormuz traffic restored within 30 days), but it defers the nuclear concessions to a separate 60-day window, making it a peace deal rather than a nuclear deal, alongside a simultaneous re-escalation of daily kinetic strikes.
data_point🗣 David Hoffmanconviction: mediumIranUnited StatesDonald TrumpStrait of Hormuzoil
So what: Markets priced the leaked deal as legitimate (S&P ATHs, oil down), but unresolved nuclear terms plus ongoing strikes keep tail risk live; a signing or breakdown is a near-term oil/risk catalyst.
“this is firmly a peace deal and not a nuclear concessions deal because Iran really does not want to concede nuclear concessions because it's part of their identity” ▶ 24:43
Brent crude fell back to 'wartime lows' of ~$93-94 (vs prior wartime highs ~$112), but David argues markets need oil to break sustainably below ~$90 to really feel good.
framework🗣 David Hoffmanconviction: mediumBrent crudeoil
So what: A clean break below $90 oil is the host's threshold for a risk-on macro green light; watch that level as a gating condition for crypto/risk recovery.
“we really need oil to break down below a like $90 and meaningfully and move there.” ▶ 26:32
10-year Treasury yields rose even as oil fell this week, breaking their usual co-movement, which David reads as a stagflation warning: sustained high oil feeds into inflation and yields regardless of an Iran resolution.
causal🗣 David Hoffmanconviction: medium10-year Treasury yieldsoilinflationstagflation
So what: Yields decoupling upward from falling oil is an unfavorable, stagflationary signal; even a peace deal may not relieve the rate/inflation pressure weighing on risk assets.
“Yields, 10-year yields are up despite oil being down. And that is not good... The stagflation word comes to mind.” ▶ 26:51
Micron surged into a ~$1.066 trillion market cap, turning $1,000 a year ago into ~$100,000 (a ~100x), illustrating that the AI/memory equity trade is producing crypto-2021-style parabolic momentum charts.
data_point🗣 David Hoffman / Ryan Sean Adamsconviction: highMicronAImemory chipsNASDAQ
So what: Capital is finding crypto-like asymmetric returns inside AI/memory equities instead of crypto; the relative pull of the AI trade is a direct headwind for crypto flows.
“If you had $1,000 of micro stock, Micron stock a year ago, you would now have $100,000.” ▶ 28:14
David frames the AI rally as a 'rolling sector' rotation (memory, then others) analogous to crypto's sector-rotation 'roller coaster,' and argues the AI 'hot ball of money' is currently snowballing/getting bigger rather than leaking and falling apart.
framework🗣 David Hoffmanconviction: mediumAIMicronequities
So what: As long as the AI money snowballs and rotates between sub-sectors, it keeps drawing capital away from crypto; a stall in that snowball would be the signal capital might rotate back.
“the question is like when it moves from sector to sector does the hot ball of money like snowball or does it leak and fall apart? And right now I think you can definitely argue that it is snowballing” ▶ 29:00
Hosts flag a '2021-like' speculative top tell: with equities at ATHs, alternative collectibles like Pokemon cards (and tokenized Pokemon card marketplaces), baseball cards and Rolexes are also pricing at all-time highs.
contrarian🗣 David Hoffmanconviction: lowPokemon cardscollectiblesequities
So what: Broad-based collectible euphoria is a classic late-cycle/froth signal; a warning sign that risk appetite may be peaking even as crypto majors weaken.
“it feels a little 2021y in that like equity markets are at alltime highs and then like weird [ __ ] like baseball cards and Rolexes are being priced at all time high.” ▶ 29:39
Stablecoins, treasuries-of-AI, and tail stories 3
SoFi launched SoFi USD, claiming the first US national-bank-issued, GENIUS-Act-compliant stablecoin embedded directly into a retail banking app (~15M users); on-chain it shows ~$100M on Ethereum versus only ~$26,000 on Solana.
data_point🗣 David Hoffman / Ryan Sean Adamsconviction: highSoFiSoFi USDstablecoinGENIUS ActEthereumSolana
So what: Bank-issued, compliant stablecoins distributed to retail are arriving, and Ethereum is capturing the overwhelming majority of issuance vs Solana—an ETH settlement-layer tailwind even amid ETH price weakness.
“If you go to the SoFi stablecoin on Salana, there's 26,000 uh dollars worth of SoFi. If you go to Ethereum, there's a hundred million.” ▶ 59:00
Andrew Kang (back from a robotics detour) launched 'Robo Strategy' (NASDAQ: BOT), a closed-end fund holding private robotics/physical-AI names (Figure AI, Apptronik, Dyna Robotics, Standard Bots) using a MicroStrategy-style ATM—issuing equity to buy more when shares trade above NAV—with Nick Carter joining as independent director.
anecdote🗣 David Hoffmanconviction: mediumAndrew KangRobo StrategyBOTNick CarterFigure AIApptronikroboticsMicroStrategy
So what: Crypto operators are porting the MicroStrategy treasury playbook into AI/robotics equities; the model only works while shares trade above NAV and buyers pay the premium—two big ifs to watch.
“if the NAV trades above if if the asset trades above NAV, they will issue new equity to purchase more shares of the robotics company. So, it's kind of doing the micro strategy ATM thing.” ▶ 56:36
A pseudonymous claimant ('Noah Do') filed in NY Supreme Court to claim ~3.8M dormant (6-10yr) Satoshi-era Bitcoin (~18% of supply, ~$285B) under 1958-era Finders Keepers/abandoned-property law, having found the wallets via a block explorer and dusted them with notice.
anecdote🗣 Ryan Sean Adams / David Hoffmanconviction: lowBitcoinSatoshiNew York Supreme Court
So what: A long-shot legal bet (<0.01% odds per hosts) that only pays off in a quantum-recovery scenario where a US entity seizes Satoshi coins; mainly a reminder of the ~18%-of-supply dormant overhang and quantum tail risk.
“the only way that this actually pays off for the individual assuming Satoshi's wallets are burned is that quantum comes” ▶ 1:02:19
SBF, Gensler, Warren and Crypto’s War for Washington61 min37 insights2026-06-04
▶ Watch on YouTube
Wintermute policy head Ron Hammond maps the 2026 state of crypto-in-Washington: the Clarity Act market-structure bill is in final agriculture/banking merge with a self-handicapped ~45% pass odds, the bank lobby has pivoted from attacking stablecoin yield to attacking AML to kill the bill, Warren/Gensler-era hostility has faded as Democrats break ranks, and the next fights are crypto tax, DeFi, and self-custody/privacy. Recurring edge: DC narratives are manufactured via proxy groups and planted letters, so on-chain/Twitter transparency now lets you front-run regulatory catalysts.
Clarity Act timeline & pass odds · 6Bank lobby tactics vs. crypto · 4Manufactured narratives & proxy lobbying · 5Warren/Gensler era unwinding · 5Democratic control = key downside scenario · 4Trump conflicts & national-security overhang · 4Next battlegrounds: tax, DeFi, self-custody, privacy · 5Industry coordination & UK onshoring · 4
Clarity Act timeline & pass odds 6
The Clarity Act (crypto market-structure bill) is in final reconciliation of its agriculture and banking committee versions, with the contested language being 'ancillary asset' definitions where two-or-three-word changes carry large legal-classification consequences.
data_point🗣 Ron Hammond (Wintermute head of policy)conviction: highClarity ActCFTCSECancillary assets
So what: How 'ancillary asset' is defined determines whether tokens fall under SEC or CFTC jurisdiction — watch the merged text for which assets get commodity treatment.
“there's differences of like ancillary asset definitions and like all these are kind of small you know two or three words but they mean like the world for the legal world” ▶ 49:46
Senator Lummis wants a Clarity Act vote announced before July 4th and held before end of July, putting a best-case presidential signing around September 2026.
prediction🗣 Ron Hammondconviction: mediumCynthia LummisClarity Act
So what: A concrete legislative calendar — a July vote announcement is the near-term catalyst to watch for crypto market-structure clarity.
“the goal from Lumis is that she wants a vote announced before July 4th and then have a vote before end of July which would end up in the best case scenario being like September time frame” ▶ 50:01
Hammond puts Clarity Act passage odds at ~45%, explicitly below Galaxy's Alex Thorne's 70% estimate.
prediction🗣 Ron Hammondconviction: mediumClarity ActAlex Thorne
So what: An insider lobbyist is more bearish than a sell-side voice — a coin-flip on passage means hedging, not pricing in clarity as a base case.
“I'm at 45%. I know Alex Thorne went on your podcast said 70%. I'm at 45.” ▶ 49:36
If the Clarity Act fails in 2026, crypto market-structure law likely slips to 2028–2030 because it almost certainly won't advance with Democrats controlling either chamber.
causal🗣 Ron Hammondconviction: highClarity ActDemocratsRepublicans
So what: The 2026 window is binary — a miss means a multi-year regulatory vacuum, raising the stakes of the July vote for US-listed crypto names.
“if it doesn't happen this year, it could be like 2028, 2030 um when this would get get done” ▶ 50:57
The Clarity Act now has crypto market-structure provisions entangled with executive-branch ethics provisions aimed at Trump's crypto conflicts, a non-crypto fight that could stall the bill.
causal🗣 Ron Hammondconviction: highClarity ActDonald TrumpWorld Liberty FinancialDemocrats
So what: Bill risk is now driven by ethics/corruption politics rather than crypto policy itself — passage could hinge on Trump-ethics negotiations, not token rules.
“now we're taking a crypto regulatory bill and we're throwing in a bunch of ethic provisions on the executive branch” ▶ 47:40
Both sides are softening on the Clarity Act ethics fight: the Trump camp moved from 'no ethics whatsoever' to openness to dialogue with Democrats, but no actual text proposals exist yet.
data_point🗣 Ron Hammondconviction: mediumDonald TrumpDemocratsClarity Act
So what: Softening signals a negotiated path is possible; absence of text means the deal is unconfirmed — watch for the first ethics-language draft as the real signal.
“the Trump camp went from like no ethics whatsoever to saying we are open to having dialogue with Democrats to find a path forward” ▶ 50:29
Bank lobby tactics vs. crypto 4
The bank lobby has conceded the stablecoin-yield fight — the yield amendment is locked and can't get 60 Senate votes to change — and pivoted to attacking the bill on AML/BSA grounds to kill it entirely.
thesis🗣 Ron Hammondconviction: highJamie DimonJPMorganClarity ActstablecoinsAMLBSA
So what: Stablecoin yield is effectively safe; the live threat to the bill is now the AML narrative, so watch whether banks' 'no AML' claim gains traction despite ~70 pages of AML text.
“the banks have now pivoted from the yield issue because they know they've lost that issue... now Jamie Diamond, all these other CEOs are saying, "Oh, there's an AML issue."” ▶ 27:12
Jamie Dimon publicly criticized the Clarity Act over the weekend, signaling JPMorgan is leading the bank pushback as the bill nears a vote.
data_point🗣 Ron Hammondconviction: highJamie DimonJPMorganClarity Act
So what: Top-bank-CEO opposition is the institutional counter-lobby to track; Dimon's framing previews the AML kill-the-bill narrative.
“Jamie Diamond, we just crap on the Clarity Act this weekend” ▶ 27:09
The crypto lobby has grown from ~4 lobbyists in 2016-17 to roughly 200 today, still far smaller than the bank lobby but repeatedly out-maneuvering it.
data_point🗣 Ron Hammondconviction: highcrypto lobbybanks
So what: Quantifies how fast crypto's DC footprint scaled — a structural tailwind for favorable legislation despite being outspent.
“when I started... 2016 2017 or four lobbyists maybe right... and now I think it's probably 200 probably. It's still a lot smaller than the banks have but... we've beaten the banks at their own game several times” ▶ 25:40
Banks use a 'rural community bank will die' fearmongering frame against stablecoin yield (e.g. small-town Nebraska losing all lending), which Hammond characterizes as empirically baseless BS.
framework🗣 Ron Hammondconviction: highbanksstablecoinscommunity banks
So what: Recognize the community-bank-collapse argument as a rhetorical device, not a real disintermediation forecast, when assessing stablecoin-yield headline risk.
“that mom and pop bank that's keep your town alive in rural Nebraska um if yield goes through that small town Nebraska won't have any lending whatsoever... which is complete BS” ▶ 26:09
Manufactured narratives & proxy lobbying 5
Opposition to crypto bills is frequently laundered through unrelated proxy groups — housing groups, AFL-CIO, and progressive orgs came out against the Clarity Act despite not being affected by it, plausibly fronting for banks like Wells Fargo.
framework🗣 Ron Hammondconviction: highAFL-CIOWells FargoClarity Acthousing groups
So what: When 'grassroots' opposition to a crypto bill appears, trace the funding — the visible opponent is often a proxy for an incumbent financial interest.
“We had like a bunch of housing groups the AFL CIO and a bunch other random progressive groups come out against the bill even though in the most part if not all the times like they're not touched by it” ▶ 17:46
Lawmakers' attack letters are often ghostwritten verbatim by interested parties; Hammond cites a short-seller (Marc Cohodes) who shopped a pre-drafted letter on Silicon Valley Bank / Silvergate that Warren then read 'word for word' shortly before the bank collapsed.
anecdote🗣 Ron Hammondconviction: highElizabeth WarrenMarc CohodesSilicon Valley BankSilvergateshort sellers
So what: A regulatory/political letter can be a short thesis in disguise — treat senator letters that precede a price collapse as potential trade signals, not neutral oversight.
“she led the letter like word for word from the short seller on Silicon Valley Bank and then what like two weeks ago or afterwards it like goes under” ▶ 20:39
Allegation-based letters with no evidence can trigger investigations that generate headlines sufficient to short an asset; Hammond cites ICE and CME writing the CFTC and Agriculture committees claiming Hyperliquid 'could be involved' in sanctions violations and money laundering.
causal🗣 Ron Hammondconviction: highICECMEHyperliquidCFTCsanctions
So what: An evidence-free 'could be involved' letter from incumbent exchanges is a competitive weapon — Hyperliquid faces headline/investigation risk engineered by ICE/CME rivals.
“ICE and CME had a letter on Hyperlid two weeks ago... Hyperlid could be involved could be involved in sanctions violation and money laundering. Again, they didn't have any evidence” ▶ 23:29
Regulators plant narratives in Wall Street Journal / Financial Times to pre-justify new authority requests (e.g. 'North Korea uses crypto' ahead of a Treasury sanction-authority proposal), so adverse mainstream articles often precede a regulatory action.
framework🗣 Ron Hammondconviction: highTreasuryWall Street JournalFinancial TimesNorth KoreaOFAC
So what: A sudden negative WSJ/FT crypto piece can be a leading indicator of an imminent Treasury/OFAC action — useful as an early-warning signal for sanction-authority risk.
“they go to Wall Street Journal Financial Times... it's usually like a plant from a regulator or interest party... North Korea is using crypto... oh and by the way, Treasury tomorrow is going to propose new authority” ▶ 46:26
On-chain and Twitter transparency now lets observers identify who is behind a lobbying attack (e.g. exposing a letter's author as a short seller), creating an information edge versus the pre-social-media era.
thesis🗣 Ron Hammondconviction: mediumTwittershort sellers
So what: Doing source diligence on who is pushing a regulatory narrative is now a tradable edge — the manipulator's identity is often discoverable before the market reacts.
“now you go on Twitter and it's like who's like Mark Hodis it's like oh this guy's a short seller this guy's probably going to make a lot of money on this stuff” ▶ 23:09
Warren/Gensler era unwinding 5
Elizabeth Warren's political power has measurably declined: when she physically posts at the Senate vote desk, the first 2-3 Democrats to defy her now trigger ~15 more to follow, a herding dynamic that broke her former deterrence.
framework🗣 Ron Hammondconviction: highElizabeth WarrenSenate DemocratsAngela Alsobrooks
So what: Warren can no longer reliably whip Democratic votes against crypto bills — reduces a key downside risk to pro-crypto legislation passing.
“the second that like two or three senators and Democrats side pass Liza Warren sign their vote, you notice about 15 more come afterwards” ▶ 34:48
Warren and the anti-crypto Democrats were primarily 'carrying Gary Gensler's water,' and Gensler's SEC took dinners and multiple meetings with FTX, giving FTX special access relative to other firms.
thesis🗣 Ron Hammondconviction: highGary GenslerSECElizabeth WarrenFTX
So what: Recasts the prior enforcement regime as personality/vendetta-driven and compromised — context for why the policy reversal under Trump has been so sharp.
“they were mostly carrying Gary Gensler's water here... he was taking dinners with FTX he was doing multiple meetings with FTX” ▶ 35:20
The SEC's suit against Coinbase was timed to land the day before Coinbase testified to the House Agriculture Committee, which Hammond reads as a deliberate political message rather than a legal necessity.
anecdote🗣 Ron Hammondconviction: highSECCoinbaseGary GenslerHouse Agriculture Committee
So what: Illustrates how enforcement timing was weaponized for narrative — a cautionary template for how a future hostile SEC could disrupt a company's DC moment.
“the SEC sued Coinbase the day of they were testifying in front of House Agriculture Committee and that was so political. The timing was so nefarious” ▶ 35:37
Prometheum functioned as essentially a Gensler/SEC-aligned front in hearings — its witness read talking points lifted directly from Gensler and Maxine Waters' staff — and the company is now effectively irrelevant in the industry.
anecdote🗣 Ron Hammondconviction: highPrometheumGary GenslerMaxine WatersSEC
So what: The 'compliant counter-example' company strategy failed and discredited itself — a reminder that regulator-endorsed industry tokens lacked durable legitimacy.
“every talking point is directly from Gary Gensler. It's directly from Maxine Waters staff... I think they're irrelevant at this point” ▶ 39:09
Warren will not pivot on crypto under any circumstances, and DC now broadly reads her as reflexively 'anti-something' rather than for anything.
prediction🗣 Ron Hammondconviction: highElizabeth Warren
So what: Don't expect any crypto détente from Warren personally; her opposition is a fixed, increasingly discounted constant in vote math.
“she's still she she doesn't pivot. She never does.” ▶ 41:30
Democratic control = key downside scenario 4
If Democrats retake the Senate, committee chairs would be Elizabeth Warren and Maxine Waters, and because Democrats have no committee-chair term limits (unlike the GOP's 6-year cap) these gatekeepers could block crypto bills indefinitely.
framework🗣 Ron Hammondconviction: highElizabeth WarrenMaxine WatersDemocratsRepublicans
So what: The single biggest structural risk to crypto legislation is a Democratic chamber flip handing agenda control to Warren/Waters — a macro-political variable to weight in 2026/2028 positioning.
“Democrats you can be in those seats for life... if they win it be Alyssa Warren and Maxine Waters” ▶ 33:06
Sherrod Brown, the anti-crypto 'lieutenant' to Warren who killed a stablecoin bill three years ago (despite Senator Toomey's sign-off) and lost his seat in 2024, is running again as newly 'pro-crypto' — a pivot Hammond explicitly distrusts.
anecdote🗣 Ron Hammondconviction: highSherrod BrownPat Toomeystablecoin billFairshake
So what: A Brown win would make him Senate Agriculture chairman alongside Warren — his election-cycle pro-crypto conversion is a credibility risk, not a reliable ally signal.
“I don't trust post pivot during election cycle while potentially trying to court crypto money” ▶ 43:13
Newer-generation Democrats are breaking ranks to admit the party 'screwed up' on crypto under Gensler in 2024, signaling a generational shift away from the old anti-crypto guard.
thesis🗣 Ron Hammondconviction: mediumDemocratsGary GenslerAngela Alsobrooks
So what: A long-term tailwind: younger Democrats becoming crypto-tolerant erodes the durability of the anti-crypto bloc even if the old guard retains chairs near-term.
“the Democrats on the newer side... like hey like we we kind of screwed up here um on the crypto side in 2024 underneath Gensler” ▶ 34:05
Crypto's electoral wins in 2024 (via Fairshake and allied PACs) materially affected races and are now actively deterring politicians in 2026 from opposing the industry.
causal🗣 Ron Hammondconviction: highFairshakeDemocrats2024 election
So what: Crypto PAC money is a demonstrated electoral force — a standing deterrent that supports favorable legislative odds independent of any single bill.
“credit to Fairshake and all these other groups that really stepped up in 2024... we had material effects in the 2024 election that's now scaring a lot of people right now in 2026” ▶ 15:30
Trump conflicts & national-security overhang 4
The Saudi investment angle into Trump's World Liberty Financial reframed his crypto conflicts as a legitimate national-security concern that 'kickstarted everything,' and is more damaging than the earlier memecoin story.
causal🗣 Ron Hammondconviction: highDonald TrumpWorld Liberty FinancialSaudi Arabianational security
So what: The Saudi/WLF national-security narrative is the live political liability dragging on the Clarity Act — distinct from generic 'crypto is scammy' attacks and harder to dismiss.
“it's more the hook of like Saudi uh investment getting involved. Like that story that dropped a couple months ago like that really kickstarted everything” ▶ 47:18
Trump-affiliated crypto ventures (the Trump memecoin, World Liberty Financial) involve a 'whole new cast of characters' outside the established Coinbase/Kraken/Binance policy world and will draw heavy hearing scrutiny if Democrats take a chamber.
thesis🗣 Ron Hammondconviction: mediumDonald TrumpWorld Liberty FinancialCoinbaseKrakenBinance
So what: Headline/hearing risk is concentrated in Trump-adjacent crypto entities, not the mainstream exchanges — useful for distinguishing idiosyncratic political risk from sector-wide risk.
“it wasn't the people that we dealt with like you know Kraken or Coinbase or Binance... there's just a whole new like cast of characters” ▶ 44:52
Hammond assesses that Trump's crypto conflicts have damaged the broader industry's reputation less than he feared, with most Republicans still solidly pro-crypto.
thesis🗣 Ron Hammondconviction: mediumDonald TrumpRepublicanscrypto industry
So what: Contains the contagion thesis — Trump's conflicts are being read as Trump-specific rather than indicting crypto, supporting continued GOP legislative support.
“actually it's not as bad as I thought it' be” ▶ 46:16
Democrats may try to extend a single bad actor's sins to the whole industry — e.g. arguing that because World Liberty Financial got an OCC charter, no other crypto firm should be granted one.
prediction🗣 Ron Hammondconviction: mediumWorld Liberty FinancialOCCDemocratsbank charters
So what: Crypto bank-charter access is a contested post-Clarity battleground — watch for WLF being used as the wedge to freeze OCC charters industry-wide.
“if Worldly Financial got an OCC charter, no one else in crypto should get another OCC charter” ▶ 51:39
Next battlegrounds: tax, DeFi, self-custody, privacy 5
Crypto tax provisions — mark-to-market for digital-asset exchanges and a de minimis exemption — are advancing, with a Ways and Means hearing imminent, though final enactment likely waits for the next tax bill.
data_point🗣 Ron Hammondconviction: mediumcrypto taxWays and Means Committeede minimismark-to-market
So what: A de minimis exemption and mark-to-market treatment would materially change US crypto usage and trading economics — track the Ways and Means hearing and the next tax bill.
“things like Litecoin exchange mark to market dimminimus like these are huge T proposals... the next tax bill is we're going to see a tax provision there on crypto. It will be huge.” ▶ 51:57
DeFi regulation is the next frontier nobody has solved: the US deliberately deferred it to do centralized-exchange rules first, while the EU and UK are now attempting DeFi rules and will likely 'mess it up.'
thesis🗣 Ron Hammondconviction: mediumDeFiEUUKMiCA
So what: DeFi remains a regulatory open question globally — first-mover EU/UK rules could set adverse precedents; an unresolved area where US framing is still up for grabs.
“the EU is now trying to tackle DeFi regulation, so they're going to probably mess it up”
Self-custody protection is becoming a defined policy fight — Warren Davidson has made it his core mantra — because self-custody has been targeted by both Republicans and Democrats.
thesis🗣 Ron Hammondconviction: highself-custodyWarren Davidson
So what: Self-custody is an under-the-radar but bipartisan-contested issue — a regulatory risk vector for wallets and non-custodial infrastructure worth monitoring.
“self custody has been about my old my old boss Warren Davidson like that's now his new mantra has been self-custody protection” ▶ 53:40
Privacy is gaining ground via a 'horseshoe' coalition where the most progressive and most conservative members align against national-security hawks, with banks drifting to the Democratic/hawk side and Republicans toward the pro-privacy tech side.
framework🗣 Ron Hammondconviction: mediumprivacyRepublicansDemocratsnational security hawksbanks
So what: Privacy coalitions are realigning along an unexpected horseshoe — a structural opening for privacy-preserving crypto tech, though the security-hawk bloc remains larger.
“the most progressives and the most conservatives. They really do meet in the middle on privacy of all issues and they're against the national security hawks” ▶ 53:55
Congress may be more amenable to financial privacy than the executive agencies (Treasury, OFAC), because regulators are institutionally incentivized to maximize data collection while representatives answer to a public that is fatigued with surveillance.
framework🗣 Ron Hammondconviction: mediumCongressTreasuryOFACprivacy
So what: Privacy advocacy may find more traction in Congress than at agencies — but Congress 'folds a lot' after any bad incident, so the path is fragile.
“the regulators like you have more incentive to get as much data as possible... But the American people are kind of done with it” ▶ 54:59
Industry coordination & UK onshoring 4
The crypto lobby has consolidated into a unified front — L1 competitors, DeFi, exchanges, market makers and token projects now coordinate in DC despite competing in the market — making it costly for any single actor to defect and cut a self-serving deal, as SBF tried.
framework🗣 Ron Hammondconviction: highBlockchain AssociationCoinbaseCrypto Council for InnovationCoin CenterDeFi Education FundSolana Policy InstituteConsensys
So what: Industry unity is a durable structural strength that raised crypto's legislative win rate — defection (SBF/FTX-style carve-outs) is now socially and politically punished.
“there's an L1 group and it's like all the L1 lobbyists from all across spectrum are all coordinating like hey what's good for us is good for us entirely and we'll compete about it outside DC” ▶ 29:40
Wintermute, despite the common 'we'll leave the US' lobbying threat, actually expanded into the US after Trump won, calling it the number-one market — a revealed preference contradicting industry exit rhetoric.
data_point🗣 Ron Hammondconviction: highWintermuteUnited StatesDonald Trump
So what: Firms are voting with their feet toward the US post-Trump — supports the thesis that favorable US policy is pulling crypto liquidity onshore.
“they actually operate United States and they came to United States after Trump won saying like, hey, like this is the number one market. We're here to play.” ▶ 52:50
The UK is reversing course on Biden-era-style 'owner' regulations after recognizing firms are leaving, and is now actively trying to onshore crypto firms and undo rules it newly deems bad.
data_point🗣 Ron Hammondconviction: mediumUKParliamentcrypto regulation
So what: Regulatory competition is intensifying — a UK turnaround to retain firms signals jurisdictional arbitrage opportunities and a softer UK stance worth tracking.
“they've kind of recognized everyone's leaving the UK... they're now saying like, "Oh, we realize these are bad now... By the way, don't leave the UK, please."” ▶ 52:41
Hammond frames competitive battles as increasingly fought through proxies across sectors — citing an 'anthropic battle' in AI run entirely through proxies and the American Gaming Association going through the Mormon church to fight prediction markets.
framework🗣 Ron Hammondconviction: mediumAnthropicAmerican Gaming Associationprediction marketsMormon church
So what: Proxy-war lobbying is a cross-sector pattern (AI, gaming, prediction markets) — applies the same who's-really-behind-this diligence to non-crypto policy fights affecting adjacent assets.
“the American Gaming Association has been going through the Mormon church to go against prediction markets” ▶ 18:22
Is Saylor About to Break Bitcoin?58 min49 insights2026-06-05
▶ Watch on YouTube
A Bankless rollup dissecting the reflexive risk in crypto treasury companies (DATs): Saylor's small Bitcoin sale signaled weakness and accelerated a selloff, MSTR's Stretch preferred trades 5% below par creating a downward feedback loop, while Bitmine/Tom Lee launch a 9.5% ETH-yield instrument. Hosts debate whether Saylor can ever sell again, whether these yield instruments are Ponzis, why revenue-generating tokens are decoupling from BTC, and why onshore US perps will likely underwhelm versus offshore venues.
Saylor/MSTR reflexivity and the no-sell trap · 6Stretch preferred as a confidence canary · 4Bitmine/Tom Lee ETH-yield instrument vs MSTR model · 7ETH concentration and Ethereum consensus risk · 5DAT yield instruments: Ponzi or not / leverage framing · 4ETF outflows and flow-driven market structure · 5Token decoupling: revenue-driven altcoins resist BTC drawdown · 8US onshore perps: regulatory greenlight, structural skepticism · 6Coinbase x Ethena DeFi-mullet yield · 4
Saylor/MSTR reflexivity and the no-sell trap 6
Strategy (MSTR) sold only 32 BTC for ~$2.5M, but the disclosure of the sale on Tuesday accelerated a Bitcoin selloff from ~$72,000 down to ~$62,000 (~17%), demonstrating the sale's signaling effect far outweighed its tiny size.
data_point🗣 host (David)conviction: highMicroStrategyMSTRMichael SaylorBitcoinBTC
So what: Any future Saylor BTC sale, however small, is now a market-moving negative signal — watch MSTR disclosures as a sentiment catalyst.
“Sailor and strategy sold 32 Bitcoin for $2.5 million. A very small sale.”
The market has effectively forbidden Saylor from selling Bitcoin: because he is 'Mr. Bitcoin' and a core reason BTC keeps rising, any sale breaks the narrative, so he has likely internalized never to sell again regardless of distress.
thesis🗣 guest (Hib)conviction: highMichael SaylorMicroStrategyBitcoin
So what: MSTR's optionality to delever via BTC sales is functionally gone — distress will be resolved by equity dilution or skipping preferred dividends, not selling, capping the downside-protection thesis.
“Mr. Bitcoin doesn't get to sell. So, if you want to be Mr. Bitcoin, like either one, you got to throw the stretch holders under the bus”
Saylor faces a zero-sum trilemma where one stakeholder must lose: he can skip/defer Stretch preferred dividends (screws preferred holders), dilute common via ATM issuance (screws common holders), or sell Bitcoin (breaks the whole system) — the third option is off the table.
framework🗣 guest (Hib)conviction: highMicroStrategyMSTRStretchMichael SaylorBitcoin
So what: MSTR preferred and common holders are structurally misaligned; in distress, watch whether MSTR defers dividends or hits the ATM to gauge which class gets sacrificed.
“one way or another, one of them has to lose because the third category of Bitcoin gets screwed does not work.”
Saylor lost far more in NAV from the Bitcoin price drop triggered by his 32-BTC sale than he could have raised in cash, proving that funding obligations via BTC sales is net value-destructive for MSTR.
causal🗣 guest (Hib)conviction: highMicroStrategyBitcoinMichael Saylor
So what: Confirms BTC sales are a self-defeating funding source for large DATs — reinforces dilution as the only viable lever.
“He lost so much more money in his nav because of how much Bitcoin went down from that sale relative to the amount of cash you would need to raise”
The contrarian alternative view is that Saylor should take a single large one-time BTC sale of ~$500-600M to fund Stretch dividends for 3+ years and restore confidence, then never sell again — the 'least bad' way if he must sell at all.
contrarian🗣 host (David)conviction: mediumMichael SaylorMicroStrategyStretchBitcoin
So what: If MSTR ever does a single large pre-funding BTC sale, it could be read as confidence-restoring rather than capitulation — a non-consensus interpretation to watch.
“I think Sailor just needs to take it on the chin, make a large single cell of Bitcoin, and that's it. And then he just clears it.”
MSTR now holds such a large share of the Bitcoin market that it has become 'the market' — the DAT endgame — so if it voluntarily capitulates, other holders front-run by selling, unlike its smaller 2022 BTC sale which markets absorbed.
framework🗣 guest (Hib)conviction: highMicroStrategyBitcoinDATs
So what: Position-size reflexivity means the largest BTC treasuries lose the ability to transact without moving price against themselves — a structural fragility as DATs scale.
“this is kind of the endgame for DATs is like once you become big enough you are the market.”
Stretch preferred as a confidence canary 4
MSTR's Stretch preferred trades at ~$95.3, about 5% below its $100 peg, with a market cap of ~$9.9B versus a ~$10.5B par — roughly $561M below par — signaling eroding market confidence.
data_point🗣 host (David)conviction: highStretchMicroStrategyMSTR
So what: Stretch's discount to par is a real-time confidence gauge for the MSTR financing machine — watch it as a leading indicator of forced action.
“Stretch is trading at $95 $95.3. That's about 5% off of its peg... We're we're $561 million below par.”
Stretch below par creates a self-reinforcing downward spiral: traders anticipate Saylor must sell Bitcoin to fund the dividend and close the gap, so they front-run by selling their own BTC, pushing price down further.
causal🗣 host (David)conviction: highStretchBitcoinMichael SaylorMicroStrategy
So what: The Stretch discount and BTC price are reflexively linked — a widening discount can mechanically pressure spot BTC via front-running flows.
≈ “I'm going to get out of the way and I'm going to sell my Bitcoin and so it's is creating a downward a downwards feedback downward spiral.” paraphrase
Stretch dividends run over $1 billion per year while MSTR has only ~$700M cash on hand, meaning the company cannot self-fund the preferred dividend from cash and must dilute or defer.
data_point🗣 host (David)conviction: mediumStretchMicroStrategyMSTR
So what: The dividend-vs-cash gap quantifies MSTR's funding squeeze; deferral or ATM dilution becomes near-inevitable in a prolonged downturn.
≈ “the stretch dividends are like over a billion a year and he has 700 million on hand” paraphrase
Stretch's structure gives MSTR discretion to skip/defer preferred dividends (they accrue and eventually pay), giving Saylor a release valve to avoid selling Bitcoin in distress.
data_point🗣 guest (Hib)conviction: highStretchMicroStrategy
So what: Stretch holders bear deferral risk; the favorable-to-issuer terms mean preferred holders, not BTC, absorb stress first.
“you can miss a dividend and and you know, they have the discretion to do that.”
Bitmine/Tom Lee ETH-yield instrument vs MSTR model 7
Bitmine (Tom Lee) filed for a Stretch-style preferred stock offering yielding 9.5%, launched amid market weakness, signaling intent to accelerate ETH accumulation rather than slow down.
data_point🗣 host (David)conviction: highBitmineTom LeeEthereumETHStretch
So what: Bitmine is replicating the DAT preferred-yield playbook on ETH — a 9.5% coupon is the new benchmark to compare against Stretch and gauge ETH-DAT financing demand.
“Bitmine has filed for a preferred stock offering offering a yield of 9.5%.”
Bitmine is structurally advantaged over MSTR because ETH has a native staking yield, so it can fund the 9.5% preferred coupon by selling staking yield rather than selling principal ETH — meaning no net new ETH sales required.
thesis🗣 guest (Hib)conviction: mediumBitmineEthereumETHTom LeeMicroStrategy
So what: ETH-based DATs have a narratively cleaner funding model than BTC-based ones; favors Bitmine's structure over MSTR's on a financing-resilience basis.
“because Ether has a yield, you can actually pay the yield uh here by just selling the yield and not selling the the principal.”
Funding ETH-DAT yield from staking is economically the same as selling principal — it is ETH inflation moving from one pocket to another, with someone still needing cash to buy the sold ETH; the difference is narrative, not economics.
contrarian🗣 guest (Hib)conviction: highBitmineEthereumETH
So what: Don't be fooled by the 'still accumulating' framing — ETH staking-funded yield still generates real sell pressure; the bull case rests on narrative-driven flows, not superior economics.
“I think that is a narrative transformation, not an actual economic transformation, per se, because of course that's, you know, inflation to the total e supply.”
Whether ETH staking yield is 'real' depends on the EIP-1559 burn: with no burn the staking yield is just issuance (nominal), but if L1 burn rises the yield becomes real — making increased ETH burn a precondition for the bull case on Bitmine's instrument.
framework🗣 host (David)conviction: mediumEthereumETHBitmineEIP-1559
So what: Watch ETH net issuance/burn dynamics as the variable that converts Bitmine's yield instrument from a dilution shell game into a real-yield product.
“with the burn that that yield goes from nominal to real. And that is the strength here that I see”
Tom Lee/Bitmine has a potential second-mover advantage by learning from Saylor's mistake of aggressively over-issuing Stretch, whose negative consequences are now surfacing.
thesis🗣 host (David)conviction: mediumTom LeeBitmineMichael SaylorStretchMicroStrategy
So what: Later-entrant ETH DATs may price/size their preferred issuance more conservatively, reducing reflexive blowup risk relative to MSTR.
“Tom Lee can just learn from Sailor's mistakes. I think Sailor... really just issued the crap out of Stretch aggressively”
Issuing a yield instrument is inconsistent with Bitmine's stated plan to cap ETH holdings at 5% of supply and slow purchases near 4.5% — a yield offering is a 'speed-up' move, suggesting Tom Lee will likely abandon the 5% cap as he approaches it.
prediction🗣 guest (Hib)conviction: mediumBitmineTom LeeEthereumETH
So what: Expect Bitmine to raise or scrap its 5% ETH-supply target; a removed cap reignites the accumulation narrative and the stock's premium.
“this um you know this offering doesn't make a lot of sense if you're slowing down, right? This is a speed up kind of offering.”
Tom Lee is personally incentivized to grow Bitmine as large as possible because his compensation scales with the entity's size, making the 5% self-imposed cap unlikely to hold.
causal🗣 guest (Hib)conviction: mediumTom LeeBitmineETH
So what: Manager incentives point toward maximal ETH accumulation — a structural driver of persistent ETH bid from Bitmine.
“Tom Lee gets paid the bigger this this thing becomes. It becomes really big he makes more money.”
ETH concentration and Ethereum consensus risk 5
Unlike Bitcoin (proof-of-work, ownership-neutral), owning large ETH and staking it confers governance/consensus power; ~40% of ETH is staked, so an entity needs ~33% of staked ETH (~13% of total supply) to threaten consensus.
framework🗣 guest (Hib)conviction: highEthereumETHBitmineBitcoin
So what: ETH DATs have a hard ceiling that BTC DATs lack — at ~13% of supply an ETH treasury becomes a consensus-security risk, capping how large Bitmine can safely grow.
“you have to get to 33% of total state teeth in order to basically be able to cause a a major failure in consensus. And so that takes you to what is that 13%.”
At 4.5% of ETH supply (~$10B), Bitmine is only about 2.5x away from the ~13% consensus-threat threshold (~$25B at current prices), meaning there is little headroom before it would break Ethereum's security model.
data_point🗣 guest (Hib)conviction: highBitmineEthereumETH
So what: Bitmine's runway for accumulation is far shorter than MSTR's effectively unlimited BTC runway — a structural growth cap unique to ETH DATs.
“if uh if Tom Lee goes to 13%, that's like a two and a half times where he currently is. Tom Lee is about 10 billion.”
A public-company ETH steward subpoenaed to enforce OFAC compliance could pressure Ethereum's credible neutrality (e.g., refusing to validate non-compliant transactions), creating a governance-capture risk if Bitmine controls a third of staked ETH.
thesis🗣 guest (Hib)conviction: lowBitmineEthereumETHOFAC
So what: A regulated megaholder introduces censorship/neutrality risk to Ethereum — a tail risk for ETH's value narrative if DAT concentration continues.
“if you have one public company that is the major, like the largest steward of Ethereum and has the ability to kind of stop the show if they want to.”
If Bitmine grows too large it could mitigate consensus-capture concerns by routing ETH into DeFi instead of staking, delegating to third-party stakers like Lido, or pledging ETH into blind trusts with independent governance.
framework🗣 guest (Hib)conviction: mediumBitmineEthereumETHLidoDeFi
So what: Watch for Bitmine governance-distancing structures (blind trusts, third-party staking) as a tell that it is approaching consensus-relevant scale.
“maybe even we put it into blind trusts and those trusts are responsible for staking”
Bitmine is in pole position to become the institutional economic advocate for ETH-the-asset, filling the vacuum left by the Ethereum Foundation's intentional step-back and EF brain-drain.
thesis🗣 host (David)conviction: mediumBitmineEthereum FoundationETHTom Lee
So what: A financially-aligned megaholder advocating for ETH financialization and market competitiveness could be a structural ETH bull catalyst, offsetting EF passivity.
“I seere increasingly Bitmine being positioned to take that torch.”
DAT yield instruments: Ponzi or not / leverage framing 4
DAT yield instruments are not Ponzis because a Ponzi is guaranteed to fail, whereas these are risky leveraged bets whose survival depends on volatility and the asset's demand floor — small leverage on BTC bought at $5K may never get margin-called.
framework🗣 guest (Hib)conviction: highDATsBitcoinStretchMicroStrategyBitmine
So what: Reframe DATs as variable-leverage trusts, not frauds; assess each by leverage ratio and liquidation level relative to the asset's historical floor.
“A Ponzi is guaranteed to fail... These are clearly not guaranteed to fail. They might fail, but they're not guaranteed to fail.”
Bitmine currently has no debt and no leverage structure on its cap table, so as-is it is functionally a trust (like GBTC) and cannot be a Ponzi; the proposed preferred would be its first structural leverage.
data_point🗣 guest (Hib)conviction: highBitmineGBTCETH
So what: Bitmine's clean balance sheet means current downside is pure NAV exposure; the preferred offering is the inflection point where leverage risk begins.
“Bitmine has no structure. There's no debt. There's no anything... clearly asis Bitmine is not a Ponzi.”
DATs are leveraged 'number-go-up' bets on very favorable terms — the leverage is small relative to the balance sheet, but it only pays if the underlying rises; if number goes down, they lose.
framework🗣 guest (Hib)conviction: highDATsBitcoinETHMicroStrategyBitmine
So what: DAT equity is a levered directional bet — size exposure accordingly and don't mistake the favorable financing terms for downside protection.
“getting levered only works when number go up.”
There is a 'goldilocks zone' of leverage where part of the market doubts the DAT will survive but it doesn't fail; if confidence is never tested (Stretch never trades off peg), the DAT is taking too little risk and leaving returns on the table.
framework🗣 host (David)conviction: mediumMicroStrategyStretchDATsMichael Saylor
So what: Periodic confidence scares in MSTR/Stretch may be a feature of optimal leverage, not necessarily a sign of impending failure — a contrarian lens on volatility.
“if you never have these moments where part of the market like shrugs off... then actually you're not taking enough risk”
ETF outflows and flow-driven market structure 5
Bitcoin spot ETFs are on a record 13-day consecutive outflow streak (per Galaxy Research), one of the most bearish stretches in BTC ETF history, with ETH ETFs likely matching.
data_point🗣 host (David)conviction: highBitcoinETHBitcoin ETFGalaxy Research
So what: Record ETF outflows confirm sustained institutional/retail de-risking — a flow headwind until the streak breaks.
“Bitcoin spot ETF outflows are on a record setting 13-day streak of consecutive out outflows.”
ETF flow data is backward-looking and tautological — outflows merely memorialize selling that already happened and cannot explain why people sold, so they should not be treated as a forward signal.
contrarian🗣 guest (Hib)conviction: highBitcoin ETFBitcoin
So what: Stop reading ETF outflows as predictive; they are a record of past price action, not a leading indicator — avoid the circular-reasoning trap.
“No this memorializes the selling. This is a record of the selling. Asking why did they sell like this this this chart cannot answer that for you.”
The current crypto market is heavily flows- and sentiment-driven rather than fundamentals-driven, with Bitcoin about to cross below its 200-day moving average after a 12-13% weekly drop.
thesis🗣 guest (Hib)conviction: highBitcoinETH
So what: In a flow/sentiment regime, technical levels (200DMA) and narrative matter more than valuation — trade reflexively, not fundamentally.
“I think it's pretty clear this market is very flows driven right now and very sentiment driven.”
ETFs are not a one-way 'up only' ratchet — they sell as readily as they buy — whereas DATs are the true permanent bid, which is why DAT narrative (perpetual buyer) drives flows.
framework🗣 guest (Hib)conviction: highBitcoin ETFDATsMicroStrategyBitmine
So what: DATs, not ETFs, provide the structural perpetual bid for BTC/ETH — but only as long as the 'always buying' narrative holds.
“the ETFs are not up only. They are not a one-way ratchet... these DATs are that.”
Tom Lee/Bitmine is down ~$8.9B on its $10B ETH position and Saylor is down ~$7.6B on $56B of Bitcoin, quantifying the unrealized losses at the major DATs during the drawdown.
data_point🗣 host (David)conviction: mediumTom LeeBitmineMichael SaylorMicroStrategyETHBitcoin

“Tom Lee is down $ 8.9 billion on 10 billion of ETH... Michael Taylor is down $7.6 billion. He has 56 billion of Bitcoin.”
Token decoupling: revenue-driven altcoins resist BTC drawdown 8
For the first time across recent cycles, several legitimate tokens hit all-time highs and showed strength in the same week Bitcoin fell to its 200-day MA and ETH sat ~30% below its 200DMA — a structural break from prior 'everything dumps together' behavior.
thesis🗣 host (David)conviction: highBitcoinETHHyperliquidVenice
So what: Crypto is bifurcating into BTC-correlated and revenue-decoupled assets — alpha is increasingly in cash-flow tokens that don't need a BTC rally.
“Never before have I seen tokens reaching all-time highs and having strength in that same week.”
Hyperliquid is decoupling from Bitcoin because a growing share of its volume comes from Trade XYZ trading RWAs rather than crypto perps, so its revenue narrative is less tied to BTC price.
causal🗣 guest (Hib)conviction: mediumHyperliquidHYPEBitcoinRWAs
So what: Hyperliquid's RWA-trading mix is its decoupling driver — track RWA volume share as the fundamental that justifies its premium independent of BTC.
“more and more of their volume is actually coming from Trade XYZ, which is trading RWAS.”
Venice's token price moves with demand for AI tokens (inference) on its platform rather than crypto prices, making it materially diversified out of the crypto market.
causal🗣 guest (Hib)conviction: mediumVeniceAI
So what: Venice is effectively an AI-demand proxy, not a crypto-beta asset — useful for exposure to AI usage without BTC correlation.
“As demand for AI tokens goes up, Venice's revenues increase and Venice goes up.”
AI human-identity tokens led the week's gainers: 'humanity' up 120%, Worldcoin up 94%, with Worldcoin's rally attributed to OpenAI IPO anticipation (alongside Anthropic and SpaceX 'infinity trade' speculation).

“we got Lighter up 30%, Athena up 11%, Hyperlid up 10%, Venice up 9%, Jupiter up 6%.”
The decoupling cuts both ways: if revenue-driven tokens like Hyperliquid are priced on revenues (much of it RWA, not crypto-correlated), they may not double just because BTC rises — they could capture less crypto-beta on the upside too.

“it may well be that actually people are pricing hyperlquid on revenues... they just don't capture as much cryptobate on the way up either.”
Revenue does not universally protect tokens: most DeFi (non-perp) is getting hammered as multiples compress and crypto-denominated revenues are expected to fall, and Near sold off hard the same day despite being revenue-driven.
contrarian🗣 guest (Hib)conviction: mediumDeFiNear

Zcash is the one token in the host's focus list that is not revenue-driven and instead trades as a 'metaphorically paired' Bitcoin proxy due to its 21M fixed supply, explaining its larger pullback.
anecdote🗣 host (David)conviction: lowZcashBitcoin
