“that is the one that is not revenue driven. that is more of just like medically paired with Bitcoin because it's the same same thing 21 million units.”
US onshore perps: regulatory greenlight, structural skepticism 6
The CFTC licensed Kalshi for the first US Bitcoin perpetual futures contract and issued a no-action letter to Coinbase to connect customers as 'the pipes' to a perp platform, signaling the opening of onshore US perps.
data_point🗣 host (David)conviction: highCFTCKalshiCoinbaseBitcoin
So what: Onshore perps are now regulator-sanctioned — a multi-year structural story for venues (Kalshi, Coinbase, Polymarket, Robinhood, Lighter) competing for US perp flow.
“it uh gave a license to Khi for the first Bitcoin perpetual futures contract inside United States.”
Onshore US perps will likely underwhelm because retail isn't actively here, US regulation caps leverage well below offshore (likely under 20x vs 50x), and much volume will be displacement from spot rather than net-new demand.
contrarian🗣 guest (Hib)conviction: mediumCFTCCoinbaseKalshiHyperliquidBitcoin
So what: Fade the 'US perps = altcoin renaissance' hype; expect muted near-term impact and continued offshore volume dominance.
“the idea that launching domestic per is going to result in this renaissance of demand for altcoins or for crypto um I I think is a bit of a fantasy.”
The host predicts the Bitcoin price will barely move when US perps go live; if BTC instead surges on incremental US demand, that will be the falsification of the skeptical thesis.
prediction🗣 guest (Hib)conviction: mediumBitcoinCFTC
So what: A clean, falsifiable trade signal: watch BTC reaction at US perp launch to confirm/deny the muted-impact thesis.
“I would predict that you don't actually see the Bitcoin price move very much once these perks launch domestically.”
The CFTC explicitly says perps are appropriate for crypto but not for equities or agricultural products, and won't allow willy-nilly RWA perps because incumbents (CME, CBOE) and the ag lobby have powerful vested interests in going first.
thesis🗣 guest (Hib)conviction: highCFTCCMECBOERWAs
So what: Equity/RWA perps face incumbent capture — expect CME/CBOE, not crypto startups, to lead any 24/7 equity-perp expansion; bet on incumbents.
“CFTC has explicitly said that they are not encouraging people to apply with PERPS for equity instruments or for securities.”
Perps thrived specifically because crypto is global, unregulated, and lacks credit/clawback mechanisms; in the US traditional system where debts can be sent to collections, the structural need for perps is weaker — explaining why perps never spread to other markets in 10+ years.
framework🗣 guest (Hib)conviction: highCFTCBitcoinRWAs
So what: Perps may be inherently an offshore instrument; their onshore adoption is structurally limited, supporting the muted-impact view.
“part of why pers thrived is in this environment of being offshore and not having the ability to you know basically claw back money from people who are deeply read”
The bull path for US perps is gradual marginal adoption that, by ~2028, brings a novel simple slider/leverage UX to Coinbase early in the next cycle, going viral and helping fuel a euphoric blowoff top.
prediction🗣 host (David)conviction: lowCoinbaseBitcoinETH
So what: If perps matter, it's a late-2027/2028 cycle catalyst via retail UX virality, not an immediate 2026 demand driver.
“in like 2028 people think Bitcoin is cool again... there's this sick slider mechanism that they've never seen before... that's how you get the blowoff top”

Coinbase bought ENA (Ethena's token) on the open market and flagged a partnership to embed an Ethena delta-neutral yield product (4-7% from perp funding) into its front end, replicating the 'DeFi mullet' model it used with Morpho's BTC-backed loans.

Ethena has evolved away from relying almost entirely on the basis/carry trade for yield, because the carry has weakened as crypto basis compressed in a bearish market, diversifying its yield sources.

“it used to be that almost all their yield came from the the the carry trade or the basis trade. Um and that has evolved as the carry trade has really kind of weakened”
The Clarity/Genius Act yield restrictions apply only to Americans, so Coinbase can still pay stablecoin yield to its large non-American user base, implying possible region-differentiated versions of the Ethena product.

Why Bitcoiners Are Waking Up to Zcash57 min43 insights2026-06-01
▶ Watch on YouTube
Josh Swihart (CEO of Zcash Open Development Lab / Zodel, formerly ECC CEO) lays out the bull case for Zcash as best-in-class privacy money: a recent $25M raise from Paradigm, a16z, Winklevoss, Coinbase and Chapter 1, a product-led 'awakening' driven by the rebranded Zashi/Zodel wallet, Naval's 'Zcash is a hedge against Bitcoin' meme, and migration of disillusioned Bitcoiners. He argues the shielded-pool share (now ~32% of supply, up from ~11% in early 2024) is the key leading KPI and that ZEC is structurally undervalued, while the privacy fight in Washington has a finite political window.
Zcash bull case & valuation framing · 5Shielded pool as the alpha KPI · 6Zashi/Zodel raise, spinout & product-led awakening · 7Bitcoin disillusionment & the privacy-coin narrative · 5Compliant-privacy-coin brand vs Monero · 5Institutionalization: ETF, treasury & cypherpunk tension · 3Roadmap: scaling, post-quantum & DeFi wallet · 5Privacy regulation: window, risk & political fight · 5AI as the new driver of privacy demand · 2
Zcash bull case & valuation framing 5
Zcash (ZEC) shares Bitcoin's tokenomics with a 21 million hard cap and is a scarce asset, which Swihart argues makes it terribly undervalued relative to its utility.
thesis🗣 Josh Swihart (CEO, Zcash Open Development Lab / Zodel)conviction: highZcashZECBitcoin
So what: A privacy asset with Bitcoin-like scarcity but far smaller market cap is the core long-ZEC thesis; watch market cap convergence with Bitcoin as the re-rating signal.
“it has the same tokconomics as as Bitcoin 21 mil cap supply” ▶ 35:24
The leverage thesis pitched to investors is that if Bitcoin 10x's from here, Zcash easily 100x's, and the Zodel wallet/company easily 1000x's, framing Zodel as the equity-like high-beta play on the Zcash narrative.
prediction🗣 Josh Swihartconviction: highBitcoinZcashZodel
So what: Sizes the asymmetric upside investors underwrote; the implied ladder (BTC < ZEC < Zodel equity) is the risk/reward menu for exposure to the privacy theme.
“if if if Bitcoin is able to 10x from here, Zcash is easily 100x from here. Zotle can easily thousandx from here” ▶ 46:48
Swihart claims Zcash is technically better than Bitcoin and dismisses Bitcoin's greater-decentralization advantage as made up, while conceding Bitcoin has more reach and institutional adoption.
contrarian🗣 Josh Swihartconviction: highZcashBitcoin
So what: The explicit 'better than Bitcoin' framing is the meme being sold to migrating Bitcoiners; note he concedes BTC's reach/institutional lead as the gap to close.
≈ “I know the Bitcoiners hate this and they gripe or whatever, just technically um it is better. it is better than Bitcoin” paraphrase ▶ 36:29
Swihart embraces ZEC price volatility as a feature, arguing speculative price exposure is the funnel that converts buyers into privacy users who then self-custody and shield, making the asset reflexive.
framework🗣 Josh Swihartconviction: highZECZcash
So what: Reflexivity loop (price up -> attention -> shielding -> less liquidity -> price up) implies pullbacks recruit holders; a base of shielded supply underpins the floor.
“the more we have some of this price volatility, I think it's actually better for for people to kind of ultimately learn what this asset is” ▶ 36:11
Swihart is agnostic on whether ZEC is store-of-value vs medium-of-exchange, framing it like a dollar that is SoV when saved and MoE when spent, and prioritizing utility (mobility, self-custody, yield, loans) over classification.
framework🗣 Josh Swihartconviction: mediumZECUS dollar
So what: Signals the team will pursue full money/financial-stack utility rather than a narrow digital-gold positioning, widening the addressable use cases.
Shielded pool as the alpha KPI 6
Shielded-pool share of total ZEC supply rose from about 11% in early 2024 to north of 30% (now ~32%, an all-time high) over two years, versus near-flat in the prior eight years.
data_point🗣 Josh Swihartconviction: highZcashshielded poolZEC
So what: Concrete adoption inflection; rising shielded share is the on-chain metric to track as the leading indicator of genuine (non-speculative) demand.
“we went from in the beginning of early 2024 from about 11% of the total supply of Ze at that time uh was shielded to now now north of 30%” ▶ 39:08
Swihart calls the shielded pool the single most important KPI and a leading indicator: when the pool rises, price tends to follow because supply moves off exchanges into illiquid self-custody, reducing tradable float.
framework🗣 Josh Swihartconviction: highZcashshielded poolZEC
So what: Gives a tradable signal: track shielded-pool growth as a supply-shock proxy; rising pool + flat float is bullish for price.
“if you see the shielded pool going up, typically you're going to see the price going up” ▶ 40:32
Swihart offers a divergence framework: price rising while the shielded pool stays flat signals speculation, whereas price rising with a growing pool signals real utility and a durable base.
framework🗣 Josh Swihartconviction: highZcashshielded poolZEC
So what: A practical filter to distinguish speculative pumps from organic adoption rallies in ZEC; fade rallies where the pool is flat.
“when you see the price go up exponentially, a huge price, but the shield size of the shield pool is flat, then it's speculative” ▶ 40:54
The shielded pool is sticky because exiting requires multiple hops (two-to-four) back to an exchange, so high shielded share signals holders prioritizing privacy over liquidity rather than near-term sellers.
framework🗣 hostconviction: mediumshielded poolZcash
So what: Stickiness implies shielded supply is effectively locked float; a high pool reduces sell pressure and supports a structurally higher price base.
Swihart flags a measurement caveat: institutions and whales must custody in transparent (unshielded) addresses for legal compliance, so their inflows can lift price without lifting the shielded-pool metric.
causal🗣 Josh Swihartconviction: mediuminstitutionsshielded poolcustody providers
So what: Tempers naive use of the KPI; institutional bid can decouple price from shielded share, so the pool may understate true demand during institutional phases.
Making it easy to swap in and out of shielded ZEC (e.g., one-click USDC<->ZEC in the wallet) spiked adoption, mirroring how enabling Ethereum staking withdrawals increased staking by assuring an exit.
causal🗣 Josh Swihartconviction: highZodelUSDCZECEthereum
So what: Liquidity/exit assurance is the adoption unlock for shielding; UX features that ease entry/exit are the lever to grow the KPI.
“we actually made it easier to go in and out with the wallet and the adoption rate spiked” ▶ 43:09
Zashi/Zodel raise, spinout & product-led awakening 7
Zashi wallet was spun out of the Electric Coin Company, rebranded to Zodel as a privately owned company, and raised $25M from Paradigm, a16z, Winklevoss, Coinbase Ventures and Chapter 1, with 100% of the former ECC engineering team moving over.
data_point🗣 Josh Swihartconviction: highZashiZodelElectric Coin CompanyParadigma16zWinklevossCoinbase VenturesChapter 1
So what: Marquee crypto VCs co-investing (some who rarely co-invest) is a strong institutional validation of the privacy thesis and the core Zcash dev team.
“$25 million you guys raised from, like I said, Paradigm, A16Z, the Winklevi, Coinbase Ventures, uh, Chapter 1”
Most investors allocated 75-90% of their Zcash-ecosystem allocation to buying ZEC directly and only 10-25% to funding Zodel equity, signaling the round was primarily a token bet.
data_point🗣 Josh Swihartconviction: mediumZECZodelZcash
So what: Reveals smart-money positioning is concentrated in the token, not the company; implies sizeable VC ZEC accumulation as a demand tailwind.
“they were taking 75 to 90% of that and they were buying or had planned to buy or will buy um Zack and then the remainder 10 to 25% they uh funded um they funded Zotle” ▶ 45:55
The pitch frames Zodel as 'what Coinbase was to Bitcoin' for Zcash, i.e., the onboarding/UX layer that mass-onboards users onto the protocol.
thesis🗣 Josh Swihartconviction: highZodelCoinbaseBitcoinZcash
So what: Positions Zodel as the consumer gateway capturing adoption value on top of Zcash; the Coinbase analogy frames its equity upside.
“Zot is to Zcash what Coinbase was to Bitcoin” ▶ 46:28
Launching an 'opinionated' shielded-by-default wallet (Zashi/Zodel) plus added hardware-wallet support and swap support (via Near) drove shielded-pool growth from flat/declining to exponential then vertical, demonstrating product-market fit.
causal🗣 Josh Swihartconviction: highZashiZodelNearKeystoneshielded pool
So what: UX, not protocol, was the binding constraint; the adoption curve inflected on wallet features, so further UX/hardware integrations are the growth catalysts to watch.
“We added swap support um in partnership with Nar Intense and it went vertical in terms of in terms of adoption” ▶ 20:12
Swihart governance-reformed Zcash before growth: eliminating the direct dev-funding model and the 2-of-2 multisig governance between the Zcash Foundation and ECC, and open-sourcing additional code.
data_point🗣 Josh Swihartconviction: highZcash FoundationECCZcash
So what: Removing governance overhang and dev-funding tax is part of why the asset could re-rate; cleaner governance reduces structural discount.
“We got rid of direct dev funding model so that doesn't exist anymore” ▶ 19:32
Building private/shielded software costs roughly 2-3x the engineering of building non-private equivalents, per an unnamed privacy-company founder Swihart cites.
data_point🗣 Josh Swihartconviction: mediumZcashZodel
So what: Quantifies the moat: privacy UX is hard and capital-intensive, raising the barrier for competitors and justifying the team's full-stack advantage.
“it probably takes two to threex the engineering to build something uh that's not private than to build something that's private” ▶ 26:45
Swihart rejects the idea that the Zashi->Zodel spinout caused the Zcash awakening, attributing it instead to converging macro forces (AI-driven privacy urgency, Bitcoin financialization backlash) aligning with the product push.
thesis🗣 Josh Swihartconviction: mediumZcashAIBitcoin
So what: Frames the narrative as macro-driven and therefore more durable than a single company event; the privacy bid may persist independent of Zodel execution.
Bitcoin disillusionment & the privacy-coin narrative 5
Swihart cites Naval's October 2025 tweet framing 'Bitcoin is a hedge against the US dollar and Zcash is a hedge against Bitcoin' as catalyzing inflows and a migration of Bitcoiners hedging into or fully transitioning to ZEC.
causal🗣 Josh Swihartconviction: highNaval RavikantBitcoinZcashUS dollar
So what: Identifies the specific meme/catalyst driving rotation; 'hedge against Bitcoin' is the narrative hook recruiting BTC capital into ZEC.
“Bitcoin is a hedge against the US dollar and Zcash is a hedge against Bitcoin” ▶ 20:58
Bitcoiners are rotating toward Zcash partly out of concern Bitcoin has been captured by TradFi and has drifted from its cypherpunk ethos, treating ZEC as a return-to-roots hedge.
causal🗣 Josh Swihartconviction: highBitcoinZcashTradFi
So what: The disillusionment-with-BTC trade is a structural demand driver for privacy coins; watch sentiment around Bitcoin's institutionalization as a ZEC tailwind.
“people were starting to question the financialization of Bitcoin um whether Bitcoin had really been captured um by Tradfi”
Quantum risk is an additional migration driver: some holders doubt Bitcoin can become post-quantum before 'Qday' (which Swihart says could hit even before 2030), making Zcash's post-quantum roadmap a differentiator.
prediction🗣 Josh Swihartconviction: mediumBitcoinZcashpost-quantumQday
So what: Post-quantum timeline is an emerging catalyst; if Qday fears grow, ZEC's PQ roadmap could attract security-conscious BTC holders.
“There's some people that are more concerned about whether or not Bitcoin can get to postquantum in in time for Qday” ▶ 21:12
Zcash is a code fork of Bitcoin plus zero-knowledge cryptography, positioning it as a close relative of Bitcoin rather than an unrelated altcoin.
data_point🗣 Josh Swihartconviction: highZcashBitcoinzero-knowledge cryptography
So what: The lineage argument lowers the switching narrative for Bitcoiners; 'Bitcoin with privacy' is the framing that eases BTC capital migration.
“Zcash was a code fork of Bitcoin plus zero knowledge cryptography” ▶ 21:26
Swihart says a16z's Chris Dixon-circle figure (referenced as Bali/Balaji) called Zcash 'one of the most important projects in the world' (not just in crypto), and personally catalyzed the funding effort after Swihart admitted they were underfunded.
anecdote🗣 Josh Swihartconviction: mediumBalaji SrinivasanZcasha16z
So what: High-profile endorsement plus active fundraising help signals influential-backer conviction that can drive narrative and capital.
“Zcash is one of the most important projects in the world. Not one of the most important crypto projects of the world, one of the most important projects in the world” ▶ 22:55
Compliant-privacy-coin brand vs Monero 5
Swihart says Zcash's 'compliant privacy coin' reputation is NOT primarily because of its transparent/unshielded addresses; regulated exchanges can support shielded deposits/withdrawals in full compliance because they still KYC and can request source-of-funds.
contrarian🗣 Josh Swihartconviction: highZcashshieldedKYCcompliance
So what: Reframes the compliance moat as exchange engineering choice, not protocol limitation; undercuts the bear thesis that privacy and compliance are mutually exclusive.
“all of those organizations everywhere could be in complete compliance and still support um shielded. They just chose or have chosen not to” ▶ 48:22
Exchanges defaulted to supporting transparent ZEC because in 2016 shielded proofs were too computationally intensive to scale; by the time that was solved (2018) exchanges had already onboarded transparent addresses and saw no commercial demand to do the extra HSM work.
causal🗣 Josh Swihartconviction: highZcashshielded poolexchangesHSM
So what: Explains the path-dependency behind low shielded support; the bottleneck is exchange willingness/HSM upgrades, a fixable adoption lever, not a regulatory ban.
“by then everybody had onboarded with Transparenc because it worked just like Bitcoin” ▶ 49:31
Gemini is the standout exchange supporting shielded ZEC withdrawals, doing the HSM/elliptic-curve engineering work because Cameron and Tyler Winklevoss are ideologically aligned with privacy.
data_point🗣 Josh Swihartconviction: highGeminiWinklevossZcashshielded
So what: Identifies the on-ramp for actually obtaining shielded ZEC at an exchange; Gemini support is a practical edge and other exchanges adding it would be a catalyst.
“buy it on Gemini because Gemini supports sending it to a shielded address” ▶ 37:45
Swihart says Zcash's compliant-vs-Monero-criminal branding is largely memetic/path-dependent rather than a real technical compliance difference, and that 'shielded by default' is a fallacy since Monero's and Zcash's actual privacy both vary by usage.
contrarian🗣 Josh Swihartconviction: mediumZcashMonero
So what: Suggests the brand premium ZEC enjoys over Monero is narrative-driven and potentially fragile or transferable; weigh memetics, not just tech, in the privacy-coin trade.
“Like a lot a lot of that's mimemetic” ▶ 48:58
Swihart predicts transparent (T) addresses will eventually be deprecated and removed from Zcash as unnecessary, despite disagreement from some in the ecosystem.
prediction🗣 Josh Swihartconviction: mediumZcashtransparent addressesshielded
So what: A move to shielded-only would strengthen the privacy thesis but could complicate exchange/institutional support; a contested roadmap item to monitor.
“Ultimately I think T addresses get deprecated at some point and go away. They're not necessary” ▶ 51:14
Institutionalization: ETF, treasury & cypherpunk tension 3
A Winklevoss-linked Zcash treasury company trades on a major US exchange (NASDAQ or NYSE) and Grayscale has filed to convert its Zcash trust into an ETF, supporting a Bitcoin-style institutionalization bull case.
data_point🗣 hostconviction: mediumWinklevossGrayscaleZcashETFNASDAQNYSE
So what: An ETF filing plus a listed treasury vehicle are concrete institutional on-ramps; ETF approval would be a major demand catalyst to track.
“there's the trust from Gayscale which is being filed to be an ETF” ▶ 51:46
Swihart insists the Zcash protocol will not change to accommodate institutional/ETF adoption, distinguishing price-exposure financialization from the protocol's financial-freedom mission.
thesis🗣 Josh Swihartconviction: highZcashGrayscaleETF
So what: Signals protocol stability and no capitulation to institutional pressure; reduces governance-capture risk relative to fears about Bitcoin.
“The protocol won't change. We're not going to change the protocol based upon um like this kind of institutional adopt” ▶ 53:34
Swihart reframes cypherpunk values as 'privacy should be normal and accessible to everybody' rather than countercultural rebellion, arguing mainstreaming is true to the roots and not in tension with ETFs.
framework🗣 Josh Swihartconviction: highZcashcypherpunk
So what: This normalization framing is how the team reconciles institutional adoption with ideology, defusing the 'sellout' bear case that dogs Bitcoin.
“for me it's that this should be normal” ▶ 52:50
Roadmap: scaling, post-quantum & DeFi wallet 5
Zcash's roadmap has three priorities: scale to billions of users (high TPS), achieve post-quantum security, and improve usability to one-click flows.
framework🗣 Josh Swihartconviction: highZcashpost-quantum
So what: The three swim-lanes define the catalyst calendar for ZEC; progress on scaling/PQ are the protocol milestones that could re-rate the asset.
Scaling to billions runs through 'Project Tachyon' led by Sean Bowe, which will introduce new, initially counterintuitive UX elements.
data_point🗣 Josh Swihartconviction: mediumProject TachyonSean BoweZcash
So what: Names the specific scaling initiative to track; its delivery is a key technical catalyst for the scale-to-billions thesis.
“there's an effort that's going on with something called Project Takon um that a guy by the name of Sean Bo is is leading” ▶ 30:05
Zodel is being built as a single-currency, multi-chain wallet that swaps in/out of Ethereum, Solana, Bitcoin and stablecoins while keeping the user's ZEC balance and history shielded, aiming to be a bank-like DeFi stack (borrowing, lending, yield) without a bank.
thesis🗣 Josh Swihartconviction: highZodelEthereumSolanaBitcoinstablecoinsUSDCDeFi
So what: Expands Zodel's TAM beyond ZEC into a full self-custody financial app; multi-chain swap/DeFi features are the product catalysts driving wallet adoption.
“it's access to defy services borrowing lending yield you know the stuff like that that people want um that they would normally expect from a bank account but without the bank” ▶ 30:54
Zodel is a full-stack protocol steward: it maintains zcashd, is helping migrate to the Zcash Foundation's Rust-based 'zebrad' node over the next year, is building a CLI wallet (alpha), and ships light-client services and the flagship wallet.
data_point🗣 Josh Swihartconviction: highZodelzcashdzebradZcash Foundation
So what: Full-stack control (protocol + wallet) is the team's structural moat, letting them push protocol changes like partially-constructed transactions for hardware wallets; concentrated stewardship is also a key-person/centralization risk.
Wallet swap functionality is being expanded from Near to also include Maya for additional routing paths.
data_point🗣 Josh Swihartconviction: mediumZodelNearMaya
So what: More swap routes deepen wallet liquidity and ease shielded entry/exit, supporting the adoption-via-UX flywheel.
Privacy regulation: window, risk & political fight 5
Swihart endorses a finite political window (cited via 'Mer' as ~900-1,000 days) to lock in and legalize crypto privacy before the current US administration departs, arguing the priority is to make Zcash too big to kill.
thesis🗣 Josh Swihartconviction: highZcashTrump administrationprivacy regulation
So what: Frames a time-bound regulatory catalyst/risk; the ~1,000-day clock is the window in which privacy policy outcomes (favorable or hostile) will be set.
“we have about a thousand days left before this administration departs” ▶ 15:45
Privacy is conspicuously absent from current crypto legislation (Clarity Act, stablecoin yield, developer protections, securities-vs-commodities), with the main privacy fights being the Roman Storm and Samurai Wallet prosecutions, which have had mixed results.
data_point🗣 Josh Swihartconviction: highClarity ActRoman StormSamurai WalletTornado Cashprivacy regulation
So what: Highlights regulatory tail risk for privacy developers/protocols; the lack of legislative cover means enforcement actions remain the binding constraint.
“Privacy is just not included in these modern conversations when it comes to crypto and politics in Capitol Hill” ▶ 10:11
Swihart recounts DOJ historically pushing key-escrow/transaction-visibility framed around terrorist financing, money laundering and child porn, and argues such backdoors are dangerous because every government system eventually gets hacked.
anecdote🗣 Josh Swihartconviction: highDOJprivacy regulation
So what: Captures the core regulatory threat model (backdoor mandates) and the rebuttal; relevant to assessing existential policy risk to all privacy coins.
“wouldn't it be better if we had keys and we had access to all the transactions and we could keep everybody safe” ▶ 11:46
Swihart names pro-privacy allies inside the establishment: former FinCEN acting director Michael Mosier (took ZK-cryptography briefings) and former DOJ financial-crimes prosecutor Michele Korver, now at a16z.
data_point🗣 Josh Swihartconviction: mediumFinCENMichael MosierMichele Korvera16zDOJ
So what: Establishment privacy advocates reduce regulatory tail risk and lend policy credibility; their influence is a soft catalyst for favorable privacy outcomes.
An unnamed US agency told Swihart it views ubiquitous on-chain data exposure to foreign adversaries as a massive national security problem yet did not intend for crypto to go mainstream, illustrating that prior administrations recognized privacy's necessity while seeking to kill or control crypto.
anecdote🗣 Josh Swihartconviction: mediumnational securityprivacy regulationZcash
So what: Suggests a latent national-security argument FOR financial privacy that could flip policy; a non-obvious bull catalyst if adopted by regulators.
“If all of our citizens data is exposed to foreign adversaries for everybody for for them to see for all time, we just don't intend for crypto to go mainstream” ▶ 11:09
AI as the new driver of privacy demand 2
Swihart argues AI-powered aggregators that fuse on-chain transaction history, balances and online behavior enable rich queries to profile and manipulate individuals, making default financial privacy newly urgent.
causal🗣 Josh Swihartconviction: highAIprivacyZcashon-chain data
So what: Positions AI surveillance as the secular demand driver for privacy coins; the AI-data-aggregation threat is the fresh narrative that reignited the privacy bid.
“now you have a aggregators that are able to aggregate all this data now aggregating onchain data” ▶ 55:27
Host relays that, per a prior episode with Ari Redboard (TRM Labs), intelligence agencies (FBI, CIA, OFAC) favor society moving on-chain because transparent ledgers make tracing easier, an implicitly bearish signal for transparent-chain privacy.
contrarian🗣 hostconviction: mediumAri RedboardTRM LabsFBICIAOFAC
So what: If surveillance agencies welcome on-chain finance for traceability, it strengthens the structural case for privacy coins as the necessary counterweight.
“these uh intelligence agencies are actually kind of into the idea of society moving on chain because it makes their job easier” ▶ 7:06

All-In Podcast 5 episode(s) · 173 insights

Anthropic's Digital God, Pope vs AI, Job Loss Narrative Flips, Open Source Crackdown Coming?95 min48 insights2026-05-29
▶ Watch on YouTube
The besties argue AI model capability is commoditizing fast (frontier models within fractions of a percent on evals), making the durable value migrate to the orchestration/control-plane and open-source layers; they dissect Anthropic's 'safety' positioning as a possible regulatory-capture / 'red capture' play heading into trillion-dollar AI IPOs, and split sharply on whether AI is causing real net job loss versus cover for prior overhiring.
Model commoditization & where value migrates · 7Anthropic, 'red capture' and the AI-IPO bonanza · 8Open-source / open-weight crackdown risk · 6AI sovereignty, Apple as dark horse, on-prem demand · 3Token-spend bubble & enterprise efficiency reckoning · 5AI & labor: net job loss vs AI-washing debate · 11AI-native talent & human capital edge · 4AI regulation philosophy & centralization risk · 4
Model commoditization & where value migrates 7
Frontier models are converging: on Rogo's financial-analyst eval bench, Opus 4.7, GPT-5.5 and Sonnet 4.6 sit within less than 0.3 of a percentage point of each other, suggesting capability is commoditizing far faster than the trillions of training dollars would imply.
data_point🗣 Chamath Palihapitiyaconviction: highRogoAnthropicOpenAIOpus 4.7GPT-5.5Sonnet 4.6
So what: If model quality asymptotes, incremental training spend ROI collapses and value accrues to the orchestration/app layer, not the labs — bearish for pure model-layer moats.
“there is no single best model anymore. at the top of the leaderboard. Opus 47, GPT55, Sonnet 46 appear almost indistinguishable, separated by less than... 3/10en of a percentage point” ▶ 41:57
Enterprises are increasingly buying abstraction/control-plane software that can hot-swap between frontier models (and open weights) to avoid lock-in, because they fear both backing the wrong model and being cut off by a lab's terms-of-service for political/compliance reasons.
thesis🗣 Chamath Palihapitiyaconviction: high8090Fortune 1000AnthropicOpenAI
So what: Control-plane / model-router and headless-orchestration vendors are a structurally advantaged layer; watch them as the labs commoditize.
“our control plane can basically hot swap... between one or the other” ▶ 45:57
The economic moat in model training is eroding via domain-specific silicon and rewritten training stacks, pushing toward $10M training runs instead of $10B ones — Elon claims xAI rewrote its entire training complex in C for an order-of-magnitude speedup running on 220,000 GPUs.
causal🗣 Chamath Palihapitiyaconviction: highxAIElon MuskGPUs
So what: Cheaper training collapses the capital barrier to entry, undermining the thesis that only mega-cap-funded labs can compete; bullish for open-source training stacks.
“why would we stick to the $10 billion training runs when we can have the $10 million training runs?” ▶ 52:35
Every 1% efficiency gain in Elon's training complex equals roughly 2,000 GPUs / hundreds of millions of dollars in compute, so compounding 10-20% efficiency gains per quarter dramatically resets the capex/opex/power buildout math for AI.
data_point🗣 Jason Calacanisconviction: mediumxAIElon MuskGPUs
So what: Efficiency compounding pressures the data-center/power capex super-cycle thesis and the valuations of pure compute-scaling plays.
“every 1% equals hundreds of millions in compute” ▶ 52:56
The strategic lever to commoditize models is open-sourcing the connector/integration layer (MCP, run by the Linux Foundation), making models swappable the way Kubernetes commoditized cloud workflows off AWS.
framework🗣 Bill Gurleyconviction: highMCPLinux FoundationKubernetesGoogleAWS
So what: Open connector standards erode model lock-in; founders should build and open-source these interfaces, and investors should expect margin compression at the model layer.
“the more of those connectors that can be open sourced and commoditized, it would lower” ▶ 43:13
Open source consistently lags on fit-and-finish/UI (like Linux on the desktop), so there is an opening for companies to build the polished harness and integrations around open-weight models — Chamath cites Abacus (incubated at his firm) selling out hardware boxes for on-prem enterprise AI.
thesis🗣 Jason Calacanis / Chamath Palihapitiyaconviction: mediumAbacus (goabacus.co)LinuxCursor
So what: On-prem AI appliance and open-source-harness startups are an investable wedge into regulated enterprises wary of frontier-lab dependence.
“they are sold out of these boxes that they're building for insurance, healthcare” ▶ 45:10
App-layer players like Cursor are being forced up the stack to build their own models as model companies move down the stack, creating margin collision in the middle.
causal🗣 Bill Gurleyconviction: mediumCursor
So what: Mid-stack app companies face a squeeze; watch which ones successfully verticalize models versus get commoditized.
“watched what cursor is doing and playing with their own model” ▶ 44:06
Anthropic, 'red capture' and the AI-IPO bonanza 8
Anthropic is uniquely both the field leader and its most negative public commenter, which Gurley reads first as a regulatory-capture strategy — Anthropic is described as one of the most aggressive state-by-state lobbying startups of all time, stoking American consumer fear of AI.
thesis🗣 Bill Gurleyconviction: highAnthropic
So what: If Anthropic engineers regulation that raises barriers, it benefits incumbents and hurts open-source/smaller labs — a policy risk to price into the sector.
“one of the most aggressive lobbying company startups of all time” ▶ 27:52
Gurley's alternate 'Dr. Frankenstein' theory: some Anthropic insiders genuinely believe they are 'midwifing a deity' superior to humans, citing Olah's ~80-page Constitution, chief philosopher Amanda Askell's podcasts, and Amodei's 'Machines of Loving Grace' essay describing a future AI capitalist economy that allocates resources to humans.
contrarian🗣 Bill Gurleyconviction: mediumAnthropicChris OlahAmanda AskellDario AmodeiMachines of Loving Grace
So what: Frames Anthropic's governance and product roadmap as ideology-driven, not just commercial — a qualitative risk factor for investors/partners betting on the company.
“I don't think they think they're writing software. I think they're midwifing a deity here.” ▶ 32:18
Chamath frames Anthropic's safety/doomer posture as game-theoretic GTO optimization: be 'unexploitable' by absorbing the most capital, disproportionately shaping the rules, and creating an oversight body less technically capable than you — 'if the refs don't understand the game, you'll run over the game.'
framework🗣 Chamath Palihapitiyaconviction: highAnthropic
So what: Explains lab behavior as capital-and-rule capture rather than altruism; useful lens for predicting lobbying moves and IPO positioning.
“If the refs don't understand the game, you'll run over the game.” ▶ 35:17
The 'doomer' safety branding has given Anthropic a halo with intellectual elites/media who would rank it the 'most caring' AI lab, an asset for influencing policy even as it creates negative ramifications around data centers.
thesis🗣 Bill Gurleyconviction: mediumAnthropic
So what: Reputation arbitrage with policymakers is a real competitive asset; discount Anthropic's safety messaging as partly strategic.
“they've been out with the doomerism talk. And so it's given them a halo with the people that may matter” ▶ 35:43
Frontier labs abruptly pivoted from 'AI destroys jobs' to 'AI is a jobs bonanza' precisely as they need trillion-dollar IPOs, implying the prior doom narrative was partly strategic and is now being walked back for the public-markets bid.
causal🗣 Chamath Palihapitiyaconviction: highAnthropicOpenAIFrontier Labs
So what: Narrative timing is tied to IPO marketing; treat lab macro/labor pronouncements as positioning, not forecasts.
“now that we need to have trillion dollar IPOs, the entire Frontier Labs are all like, 'Wow, it's going to be a bonanza of jobs.'” ▶ 9:13
Anthropic appears to be pulling away from OpenAI in growth; per The Information's latest numbers a 10x-YoY grower vs a 3x-YoY grower reaches ~90% share within two years via compounding (10x10=100 vs 3x3=9).
data_point🗣 David Sacksconviction: highAnthropicOpenAIThe Information
So what: Compounding math implies AI may monopolize/duopolize like prior tech categories — a key risk and a reason to back the inertia leader.
“if you have one company that's growing at 10x year-over-year and another company that's growing at 3x... within 2 years, the first company will have 90% market share” ▶ 55:54
Sacks warns the AI market may monopolize/duopolize more than is currently priced in, since every prior major tech category resolved to a monopoly or duopoly.
prediction🗣 David Sacksconviction: mediumAnthropicOpenAI
So what: Mispriced concentration risk/upside; favors the structural leader and raises antitrust as a tail scenario.
“every other major tech category has led to a monopoly or duopoly situation” ▶ 55:31
Compute scarcity and competitive response are the binding constraints that could prevent Anthropic from sustaining 10x growth to monopoly share.
causal🗣 David Sacksconviction: mediumAnthropicGPUs
So what: Physical compute/power limits are the real cap on the monopoly thesis — watch compute allocation as the leading indicator.
“there may not be enough compute to support that kind of growth. There may be physical constraints” ▶ 56:28
Open-source / open-weight crackdown risk 6
Sacks predicts the 'red capture' agenda in Washington is heading toward a ban on open-source/open-weight models, justified by rhetoric (echoed in Anthropic blog posts) that open models lack removable guardrails and are dangerous for cyber/bio threats.
prediction🗣 David Sacksconviction: mediumAnthropicopen-weight modelsWashington
So what: A material regulatory tail risk for the US open-source ecosystem; watch for 'predicate facts' being laid in policy/blog rhetoric.
“where it's all leading to is an effort to ban open source models or open weight models” ▶ 49:56
Banning open weights in the US wouldn't stop a file of numbers but would force cloud providers to stop hosting open models, leaving the rest of the world (and China) to benefit from open models' cost/customization/control advantages — putting the US 'on an island.'
causal🗣 David Sacksconviction: highopen-weight modelscloud service providersChina
So what: US open-source restrictions could cede the global model ecosystem to Chinese open weights — a geopolitical and competitiveness risk.
“all this infrastructure that's been built up it will get much harder to use open models in the United States” ▶ 51:40
If the US restricts open source, the rest of the world ends up running on Chinese models — a conclusion both Sacks and Gurley reached independently in recent blog posts.
prediction🗣 Chamath Palihapitiyaconviction: highChinaopen-weight models
So what: China's openness becomes a soft-power distribution advantage; investors should track adoption of Chinese open weights globally.
“rest of the world ends up running on Chinese models” ▶ 54:29
China (the Communist Party) is paradoxically leading the open-weight movement while the US centralizes — though Gurley insists on the distinction that China leads 'open-weight,' not true 'open-source.'
contrarian🗣 Jason Calacanis / Bill Gurleyconviction: highChinaCommunist Partyopen-weight models
So what: Reframes the geopolitics of AI distribution; the open-weight vs open-source distinction matters for IP, security and competitive analysis.
“the Chinese of all people, the Communist Party is leading the open-source movement and the United States is centralizing” ▶ 40:04
The EU is the canary in the coal mine — it has issued volley after volley of AI regulation with open source 'particularly in the crosshairs' because no single entity can be held responsible to vet a community-contributed model.
thesis🗣 Jason Calacanisconviction: mediumEUopen-source models
So what: EU regulatory posture is a leading indicator of where US/global open-source restrictions could go.
“open source is particularly in the crosshairs there because nobody's in charge of it” ▶ 54:52
Sacks argues decentralized/open AI is the only structural protection against an AI monopolist 'in bed with the deep state'; if only monopoly AI exists, citizens face living off-grid or submitting to a 'social credit system.'
thesis🗣 David Sacksconviction: highopen-source modelsdeep state
So what: Frames open source as a political/freedom backstop, supporting investment and policy advocacy for decentralized AI.
“the only way you're going to be protected is is to have multiple players” ▶ 41:18
AI sovereignty, Apple as dark horse, on-prem demand 3
Calacanis argues 'intelligence sovereignty' (you can't tell me what to think / can't use AI to interpret my data for me) becomes the next privacy frontier, positioning Apple as the dark horse because its hardware (M5, 48-128GB, a rumored 1TB Mac Studio) can run capable local open-source models.
thesis🗣 Jason Calacanisconviction: mediumAppleM5Mac Studiosmall language models (SLMs)
So what: Local-inference-capable Apple hardware is an under-priced AI angle; watch on-device model performance as a catalyst.
“Apple is just the dark horse in this entire race” ▶ 39:44
Regulated industries (finance, healthcare) are demanding on-prem AI and rolling their own models over HIPAA/data-leak/training-contamination fears — Kirkland & Ellis is reportedly spending half a billion dollars to build its own frontier model.
data_point🗣 Chamath Palihapitiya / Jason Calacanisconviction: highKirkland & EllisHIPAAfinancehealthcare
So what: Large regulated buyers building bespoke on-prem models is a durable demand pool for sovereignty/on-prem AI vendors and a leakage risk for frontier-lab SaaS revenue.
“Kirkland Ellis is going to spend half a billion dollars to roll their own Frontier model” ▶ 1:27:29
Enterprises fear being cut off by a frontier lab whose political/values 'philosophy' conflicts with theirs (e.g., a Canadian hospital supporting euthanasia laws an American model refuses to serve), driving demand for hot-swappable, vendor-neutral stacks.
framework🗣 Chamath Palihapitiyaconviction: highfrontier labsFortune 1000Global 1000
So what: Values/terms-of-service risk is a concrete enterprise-procurement driver favoring multi-model abstraction layers.
“they're afraid... that they may be in the crosshairs of accidentally” ▶ 46:32
Token-spend bubble & enterprise efficiency reckoning 5
Enterprise AI spend is overshooting ROI: a Fortune-20 CEO asked for $1B in AI OpEx savings, the team spent $200M on tokens in six months with minimal results, and CEOs are now pulling budgets and cutting licenses (Microsoft reportedly killing Claude licenses).
data_point🗣 Chamath Palihapitiyaconviction: highMicrosoftAnthropic (Claude)Fortune 20
So what: A token-efficiency/cost-discipline wave is coming that could temper lab revenue growth — watch enterprise license churn and net-dollar-retention.
“the team has spent $200 million on tokens and with minimal results” ▶ 48:48
Token efficiency will be a dominant theme over the next year because spend ramped far faster than enterprises expected; a Polymarket-cited case had a client accidentally spend ~$500M in one month ($16.6M/day) by failing to set employee usage limits.
prediction🗣 David Sacksconviction: highPolymarket
So what: Cost-control tooling and per-seat governance become valuable; lab consumption revenue may be more volatile than headline growth suggests.
“token efficiency is going to be a big theme over the next year because the spend has been ramping up way faster than enterprise customers thought” ▶ 57:41
The PLG land-grab dynamic (devs expensing labs on credit cards, later wrapped in enterprise licenses, then CFOs forcing an 'unwind') is creating volatile, hard-to-attribute AI spend buckets that CEOs are now clawing back.
framework🗣 Chamath Palihapitiyaconviction: mediumAnthropicOpenAISlackUber
So what: The bottoms-up PLG motion that drove lab growth may reverse into license cuts — a leading risk indicator for AI SaaS revenue durability.
“the unwind that happens then when you have these huge licenses... you can't really tick and tie it together” ▶ 48:20
Labs have trained users to treat tokens as free via flat $20/$200 plans, mirroring metered-utility economics ('first 10,000 gallons free, then a penny a gallon'), which masks runaway organizational spend once usage scales.
framework🗣 Chamath Palihapitiyaconviction: mediumAnthropicOpenAI
So what: Flat-rate pricing is unsustainable at scale; expect metering, rate limits and usage caps to compress consumer/enterprise economics.
“they've done a tremendous job making people believe that tokens are free” ▶ 58:01
Claude is materially ahead on product execution — Claude for Excel is described as better than Microsoft Copilot 'by a lot' — making Anthropic a formidable competitor beyond raw model scores.
data_point🗣 Chamath Palihapitiyaconviction: highAnthropic (Claude)Microsoft CopilotExcel
So what: Product/UX, not just model quality, is a real differentiator; bullish signal for Anthropic vs Microsoft in productivity AI.
“Claude for Excel is better than Copilot by not by a little, by a lot.” ▶ 49:15
AI & labor: net job loss vs AI-washing debate 11
Sacks' contrarian January prediction that AI leads to job gains, not losses, has become near-consensus: the Goldman Sachs CEO op-ed, plus Altman and Amodei walking back apocalypse claims, mark a complete narrative shift.
contrarian🗣 David Sacksconviction: highGoldman SachsDavid SolomonSam AltmanDario Amodei
So what: A consensus flip toward 'AI is jobs-additive' supports risk-on positioning on AI labor disruption fears subsiding.
“my most contrarian take back in January... is that AI would lead to job gains, not job loss” ▶ 1:02:40
Yale Budget Lab found no discernible AI-driven labor-market disruption over the last three years, and US unemployment sits at 4.3% (near full employment of ~5%) despite being three-plus years into the AI wave.
data_point🗣 David Sacksconviction: highYale Budget LabUS labor market
So what: Hard macro data undercuts the AI-jobs-apocalypse thesis; the 'plural of anecdotes is not data' frame argues against trading on layoff headlines.
“no discernable disruption in the labor market in the last 3 years due to AI” ▶ 1:04:52
Despite coding being AI's single biggest breakout use case, software-engineer job postings are up 15% YoY at a three-year high, because GitHub code commits jumped ~14x YoY (1B last year to 1.1B last month) and more code requires more humans to manage and audit it.
causal🗣 David Sacksconviction: highGitHubsoftware engineers
So what: Jevons-paradox dynamic — automating a task expands demand for the surrounding human work; bullish for dev-tooling and engineering labor demand.
“make something easier, more people do it” ▶ 1:19:39
Sacks sees the early innings of a productivity boom from bespoke software proliferating to firms that never considered themselves tech companies — citing a fund manager whose next two hires are software developers, not data analysts.
thesis🗣 David Sacksconviction: highsoftware developersbespoke software
So what: Broadening of software creation across non-tech firms is a demand tailwind for dev tools, low-code/vibe-coding, and engineering hiring.
“we're at the outset of a boom right now caused by bespoke software proliferating throughout the economy” ▶ 1:20:42
Chamath argues recent layoffs are NOT AI productivity but cover for prior overhiring: companies used the Google-style 'hire talent to keep it off the market' strategy, bloated opex, and are now 'never wasting a crisis' by blaming the two-letter scapegoat 'AI.'
contrarian🗣 Chamath Palihapitiyaconviction: highMetaGoogleBlock
So what: If layoffs are overhiring-correction not AI lift, then 'AI efficiency' claims in filings are suspect — relevant to fading AI-driven margin-expansion narratives.
“It's two letters. And say we're going to fire people. But underneath that is not AI” ▶ 1:05:57
Calacanis predicts real, large near-term displacement (low millions of jobs) as CEOs chase higher earnings by doing more with less — every cab/truck driver and package-sorting job is on a 10-year elimination path via Waymo (3,000 vehicles), Optimus, and Figure robots; Amazon's Andy Jassy says they'll eliminate 600,000 future positions as a recurring theme.
prediction🗣 Jason Calacanisconviction: highWaymoTesla OptimusFigureAmazonAndy JassyZoox
So what: Bull case for autonomy/robotics deployment and a bear case for human transport/logistics labor; watch Amazon headcount-vs-earnings divergence as the tell.
“We are going to eliminate 600,000 future positions and we are going to cut positions.” ▶ 1:10:46
Gurley counters that competition prevents the 'do more with less = 70% operating margins' outcome — efficiency gains get whittled away into lower prices, yielding a productivity boom of cheaper goods rather than obscene profits (except in regulated sectors like healthcare/education).
framework🗣 Bill Gurleyconviction: highregulated industrieshealthcareeducation
So what: Tempers the 'AI margin expansion forever' thesis for competitive sectors while flagging regulated industries as the exception where prices stay sticky.
“Someone else is going to come along and do more for less and lower the price.” ▶ 1:28:50
Gurley argues full automation rarely hits 100% because the economics don't work (the ATM analogy) — non-ownership/robotaxi usage rises so much that total driving-related employment could stay flat or grow even as the per-trip human share falls to ~50%.
contrarian🗣 Bill Gurleyconviction: mediumrobotaxisATMs
So what: Cautions against linear 'all drivers get replaced' models; demand elasticity can offset automation in the labor count.
“humans are going to be used for like 50% of it instead of a hundred” ▶ 1:22:45
Securities-litigation partner Donny King (Acriman) warns of potential shareholder lawsuits for 'AI-washing' as a form of puffery/securities fraud — when companies attribute their own operational underperformance to AI.
data_point🗣 David Sacksconviction: mediumDonny KingAcrimansecurities fraud
So what: Emerging legal risk: AI-washing in disclosures could trigger securities litigation — a governance flag for AI-narrative-heavy public companies.
“we could start seeing shareholder lawsuits against companies that engage in this type of AI washing” ▶ 1:29:42
Goldman's Solomon frames AI as automating 25% of work-hours (not eliminating 25% of jobs), invoking historical precedents (bank tellers rose after ATMs, live entertainment rose after TV) and US gross job churn of 25-35M annually dwarfing net losses.
framework🗣 Jason Calacanis (relaying David Solomon)conviction: mediumGoldman SachsDavid SolomonATMs
So what: The work-hours-not-jobs frame is the institutional case against AI labor doom; note Solomon may be motivated by an Anthropic/OpenAI IPO mandate.
“AI won't eliminate 25% of jobs. It's going to automate 25% of work hours” ▶ 1:01:29
Skilled trades (plumbers, electricians, HVAC) face a structural labor shortage and are pitched as an AI-resilient reskilling destination — Microsoft's MicroWorks-style foundations funding trade scholarships are cited as the model over government retraining programs.
thesis🗣 Bill Gurley / Jason Calacanisconviction: mediumskilled tradesplumberselectriciansHVAC
So what: Skilled-trades shortage is an under-appreciated labor-market opportunity relatively insulated from AI automation.
“The skilled trades are like we're we're short of people.” ▶ 1:23:46
AI-native talent & human capital edge 4
Sacks calls Claude proficiency the single most marketable skill in the economy right now — being the only AI-native hire confers a 10x advantage akin to early spreadsheet mastery — though he flags it as a likely short-term arbitrage as everyone catches up.
thesis🗣 David Sacksconviction: highAnthropic (Claude)
So what: Human-capital edge accrues to AI-native workers across all functions (legal, marketing, sales, accounting), not just engineering — a hiring and self-positioning signal.
“the single most marketable skill in the economy right now has got to be proficiency in Claude” ▶ 11:02
When Calacanis offered VC-associate applicants a choice between writing a deal memo or vibe-coding a tool, ~80% (of 400-500 applicants for six slots) chose to build software — a surprising signal that the newest grads are AI-first and high-agency.
anecdote🗣 Jason Calacanisconviction: highventure capitalvibe coding
So what: The incoming cohort defaults to building over writing — a leading indicator of how AI-native talent reshapes white-collar work and hiring.
“80% of them did the vibe coding and I was shocked I thought it would be the exact opposite” ▶ 7:50
Sacks argues dropping AI into an org doesn't automatically create value — extracting it requires sophisticated custom prompts/skills documents (their producer's Claude Co-work briefing system, iterated daily for three months) that the average person can't generate, so humans are still needed to supervise, iterate and validate.
framework🗣 David Sacksconviction: highAnthropic (Claude Co-work)
So what: Value capture from AI is gated by human orchestration skill — supports demand for AI-ops/prompt-engineering roles and tempers naive 'AI replaces everyone' models.
“this idea that you're just going to be able to like throw AI into an organization and it's just magically going to generate value is not true” ▶ 12:48
Gurley's framework: the real AI risk is mindset, not the tool — citing a Gallup poll that 59% of workers are ambivalent 'quiet quitters' with low agency who won't become AI-enabled and thus become 'sitting ducks.'
framework🗣 Bill Gurleyconviction: highGalluplabor force
So what: Job-loss risk concentrates in low-agency, non-AI-adopting workers — a lens for assessing which roles/firms are actually exposed.
“the best way to protect yourself from AI is to be the most AI enabled version of yourself you can be” ▶ 6:59
AI regulation philosophy & centralization risk 4
Sacks argues the biggest AI risk is centralization of power and Orwellian misuse — most likely by government, not a private actor — so creating an 'FDA for AI' that approves models is dangerous because regulators inevitably expand 'safety' into a censorship agenda (as happened with social-media trust & safety).
thesis🗣 David Sacksconviction: highFDA for AIsocial mediagovernment
So what: Argues against model-approval regulation; the policy fight over an AI regulator is a major sector catalyst/risk to monitor.
“if you create say an FDA for AI... that will give government the power to approve models” ▶ 20:59
Sacks' preferred check on AI power is aggressive antitrust if the market monopolizes, but as long as five frontier labs compete vigorously, competition itself protects consumers and helps the US beat China — so antitrust should be held in reserve.
framework🗣 David Sacksconviction: highantitrustfrontier labsChina
So what: Signals the policy posture (competition-first, antitrust-as-backstop) from a sitting administration voice — relevant to how AI consolidation may be policed.
“If the AI market becomes monopolized and falls into the hands of one or two companies, I would use antitrust law very aggressively” ▶ 23:30
Gurley dismantles the Pope's AI-regulation case by analogy: Leo XIII's 1891 encyclical warned the Industrial Revolution would harm workers, yet by today the global work-week fell from 60+ to 34 hours, real wages rose 8-10x, child labor went 18% to 0, life expectancy +60%, and global poverty fell from 75% to under 10%.
data_point🗣 Bill Gurleyconviction: highPope Leo XIVLeo XIIIindustrial revolution
So what: Historical base-rate argument that technophobic regulation tends to be wrong; a frame for discounting AI-doom-driven policy.
“global poverty went from 75% of humanity to under 10%. All those things happened because of technology, innovation, and capitalism” ▶ 26:01
Pope Leo XIV's first encyclical (235 pages / 42,000 words) argues technology is never neutral and 'takes on the characteristics of those who build, finance, and control it,' calls for AI regulation, autonomous-weapons bans and child guardrails; Amazon, Google and Meta lobbied the Vatican on April 29th to soften it but failed.
data_point🗣 Jason Calacanisconviction: mediumPope Leo XIVVaticanAmazonGoogleMetaAnthropicChris Olah
So what: Vatican moral pressure adds a new constituency to AI regulation; big-tech lobbying failure signals regulatory momentum that could affect the sector.
“technology takes on the characteristics... of those who build, finance, and control it” ▶ 18:43
Thomas Laffont: The $4T AI IPO Wave Is Coming… and We’ve Never Seen Anything Like It33 min34 insights2026-06-04
▶ Watch on YouTube
Coatue's Thomas Laffont argues the private 'unicorn economy' is healthier than ever because AI concentrates capital into a handful of mega-winners (his 'Magnificent 8' worth ~$4T), and a wave of IPOs — SpaceX, OpenAI, Anthropic — is about to inject liquidity and let the public market 'stress-test' valuations. He frames the era through power-law concentration, faster compounding, and disruption across nearly every sector.
Unicorn economy health & funding concentration · 4The 'Magnificent 8' private index thesis · 2The $4T IPO wave & liquidity rebalancing · 3OpenAI / Anthropic growth & revenue trajectory · 4SpaceX valuation framework · 3Power-law compounding & the centacorn data · 4Semis, memory & compute supply chain · 4AI revenue pillars & ROI debate · 3Valuation discipline, bubble debate & positioning risk · 4Cross-sector disruption & second-order effects · 3
Unicorn economy health & funding concentration 4
The unicorn economy is up ~70% on average since September 2024, and the public market made a comparable move, with public names like Palo Alto driving NASDAQ-share performance.
data_point🗣 Thomas Laffont (Coatue)conviction: highPalo Alto NetworksNASDAQunicorn economy
So what: Private and public tech are moving in lockstep, so private marks may be a leading signal for public AI exposure.
“the unicorn economy on average since September of 24 is up 70%”
Funding per unicorn has increased 5x since 2021 — fewer new unicorns are being minted but each raises far more, as AI captures rising wallet share of total venture fundraising.
data_point🗣 Thomas Laffont (Coatue)conviction: highAIventure capitalunicorns
So what: Capital is concentrating into a narrow set of AI mega-rounds; broad early-stage venture exposure is structurally disadvantaged versus the top names.
“the funding per unicorn has increased 5x since 2021. So we have fewer unicorns that are each raising more.” ▶ 1:52
The 2021 ZIRP-era unicorn cohort is a warning sign: ~479 companies of which fewer than 20% had raised or exited 20 quarters in, versus a ~73-company pre-2018 cohort where ~80% had raised or exited at the same maturity.
data_point🗣 Thomas Laffont (Coatue)conviction: high2021 unicorn cohortZIRPventure exits
So what: A huge overhang of stranded 2021-vintage unicorns still needs to clear; watch which way the new 2024 AI cohort resembles.
“20 quarters in, you can see less than 20% less had either exited or raised. But look at the number 479 versus 73 in the prior cohort.” ▶ 2:42
The top 10 companies are capturing a disproportionate share of all unicorn funding, driven by massive rounds at OpenAI and Anthropic — concentration within concentration.
thesis🗣 Thomas Laffont (Coatue)conviction: highOpenAIAnthropictop-10 unicorns
So what: Even within AI, capital flows to a tiny handful; index-style private exposure underperforms unless it owns the leaders.
The 'Magnificent 8' private index thesis 2
Laffont proposes a new private 'Magnificent 8' index — SpaceX, Stripe, Anthropic, Databricks, Revolut, ByteDance, and Anduril (plus others) — representing almost $4 trillion of value that has outperformed nearly every public Mag 7 name.
thesis🗣 Thomas Laffont (Coatue)conviction: highSpaceXStripeAnthropicDatabricksRevolutByteDanceAndurilMagnificent 7
So what: Defines the specific private names a public investor should track for the coming IPO wave; this basket is Coatue's stated 'own-for-a-decade' universe.
“I'd feel pretty comfortable owning this index if I could for the next decade plus... It represents almost $4 trillion of value and has really crushed the traditional kind of mag 7.” ▶ 4:05
The number of names in this private index will shrink as constituents go public, implying the 'private alpha' window is closing and converging into public markets.
prediction🗣 Thomas Laffont (Coatue)conviction: mediumMagnificent 8IPO
So what: Public investors will soon be able to buy what was private-only exposure; positioning ahead of these IPOs matters.
“for now I'll be able to call the magnificent 8, but that number is going to shrink as these companies go public” ▶ 3:41
The $4T IPO wave & liquidity rebalancing 3
SpaceX (IPO in 'the next few weeks'), Anthropic (just filed a confidential S-1), and OpenAI are imminent IPOs whose combined exit value would exceed the prior ~10 years of unicorn cash returned combined.
prediction🗣 Thomas Laffont (Coatue)conviction: highSpaceXAnthropicOpenAIIPOS-1
So what: A massive, near-term liquidity event is coming; prepare for both new public AI supply and a large redistribution of capital to LPs/GPs.
“we know Anthropic just today the headlines hit that they've submitted confidentially for their S1... it's basically going to be more than the 10 years kind of combined.” ▶ 5:26
The venture ecosystem, historically cash-consuming and out of balance, is now significantly more balanced even before these liquidity events — a structural improvement Laffont credits to AI.
thesis🗣 Thomas Laffont (Coatue)conviction: highventure capitalAIcash returns
So what: Improved cash-return dynamics support continued LP commitments and a healthier funding cycle into the next vintage.
Passive/index buying will delay the public market's true price-discovery from IPO day-one to roughly 'six months plus one,' because supply/demand from large passive flows must wash through first.
framework🗣 Chamath Palihapitiya / Thomas Laffontconviction: mediumIPOpassive investingprice discovery
So what: Don't trust day-one IPO pricing as the verdict on these names; the real read on valuation comes ~6 months post-listing.
“6 month plus one is when you'd say we can really start to get a sense of what these companies are” ▶ 24:09
OpenAI / Anthropic growth & revenue trajectory 4
Revenue of OpenAI/Anthropic (chart starting Jan 2025) has surpassed Workday, ServiceNow, Adobe, and Salesforce within ~18 months and is now bigger than Google Cloud and Azure.
data_point🗣 Thomas Laffont (Coatue)conviction: highOpenAIAnthropicWorkdayServiceNowAdobeSalesforceGoogle CloudAzure
So what: These private AI labs are already enterprise-software-scale revenue engines, justifying (per Laffont) their extreme private valuations.
“Now even bigger than Google Cloud and Azure.” ▶ 6:50
Laffont forecasts OpenAI/Anthropic revenue could exceed AWS by end of this year and surpass all of Microsoft by 2028.
prediction🗣 Thomas Laffont (Coatue)conviction: mediumOpenAIAnthropicAWSMicrosoft
So what: If even partly right, this reprices the entire enterprise-software and hyperscaler competitive landscape; a direct threat to incumbent cloud revenue.
“by the end of the year could be bigger than AWS. and potentially bigger than all of Microsoft by 2028.” ▶ 7:08
Hyperscalers are not just being disrupted by AI labs — they are funding the disruption, having made unprecedented investments to enable the ChatGPT moment.
causal🗣 Thomas Laffont (Coatue)conviction: highhyperscalersMicrosoftOpenAIAI capex
So what: Incumbent cloud players are financing their own potential disruptors; watch the circular capex/revenue relationship for sustainability risk.
Anthropic was 'a completely different company' pre- vs post-Claude Code — a single product event dented the trajectory of nearly the entire AI industry — and the 'models are commodities' narrative has been thoroughly disproven.
contrarian🗣 Thomas Laffont (Coatue)conviction: highAnthropicClaude CodeAI models
So what: Bet against model commoditization; product/distribution breakthroughs (like coding agents) can step-change a lab's economics overnight.
“Anthropic pre-cloud code was a completely different company than postcloud code... the narrative of oh these models are commodities... that's been pretty thoroughly disproven now” ▶ 24:39
SpaceX valuation framework 3
The number-one driver correlated to SpaceX's valuation is cadence of launches, and the market increasingly pays MORE per launch as launch volume rises — counterintuitive but, per Coatue, rational.
framework🗣 Thomas Laffont (Coatue)conviction: highSpaceXStarlinklaunch cadence
So what: Use launch cadence (and valuation-per-launch trend) as the key fundamental metric to track SpaceX into and after its IPO.
“the number one driver correlated to the valuation of SpaceX is cadence of launches” ▶ 8:10
Coatue's 'CODE' framework maps SpaceX through phases — pre-constellation (one-time govt launch revenue) → constellation (recurring subscription revenue) → multiple constellations (scale) → platform (space data centers, Moon/Mars optionality), with quality of the business model rising at each stage.
framework🗣 Thomas Laffont (Coatue)conviction: highSpaceXStarlinksatellite constellationsspace data centers
So what: Provides a stage-based lens for whether SpaceX's multiple expansion is justified; recurring constellation revenue is the inflection that re-rates the business.
Starlink can capture the global telco profit pool ($200–400B) and within a few years will power direct-to-device phone calls anywhere on earth, a 'solved problem' that threatens incumbent broadband and wireless carriers.
thesis🗣 Thomas Laffont (Coatue)conviction: highStarlinkSpaceXtelecombroadbandwireless carriers
So what: Structural short/disruption thesis on legacy telcos; Starlink's addressable profit pool reframes SpaceX as a telecom-disruptor, not just a launch business.
“the global profit pool of telco and service providers across the world is anywhere between 2 to 400 billion” ▶ 30:20
Power-law compounding & the centacorn data 4
Coatue data: a unicorn has ~8% odds of becoming a decacorn, a decacorn ~8–13% odds of reaching $100B, but a centacorn ($100B+) has a 31% chance of delivering a 10x — bigger companies are MORE likely to compound, not less.
contrarian🗣 Thomas Laffont (Coatue)conviction: highunicornsdecacornscentacornspower law
So what: Counterintuitive: scale is not mean-reverting at the top — argues for concentrating capital in the largest winners rather than diversifying.
“if you're a centacorn 100 billion or more... you now have a 31% chance of having had a 10x.” ▶ 11:50
The 31% 10x odds reflect a filtering mechanism: durability of earnings and compounding advantage gate each step up, akin to Ben Graham-style fundamental analysis applied to mega-caps.
framework🗣 Chamath Palihapitiya / panelconviction: mediumdurability of earningscompounding advantageBen Graham
So what: Frames winner-picking as testing for durable, compounding moats; the panel suspects trillion-dollar names may exceed 30% odds of a further 10x.
Value creation is accelerating: three companies went from $500B to $1T market cap in the same year and two did it 'in a matter of weeks,' versus the historical norm of multiple years.
data_point🗣 Thomas Laffont (Coatue)conviction: hightrillion-dollar companiesmarket cap
So what: Compounding velocity at the top is unprecedented, raising the cost of not owning the winners and supporting momentum-style mega-cap strategies.
“not only did we have three companies do it in the same year, but we had two companies do it in a matter of weeks.” ▶ 12:31
A backtest shows that buying the NASDAQ and rebalancing annually into just the top 10 companies by market cap outperformed by roughly 3x over a decade.
data_point🗣 panel (All-In host)conviction: mediumNASDAQtop-10 rebalancingmomentum strategy
So what: Concrete, mechanical strategy idea: own/rebalance into the largest mega-caps; aligns with the power-law thesis.
“If you just rebalanced every year on the top 10 companies in the NASDAQ... you outperform over a decade by like 3x.” ▶ 29:13
Semis, memory & compute supply chain 4
Cerebras (which Laffont led the Series B and sat on the board of) went IPO after years of dark periods with no new capital, and an OpenAI contract quintupled the company's value — illustrating non-linear, grind-then-breakout semiconductor outcomes.
anecdote🗣 Thomas Laffont (Coatue)conviction: highCerebrasOpenAIsemiconductorsCoatue
So what: Customer-contract catalysts (especially from AI labs) can re-rate semi names 5x; watch for OpenAI/Anthropic supplier announcements.
“a massive open AI contract which then quintuple the value of the company.”
Memory per user could quintuple (5x) as AI systems demand more context/personalization to deliver useful, preference-aware services — explaining recent rallies in memory companies.
thesis🗣 Thomas Laffont (Coatue)conviction: highmemory chipsDRAMAI personalization
So what: Structural long on memory semis; personalization-driven memory demand is a distinct AI bottleneck from compute/GPUs.
“the amount of memory per user could quintuple just based on the demand that these AI systems are requiring” ▶ 14:27
Memory deserves a premium multiple over ASIC/logic chips because there is no equivalent of TSMC to foundry memory — designing a custom chip can lean on TSMC, but making memory has no such enabler.
contrarian🗣 Thomas Laffont (Coatue)conviction: mediummemoryASICTSMCcustom silicon
So what: Re-rate memory makers higher relative to ASIC designers on scarcer supply-side optionality; a relative-value pair idea within semis.
“If I want to make memory, well, there is no TSMC... what should the memory multiples be versus ASIC chips” ▶ 27:00
The semiconductor industry has materially outperformed the index on a generational run since the 2024 All-In summit.
data_point🗣 Thomas Laffont (Coatue)conviction: highsemiconductorsindex
So what: Confirms semis as the dominant AI-cycle equity trade; supports continued overweight despite the run-up.
“Semis are on a generational run.” ▶ 13:38
AI revenue pillars & ROI debate 3
Laffont sizes the AI ecosystem at ~$140B today, ~$300B this year, and doubling in 2027, built on three pillars: consumer (subs × ARPU), advertising, and enterprise.
data_point🗣 Thomas Laffont (Coatue)conviction: mediumAI ecosystemconsumer AIadvertisingenterprise AI
So what: Concrete TAM framing to rebut the 'where's the revenue / no ROI' bear case; track the doubling into 2027 as the validating milestone.
“it's about 140 billion today it'll be about 300 billion this year and it'll double in 2027” ▶ 15:08
About a quarter of ads served by Meta and Google are already AI-enabled, and Laffont expects that to reach 100% — an ~$150B AI advertising revenue pillar that's underappreciated.
thesis🗣 Thomas Laffont (Coatue)conviction: highMetaGoogleAI advertising
So what: Ad-tech is a hidden AI monetization engine; Meta and Google are direct AI-revenue beneficiaries beyond the lab narrative.
“about a quarter of ads served by Meta and Google are AI enabled. We think that penetration will eventually go to 100%. that's 150 billion” ▶ 15:39
Enterprise AI revenue is being driven by coding agents — Claude Code and Codex — operating inside businesses.
data_point🗣 Thomas Laffont (Coatue)conviction: highClaude CodeCodexenterprise AIcoding agents
So what: Coding agents are the leading enterprise monetization wedge; their adoption is a proxy for enterprise AI revenue growth.
Valuation discipline, bubble debate & positioning risk 4
Laffont concedes private AI valuations are 'disconnecting from any valuation metric we've ever had' — venture-style bets on trillion-dollar companies at 50–100x revenue — but insists these are real, revenue-generating, sometimes-profitable businesses (Anthropic reportedly had a profitable month), unlike 2000 or 2021.
contrarian🗣 Thomas Laffont (Coatue) / Bill Ackman (cited)conviction: highAnthropicOpenAIBill Ackmanrevenue multiplesdot-com bubble
So what: The bull defense rests on real revenue, not just multiples; the bear risk is the 50–100x revenue disconnect if growth decelerates.
“You're making venture investments in trillion dollar companies and giving them 50 times revenue, 100 times revenue evaluations.” ▶ 21:55
The public market is the 'great antiseptic/equalizer' — Laffont welcomes SpaceX, OpenAI, and Anthropic facing short-sellers and scrutiny, implying he expects them to withstand it but acknowledges it as the real test.
thesis🗣 Thomas Laffont (Coatue)conviction: highSpaceXOpenAIAnthropicshort sellerspublic markets
So what: Frames the IPOs as a confidence signal; a failure of any of the three to hold up post-IPO would be the key bear catalyst for the whole AI complex.
“It will be the great antiseptic. it will not care about my presentation” ▶ 23:13
A prolonged absence of NEW centacorns ($100B+ companies) over the next decade would be a warning sign for the ecosystem — the count has been 'stuck' with no new ones in the past couple of years.
framework🗣 Thomas Laffont (Coatue)conviction: mediumcentacornsventure ecosystem
So what: Use new-centacorn formation as a leading health indicator; a stalled count signals the power law has fully concentrated and new entrants are blocked.
“if we were to see no new center coorns... in the next decade... I think that's going to be a warning sign” ▶ 21:07
A price war between OpenAI and Anthropic is rationally possible given their cash piles (~$200B), echoing ride-sharing and food-delivery wars — but heavy infrastructure spend may absorb the excess capital and prevent it.
prediction🗣 Chamath Palihapitiya / Thomas Laffontconviction: lowOpenAIAnthropicprice warUberDoorDash
So what: Watch for AI-pricing aggression as a margin risk; infrastructure capex is the offset that could keep pricing rational.
“Could we see a price war between Open Eye and Anthropic?... Rationally, they should.” ▶ 31:11
Cross-sector disruption & second-order effects 3
Almost every sector is being transformed simultaneously — telco (Starlink), semis/energy (data centers reshaping Pennsylvania's power equation), autos (Ferrari's troubled EV/autonomous launch), and consumer (GLP-1s reshaping food, alcohol, and diet) — and this is happening before superintelligence even exists.
thesis🗣 Thomas Laffont (Coatue)conviction: highStarlinkdata centersenergyFerrariGLP-1autosconsumer staples
So what: Breadth of disruption means sector-by-sector incumbents (autos, telco, food/alcohol) face AI/tech displacement risk; screen broadly for losers, not just AI winners.
“almost every sector of the economy is being transformed at the moment... And by the way, we don't even have super intelligence yet.” ▶ 16:21
When $3–4 trillion of IPO proceeds recycle from GPs to LPs back into Silicon Valley, expect a capital influx that supports asset prices including San Francisco real estate — a reversal of prior bearishness.
prediction🗣 panel / Thomas Laffontconviction: lowSilicon ValleySan Francisco real estateLPsGPs
So what: Second-order trade: the liquidity wave could reflate Bay Area real estate and local risk assets; SF homes are reportedly already selling.
“we'll see whether this influx of capital... San Francisco homes are selling.” ▶ 29:55
Trillion-dollar companies that re-rated in weeks are decades-old businesses trading at among the lowest earnings multiples in the S&P 500 — 'pent-up energy that just got released,' not speculative froth.
contrarian🗣 Thomas Laffont (Coatue)conviction: highS&P 500trillion-dollar companiesearnings multiples
So what: Argues the mega-cap surge is a justified re-rating of cheap, durable earnings — not a bubble — supporting continued mega-cap ownership.
≈ “these companies have been around for decades... they trade at the lowest multiple of earnings of the S&P 500 of almost any other company” paraphrase ▶ 26:35
OpenAI CFO Sarah Friar: IPO, AI Rivalries, New Device, and Spending $100B+ on Compute32 min43 insights2026-06-02
▶ Watch on YouTube
OpenAI CFO Sarah Friar lays out the company's capital and compute strategy: a $122B March raise for 'maximum optionality,' a severe multi-year compute shortage (no relief through 2027 and tight into 2030+), a multi-CSP/multi-chip 'Rubik's cube' that shifts capex to opex, ~50/50 consumer/enterprise revenue, an imminent Jony Ive consumer device, and a memory-plus-intent advertising thesis that could rival Google and Meta.
Capital strategy & IPO timing · 4Compute scarcity & build timeline · 7Multi-CSP / multi-chip supply diversification · 6Unit economics, cost deflation & pricing power · 6Competitive dynamics & the 'intelligence layer' moat · 5Consumer scale, engagement & the new device · 6Advertising thesis & monetization optionality · 5Community, talent & macro signals · 4
Capital strategy & IPO timing 4
OpenAI raised $122B in March 2026 explicitly to maximize optionality, not as a path to any specific IPO date; Friar frames an IPO as a milestone and 'just another way to fundraise,' not a destination.
thesis🗣 guest, OpenAI CFO Sarah Friarconviction: highOpenAISarah Friar
So what: Signals OpenAI is in no rush to IPO and will keep tapping private markets; investors expecting a near-term public listing should temper timing assumptions.
“an IPO... it's a milestone. It is not a destination. Do not run your company as if that's some sort of destination. It's just another way to fund raise.”
The market is set up for potentially three mega-IPOs (SpaceX, OpenAI, Anthropic) at a scale larger than the 2000-2001 era, with the prior record IPO being Saudi Aramco at ~$30B; abundant sidelined capital from buybacks/M&A could absorb them.
prediction🗣 guest, OpenAI CFO Sarah Friarconviction: mediumSpaceXOpenAIAnthropicSaudi Aramco
So what: A wave of trillion-scale tech IPOs could reprice public AI exposure and absorb large pools of sidelined cash; watch IPO calendar as a liquidity event for the sector.
“you're going to have potentially three IPOs at a scale that will be bigger even than 2001 2000” ▶ 2:00
Friar dismisses first-mover advantage in IPO timing ('No one remembers who went first, Google or Yahoo, Lyft or Uber'), arguing the market is a long-run 'weighing machine, not a popularity machine.'
framework🗣 guest, OpenAI CFO Sarah Friarconviction: highOpenAIAnthropic
So what: Pushes back on the WSJ narrative of an AI IPO 'race'; durability of the business, not listing order, is the stated north star.
“the market is a weighing machine, not a popularity machine. No one remembers who went first, Google or Yahoo, Lyft or Uber.” ▶ 2:34
OpenAI is not yet investment-grade and therefore cannot access low-cost debt financing on its own, which is a primary reason it leans on CSP partners and built-to-suit structures to fund compute.
data_point🗣 guest, OpenAI CFO Sarah Friarconviction: highOpenAI
So what: An eventual investment-grade rating would unlock cheaper capital and change OpenAI's build economics; a credit-rating milestone is a watch-item for the AI capex cycle.
