“in a moment where I'm not yet an investment grade type of entity where I can go get lowerc cost debt financing, being able to work with partners to do that is really important” ▶ 25:40
Compute scarcity & build timeline 7
Compute is severely supply-constrained: OpenAI still won't have enough compute in 2026, 2027 is 'pretty limited as well,' and Friar feels most short of compute when looking at 2030-2032.
prediction🗣 guest, OpenAI CFO Sarah Friarconviction: highOpenAI
So what: A multi-year structural compute deficit supports sustained demand for power, data centers, chips, and memory; the bottleneck is not near-term-transient, favoring infrastructure suppliers.
“in 26 we still won't have enough compute... in 27 it's pretty limited as well frankly... where I feel most short of compute right now is starting to look at 30, 31, 32” ▶ 8:59
The compute bottleneck is multi-node with shifting choke points: energy/power first, then powered land, regulatory speed, racks/chips, a current memory price spike, talent, and even community 'trust.'
framework🗣 guest, OpenAI CFO Sarah Friarconviction: highOpenAImemorysemiconductorsenergy
So what: Identifies where to hunt for bottleneck winners; the called-out memory spike is a concrete near-term signal for HBM/DRAM suppliers and pricing.
“energy first and foremost um land power... racks and chips... in that supply chain memory spike is is on at the moment access to great talent” ▶ 9:13
OpenAI is breaking ground on a 1-gigawatt data center in Saline, Michigan as part of its Oracle complex, but won't get usable compute from it until end of 2027 / early 2028.
data_point🗣 guest, OpenAI CFO Sarah Friarconviction: highOpenAIOracleMichigan
So what: Quantifies the ~2-year lag from groundbreaking to live compute, explaining why capital must be deployed years ahead of demand; Oracle is a named build partner.
“that Michigan data center in Seline, I don't think we will be getting compute out of it until probably end of 27, early 28” ▶ 19:09
Standing up 1 gigawatt of AI compute costs roughly $50B all-in (land, power, chips), per estimates Friar engages with, and OpenAI must invest ahead of demand to secure it.
data_point🗣 host / guest, OpenAI CFO Sarah Friarconviction: mediumOpenAI
So what: A ~$50B/GW capex benchmark lets investors size the total addressable spend across all frontier labs and the suppliers capturing it.
“to stand up 1 gawatt of AI compute costs about $50 billion in cap land, PowerShell chips, everything all in around 50 billion” ▶ 22:43
Friar reaffirms her ~18-month-old framework that 1 gigawatt of compute equates to roughly $10B/year of revenue for OpenAI, a ratio she says extrapolates to Anthropic and Gemini.
framework🗣 host / guest, OpenAI CFO Sarah Friarconviction: highOpenAIAnthropicGeminiGoogle
So what: A gigawatts-to-revenue conversion lets investors back into frontier-lab revenue from announced power buildouts; pairs with the ~$50B/GW capex figure to model returns on AI infrastructure.
“one gigawatt is roughly equivalent to about $10 billion a year of revenue to open AAI” ▶ 7:55
OpenAI deliberately throttled Sora/video compute to redirect tokens, but Friar insists video is 'not over' and will demand much more real-time compute as multimodality and voice scale.
thesis🗣 guest, OpenAI CFO Sarah Friarconviction: mediumOpenAISora
So what: Multimodal/voice and agentic workloads shift demand toward distributed real-time inference, expanding the addressable market for edge/regional inference capacity.
“video does, but video is not over... when you start to think about where AI is taking us into more multimodality” ▶ 13:59
Training stays concentrated in the US for national-security reasons ('USG reasons... national asset'), while inference is intended to go global and increasingly real-time for agentic workloads.
thesis🗣 guest, OpenAI CFO Sarah Friarconviction: highOpenAIUnited States
So what: Bifurcation implies US-located training megaclusters plus a global distributed inference footprint, shaping where data-center and power demand lands geographically.
“training that mostly still all happens here in the United States for USG reasons... For inference, we want that to be global” ▶ 13:31
Multi-CSP / multi-chip supply diversification 6
OpenAI has gone from one CSP (Microsoft Azure) and one chip (Nvidia) two years ago to sitting on top of every major CSP: Oracle, CoreWeave, Microsoft, GCP, AWS plus smaller neoclouds.
data_point🗣 guest, OpenAI CFO Sarah Friarconviction: highOpenAIMicrosoft AzureOracleCoreWeaveGoogle CloudAWS
So what: Microsoft's exclusivity is clearly over; Oracle, CoreWeave, GCP and AWS all now have OpenAI demand, broadening which cloud names benefit from OpenAI capex.
“today we sit on top of every CSP Oracle um coreweave um Microsoft GCP AWS and a bunch of small neocalars” ▶ 23:55
Using multiple investment-grade CSPs is a deliberate financial engineering move to shift capex into opex, letting OpenAI pay as it earns revenue rather than fronting build costs.
framework🗣 guest, OpenAI CFO Sarah Friarconviction: highOpenAI
So what: Explains why CSPs (not OpenAI) carry much of the AI capex and balance-sheet risk; the CSPs' creditworthiness underwrites OpenAI's growth.
“what CSPs do for us in effect is they shift capex into opex so you pay as you get the revenue” ▶ 23:41
Nvidia remains OpenAI's absolute priority/frontier chip partner, with the next big training run in fall on Vera Rubin and the Feynman series being planned next.
data_point🗣 guest, OpenAI CFO Sarah Friarconviction: highOpenAINvidiaVera RubinFeynman
So what: Confirms Nvidia's Rubin generation has secured OpenAI's flagship training demand; Feynman roadmap visibility supports multi-generation Nvidia revenue.
“Nvidia remains our absolute priority partner. They have the Frontier chip. Our next big trading run in the fall will be done on Ver Rubin” ▶ 24:20
OpenAI is multi-chip beyond Nvidia: AMD chips are in the pipeline, Cerebras is already online for low-latency real-time coding, and OpenAI is building its own chip with Broadcom.
data_point🗣 guest, OpenAI CFO Sarah Friarconviction: highOpenAIAMDCerebrasBroadcomNvidia
So what: AMD and Broadcom (custom silicon) are named beneficiaries of OpenAI diversification; Cerebras is validated in production for inference, a competitive signal against Nvidia's monopoly narrative.
“we also now have chips in the pipeline from AMD. Um Cerebrris is already online... And there's our own chip that we're working on with Broadcom.” ▶ 24:32
OpenAI is shifting toward built-to-suit data centers beyond the CSP model, starting with a Texas facility built with SoftBank Energy that requires more direct capex.
data_point🗣 guest, OpenAI CFO Sarah Friarconviction: highOpenAISoftBankTexas
So what: Marks a strategic move up the capex stack; SoftBank is a named energy/build partner, and built-to-suit implies OpenAI taking on more balance-sheet risk over time.
“We announced a data center we're building with SoftBank Energy um down in Texas. That's the beginning of something that's beyond a CSP.” ▶ 25:08
Being locked to a single chip vendor guarantees periods off the frontier due to leapfrogging, so multi-chip sourcing is framed as a frontier-access necessity, not just cost arbitrage.
causal🗣 guest, OpenAI CFO Sarah Friarconviction: highOpenAINvidiaAMD
So what: Implies sustained demand diversification away from any single accelerator vendor whenever a competitor leapfrogs, supporting a multi-vendor accelerator market.
“if you're only on one chip, there's just inherently a moment where you can't be on the frontier because there's some leaprogging that happens” ▶ 24:12
Unit economics, cost deflation & pricing power 6
Cost per equivalent capability fell ~97% from GPT-4 to GPT-4.5-class models in about two years, a deflationary curve OpenAI relies on to expand gross margin.
data_point🗣 guest, OpenAI CFO Sarah Friarconviction: highOpenAIGPT-4GPT-4.5
So what: Rapid token-cost deflation is the core margin engine; it both lowers customer prices and lets OpenAI capture spread, a key variable for any AI margin model.
“from four to 54, it was 97%. But that happened in like two years.” ▶ 18:18
OpenAI raised list prices on its newest model (GPT-5.5) by ~2x but customers still net a 20-30% per-token cost reduction because the model is far more efficient per token.
data_point🗣 guest, OpenAI CFO Sarah Friarconviction: highOpenAIGPT-5.5
So what: Demonstrates pricing power coexisting with falling customer cost; OpenAI can raise headline prices while still undercutting on real cost-per-task, a durable margin lever.
“we actually raise prices on 552x. But if you look at what the cost of the customer is, they're probably still getting a break of about 20 to 30% cost reduction per token”
OpenAI is moving away from cost-plus toward value-based pricing, and token scarcity is causing price 'compression' (upward) that improves economics.
thesis🗣 guest, OpenAI CFO Sarah Friarconviction: highOpenAI
So what: Scarcity plus value-based pricing argues for improving frontier-lab gross margins even as capex rises; counters the 'LLMs are commoditizing to zero margin' bear case.
“you get beyond kind of a cost plus type pricing into something that feels more akin to the value being created. Now, scarcity of tokens helps because it's causing a bit of a compression.” ▶ 11:40
Per-gigawatt compute cost is rising (power and memory getting more expensive), but chip-side deprecation/efficiency more than offsets it, so cost per unit sold to a customer still falls.
causal🗣 guest, OpenAI CFO Sarah Friarconviction: highOpenAImemorysemiconductorsenergy
So what: Even with power/memory inflation, the net economics improve via silicon efficiency; the bull case depends on chip efficiency outrunning power/memory cost increases.
≈ “we assume right now that compute actually on a per gigawatt is getting more expensive... However, the intelligence that we get on the other side out because of the deprecation on the chip side is more than making up for that” paraphrase ▶ 20:13
Capital-allocation decisions must be made on future (not today's) cost profile, or OpenAI risks mispricing outcomes; it deliberately 'leans in' on assumed future cost declines.
framework🗣 guest, OpenAI CFO Sarah Friarconviction: highOpenAI
So what: Reveals OpenAI underwrites investments assuming continued steep cost declines; if deflation stalls, current capex bets could prove mispriced, a key risk to monitor.
≈ “if you make it on today's cost profile, you actually might mispric the outcome. So you have to lean in a little on the cost profile.” paraphrase
For near years (2026-2027) OpenAI builds revenue bottoms-up (price x quantity, subscribers, ads tied to weekly/daily actives); for outer years it inverts the model, mapping bought compute to implied revenue.
framework🗣 guest, OpenAI CFO Sarah Friarconviction: highOpenAI
So what: The 'compute-implies-revenue' inversion for outer years means OpenAI's long-range revenue confidence rests on the gigawatt-to-dollars ratio holding, not on product-level forecasts.
“When you get into the outer years, you're actually looking more at the compute you've bought and almost just doing an algorithm the other way that's saying this amount of compute should equate somewhat to this amount of revenue.” ▶ 21:40
Competitive dynamics & the 'intelligence layer' moat 5
Friar implicitly concedes Anthropic has gained ground in developers, corporations and revenue, but reframes it as OpenAI running a different 'single foundation, many interfaces' strategy rather than a head-to-head loss.
contrarian🗣 guest, OpenAI CFO Sarah Friarconviction: mediumOpenAIAnthropic
So what: Confirms the consensus that Anthropic has closed the enterprise/developer gap; OpenAI's counter is breadth (consumer + enterprise) and a compounding single-model flywheel.
“Our strategy is different... we are building the AI layer, the infrastructure and it's really important that there's a single foundation but then with many interfaces out into the world.” ▶ 3:59
Friar argues LLMs have not commoditized as feared; the 'harness' (context, memory, intuition) is re-concentrating value and making the model more powerful per user, especially in enterprise.
contrarian🗣 guest, OpenAI CFO Sarah Friarconviction: highOpenAI
So what: Directly rebuts the 'models commoditize to zero' thesis; memory/context lock-in is the proposed moat, favoring incumbents with the most user and enterprise data.
“a year ago people talked about the commoditization of the LLMs. Um and frankly it's gone the opposite because as you start building an agentic layer... the harness is what brings the context the memory” ▶ 27:07
As the stack vertically integrates (chip makers building models, clouds building chips, labs building silicon), everyone is racing to own the layer closest to the customer, which captures the largest share of ecosystem profit.
framework🗣 guest, OpenAI CFO Sarah Friarconviction: highOpenAINvidiaGoogleMicrosoft
So what: Predicts margin migration toward whoever owns the customer relationship; investors should watch who controls the end-user interface, not just the infrastructure.
“where everyone is trying to make sure they reside is the layer that is closest to the customer where usually you take the the largest portion of the profits of the ecosystem” ▶ 26:40
Friar notes Elon's xAI 'ironically ended up being the one person that had too much compute' and successfully sold the excess off, implying competitors' overbuild created a tradable surplus.
anecdote🗣 guest / host, OpenAI CFO Sarah Friarconviction: lowxAIElon Musk
So what: Suggests compute can be monetized as a secondary commodity and that even apparent overbuilds find buyers in a supply-starved market, reinforcing the scarcity thesis.
“Elon ironically ended up being the one person that had too much compute in a way but good job on like figuring out how to sell that off” ▶ 13:13
Access to compute is named as 'one of the really big competitive advantages at the moment,' framing capital and supply-chain reach (not just model quality) as the primary moat in the current phase.
thesis🗣 guest, OpenAI CFO Sarah Friarconviction: highOpenAI
So what: In a compute-starved market, the labs with the deepest capital and best supplier relationships win; favors well-funded incumbents over capital-light challengers.
“access to compute is one of the really big competitive advantages at the moment” ▶ 5:35
Consumer scale, engagement & the new device 6
ChatGPT has over 900M weekly users and OpenAI's revenue is now roughly 50/50 consumer vs enterprise, rebutting the idea that OpenAI is purely a consumer or purely an enterprise company.
data_point🗣 guest, OpenAI CFO Sarah Friarconviction: highOpenAIChatGPT
So what: Balanced revenue mix de-risks OpenAI's model versus single-segment peers; 900M weekly users is the denominator for the advertising and upsell thesis.
“Over 900 million people use Chat GPT weekly and it's become the noun and the verb.” ▶ 4:16
Codex hit 5M users over a single weekend from almost zero in January, and the fastest internal Codex adoption at OpenAI is in go-to-market (not engineering) teams.
data_point🗣 guest, OpenAI CFO Sarah Friarconviction: highOpenAICodex
So what: Codex's vertical growth and GTM-team adoption show coding agents spreading beyond developers into general knowledge work, expanding the enterprise TAM.
“codeex um just hit 5 million over the weekend... coming from almost zero in January” ▶ 4:49
Engagement scales sharply with tier: free users average ~7 turns/day, the first paid tier ~15 (2x), Plus ~3x a free user, and Pro ~11x a free user, illustrating a steep 'commitment curve.'
data_point🗣 guest, OpenAI CFO Sarah Friarconviction: highOpenAIChatGPT
So what: The 11x Pro engagement multiple quantifies upsell value and supports a free-to-paid funnel; rising engagement per tier underpins both subscription and ad monetization.
“Our free users do about seven turns... Our our real paid tier the plus 20 bucks... about 3x and pro about um 11x over a free user.” ▶ 7:14
OpenAI will unveil a new Jony Ive-designed consumer 'substrate' device by end of 2026 with launch early 2027; Friar (a self-described hand-talker) calls it a paradigm shift that feels 'natural' and 'lovable.'
prediction🗣 guest, OpenAI CFO Sarah Friarconviction: highOpenAIJony Ive
So what: A concrete device timeline (reveal 2026, launch early 2027) is a catalyst that could open a post-smartphone hardware platform and challenge Apple's interface dominance.
“we're changing into a consumer substrate that I cannot tell you what it is but by the end of this year we will unveil it early next year I have seen it I've tried it” ▶ 14:57
Multimodal voice interaction is positioned as the next interface ('talking to your tool'), which Friar says requires much more real-time compute than text and will reshape inference demand.
thesis🗣 guest, OpenAI CFO Sarah Friarconviction: highOpenAICodex
So what: Voice-first, always-on interfaces increase real-time inference intensity, tying device strategy directly to the global inference-capacity buildout.
“Multimodality is here... You're talking to your tool. I talk to codeex every day. And so that is changing rapidly, but that is going to need much more kind of real time compute” ▶ 14:27
OpenAI's free-tier generosity is a deliberate funnel: free users don't get the latest model but get a 'taste of intelligence' to climb the commitment curve, framed via the flip-phone-to-smartphone analogy.
framework🗣 guest, OpenAI CFO Sarah Friarconviction: highOpenAIChatGPT
So what: Loss-leading free access is a customer-acquisition strategy underwritten by the mission and the upsell curve; subsidized free usage is a structural cost to model.
“it's why we offer so much free because we want people to get a taste. Once they get a taste of intelligence, the ability to come up a commitment curve is incredible.” ▶ 7:04
Advertising thesis & monetization optionality 5
Friar pitches ChatGPT as 'if Google and Meta had a baby' for ads: it combines Google-style high-intent signals with Meta-style audience data plus persistent memory/context, potentially a more potent ad platform than either.
thesis🗣 guest, OpenAI CFO Sarah Friarconviction: highOpenAIChatGPTGoogleMeta
So what: Frames a future ad business that could threaten Google/Meta's core franchises by fusing intent + identity + memory; a major long-term competitive overhang for ad incumbents.
“if you know Google and Meta had a baby, it would be chat GPT... imagine putting memory and context next to intent. You should have a very potent ad platform” ▶ 30:09
OpenAI claims at least 11% of the search market by counts, but argues its true share is far higher because a 50-question ChatGPT conversation registers as one 'search' versus a Google page-refresh counting individually.
data_point🗣 guest, OpenAI CFO Sarah Friarconviction: mediumOpenAIChatGPTGoogle
So what: If usage-adjusted search share materially exceeds 11%, Google's search query volume and ad inventory are being eroded faster than headline metrics suggest.
“we know we have at least 11% of the search market. It's a lot more because actually when you do a Google search and the page refreshes that counts as one in chat GBT when you do a whole conversation where you might ask 50 questions that also only counts as one” ▶ 30:16
OpenAI will keep ads from biasing results (best answer by the model, not by sponsorship) and will always offer an ad-free paid tier, constraining how aggressively it can monetize ads.
thesis🗣 guest, OpenAI CFO Sarah Friarconviction: highOpenAIAnthropic
So what: Self-imposed guardrails (non-sponsored results, ad-free tier) cap ad-load upside relative to legacy ad models, a structural difference for ad-revenue forecasts.
“we want to make sure that you know you're always getting the best result based on the model, not by something that was sponsored... we'll always provide... an ad free tier” ▶ 29:48
API tokens generate an order of magnitude more revenue per token than consumer tokens today, yet OpenAI deliberately allocates compute to consumers to build the 'utility like electricity' infrastructure layer.
data_point🗣 guest, OpenAI CFO Sarah Friarconviction: highOpenAI
So what: Reveals OpenAI is sacrificing near-term revenue-per-token to build long-term consumer reach; investors should treat consumer compute allocation as a strategic, not profit-maximizing, choice.
“If I was optimizing only for today, I would give every token to the API... order of magnitude more than to the consumer. However... we're playing our own game.” ▶ 31:15
OpenAI floated developers paying upwards of ~$2,000/month for agentic capability a year ago (met with disbelief), drawing the parallel to skepticism over ChatGPT Pro at $200/month that later proved wrong.
anecdote🗣 guest, OpenAI CFO Sarah Friarconviction: mediumOpenAIChatGPT
So what: Signals OpenAI sees high-ticket agentic subscriptions as a real revenue line; pricing ceilings for AI agents may be far higher than consensus assumes.
“we think they will pay upwards of maybe $2,000 a month for it, which is kind of laughable in hindsight, but nobody believed” ▶ 22:10
Community, talent & macro signals 4
The single Michigan data center will generate ~2,500 union jobs, ~$1B in taxes to Michigan, $45M in education/Codex credits, and OpenAI pledges not to raise local ratepayers' electricity bills.
data_point🗣 guest, OpenAI CFO Sarah Friarconviction: highOpenAIMichiganCodex
So what: Quantifies the local economic and political 'license to operate' costs of data-center buildouts; community trust is being treated as part of the supply chain and a gating risk.
“2500 union jobs... a billion dollars in taxes just for that data center into Michigan... we're not going to raise your electricity bills” ▶ 10:35
Friar (a Stanford trustee) flags a US talent/education shortage as a real supply-chain constraint on AI, worrying whether enough qualified people are coming through the education system.
thesis🗣 guest, OpenAI CFO Sarah Friarconviction: mediumOpenAIStanford
So what: Names human capital as a binding AI constraint alongside power and chips; reinforces the premium on AI talent and the strategic value of education/upskilling tools like Codex.
“access to great talent um do we have enough people coming through our education system I really worry about this right now I'm a trustee at Stanford” ▶ 9:32
Friar treats fluency in AI coding tools as a baseline hiring requirement, saying she likely wouldn't hire a finance person today who can't use a tool like Codex, just as she'd require Excel.
anecdote🗣 guest, OpenAI CFO Sarah Friarconviction: highOpenAICodexMicrosoft Excel
So what: Signals AI-tool fluency becoming a labor-market table-stake, accelerating enterprise adoption of agentic coding/productivity tools across non-engineering roles.
“I pretty much probably wouldn't hire a finance person today that doesn't know how to use a tool like Codeex”
ChatGPT's fastest-growing user geographies are now in Africa and fastest-growing languages are Azerbaijani and Kazakh, indicating rapid adoption from low penetration in emerging markets.
data_point🗣 guest, OpenAI CFO Sarah Friarconviction: mediumOpenAIChatGPTAfrica
So what: Emerging-market and non-English growth is the long-tail expansion driving the global inference footprint and the 'serve the world at large' utility thesis.
“the fastest growing continents now are Africa... fastest growing languages are um Azerbjani and... Kazak” ▶ 4:28
Dan Loeb: The Lost Art of Short Selling, and Why Stock Picking is Back31 min21 insights2026-06-05
▶ Watch on YouTube
Third Point CIO Dan Loeb argues short selling has re-emerged as a critical, underused skill and that today's market rewards security selection in credit and equities over indexing. He says NVIDIA is undervalued on the next 2-3 years of earnings, walks through a structural short thesis on homebuilders, and stresses durable moats, adaptable management, and human relationships as the edge AI can't replicate.
Short selling as a revived edge · 4NVIDIA and mega-cap valuation · 3Homebuilder short thesis · 3Evolution of Third Point's strategy · 4Moats, management quality, and the human edge · 3Distribution and the difficulty of selling winners · 2Government as investor-customer and quantum · 1Activism and public persona · 1
Short selling as a revived edge 4
Dan Loeb argues short selling has resurfaced as a critical, underutilized skill and that the current market rewards highly selective security picking rather than broad exposure.
thesis🗣 Dan Loeb (guest, Third Point CEO/CIO)conviction: highThird Pointshort selling
So what: Signals a regime where dedicated short books and selective long/short managers can generate alpha again; favors active over passive.
“The lost art of shortselling has come back and it's absolutely critical.”
Loeb frames the current environment as a bond and credit pickers' market rather than purely a stock pickers' market, emphasizing selectivity within credit.
thesis🗣 Dan Loeb (guest)conviction: highcreditbondsfixed income
So what: Points investors toward dispersion trades and security selection in high-yield and structured credit, not just equities.
“People talk about stock pickers market. This is a bond and credit pickers market.”
Loeb warns against purely valuation-based shorting, citing repeated cases of expensive stocks being short-squeezed by retail communities like Reddit and by speculative space companies with no fundamental rationale.
framework🗣 Dan Loeb (guest)conviction: highRedditspace companiesshort selling
So what: Shorting requires a catalyst or structural thesis, not just a high multiple; valuation-only shorts carry squeeze risk.
“I've seen too many people get run over by shorts that have dumb valuations, but they get captured on, you know, Reddit or one of these other things”
The 1990s offered abundant short opportunities because markets were unsupervised and full of fraudulent companies, exemplified by ACT Trade, a factoring company trading at a large multiple of book that repackaged factored securities as proprietary 'TADS' technology.
anecdote🗣 Dan Loeb (guest)conviction: highACT Trade
So what: Illustrates the fraud-detection mindset and forensic skepticism that underpins effective short selling.
NVIDIA and mega-cap valuation 3
Loeb states NVIDIA is undervalued on its earnings over the next two to three years given its dominant position, and predicts the market will look back on current bearishness as a foolish framing.
prediction🗣 Dan Loeb (guest)conviction: highNVIDIA
So what: Direct bullish call on NVDA from a major fund CIO, anchored to forward earnings rather than current multiple.
“I think we'll look back at some point in time and say that was a foolish way to think about Nvidia given its dominant position and its valuation relative to”
Loeb argues long/short pods are structurally forced to be short something, which makes dominant mega-caps like NVIDIA, and previously Google and Amazon, into 'safe shorts' that languish before breaking out higher.
framework🗣 Dan Loeb (guest)conviction: highNVIDIAGoogleAmazonmulti-manager pods
So what: Crowded short positioning in dominant names can suppress valuation temporarily and set up sharp upside breakouts.
“the long short pods are structured such that they have to be short something. So Nvidia feels like a safe short by the way Google was a safe short um Amazon was a safe short.”
Loeb contends investors are mis-anchoring on round-number market caps for NVIDIA the same way they once capped Facebook's upside at $100B, when today multi-trillion-dollar companies have made those ceilings obsolete.
contrarian🗣 Dan Loeb (guest)conviction: mediumNVIDIAFacebookMeta
So what: Cautions against using historical market-cap ceilings as a short thesis; the addressable scale of winners has structurally expanded.
Homebuilder short thesis 3
Third Point has been short homebuilders, arguing the industry is structurally impaired because builders pretend to be asset-light like NVR but actually hold massive committed capital in land pools dressed up as 'options.'
thesis🗣 Dan Loeb (guest)conviction: highhomebuildersNVRhousing
So what: The asset-light narrative masks real balance-sheet land commitments, making builders more cyclically exposed than they appear.
“they were all pretending to be NVR which is say all pretending to be asset light but they had massive commitments to these land pools which in in things that they said were options but they were really very committed in the capital”
Loeb argues homebuilding is the last industry still working through a post-COVID inventory and pricing hangover, with home prices and build costs at unsustainable levels that buyers can no longer afford in the current financing environment.
causal🗣 Dan Loeb (guest)conviction: highhomebuildershousingmortgage spreadsinflation
So what: Margin squeeze from elevated land/build costs plus affordability ceiling supports continued downside in homebuilder equities.
Loeb identifies mortgage spreads, not just the level of rates, as a distinct driver depressing housing prices in the homebuilder thesis.
data_point🗣 Dan Loeb (guest)conviction: mediummortgage spreadshousinginterest rates
So what: Watch mortgage-spread compression as a potential reversal signal for the homebuilder short.
Evolution of Third Point's strategy 4
Third Point's original edge was event-driven investing in complex transactions (spin-offs, risk arb, bankruptcies, privatizations, demutualizations) where dislocation, opacity, and management incentives to sandbag numbers created alpha for co-investors riding the re-rating.
framework🗣 Dan Loeb (guest)conviction: highThird Pointevent-driven investingrisk arbitragespin-offs
So what: Maps the classic event-driven alpha source: buy depressed projections set during excess supply, then capture transparency-driven re-rating.
Loeb says the rise of technology forced a shift from cheap-securities-with-catalysts toward prioritizing business quality, moats, and durability, because companies once assumed to have impregnable moats (IBM, AOL, Yahoo) proved otherwise.
framework🗣 Dan Loeb (guest)conviction: highIBMAOLYahoomoats
So what: Reinforces durability/moat analysis over static cheapness; historical moats can erode, so management adaptability matters.
Loeb argues that any pool of capital that was previously uncorrelated is now effectively correlated through the tech 'through line,' so investors can no longer be technologically or economically illiterate as they could before the GFC.
thesis🗣 Dan Loeb (guest)conviction: hightechnologyAIBlue OwlGFC
So what: Diversification benefits have eroded; tech literacy is now mandatory across all asset classes including private credit.
“any pool of capital that used to not be correlated is effectively correlated”
Third Point has grown from minimal startup capital to nearly $30B AUM and now operates as a multi-strategy platform spanning the main hedge fund (equity/credit long-short), a CLO business, private credit (direct lending, sponsor financing, workouts/credit solutions), and a wholly-then-half-owned insurance company.
data_point🗣 Dan Loeb (guest)conviction: highThird PointCLOprivate creditinsurance
So what: Shows the build-out of a permanent-capital and credit platform; insurance float captures the investment-grade slice and frees surplus capital.
Moats, management quality, and the human edge 3
Loeb prioritizes adaptable management teams with a proven ability to stay ahead over fixed product or technology advantages, because no product can be assumed dominant forever.
framework🗣 Dan Loeb (guest)conviction: highmanagement qualitymoats
So what: Underwrite the operator's adaptability, not just the current moat; this is the durable filter when technology disrupts incumbents.
Loeb says assessing management quality remains entirely subjective and qualitative pattern recognition built over 30 years, with no quantifiable rubric, and is something AI agents will never replicate by reading a person.
thesis🗣 Dan Loeb (guest)conviction: highmanagement qualityAI agents
So what: The human relationship and judgment layer is the defensible edge as AI commoditizes data analysis.
≈ “There is a thing that I think the agents of the AI will never really be able to look in your eye and assess all the things that” paraphrase
Loeb embraces Chamath's 'time-bounded value of companies' concept as essential, asking which companies will durably exist 7, 10, or 20 years out, while conceding such long-horizon moat predictions are harder now.
framework🗣 Dan Loeb (guest)conviction: mediumChamath Palihapitiyamoats
So what: Frame holding periods around defensible time horizons rather than assuming perpetual durability.
Distribution and the difficulty of selling winners 2
Loeb cites costly early exits as proof that knowing when to sell winners is among the hardest problems: Third Point sold all its private Palantir stock in the $20s, missing a ~10x post-IPO move, and sold Enphase below a dollar after an IPO tax hit when staying would have meant roughly $4 billion.
anecdote🗣 Dan Loeb (guest)conviction: highPalantirEnphaseUpstartThird Point
So what: Premature distribution of high-conviction compounders is a major source of forgone returns; the harder discipline is holding winners.
“We were um we were private investors in Palunteer and I think we sold all our stock in the 20s. Huge mistake.”
Loeb says going on company boards (e.g., Upstart) restricts an investor's ability to be liquid, leading Third Point to avoid board seats.
framework🗣 Dan Loeb (guest)conviction: highUpstartboard seatsThird Point
So what: Board seats trade governance influence for liquidity constraints; weigh information access against trading flexibility.
“that was one I think we learned not to go on boards anymore because it restricts your ability to be liquid”
Government as investor-customer and quantum 1
Loeb highlights Third Point portfolio company Atom Computing as a model public-private partnership where the U.S. government is both an investor and a tough-bargaining customer in quantum/cryptography, positioning taxpayers to profit while validating the business.
anecdote🗣 Dan Loeb (guest)conviction: mediumAtom Computingquantum computingcryptographyUS government
So what: Government equity-plus-procurement deals can de-risk and add value to frontier-tech portfolio companies; watch quantum names with federal backing.
Activism and public persona 1
Loeb characterizes Third Point's activism as having shifted from early-stage 'shame and humor' tactics toward a constructive 'dare to be great' message, while maintaining that activism without proxy contests loses its teeth.
framework🗣 Dan Loeb (guest)conviction: highThird PointNestleshareholder activism
So what: Signals continued willingness to escalate to proxy fights (e.g., the Nestle campaign), a credible-threat lever activists need.
“Activism without proxy contest is like Catholicism without hell.”
Bill Ackman: Here's What the Market is MISSING30 min27 insights2026-06-03
▶ Watch on YouTube
Bill Ackman argues the market is mispricing the durable mega-cap incumbents (Microsoft, Meta, Amazon) as 'old-fashioned' while chasing chips, semis and AI startups, leaving high-quality companies historically cheap on a DCF basis. He details Pershing Square's pivot to permanent capital and a Berkshire-style insurance flywheel at Howard Hughes, his venture-style underwriting of SpaceX/OpenAI/Anthropic, and why founder-led, low-cost-of-capital companies win in the AI disruption era.
Mega-cap incumbents left behind by the AI trade · 3AI disruption risk as the core underwriting problem · 4Underwriting AI/space mega-startups like venture · 5Pershing Square's pivot to permanent capital and the Berkshire model · 5Valuation mechanics, reflexivity and cost of capital · 3Founder control as a durable edge · 2Three ways to invest with Pershing Square · 3Activism evolution and the going-direct era · 2
Mega-cap incumbents left behind by the AI trade 3
Ackman says high-quality mega-caps Microsoft, Meta and Amazon are undervalued because capital is chasing the 'new new thing' (chips, semiconductors, energy, AI startups), leaving durable incumbents behind as 'old-fashioned'.
contrarian🗣 Bill Ackmanconviction: highMicrosoftMetaAmazonsemiconductorsOpenAI
So what: Implies a long in mega-cap incumbents over the crowded semis/AI-infrastructure trade; the perceived AI 'threat' to these names is overdiscounted.
“people always bring their eye to the new new thing... what tends to happen is really high quality things get left behind” ▶ 6:05
Ackman draws an explicit analogy to 2000, when investors dumped Berkshire Hathaway to its lowest-ever valuation as 'old stuff' while chasing internet stocks, arguing a similar mispricing of incumbents is happening now.
framework🗣 Bill Ackmanconviction: mediumBerkshire Hathawaydot-com bubbleAmazonMetaMicrosoft
So what: Historical template suggesting incumbents that survive the hype cycle re-rate sharply; watch for capitulation in quality names as a buy signal.
“Bergkshire Hathway traded at the lowest valuation I think it ever traded out of it history is people said, "Okay, that's all old stuff."” ▶ 6:34
Ackman states stocks of really high-quality companies recently got 'crazy cheap' on a fundamentals/DCF basis (present value of cash generated over the asset's life), which prompted his public bullish call.
thesis🗣 Bill Ackmanconviction: highS&P 500
So what: A named catalyst-free valuation call; the cheapness is in quality compounders rather than speculative names.
“Stocks just got crazy cheap. Just incredibly cheap of really high quality companies” ▶ 10:25
AI disruption risk as the core underwriting problem 4
Ackman says the single hardest and most important job for a long-term concentrated investor is assessing disruption risk, and that risk has gone up dramatically because startups now have near-unlimited access to compute, capital and talent.
framework🗣 Bill Ackmanconviction: highAIStanford
So what: Raises the bar on what counts as a 'durable' moat; reframes quality investing around non-disruptibility, not just current margins.
“the probability of your being disrupted has gone up enormously... that's really where we spend most of our time” ▶ 5:41
Ackman is more worried about Salesforce than about platform incumbents in the 'SaaS apocalypse', arguing niche software vendors extracting monopolistic pricing (e.g. ~$30k/year) are at high disruption risk while low-per-seat platforms like Microsoft (~$50/seat) are far safer.
contrarian🗣 Bill Ackmanconviction: mediumSalesforceMicrosoftSaaS
So what: A pairs-style framework: short/avoid high-ARPU niche SaaS, favor low-priced embedded platforms; price-per-seat is a proxy for AI-disruption vulnerability.
“there have been sort of monopolistic type profit taking off of customers when someone had a kind of a niche software product they're charging you know 30,000 a year... those companies are really at risk” ▶ 7:17
Ackman says enterprise AI adoption is the #1 board-level concern for essentially every CEO in America, simultaneously as an opportunity and a threat, with boards now opening meetings on AI strategy.
data_point🗣 Bill Ackmanconviction: highAIFortune 500
So what: Confirms durable demand for AI enablement/consulting and pressure on laggard incumbents; AI strategy is now a governance, not just an IT, issue.
“your board is going to be you know asking you first question every meeting about... the AI threat... the AI opportunity” ▶ 14:37
Despite the hype, Ackman says he has seen little actual enterprise AI success; even at tiny Pershing Square the only live use case is legal/compliance back-office work, and big companies remain 'super super early' at deploying AI effectively.
data_point🗣 Bill Ackmanconviction: highPershing SquareMcKinsey
So what: Tempers near-term AI-ROI expectations; gap between AI promise and realized ROI supports the 'forward-deployed engineer' / implementation-services thesis over premature productivity bets.
“So I haven't seen much success... I think we're still super super early in terms of big companies using AI effectively.” ▶ 15:31
Underwriting AI/space mega-startups like venture 5
Ackman underwrites pre-IPO giants like SpaceX as venture investments using a 'people, opportunity, context, deal' framework, rating SpaceX one-of-one on people and opportunity, leaving valuation ('deal') as the only hard question.
framework🗣 Bill Ackmanconviction: highSpaceXStarlinkBlue Origin
So what: Provides a structured way to value 100x-revenue private names; signals he discounts revenue-multiple objections when founder/opportunity quality is exceptional.
“I think you underwrite a SpaceX the way you underwrite a venture capital investment.” ▶ 11:19
Ackman sees SpaceX as a near-monopoly in low-cost space launch whose value rises as 'time' becomes more valuable in the AI era, forcing even Amazon to become a bigger Starlink/launch customer because Blue Origin is far behind.
thesis🗣 Bill Ackmanconviction: highSpaceXStarlinkAmazonBlue Origin
So what: Bullish structural read on SpaceX/Starlink with Amazon as a forced customer; Blue Origin's lag is a competitive moat, not a threat.
“SpaceX is, you know, near monopoly in terms of lowcost space launch... even Amazon is going to have to become an even bigger customer” ▶ 12:15
Ackman discloses he is personally invested in SpaceX (via an SPV, prompted by Ron Baron) and in xAI, while admitting he has not yet done the valuation math on the rumored ~$1.75 trillion SpaceX IPO.
anecdote🗣 Bill Ackmanconviction: mediumSpaceXxAIRon BaronElon Musk
So what: Reveals personal positioning and a candid valuation caveat at the ~$1.75T mark; flags SPV exposure to Musk's private complex.
“I actually invested in X. I invested in XAI. I'm in an SPV. Ron Baron said Bill you got to invest in SpaceX. So... I'm I'm in.”
Ackman classifies OpenAI, Anthropic and Palantir-like names as later-stage (Series D/E) venture investments that have proven revenue generation, but flags OpenAI's model as 'hard' because capital commitments massively exceed revenues and it hasn't explained how it funds them.
thesis🗣 Bill Ackmanconviction: mediumOpenAIAnthropicPalantirSpaceX
So what: Highlights OpenAI's commitment-vs-revenue gap as the key risk and a communications failure relative to peers; later-stage AI names still carry venture-grade uncertainty.
“You got a company that's spending making capital commitments. They're massively in excess of... revenues and how do you do that... degree of difficulty, I would say, is hard.” ▶ 13:44
Ackman says OpenAI CFO Sarah Friar's framework for committing capital impressed him and made him 'a lot more bullish' on OpenAI, and that OpenAI should be broadcasting that capital-allocation message externally.
anecdote🗣 Bill Ackmanconviction: mediumOpenAISarah FriarSam Altman
So what: Soft positive signal on OpenAI sentiment from a marquee investor; suggests narrative/communication is a swing factor in valuing the AI leader.
“made me a lot more bullish on Open AI” ▶ 0:34
Pershing Square's pivot to permanent capital and the Berkshire model 5
Ackman frames Pershing Square as 'the rebirth of the closed-end investment company universe', shifting from activist enter/exit trades toward permanent long-term holdings prioritizing durable, non-disruptible business quality.
thesis🗣 Bill Ackmanconviction: highPershing SquarePSUS
So what: Signals a structural strategy shift toward permanent capital and quality compounders; relevant to how his public vehicles should be valued.
“the biggest change over time is an appreciation for the importance of I call business quality long-term durable protected non-disruptible growth” ▶ 1:29
Ackman lays out a Berkshire-style insurance flywheel at Howard Hughes: redirect the company's cash from real estate into insurance, invest 100% of float in short-term treasuries and 100% of equity surplus in common stocks, aiming to compound a ~$4B market cap toward $1 trillion over ~50 years.
thesis🗣 Bill Ackmanconviction: highHoward Hughes CorporationBerkshire HathawayWarren Buffettinsurance
So what: The core long thesis on Howard Hughes as 'Berkshire 2.0'; the insurance transformation is the value-creation engine, not the real estate.
“instead of reinvesting all the cash the business generates into real estate, we're going to reinvest all the cash into insurance” ▶ 21:43
Ackman argues Howard Hughes is mispriced because the market assigns real estate/land development a high cost of capital capping returns; Pershing's plan is to repurpose the assets into a higher-returning business so the stock can exceed its cost of capital.
causal🗣 Bill Ackmanconviction: highHoward Hughes CorporationSummerlin
So what: Names the re-rating mechanism: lowering perceived cost of capital via business-mix transformation; a company must earn above its cost of capital for the stock to rise.
“a company has to earn a return in excess of its cost of capital in order for a stock to go up” ▶ 24:08
Ackman positions Howard Hughes as a discount-to-liquidation-value bet directly analogous to Buffett buying Berkshire's textile mill at $63/share, owning a 26,000-acre 'small city' (Summerlin) benefiting from the tech migration from California to Las Vegas.
anecdote🗣 Bill Ackmanconviction: highHoward Hughes CorporationSummerlinIrvine CompanyBerkshire HathawayLas Vegas
So what: Frames Howard Hughes as deep-value land plus a Buffett-style transformation; Summerlin's tech-migration tailwind underpins the asset value.
“you can buy it at, you know, whatever 60 cents on the dollar” ▶ 22:06
Ackman explains why no one has replicated Buffett's insurance machine: insurers can't recruit top investment talent (which goes to hedge funds, Fidelity, Wellington), so Buffett's edge was owning half the company and being a great investor himself.
framework🗣 Bill Ackmanconviction: highWarren BuffettBerkshire HathawayFidelityWellingtoninsurance
So what: Identifies the structural moat Pershing intends to exploit at Howard Hughes: pairing insurance float with elite in-house investment talent.
“if you're really good at investing, you go work for a hedge fund... but you don't go work for an insurance company” ▶ 20:22
Valuation mechanics, reflexivity and cost of capital 3
Ackman describes valuation as a 'tether/rubber band' that mean-reverts in both directions: stretched-high valuations snap back down, but when stocks get too cheap the band pulls valuations up, and publicly calling out extremes can trigger a psychological reset.
framework🗣 Bill Ackmanconviction: highvaluation
So what: A reusable mean-reversion mental model; explains his table-pounding public calls as deliberate catalysts at valuation extremes.
“valuation is like a tether on the market... it's like this rubber band that's stretching and inevitably it bounces back. But it works the other way as well.” ▶ 9:58
Ackman articulates a reflexivity thesis: a higher stock price itself makes a company more valuable by lowering its cost of capital and enabling stock issuance, capital raising and acquisitions, citing Elon Musk lowering Tesla/SpaceX cost of capital via an 'army of believers'.
framework🗣 Bill Ackmanconviction: highTeslaSpaceXElon Musk
So what: Cost of capital as a competitive weapon; a rumored ~$1.75T SpaceX IPO would be the lowest-cost-of-capital equity raise ever, a structural advantage rivals can't match.
“the increase in value of the company increases the value of the company... Because it lowers the cost of capital, it gives you more flexibility” ▶ 25:55
Ackman cites GameStop and Ryan Cohen as evidence markets have structurally changed: a stock can now trade well above intrinsic value purely on a personality's ability to mobilize armies of retail followers ('vibes').
causal🗣 Bill Ackmanconviction: mediumGameStopRyan CohenWall Street Bets
So what: Persistent retail-driven valuation dislocations are a real regime feature; momentum/personality can sustain prices detached from fundamentals.
“a stock can trade at a valuation well above its value simply on the personality and the ability to... gather up... armies of followers” ▶ 25:26
Founder control as a durable edge 2
Ackman argues founder-led companies structurally outperform in disruptive environments because founders have the authority, economic stake and reputational commitment to make radical bets, unlike the average S&P 500 CEO with a ~3.5-year tenure incentivized to avoid mistakes.
thesis🗣 Bill Ackmanconviction: highS&P 500Mark ZuckerbergMeta
So what: Supports a founder-led tilt, especially for SaaS names facing AI disruption; cites Zuckerberg's Instagram/WhatsApp deals as proof founders make hard calls non-founders won't.
“If you're not founder led, you have an incentive to not make a mistake and get fired. If you're founderled... Your job is to make sure the company [survives]” ▶ 16:44
Ackman notes Pershing Square is typically the largest or second-largest non-index-fund shareholder when it joins a board, giving it disproportionate boardroom voice—an advantage founders inherently possess.
framework🗣 Bill Ackmanconviction: highPershing Square
So what: Explains the activist value-add and why concentrated ownership/founder control correlates with the ability to make long-horizon decisions.
“when we join a board of a company, we're often the largest or... the largest non index fund type shareholder” ▶ 17:22
Three ways to invest with Pershing Square 3
Ackman pitches three distinct ways to invest with him: the Pershing Square management company (a capex-free royalty on AUM compounding across three permanent-capital vehicles that pays out nearly all profits), PSUS (a closed-end portfolio of best ideas), and Howard Hughes (the Berkshire 2.0 bet).
thesis🗣 Bill Ackmanconviction: highPershing SquarePSUSHoward Hughes Corporation
So what: Lays out the explicit menu of public vehicles and their differing risk/return profiles for retail and institutional allocators.
“it's the entity that receives fees on these three permanent capital vehicles we manage. So, it's a royalty on the compounding... there's no capex in the business” ▶ 26:58
Ackman says PSUS trades at an 18% discount to cash, framing it as a discounted way to own his best ideas.
data_point🗣 Bill Ackmanconviction: highPSUSPershing Square
So what: A concrete, named discount investors can act on; closed-end discount is the entry edge.
“invest in something called PSUS and you own a portfolio of our best ideas and it's trading at an 18% discount to cash” ▶ 28:10
Ackman states a dollar in Pershing Square 22 years ago compounded to ~27-28x net of all fees (or mid-40s gross), and projects the management company could grow AUM ~35x in 22 years—from ~$25 billion toward ~$1 trillion—without hiring anyone or adding overhead.
data_point🗣 Bill Ackmanconviction: highPershing Square
So what: Quantifies the track record and the operating-leverage scaling thesis behind owning the management company itself.
“we'll have 35 times the assets under management uh in 22 years. So we'll go from 25 billion of assets to something approaching a trillion. We don't have to hire another person” ▶ 27:52
Activism evolution and the going-direct era 2
Ackman says he is 'as activist as I've ever been' but the activism has shifted from corporate-context fights to Twitter; reputation now means CEOs open the door (sometimes publicly welcoming Pershing) rather than ignoring his calls as in the early Wendy's/Tim Hortons days.
thesis🗣 Bill Ackmanconviction: highPershing SquareTwitter/XWendy'sTim Hortons
So what: The activist playbook has moved to public/social pressure with lower friction; reputation reduces the cost of effecting change.
“I would say I'm as activist as I've ever been... but more of it's on uh Twitter than... in the corporate context” ▶ 1:56
Ackman highlights the power of 'going direct'—reaching 2.2 million followers with one button to communicate his thesis—arguing it makes it easier for investors to underwrite the bet, while conceding Pershing has not exploited the reflexive cost-of-capital benefit the way Musk has.
anecdote🗣 Bill Ackmanconviction: mediumTwitter/XElon Musk
So what: Direct communication is now a capital-markets tool; investors can front-run Ackman's narrative-driven re-ratings by following his public posts.
“it's a very powerful thing to be able to share your view and push a button and reach 2.2 million people” ▶ 29:29

1000x Podcast 3 episode(s) · 138 insights

Is This The End Of Crypto?65 min46 insights2026-05-29
▶ Watch on YouTube
Two traders argue crypto-as-asset-class is dead except for cash-flow-generating, 'right-sized-decentralization' tokens (Hyperliquid, Solana), flag Michael Sailor/MSTR as a forced-seller blow-up risk and Bitcoin's loss of leadership, and pivot to a late-innings AI/memory super-cycle they are trading via cash plus high-beta names while watching for top signals.
Michael Sailor / MSTR as the 'Eggman' single-buyer blow-up risk · 4Bitcoin: trimmed long, lost leadership, levels to re-enter · 5Crypto is dead, long live cash-flow crypto (K-shaped) · 4Token picks: Hyperliquid, Solana vs Ethereum, privacy coins, equities · 6Right-sized decentralization as a blockchain valuation framework · 3Robinhood (HOOD): de-correlating from crypto, AI agents, Trump accounts · 4AI/memory super-cycle: data, China supply, and demand cracks · 6Late-innings positioning: cash, high-beta, and narrative laggards · 5Structural alpha: research-community incentive gap and IPO top signals · 5Iran deal, the Strait of Hormuz, and the oil-inflation transmission · 4
Michael Sailor / MSTR as the 'Eggman' single-buyer blow-up risk 4
Michael Sailor / MicroStrategy is now effectively the only marginal buyer of Bitcoin, making him a cornered forced-seller ('the Eggman') with no off-ramp; the speaker expects him to blow up.
contrarian🗣 Avi (host)conviction: highMichael SailorMicroStrategyMSTRBitcoin
So what: A Sailor unwind would force MSTR/BTC selling into thin liquidity; treat MSTR as a leveraged short-vol position and Bitcoin's overhang.
≈ “He has 265,000 contracts of these eggs in a market that trades 100,000 a day... Who am I going to sell him to? You're the egg guy.” paraphrase ▶ 6:55
Sailor reportedly raised ~$2 billion to cover interest expense for a couple of years but instead used it to buy back some of his debt, signaling balance-sheet stress rather than fresh Bitcoin accumulation.
data_point🗣 Jonah (host)conviction: mediumMichael SailorMicroStrategy
So what: Cap-raise being diverted to debt buybacks/financing obligations suggests the BTC-accumulation flywheel is reversing; watch MSTR convertible maturities.
≈ “He raised like $2 billion to cover his interest expense for a couple of years and then instead he used it to buy back some of his debt.” paraphrase
A producer audience poll on the show found 67% of listeners believe Sailor will blow up, an emerging crowd-consensus 'top signal' around MSTR.
data_point🗣 Jonah (host)conviction: lowMichael SailorMicroStrategy
So what: Consensus that a forced unwind is coming can itself accelerate the unwind; also a contrarian flag if everyone is already positioned for it.
“Producer Brad did a poll asking the audience if they think Sailor will blow up. 67% said yes.” ▶ 47:41
Overengineered leverage on assets at all-time highs (Lehman's COs/subprime/Archstone) historically ends in tears, and Sailor is running the same playbook of borrowing against an asset on the highs.
framework🗣 Jonah (host)conviction: highLehman BrothersMichael SailorMicroStrategy
So what: Use financial-history pattern-matching: levered bets dependent on continued all-time-high rallies are the classic blow-up setup.
“Financial overengineering results in tears... reckless risk behavior results in tears.” ▶ 9:55
Bitcoin: trimmed long, lost leadership, levels to re-enter 5
Jonah sold roughly a quarter of his Bitcoin (>10% ago, via GBTC for tax efficiency) because BTC was underperforming, the rate trajectory shifted from cuts toward hikes, and activity collapsed to where Sailor is the only active trader.
anecdote🗣 Jonah (host)conviction: highBitcoinGBTCMichael Sailorinterest rates
So what: A vocal BTC bull de-risking on relative weakness and shifting rate odds is a positioning tell; underperformance vs macro peers is the trigger to trim.
“It was underperforming in a way that freaked me out... the only active trader to speak of is Michael Sailor.” ▶ 3:26
Bitcoin has serially lost every correlation anchor (tech, commodities, gold, M2) and now correlates with nothing but itself, leaving 'no interest' / no marginal bid for now.
causal🗣 Avi (host)conviction: mediumBitcoingoldNASDAQM2
So what: With no macro driver, BTC trades on its own thin flows; expect range-bound chop until a new narrative or flow re-emerges.
“Now it has correlation with absolutely nothing but itself.” ▶ 11:51
Best risk/reward to re-enter Bitcoin is around $50–60K (called 50–55K); ~$85K has been a hard ceiling with $100K the next magnet, so the host is uninterested in BTC between those levels.
prediction🗣 Avi (host)conviction: mediumBitcoin
So what: Specific actionable levels: accumulate near $50–55K targeting a double; avoid chasing in the $85K–$100K congestion zone.
“The best riskreward for BTC is probably buying at around 50 to 60K per coin... I'm buying Bitcoin 50 probably.” ▶ 12:14
Jonah maintains a $1,000,000 long-term Bitcoin price target while actively trading around the position, arguing trading around a core long can add 50–100% more on the trade versus set-and-forget.
thesis🗣 Jonah (host)conviction: highBitcoin
So what: Reconciles long-term mega-bull view with near-term de-risking; signals tactical selling without thesis abandonment.
≈ “I maintain my million-doll price target for Bitcoin... trading around a position is often a way to make 50 to 100% more money on the trade.” paraphrase
Bitcoin has stopped acting like 'uncooped' private money because Wall Street banks have absorbed it into the financial system, vacating the cypherpunk store-of-value mantle.
causal🗣 Avi (host)conviction: mediumBitcoinWall Street banks
So what: Institutionalization changes BTC's behavior and opens a gap for privacy-coin substitutes to capture the censorship-resistant-money trade.
“Bitcoin has been taken by the Wall Street banks and really integrated into the financial system.” ▶ 19:08
Crypto is dead, long live cash-flow crypto (K-shaped) 4
Crypto as an asset class is over; only tokens with genuine product-market-fit and revenue survive, while most tokens go to zero ('dog-shit will go to zero') in a K-shaped recovery.
thesis🗣 Avi (host)conviction: highcryptoHyperliquidSolana
So what: Screen tokens like equities on revenue vs market cap; abandon non-revenue 'dream' tokens entirely.
“Things that make money will rally. Dog [__] will go to zero. Most of crypto falls into the latter category.” ▶ 15:15
Actionable edge: screen the top 300–500 crypto assets for revenue relative to market cap and reallocate to the cheapest cash-flow producers, because few large allocators are doing this work.
framework🗣 Avi (host)conviction: highcrypto
So what: A repeatable, under-crowded screen; revenue/market-cap ranking is the alpha source while attention is scarce.
≈ “Go through the top 300 500 assets. What's actually making money and where should I be allocating my capital.” paraphrase ▶ 28:02
Recommended portfolio cap is no more than ~20% of net worth in crypto, and that sleeve should be concentrated only in revenue-generating tokens.
framework🗣 Avi (host)conviction: highcrypto
So what: Position-sizing guidance: cap crypto exposure and concentrate it in cash-flow assets rather than the long tail.
≈ “You probably don't want more than 20% of your net worth in crypto anyway at this point.” paraphrase
A repeatable momentum tactic: the moment a crypto product shows real adoption/uptake, buy the token early — exemplified by a Hyperliquid 'two-bagger' in ~a month and a half.
framework🗣 Jonah (host)conviction: mediumHyperliquid
So what: Adoption-triggered token buying is the playbook; act on early product traction before broader capital arrives.
“The second something seems to be getting uptake, buy the token... we got a twoagger on Hyperlquid in like a month and a half, just keep doing that.” ▶ 26:53
Token picks: Hyperliquid, Solana vs Ethereum, privacy coins, equities 6
Hyperliquid (HYPE) was the best-performing crypto asset, ripping from ~$22 to ~$66 (a 3x) and hitting $67; with the ICE CEO taking it seriously, $150 HYPE by year-end is plausible though upside may be capped given its size.
prediction🗣 Avi (host)conviction: highHyperliquidHYPEICEPerp
So what: Specific target ($150 by YE) and catalyst (ICE/exchange attention); still a sizable token but late relative to the low-$30s entry the hosts called.
≈ “You probably can see 150 hype by the end of the year... I bought some... it was very very obvious that there was a market inefficiency.” paraphrase ▶ 14:43
Solana is favored over Ethereum because it sits at the 'right' decentralization/performance trade-off, has proven tech and network effects, and can centralize via big node operators, whereas ETH is too decentralized with 'no way forward.'
thesis🗣 Avi (host)conviction: highSolanaEthereumAnatoly
So what: Rotate L1 exposure toward Solana/performant chains; treat ETH's radical decentralization as a structural disadvantage, not a moat.
“A Salana for example might be... whereas Ethereum I think unfortunately is just too far on the decentralized side.” ▶ 21:42
Bankless reportedly finally sold out of their Ethereum and Vitalik has 'clocked out,' which the hosts read as bearish disillusionment signals for ETH.
anecdote🗣 Jonah (host)conviction: mediumEthereumVitalik ButerinBankless
So what: Capitulation by ETH's most loyal promoters and founder disengagement is a sentiment marker worth tracking for ETH.
“You saw our favorite people over at Bankless finally sell out of their Ethereum.” ▶ 20:15
Zcash is rising because it inherits Bitcoin's vacated cypherpunk private-money thesis; a specific trade is to rotate some Zcash gains into the lagging Monero, framed as 'hyped asset' (ZEC) vs 'used asset' (XMR).
thesis🗣 Avi (host)conviction: mediumZcashZECMoneroXMRBitcoin
So what: Pairs-trade idea: rotate ZEC outperformance into laggard XMR if bullish privacy-money; XMR has real usage underpinning it.
≈ “If you've been heavy in Zcash, you probably can rotate a little bit of those gains into Monero. Monero's really lagged behind.” paraphrase
Best crypto-adjacent products are increasingly equities (Coinbase, Robinhood) and tokens tied to revenue; speculative names cited include fun.xyz, Plasma, Sky (stablecoin), and perp tokens, while DAO/governance plays (e.g. buying the Constitution) are 'over.'
thesis🗣 Avi (host)conviction: mediumCoinbaseRobinhoodfun.xyzPlasmaSkyKen Griffin
So what: Express the crypto thesis partly through public equities and revenue tokens; avoid speculative governance/DAO structures.
“Crypto is dead. Long live crypto.” ▶ 0:24
VVV performed well after the hosts flagged it (admittedly late, crediting Rollup for the early call), reinforcing the early-adoption token-buying playbook.
anecdote🗣 Avi (host)conviction: lowVVVRollup
So what: Confirms the adoption-momentum tactic; follow specialized early-coverage sources to front-run attention.
≈ “VVV did really well from when we brought it up.” paraphrase
Right-sized decentralization as a blockchain valuation framework 3
Blockchain demand is a barbell: radically-decentralized Bitcoin as alternative reserve money at one end, and highly-performant 'shared ledger with mutual transparency' chains (Solana, Hyperliquid, Arbitrum, Base) at the other — with Ethereum stuck in the unwanted 'Death Valley' middle.
framework🗣 Jonah (host)conviction: highBitcoinSolanaHyperliquidArbitrumBaseEthereum
So what: Use the barbell to allocate: own BTC for the decentralization extreme and performant chains for utility; underweight the middle.
“Crypto is now a barbell radically decentralized stuff... and then everything else needs to be pretty freaking performant.” ▶ 26:28
What capitalism actually wants from blockchain is a transparent shared ledger that can't be unilaterally turned off or rugged — not radical decentralization — so efficiency-preserving 'enough' decentralization wins.
thesis🗣 Jonah (host)conviction: highblockchainSolanaHyperliquid
So what: Value chains on the utility/transparency they deliver to institutions, not on decentralization purity.
≈ “What capitalism wants out of blockchain isn't radical decentralization. They want a shared ledger where there's mutual transparency.” paraphrase ▶ 24:34
L2s like Arbitrum and Base sit at the same decentralization/performance trade-off as Solana/Hyperliquid; their value is in what's built on them, not the base layer — evidenced by arbitrary interventions during the Kelp DAO / LayerZero-type hack.
framework🗣 Jonah (host)conviction: mediumArbitrumBaseSolanaHyperliquidLayerZeroKelp DAO
So what: Treat the application/usage layer as where value accrues; base-layer decentralization claims are porous in practice.
“What he built doesn't matter. It's what's being built on top of it that matters.” ▶ 25:52
Robinhood (HOOD): de-correlating from crypto, AI agents, Trump accounts 4
Robinhood (HOOD) is bullish because it's breaking its historically high beta to Bitcoin: equities-trading revenue is rising while crypto revenue falls, and the market is re-rating its non-crypto business lines.
thesis🗣 Avi (host)conviction: highRobinhoodHOODBitcoin
So what: An asset breaking away from a market that suppressed it is a classic inefficiency to buy; HOOD de-coupling from BTC is the catalyst.
“It had a massive beta to Bitcoin and finally it's broken away.” ▶ 28:51
Robinhood is enabling AI agents (e.g. hooking Claude to a brokerage account) to natively analyze and execute trades, which the hosts call the next stage of evolution.
data_point🗣 Avi (host)conviction: mediumRobinhoodHOODClaudeAI agents
So what: Native agentic trading is a structural product differentiator for HOOD and a new competitive axis among brokerages.
≈ “You can hook up Claude to your brokerage account and have it do a lot of the analysis and actually execute for you.” paraphrase ▶ 29:13
Robinhood benefits from ~6 million 'Trump accounts' funneled to its platform, capturing customers at their first-ever stock-market allocation and creating lifelong-customer lock-in.
causal🗣 Avi (host)conviction: mediumRobinhoodHOODTrump accounts
So what: Structural customer-acquisition tailwind and durable LTV; a non-crypto growth driver supporting the de-correlation thesis.
“The first allocation to the stock market that you probably have as a child now is through your Trump account.” ▶ 30:08
Robinhood is reportedly working with an Arbitrum-based chain to build back-end financial infrastructure and run tokenized securities on its own chain.
data_point🗣 Avi (host)conviction: lowRobinhoodArbitrumtokenized securities
So what: Banks/brokers co-opting blockchain rails means you express the thesis via the equity capturing the efficiency gain (HOOD).
≈ “Robin Hood working with potentially arbitrum based chain to build back-end financial system and run tokenized securities on their own chain.” paraphrase ▶ 20:51
AI/memory super-cycle: data, China supply, and demand cracks 6
DRAM contract prices surged 95% in Q1 2026 vs Q4 2025; per Goldman the supply-demand gap widens from 3.3% to 4.9% — the most severe memory shortage in 15 years — with a potential 130% price increase by end-2026.
data_point🗣 Avi (host)conviction: highDRAMMicronSK HynixSamsungGoldman Sachs
So what: Hard data underpinning the memory super-cycle; supports being long Micron/Hynix/Samsung while the shortage persists.
≈ “DRAM contract prices surge 95% in Q1 of 2026 versus Q4 of 2025... the most severe memory shortage in 15 years.” paraphrase
Unlike most AI names, Nvidia trades at a rational PE and memory stocks at a 'somewhat rational' forward multiple against vastly under-supplied demand — the one part of the AI bubble still fundamentally supported.
thesis🗣 Jonah (host)conviction: mediumNvidiaMicronSK HynixSamsungmemory
So what: Valuation argues memory/Nvidia are the 'least bubbly' AI exposures; favor them over more stretched downstream AI plays.
“Nvidia stock trades at a rational PE multiple. The memory stocks trade at a somewhat rational forward multiple.” ▶ 48:06
China is flooding the market with cheaper DRAM via CXMT and Corsair (DDR5/DDR4); these may be FFF-fungible at the edge (e.g. Chinese-owned Volvo using Corsair in EVs) even if banned from Western data centers for security reasons, easing demand at the margin.
causal🗣 Jonah (host)conviction: mediumCXMTCorsairDDR5VolvoGeelyMicron
So what: Edge substitution by Chinese DRAM is a slow-leak bearish risk to the memory shortage; watch for switching even if data centers stay closed.
≈ “Maybe Volvo... will buy Corsair X90 because Volvo is owned by Gil, which is a Chinese company.” paraphrase
GPU rental prices (B200, H100) are 'no longer up only' and rolling off highs, which the host argues should lead memory prices lower — fewer GPUs bought eventually means less memory demand.
causal🗣 Jonah (host)conviction: mediumNvidiaB200H100GPUmemory
So what: GPU rental/spot prices are a leading indicator for the memory cycle; a sustained roll-over is the first 'yellow light' to fade memory longs.
≈ “These charts to me suggest these should lead memory prices... it can sell off as well as rally which is a first.” paraphrase
The Korean stock market, driven by SK Hynix and Samsung, has outperformed the S&P over the last 10 years but is propelled by the same leveraged 'DGEN' retail gamblers who bought Luna at the highs — a possible top signal.
contrarian🗣 Avi (host)conviction: lowKorean stock marketSK HynixSamsungLunaS&P 500
So what: Same leveraged retail base that blew up on Luna now drives Korean memory names; frothy participation is a caution flag.
“It's being driven by the same leveraged DGEN Korean gamblers that borrowed money to buy Luna on the highs.” ▶ 44:57
Memory is treated as a commodity (like oil/gas) where 'high prices are the solution for high prices,' implying the current shortage will self-correct via demand destruction and new supply.
framework🗣 Jonah (host)conviction: mediumDRAMmemory
So what: Frame memory as cyclical/commodity, not secular; don't extrapolate the shortage indefinitely.
“High prices are the solution for high prices. Memory is just a commodity like oil or gas.” ▶ 53:26
Late-innings positioning: cash, high-beta, and narrative laggards 5
The market is in roughly the 'seventh or eighth inning' by time but most of the price rally comes in the eighth/ninth inning, so being near the end in time does not mean near the end in price — a 100% melt-up can still occur a month before the top.
framework🗣 Jonah (host)conviction: highS&P 500NASDAQ
So what: Don't get shaken out: late-cycle melt-ups are the most violent upside; stay partly allocated while raising cash.
≈ “You could be one month from the end but quite literally 100% rally away from where the top is going to be.” paraphrase ▶ 42:02
When volatility is high the optionality value of cash rises (a 20% up-day implies a symmetric 20% down-day risk), so the late-innings playbook is to raise cash while staying in the names that benefit most — now memory, AI, and defense rather than crypto.
framework🗣 Avi (host)conviction: highcashmemoryAIdefenseMicron
So what: Barbell: hold dry powder for symmetric downside while keeping exposure to the highest-beta late-cycle leaders.
“When things like micron are going up 20% in a day, the value of your cash exponentially increases.” ▶ 42:45
The speaker is ~50% cash and is buying narrative laggards that haven't run yet within hot verticals — explicitly Palantir (AI+defense, off its Oct highs, target ~$200) and ILMN (up 22% on the Trump-China-plane narrative).
thesis🗣 Avi (host)conviction: highPalantirILMNIlluminaAIdefense
So what: Specific late-cycle longs: rotate small allocations into un-run narrative names; a Palantir run to $200 would itself flag market froth.
≈ “Palunteer probably takes a bit of a run now... because it's an AI defense stock and both defense and AI are sort of hot now.” paraphrase
Junk/most-downstream assets rallying is the late-stage bubble signal; AI research tools now let retail find tiny low-quality 'AI bottleneck' microcaps (e.g. a $45M-cap fiber-optic adhesive maker) that rally despite bad fundamentals — the host is watching this junk-rally as the lighten-up trigger.
framework🗣 Avi (host)conviction: mediumAIClaudemicrocaps
So what: Use 'is the junk flying?' as a timing indicator; broad low-quality microcap melt-ups signal it's time to de-risk.
≈ “I'm watching to see is that junk rallying and we're starting to see some stupidity in the market.” paraphrase ▶ 34:23
The speaker is uncomfortable because the equity rally looks purely technical (overbought) rather than fundamental, and suggests trimming ~20% of a HYPE position as a discipline to test whether things are overheated.
thesis🗣 Avi (host)conviction: mediumS&P 500NASDAQHyperliquidHYPE
So what: When a rally is too white-hot you trade around the position; partial profit-taking on winners like HYPE is the prescribed move.
“It's just rallied too freaking hard... whenever anything goes this well, you're supposed to trade around the position.” ▶ 39:51
Structural alpha: research-community incentive gap and IPO top signals 5
Wall Street research systematically under-prices exponential change because analysts who make bold-and-wrong calls get fired while consensus-and-wrong calls are safe — capping the most bullish analyst at ~25% upside even when reality is +100%.
framework🗣 Jonah (host)conviction: highWall StreetMicronresearch analysts
So what: Trillions of passive capital follow these throttled estimates, creating a structural 'Grand Canyon' arb for retail in fast-adopting tech.
“If you're a research analyst and you make a bold call and you're wrong you get fired... a consensus call and you're wrong you keep your job.” ▶ 31:35
Large hyped IPOs typically mark tops (Coinbase's 2021 IPO marked the crypto peak), so the upcoming SpaceX, Anthropic and OpenAI IPOs are likely to mark the top of the memory/AI boom.
prediction🗣 Avi (host)conviction: mediumSpaceXAnthropicOpenAICoinbaseIPO
So what: Treat the AI-mega-IPO calendar as a timing tool; their pricing/pop may signal the cycle top.
“It's highly likely if not almost toological at this point that the anthropic and open AI IPOs will mark the top of the memory boom.” ▶ 47:23
The SpaceX IPO is allocating 30% to retail (vs the usual ~10%) because retail pays the spread above institutions, setting up a large day-one pop and a wave of capital that then rotates elsewhere.
data_point🗣 Avi (host)conviction: mediumSpaceXIPOretail
So what: Outsized retail allocation signals froth and a likely pop; the post-IPO capital rotation is tradable flow to track.
“The SpaceX IPO is allocating instead of 10% to retail, 30% to retail... a massive pop on day one.” ▶ 46:20
The hosts watch for a Kevin Warsh-led Fed reaction: if hard inflation prints recur next month, rate-hike contract pricing rises further, spooking the market and triggering the desired leverage unwind they want to buy.
prediction🗣 Avi (host)conviction: mediumKevin WarshFederal Reserveinflationinterest rates
So what: Watch monthly inflation and rate-hike odds as the catalyst for a corrective shakeout that creates a buying opportunity.
“If he gets hit with hard inflation numbers again next month, we're probably going to see rate hike prices go up.” ▶ 45:55
Anecdotal macro-unease 'top signals' cited include Peter Thiel moving to Argentina out of concern about the US, alongside frothy San Francisco real estate chatter.
anecdote🗣 Jonah (host)conviction: lowPeter ThielSan Francisco real estate
So what: Soft sentiment flags from a contrarian who is 'usually right about big societal shifts'; corroborating, not actionable on their own.
“Peter Teal... just moved to Argentina because he's worried about the United States.”
Iran deal, the Strait of Hormuz, and the oil-inflation transmission 4
The Iran outcome is already priced; a deal is likely in 4–6 weeks because all parties (China, US, even Iran's now-more-moderate post-decapitation leadership) want low oil prices, so the market may not rally on news and could even dip.
thesis🗣 Avi (host)conviction: highIranoilStrait of Hormuz
So what: Don't chase a 'deal' melt-up — it's discounted; positioning should account for a possible sell-the-news.
≈ “It's all priced at this point. People are going to be shocked... It's possible the market goes down.” paraphrase
A deal could be a net negative for markets if it includes a Hormuz transit toll and/or allows continued uranium enrichment (i.e. worse than the JCPOA), causing the market to fade rather than rally.
causal🗣 Avi (host)conviction: mediumIranJCPOAStrait of Hormuzuranium enrichment
So what: Read the deal terms closely — a Hormuz toll or enrichment carve-out flips the trade bearish despite a 'deal' headline.
“If the deal looks worse than the JCPOA possible the market doesn't really do anything might even go down half a percent.”
A durable deal reopens Hormuz and unleashes an armada of floating-storage tankers plus newly de-sanctioned NITC tankers, slamming oil toward ~$50 and driving the chain: lower oil → lower inflation → higher risk-asset prices.
causal🗣 Jonah (host)conviction: highoilStrait of HormuzNITCinflation
So what: A sustained reopening is a tradable bullish-risk catalyst via the oil→inflation→risk transmission; especially bullish EM (India, Philippines).
“There's an armada of floating storage trapped in the Gulf right now. All that oil is going to hit the market like a sack of bricks.” ▶ 58:37
Russia ('Vlad') is the main beneficiary of high oil prices; the Saudis can't even benefit because their oil is locked in the Gulf, and the UAE now wants low prices given its conflict with Iran.
data_point🗣 Jonah (host)conviction: mediumRussiaSaudi ArabiaUAEIranoil
So what: Maps the petro-state incentive structure: nearly everyone is aligned toward lower oil, reinforcing the bearish-oil base case.
“Only one guy who's benefiting big from this and his name is Vlad.” ▶ 56:10
Is Robotics The Next Megabubble?59 min45 insights2026-06-04
▶ Watch on YouTube
Andrew Kang (ex-Mechanism Capital crypto/DeFi investor) argues robotics is a tens-of-trillions TAM at a crypto-2014-like inflection point, and pitches Robo Strategy — a NASDAQ-listed 'MicroStrategy-for-robotics' closed-end vehicle holding private humanoid/industrial-robot startups (Figure, Apptronik, Standard Bots, Dyna/Dexmate) that monetizes an accretive premium-to-NAV to give public investors otherwise-inaccessible exposure.
Robotics TAM and inflection-point thesis · 7Robotics intelligence / physical AI capability curve · 6VC capital rotation into robotics · 5Risks, bubble framing and failure rate · 4Robo Strategy: MicroStrategy-style closed-end vehicle · 10Portfolio companies and competitive positioning · 8Public-market robotics access and proxies · 2Investing process and edge · 3
Robotics TAM and inflection-point thesis 7
Andrew Kang frames robotics as a tens-of-trillions-of-dollars industry still in its infancy, with the whole sector retaining 100x-to-1000x upside from here.
thesis🗣 Andrew Kang (guest, Robo Strategy founder)conviction: highroboticsRobo Strategy
So what: Sets the bull-case ceiling for sizing any robotics allocation; an investor treating this as a multi-decade secular theme would accumulate private/public proxies early.
“the industry as a whole still has 100x, you know, potentially a thousandx upside remaining” ▶ 8:41
The total market cap of private robotics companies has roughly grown from ~$20B two years ago to ~$100-200B today, which Kang uses to argue the sector is still extremely early.
data_point🗣 Andrew Kang (guest)conviction: highroboticsprivate markets
