So what: A ~5-10x cap expansion off a tiny base signals the theme is in early-mainstreaming; useful baseline for tracking whether the 'megabubble' is inflating.
“private robotics companies at the time... probably was like $20 billion. Right now, it's probably around 100 150, maybe pushing $200 billion” ▶ 8:08
Kang contextualizes robotics' small size by noting the entire private-robotics market cap is comparable to XRP (~$100B) and only a few times the Pokemon trading-card market (~$50B).
data_point🗣 Andrew Kang (guest)conviction: mediumXRPPokemonrobotics
So what: The deliberate comparison to speculative collectibles cuts both ways — it dramatizes upside but also flags how unanchored current valuations are.
“XRP is is what is it's close to hundred billion. Poke the total market cap of Pokemon trading cards is around $50 billion”
Kang's core timing edge is buying at technological inflection points where the market underprices how fast the field will reach its future state.
framework🗣 Andrew Kang (guest)conviction: highroboticscrypto
So what: Reusable mental model: hunt sectors where capability is about to bend sharply upward and consensus pricing lags; the alpha is in the repricing, not the end state.
“the market is going to you know reach that future state a lot sooner than everyone would think and the market's not always pricing it that way” ▶ 5:28
Kang explicitly analogizes robotics today to crypto/DeFi in 2014-2015 and 2019 — a complex, poorly-understood field with no standard valuation framework, which is exactly where he believes durable edge is created.
framework🗣 Andrew Kang (guest)conviction: highcryptoDeFiroboticsSaaS
So what: Implies the same playbook that minted crypto-era returns (early, interdisciplinary, build your own valuation framework) is being ported to robotics; investors with no robotics framework are at a structural disadvantage.
“there's no like framework for evaluating it like there is for say like SAS companies... if you can do that, you have tremendous edge over all the other investors” ▶ 6:11
Kang prefers robotics over space (SpaceX) as a theme because robots will permeate everyday life far sooner and more tangibly than consumer space travel.
thesis🗣 Andrew Kang (guest)conviction: mediumSpaceXroboticsspace
So what: A relative-attractiveness call: capital chasing 'frontier tech' may rotate from space narratives toward robotics on faster real-world adoption.
“space is cool to look at... I don't think most people are going to ride a rocket ship... but robots are going to permeate everyday life” ▶ 8:57
Kang projects a humanoid robot priced at ~$20,000 (economy) to ~$50,000 (premium) as a one-time cost, versus $40-50K/year (and up to $200-400K all-in for executive assistants) for a human assistant.
data_point🗣 Andrew Kang (guest)conviction: mediumhumanoid robotslabor market
So what: If unit economics hit these levels, the labor-substitution ROI is dramatic — the implied payback period under a year is the demand engine behind the whole TAM claim.
“a humanoid robot that costs say $20,000 for an economy one or $50,000 for a premium one... A good personal assistant could cost, you know, 40 50,000 a year” ▶ 9:42
Robotics intelligence / physical AI capability curve 6
Kang assesses robotics intelligence is roughly at the 'GPT-3 level' today — succeeding maybe half the time at physical tasks — implying a steep capability ramp is imminent rather than mature.
framework🗣 Andrew Kang (guest)conviction: highGPT-3physical AIroboticsLLMs
So what: A capability-stage map: if robotics is pre-GPT-3.5, the 'ChatGPT moment' (mass attention + capital) is still ahead, suggesting the narrative bubble hasn't peaked.
“we're pretty much at the GBT3 level of robotics in terms of intelligence right now” ▶ 17:53
Kang predicts the jump from 'GPT-3' to 'GPT-5' of robotics will take ~1 year (vs ~2 years for LLMs) because research learnings on RLHF, mid-training, data mix and annotation transfer over and compress the timeline.
prediction🗣 Andrew Kang (guest)conviction: highGPT-5RLHFphysical AIrobotics
So what: A faster capability cycle compresses the window to invest before repricing; a ~12-month horizon to 'GPT-5 of robotics' is the catalyst clock for the thesis.
“the time from 3 to five that was two years... I think it's going to be way less than two years. I think it's going to be something like a year” ▶ 21:22
Generalization to unseen environments is the key signal Kang watches for working robot models — picking up novel objects or operating in untrained homes/factories as if trained on them.
framework🗣 Andrew Kang (guest)conviction: highrobot foundation modelsgeneralization
So what: Gives investors a concrete due-diligence metric to separate real robotics breakthroughs from staged demos: does it generalize off-distribution?
“the hallmark of like what you want to see AI models that actually work is that they can face environments that they've never seen before” ▶ 18:32
Kang says physical-AI models are starting to exhibit in-context learning and emergent internal memory (e.g. RoboAI's robot winning the shell game) as data and compute scale.
data_point🗣 Andrew Kang (guest)conviction: mediumRoboAIin-context learningphysical AI
So what: Emergent capabilities mirror the LLM scaling story, supporting the bet that compute/data scaling unlocks robotic generality — and that frontier-model labs lead.
“there is an example shown by ROA AI where uh they played the the shell game... and who was able to pick that up” ▶ 20:22
Kang argues you cannot simply bolt a general LLM (e.g. Claude/GPT) onto hardware to get a working robot, because robot foundation models must encode physics, force/pressure feedback, affordances and embodied priors text-trained LLMs lack.
contrarian🗣 Andrew Kang (guest)conviction: highClaudeAnthropicOpenAIrobot foundation modelsVLMs
So what: Directly rebuts the bear case that AGI commoditizes robot software; defends a durable moat for specialized robot-model builders and the hardware-data flywheel.
“You can't just like hook up claw into a robot and have it work” ▶ 22:52
Robot models need embodied 'priors'/affordances (how much pressure to grip, one vs two hands, not spilling water) that humans take for granted, and must learn to self-adapt to wholly novel situations.
framework🗣 Andrew Kang (guest)conviction: highaffordancesrobot learning
So what: Frames the remaining technical moat and where R&D spend concentrates; companies solving embodied priors / self-adaptation are the differentiated bets.
“we there's a lot of that intuition in in robotics research, they call that prior or affordances, right? That we take for granted as as humans” ▶ 25:55
VC capital rotation into robotics 5
Kang says VC interest in robotics began inflecting only in the last ~3-4 months, triggered by a software 'wakeup call' earlier in the year that made many software businesses look less durable/venture-fundable.
causal🗣 Andrew Kang (guest)conviction: highventure capitalsoftwareroboticsAI
So what: Identifies the capital-rotation catalyst: software disillusionment is pushing VC dollars toward physical AI — a flow tailwind for robotics valuations.
“there was like this huge wakeup call earlier this year that software businesses some of them were not as durable as we thought they were” ▶ 15:02
Kang argues robotics VC interest today is roughly where AI was 1-2 years before its peak, implying substantial room for further inflows and multiple expansion.
prediction🗣 Andrew Kang (guest)conviction: highroboticsAIventure capital
So what: Positions robotics as 'pre-peak hype,' encouraging front-running the AI-style capital wave; also a tell that valuations may run well past fundamentals.
“it's anywhere near where it's going to what what it could be as in the amount of interest we have in AI companies right now” ▶ 16:24
Tier-1 and tier-2 VCs are now writing robotics term sheets at multiples meaningfully higher than companies raised at just months ago.
data_point🗣 Andrew Kang (guest)conviction: highventure capitalrobotics startups
So what: Confirms active, accelerating valuation inflation in private robotics rounds — relevant to anyone marking or entering these names now.
“some of that is like turning into term sheets at you know multiples higher valuation than these companies were raising at in, you know, just a few months ago” ▶ 16:49
Kang expects power-law/winner-take-most dynamics (as seen with OpenAI and Anthropic) to apply to robotics, producing a multi-trillion-dollar winner.
thesis🗣 Andrew Kang (guest)conviction: highOpenAIAnthropicpower lawrobotics
So what: Implies concentration over diversification — capturing the eventual category leader matters far more than broad exposure; index-style robotics bets may underperform.
“we're going to be able to put tens of billions of dollars to work in the entropic of robotics because there's going to be a multi- trillion dollar winner” ▶ 39:54
Scale is decisive in robot manufacturing economics: without scale a robot's bill-of-materials runs ~$500K-$1M, but at scale it can be produced ~90% cheaper.
data_point🗣 Andrew Kang (guest)conviction: highrobotics manufacturingeconomies of scale
So what: Cost curve favors the largest-volume producer, reinforcing winner-take-most and explaining why funding scale is itself a moat for portfolio companies.
“it's going to cost you what, like 500,000 a million to produce a robot just from a bomb perspective... if you have scale, you can produce it 90% cheaper” ▶ 39:34
Risks, bubble framing and failure rate 4
Kang concedes robotics is a hard industry with a very high startup failure rate, and that the breadth of eventual winners is still undecided even if a few will be enormous.
contrarian🗣 Andrew Kang (guest)conviction: mediumrobotics startupsventure capital
So what: A rare bear-acknowledgment within the pitch: naive 'find the next Figure' retail copycats face severe dispersion risk — selection/diligence is paramount.
“robotics is a really hard industry... there's going to be a lot of failures... there's going to be very very high failure rate” ▶ 37:26
Kang flags hype-chasing as a current market dynamic — investors playing catch-up trying to find 'the next Figure AI' without modeling the ways these companies fail.
contrarian🗣 Andrew Kang (guest)conviction: mediumFigure AIrobotics
So what: Signals frothy, FOMO-driven capital entering late — a contrarian caution that diligence-light money is the marginal buyer.
“it feels like people are trying to play catchup a little bit... they're trying to find hey look I want the next figure AI” ▶ 37:12
Prior headwinds that suppressed robotics VC — capex costs, China competition, and US manufacturing capability — were key reasons the trend stayed niche until recently.
causal🗣 Andrew Kang (guest)conviction: mediumChinacapexUS manufacturingrobotics
So what: These concerns haven't vanished; they're the watch-list risks that could deflate the megabubble if China cost advantage or capex intensity reassert.
“There were a lot of concerns previously around, you know, capex costs, competition with China, um the ability for us to manufacture in America” ▶ 15:56
Kang positions deep, specialized industry expertise as a way to underwrite later-stage robotics deals at ~100% year-over-year return targets at risk levels comparable to traditional ~20-25% growth-stage targets.
thesis🗣 Andrew Kang (guest)conviction: highgrowth-stage investingrobotics
So what: The return pitch (100% YoY at 'similar' risk) is the core sell; investors should weigh whether that risk-adjusting truly holds or is narrative-driven.
“previously you look at growth stage investments... you're targeting maybe 20 25%... and now I think it's reasonable belief... I can target 100% year-over-year” ▶ 40:05
Robo Strategy: MicroStrategy-style closed-end vehicle 10
Robo Strategy is a NASDAQ-listed public company that rolled up Kang's private robotics investments into a single vehicle so public investors can buy otherwise-inaccessible private exposure.
thesis🗣 host / Andrew Kang (guest)conviction: highRobo StrategyNASDAQ
So what: Creates a tradeable public proxy for private humanoid/industrial-robot startups — the actionable instrument for retail/restricted-capital exposure to the theme.
“you launched a public company called Robo Strategy... which now trades on... the NASDAQ that basically rolled up your investments” ▶ 34:34
Kang's model copies MicroStrategy's private-equity 'public rollup' playbook (a la Transdigm/Constellation Software) where public vehicles trade at 15-40x while private peers trade at 3-8x, making M&A immediately accretive.
framework🗣 Andrew Kang (guest)conviction: highMicroStrategyTransdigmConstellation SoftwareBitcoin
So what: The accretion engine is multiple arbitrage: buy private at low multiple, host it inside a high-multiple public shell, repeat — the mechanical driver of NAV-per-share growth beyond underlying returns.
“I buy this private company... maybe I spend 100 million once it's on my books, it rerates immediately to 400 million. And so it is immediately accretive” ▶ 43:34
Kang argues the 'access premium' that drove MicroStrategy demand is multiplied ('thousandx') for private robotics, because almost no public-market vehicle exists for private venture robotics exposure.
thesis🗣 Andrew Kang (guest)conviction: highMicroStrategyRobo Strategyprivate markets
So what: The scarcity-of-access argument is the demand thesis supporting a durable premium to NAV — and the main reason the vehicle could sustain a high multiple.
“If we think about access as really the primary demand driver, that issue is multiplied to thousandx for private robotics investments” ▶ 44:53
Even after a spot Bitcoin ETF launched, MicroStrategy's premium stayed strong and it issued billions in equity, which Kang cites as evidence restricted institutional capital (RIAs, pension funds) sustains demand despite alternatives.
data_point🗣 Andrew Kang (guest)conviction: mediumMicroStrategyBitcoin ETFpension fundsRIAs
So what: Counters the obvious bear case (premium collapses once direct access appears); used to argue Robo Strategy's premium is more resilient than skeptics expect.
“even after the ETF came out I I thought the premium would collapse, right? But it it still stayed really strong for a while” ▶ 44:36
Kang argues robotics' TAM is an order of magnitude larger than Bitcoin's (<$1.5T market cap, smaller than Amazon/Nvidia/Apple/Google), so a robotics rollup could surpass MicroStrategy's ~$100B peak and reach SoftBank-scale hundreds of billions.
prediction🗣 Andrew Kang (guest)conviction: highBitcoinMicroStrategySoftBankrobotics
So what: Sets the explicit valuation ambition for Robo Strategy and the upside case for early shareholders — a 'SoftBank 2.0' framing.
“micro Azure at their peak market cap was around 100 billion. I I'm reaching for something that's more like SoftBank scale uh in the in the hundreds of billions” ▶ 46:02
Kang differentiates Robo Strategy from MicroStrategy by keeping the accretive-issuance engine but largely avoiding leverage and preferred shares, using leverage only selectively and briefly.
framework🗣 Andrew Kang (guest)conviction: highRobo StrategyMicroStrategyleverage
So what: Lower structural leverage means less forced-deleveraging/blow-up risk than MicroStrategy — a key differentiator for risk-aware buyers of the vehicle.
“What we're leaving out is is the leverage part of it. Um we're not going to do um preferred shares” ▶ 47:12
Kang reframes the premium-to-NAV as a feature, not a flaw: issuing shares above NAV is accretive and can grow NAV-per-share even in a flat/bear market with no portfolio markups, though a premium compression (e.g. 4x to 2x) is a 50% price loss.
framework🗣 Andrew Kang (guest)conviction: highNAV premiumRobo Strategymultiple arbitrage
So what: The premium is both the second growth engine and the dominant risk; an investor must monitor the premium multiple as closely as underlying NAV.
“the premium adds extra risk because if the premium goes down... it compresses from say 4x to 2x that is a 50% loss in market price” ▶ 48:05
Kang notes Robo Strategy's NAV is marked to last private-round valuations (with discounts for earlier share classes) and thus lags real-time growth, whereas the public market prices forward-looking milestones — a structural reason the vehicle can trade above NAV.
framework🗣 Andrew Kang (guest)conviction: mediumNAVprivate marketsRobo Strategy
So what: Explains why a persistent premium can be 'rational' — NAV is stale; but it also means reported NAV understates volatility risk if private marks reset down.
“the NAV... it looks at the last round valuations... but doesn't take into account hey look... a huge amount of growth that is not encompassed in NAV” ▶ 48:32
Both host and Kang raise the reflexive idea that a large public holding vehicle trading at a premium could effectively set the price for illiquid private assets that don't trade 24/7 — pulling forward future round valuations.
contrarian🗣 host / Andrew Kang (guest)conviction: exploratoryRobo Strategyprivate marketsprice discovery
So what: Reflexivity risk: the vehicle's own premium may inflate the marks it reports, a feedback loop that can amplify both upside and a downside unwind.
“if people are willing to pay 2x for your nav, maybe these companies are going to raise like a 3x in a year or two years anyway. So... people are just pulling forward that price appreciation” ▶ 51:17
Kang argues the public capital markets are ~100x larger than private markets (citing Elon Musk), justifying inventing a new public-market venture model that can scale to tens of billions where private funds cannot.
thesis🗣 Andrew Kang (guest)conviction: highpublic marketsventure capitalElon Musk
So what: The scale argument for why the vehicle structure (not a private fund) is the chosen path — and why he expects to deploy tens of billions, dwarfing his crypto-era AUM.
“the public capital markets, Elon says, right? Like it's 100 times bigger than the private capital markets” ▶ 41:32
Portfolio companies and competitive positioning 8
Kang calls Figure AI the 'Apple of this generation' of robotics, citing superior design taste and user experience versus 'cookie-cutter' Chinese humanoids, and expects humanoids to become a status symbol like the iPhone.
thesis🗣 Andrew Kang (guest)conviction: highFigure AIApplehumanoid robotsChina
So what: Names Figure as his top conviction humanoid bet on brand/design moat — a consumer-premium thesis distinct from pure capability races.
“figure AI, I think, is... the Apple of this generation” ▶ 52:14
Kang holds Apptronik (referred to as 'Eptronic/Apptronik') as a leading general-purpose humanoid maker focused on the most versatile form factor, alongside Figure.
thesis🗣 Andrew Kang (guest)conviction: highApptronikFigure AIhumanoid robots
So what: Identifies a second core humanoid holding; the humanoid form factor is his highest-versatility, broadest-TAM bet.
“those are companies like Eptronic and Figure uh because they're focused on the humanoid form factor which is the most versatile” ▶ 11:31
Kang describes a portfolio humanoid company (positioned as ~1-2 years behind Figure) with deep power-design/manufacturing experience and partnerships with Jabil (manufacturing) and Google DeepMind (AI).
data_point🗣 Andrew Kang (guest)conviction: highJabilGoogle DeepMindhumanoid robotsFigure AI
So what: The Jabil + DeepMind partnership combo is a concrete de-risking signal (scaled manufacturing + frontier AI) for evaluating the #2 humanoid contender.
“they have a partnership with Jable... and they also have a partnership with Google DeepMind which is... one of the leading AI organizations in the world” ▶ 54:15
Standard Bots is Kang's key bet on US re-industrialization: the only at-scale, vertically-integrated US industrial-arm/cobot maker not reliant on a China-based supply chain, with a TAM Kang sizes at billions of mechanical arms.
thesis🗣 Andrew Kang (guest)conviction: highStandard Botscobotsindustrial armsUS manufacturingChina
So what: A non-humanoid, policy-aligned (reshoring) bet with national-strategic framing — a differentiated way to play robotics distinct from the humanoid hype.
“there's only one uh that really exists at scale and and that's Standard Bots... homegrown, vertically integrated, not reliant on... China” ▶ 13:31
Kang holds Dyna Robotics ('Dino Robotics') as a top robot-learning/post-training group, among the first to reach 99.9% task success rates and building its own differentiated hardware platform.
data_point🗣 Andrew Kang (guest)conviction: highDyna Roboticsrobot learningpost-training
So what: Highlights reliability (99.9% success) as the deployment threshold; Dyna is his bet on the software/post-training layer plus vertical hardware.
“they were one of the first companies that were able to get these robot models to perform at 99.9% um success rate”
Kang argues robots must hit near-zero intervention rates to be deployable — even 1% failure is too high — making reliability the gating commercial metric, not raw capability demos.
framework🗣 Andrew Kang (guest)conviction: highrobot reliabilitydeployment
So what: A diligence yardstick: discount companies showcasing flashy but unreliable demos; commercial value accrues to those crossing the 99.9%+ reliability bar.
“It can't even be messing up 1% of the time. That's too much... the goal of these robots is that humans don't have to intervene ever” ▶ 55:20
Dexmate is Kang's bet on a 'developer-platform' model: the first US company to commercially sell humanoid robots (wheeled version sold last year to major US research labs/companies, legged version due later this summer).
thesis🗣 Andrew Kang (guest)conviction: highDexmatehumanoid robotsresearch labs
So what: Positions Dexmate as the 'commercially available robot to build on' — an underappreciated platform play with near-term revenue and a catalyst (legged launch) this summer.
“Dexmate is the first company in in the US to have commercially sold uh humanoid robots” ▶ 56:44
Kang predicts robots will become a platform like the iPhone, spawning a third-party 'developer economy' of downloadable skills (e.g. cooking like Gordon Ramsay) that makes commercially-available hardware far more valuable.
prediction🗣 Andrew Kang (guest)conviction: highDexmateiPhonedeveloper economyrobotics platforms
So what: Implies platform/ecosystem economics (not just hardware margins) accrue to whoever owns the dominant commercially-available robot — a winner-take-most software-style upside.
“robots are going to be a platform in the same way that the iPhone is a platform. People are going to be able to develop their own skills” ▶ 57:11
Public-market robotics access and proxies 2
Kang notes public-market robotics exposure today is essentially just Tesla (~$2T market cap) plus one or two names, with the trillions of value creation happening in private companies (Figure, plus SpaceX/Anthropic/OpenAI reaching $1T+ before IPO).
data_point🗣 Andrew Kang (guest)conviction: highTeslaSpaceXAnthropicOpenAIpublic markets
So what: Quantifies the access gap that Robo Strategy is built to fill; explains why retail can't currently express the robotics thesis except via Tesla.
“in the in the public markets, it's really just Tesla, maybe like one or two other companies. And Tesla's two trillion market cap” ▶ 38:07
Kang reports receiving 1000+ DMs/replies on X asking how to get robotics exposure, which he cites as the demand evidence that prompted launching a public vehicle.
anecdote🗣 Andrew Kang (guest)conviction: mediumX (Twitter)Robo Strategyretail demand
So what: Retail demand signal underpinning the access thesis — strong inbound interest supports the premium-to-NAV demand case for the vehicle.
“I probably got over a thousand DMs and replies people asking had we get exposure to space and what companies should we be buying” ▶ 38:00
Investing process and edge 3
Kang attributes his diligence edge to a robotics-veteran team (e.g. Scott Walter, ~40 years' experience, two robotics exits including one sold to KUKA) plus a network to validate supply-chain orders, customer performance and founder claims that retail can't access.
thesis🗣 Andrew Kang (guest)conviction: highRobo StrategyScott WalterKUKA
So what: The team/network is the stated moat justifying the vehicle's fees and premium; it's also the bull case for why his marks should be trusted over retail copycats.
“Scott Walter on our team, he's had 40 years of robotics experience... he's built two companies and sold both of them in the space, the last one to CUKA” ▶ 29:38
Kang frames investing from first principles as probabilistic risk/reward estimation requiring deep industry knowledge plus meta-awareness of one's own knowledge gaps to avoid overestimating edge.
framework🗣 Andrew Kang (guest)conviction: highinvesting processrisk/reward
So what: A transferable framework: calibrate conviction to genuine, validated understanding; the warning is that most robotics investors overestimate their knowledge.
“if I overestimate my understanding of the space, then maybe I won't dig in it enough and I... will have a wrong estimate of what the real riskreward of this investment is” ▶ 32:14
Kang cites Figure AI as a case where validating multiple avenues of 'where I could be wrong' and finding he wasn't gave him conviction the market was mispricing it.
anecdote🗣 Andrew Kang (guest)conviction: highFigure AIinvesting process
So what: Illustrates his disconfirmation-based conviction method and signals Figure was an early high-conviction, against-consensus position.
“if you've maybe like validated like five different avenues of like where you could be wrong... maybe the market's wrong... That's I think that's what happened with with figure AI” ▶ 33:23
The Market Is Splitting In Two — How Do We Navigate it?52 min47 insights2026-06-03
▶ Watch on YouTube
Avi Felman and Jonah Vanberg argue the market is K-shaped/splitting in two — a narrow AI-and-memory-driven equity melt-up alongside a deteriorating crypto-majors complex — and advocate high cash plus concentration in single names, narrative-driven sector rotation (software shorts, healthcare/biotech longs, robotics, energy), and a battery of crypto pair trades (XMR/ZEC, IBIT/MSTR) to capture record dispersion while managing late-cycle risk.
K-shaped market bifurcation thesis · 6Positioning: cash + concentration in single names · 4Memory/DRAM as the leading indicator of the AI chip cycle · 5Bitcoin breakdown and the Saylor/MSTR unwind · 6Crypto dispersion and pair trades · 6Narrative rotation into equities: software, healthcare, robotics, energy · 8Macro crosscurrents: AI supply wall, Iran/oil, the Fed · 6Trading frameworks: holder analysis, lead-lag, and AI-as-tool · 6
K-shaped market bifurcation thesis 6
The market is splitting into a K-shape: winners are being held onto while losers get puked out via tax-loss harvesting, producing a two-tier market where a handful of AI/memory names melt up and the majors lag.
thesis🗣 Jonah Vanbergconviction: highS&P 500memory stocksBitcoinAI
So what: Index exposure understates the divergence; investors should select the narrow set of winners rather than own the benchmark.
“I think we're in a K-shaped uh the mic market is splitting in two basically.”
The S&P is up ~7% YTD but the Mag 7 leaders that drove the prior year's gains are now sputtering — Nvidia only up ~10%, Microsoft and Meta struggling — signaling the crazy late stage of the bull where derivative plays run instead of the direct leaders.
data_point🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: highS&P 500NASDAQMag 7NvidiaGoogleMicrosoftMeta
So what: Leadership narrowing/fatigue is a classic late-cycle tell; raise cash and wait for better entries rather than chase.
“the larger assets are struggling to rally right now. that that makes me worried” ▶ 4:23
When retail piles into derivative/catch-up trades (HP, Dell) rather than the direct AI play, it is a signal the rally is nearing exhaustion.
framework🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: highHPDell
So what: Watch breadth of leadership: derivatives outperforming the primary names is a sell-discipline trigger.
“one way to tell that the rally is coming to an end is when people are buying the things that are derivative plays and not necessarily the direct play itself” ▶ 2:28
Today's equity rally is leverage-driven with extremely high dispersion and weak breadth, so holders are mostly weak hands chasing momentum — making the market far more vulnerable to shocks than the healthier, strong-hand market of April.
causal🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: highequities
So what: Weak-hand ownership raises crash risk on any catalyst (Iran, inflation); size and hedge accordingly.
“A lot of the people that are holding assets now are in it because they are going up” ▶ 45:25
RKLB roughly doubling its market cap from $4.5B to $9B on the back of a single $90M contract exemplifies retail money chasing and excess liquidity sloshing around late in the cycle.
data_point🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: highRKLBRocket Lab
So what: Wildly disproportionate cap moves on small fundamentals flag froth; Avi exited RKLB after the run.
“It literally went from a 4.5 to9 billion market cap because it signed a $90 million contract. And I mean, that's just nuts.” ▶ 4:52
The current alt-season-style melt-up is analogous to EOS running in April 2018 after Bitcoin had already topped — i.e. excess liquidity rotating into derivatives is a late-cycle topping signature.
framework🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: mediumEOSBitcoin
So what: Historical analog warns that the broadening rally may be the last gasp before a top.
“it's like the equivalent of EOS running in April of 2018 after Bitcoin had topped” ▶ 4:31
Positioning: cash + concentration in single names 4
The optimal late-cycle posture is high cash plus high concentration in a few single names you believe in, so you stay allocated to the bull market while holding dry powder to buy dips.
thesis🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: highcashsingle-name equities
So what: Barbell of concentrated conviction longs and cash is the prescribed playbook for a volatile, splitting market.
“the best way to navigate these markets when they're high volume is to have those single names that you think are going to do really well and then have cash so that you can buy the dips” ▶ 1:46
Buying the index is a mistake because the bull market lives in only ~30 companies; owning the rest of the NASDAQ 100 / S&P 500 dilutes exposure to where the economy is actually growing.
contrarian🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: highNASDAQ 100S&P 500
So what: Argues against passive indexing here; concentrate in the growth cohort and hold cash instead of the laggards.
“the reality is the bull market is in like 30 companies. And so why would you allocate to the rest of it” ▶ 39:31
Traders sitting on large unrealized gains (UPNL) in memory/compute/AI-adjacent stocks should take profits — 'if you traded like a god, it's time to pay yourself like a god' — rather than ride the position indefinitely.
thesis🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: highmemory stocksAI stocks
So what: Profit-taking discipline after 3x-10x runs; don't let unrealized gains become a round trip.
“it's time to pay yourself like a god. You can't just you can't just sit on that UPNL forever.” ▶ 5:30
Avi's go-forward style is shifting to highly tactical trading rather than buy-and-hold, accepting the 'time in market beats timing the market' counterargument but rejecting it because volatility favors active traders.
thesis🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: mediumvolatility
So what: Signals a regime where active rotation is expected to outperform passive holding.
“my my general approach now is is going to be very tactical relative to the to the past” ▶ 40:09
Memory/DRAM as the leading indicator of the AI chip cycle 5
Physical DDR4 memory pricing is the most leading, 'squeeziest' indicator of the AI/compute boom — DDR4 has the biggest physical shortage and will lead every other AI-related asset.
framework🗣 Jonah Vanbergconviction: highDDR4DRAMmemorycompute
So what: Track a DDR4 index as the upstream tell; AI-equity tops should follow memory rolling over, not the other way around.
“To me this is the most leading indicator. This is physical demand. This is like as leading as it gets.” ▶ 10:16
Per DRAM Exchange data, a DDR4 16Gb module went from ~$8.50 last August to a ~$77-78 peak (roughly a 9x), sold off ~30% (from ~$80 to ~$58) between March and May, then resumed rising ~10% in the last week.
data_point🗣 Jonah Vanbergconviction: highDDR4DRAM Exchange
So what: The reacceleration after a 30% pullback supports staying long memory-levered names; a sustained downtrend would be the sell signal.
“the price of a DDR416 gigabyte gigabit module was let's call it $8.50 then it topped out around $778 bucks” ▶ 7:54
Dell is not in a bubble at 23x forward PE (vs ~50x trailing) because earnings are rising on new AI-related sales; forward earnings only revert if the DDR4 chart fully rolls over.
thesis🗣 Jonah Vanbergconviction: highDellDDR4
So what: Forward, not trailing, PE is the right lens; Dell stays a buy unless memory prices collapse.
“I'm not worried about Dell trading at 23 times forward earnings unless those forward earnings are going to revert to what they looked like in the past” ▶ 10:49
Dell stock traced the inverse of DDR4: it trended down through Oct-Dec while DDR4 went vertical, bottomed around $110-125 in February, then ran straight to ~$475 a share.
data_point🗣 Jonah Vanbergconviction: highDellDDR4
So what: Illustrates the lead-lag: the commodity led, the equity followed with a delay — a template for finding mispriced AI plays.
“It bottoms out in February at $125, $110 a share, then that's it. Then it goes straight up to $475 a share.”
Most fundamentals are mean-reverting (higher prices bring supply, dampen demand) unless a paradigm shift breaks the range, so the key analytical task is identifying the underlying fundamental asset and judging whether it has broken its range.
framework🗣 Jonah Vanbergconviction: highcommoditiesmemory
So what: Distinguishes durable secular moves from reversion traps; apply the 'has the range broken?' test before chasing.
“most fundamentals are mean reverting right like econ 101” ▶ 36:25
Bitcoin breakdown and the Saylor/MSTR unwind 6
Bitcoin's technical setup is terrible: it broke down out of a 62-71k range it held Feb-April, and the bullish breakout reversed once the Saylor-driven flywheel failed.
thesis🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: highBitcoinBTCMicroStrategyMichael SaylorSTRC
So what: Failed range breakout plus broken reflexive bid argues for further downside in BTC.
“the the technical analysis here looks really terrible as well” ▶ 14:18
The MSTR/STRC front-running trade — buying ahead of Saylor's predictable Bitcoin purchases — worked for one month, then in month two markets adjusted and traders used Saylor's buying as exit liquidity, and he bought far less than before as people recognized the product as Ponzi-like.
causal🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: highMicroStrategyMSTRSTRCMichael SaylorBitcoin
So what: The collapse of the reflexive corporate bid removes a key pillar under BTC's prior rally.
“people use a trade as exit liquidity they use sailor's buying to basically get out” ▶ 15:44
Saylor/MicroStrategy is facing a survival crisis, signaled by selling Bitcoin in escalating tranches ('$35 worth... $2 billion worth'); the unwind is only in its first inning, so Avi will not re-buy Bitcoin until Saylor is finished blowing up.
prediction🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: highMichael SaylorMicroStrategyMSTRBitcoin
So what: An MSTR forced-deleveraging cascade could be a continued source of BTC supply; wait for it to exhaust before buying.
“He's obviously facing a survival crisis... I'm not going to buy Bitcoin in the reby Bitcoin in the first inning of that unwind.” ▶ 13:33
MicroStrategy trades at only 1.2x NAV and that premium likely flips to a discount soon as holders get blown up.
prediction🗣 Jonah Vanbergconviction: highMicroStrategyMSTRNAV
So what: A premium-to-discount compression is the engine of the long-IBIT/short-MSTR trade.
“Micro Strategy is still trading at 1.2x NAV. That doesn't seem like it's going to last. I bet it goes to a discount pretty soon” ▶ 18:56
Avi sold the Bitcoin lots he had bought above $70k to harvest a tax loss (crypto has no wash-sale rule, BTC is property under IRS) but deliberately did not re-buy six seconds later, choosing to wait for the Saylor unwind to finish.
anecdote🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: highBitcoinIRStax-loss harvesting
So what: Tax-loss harvesting in crypto is frictionless (no wash rule), and the choice not to rebuy is itself a bearish BTC signal.
“I didn't buy it back 6 seconds later.” ▶ 13:07
Year-end seasonality: crypto tends to fall harder in the last week of December as holders dump assets for tax purposes and rebuy in the new year, creating a recurring tradable dip.
framework🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: mediumcryptoBitcointax-loss harvesting
So what: A calendar edge: fade December tax-loss selling and position for the January rebuy.
“the end of the year, like last week of December, things tend to go down a lot more because people sell out all of their assets and then reby them back in the new year”
Crypto dispersion and pair trades 6
Crypto is showing the most dispersion ever — hype, Zcash and VVV crushing it while Bitcoin, Ethereum and the majors do very poorly — driven partly by quantum fears pushing flows into 'quantum-resistant' names.
data_point🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: highZcashZECVVVhypeBitcoinEthereum
So what: Record dispersion rewards relative-value/pair trading over directional beta.
“a market that maybe has the most dispersion that I've ever seen in my entire life” ▶ 16:17
Large crypto allocators remain overweight majors (SOL, ETH, BTC) purely because of liquidity constraints ($100M can't buy size in alts), but a convergence is underway as good altcoins grow market cap relative to the majors.
thesis🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: highSOLETHBTCaltcoins
So what: Structural overweight in majors plus alt convergence supports long-alt / short-major pair positioning.
“the market right now is still very overweight majors... all the large allocators are still in soul, they're in ETH, they're they're in BTC. And the reason is because of liquidity.” ▶ 16:39
TON is outperforming on a real catalyst — Pavel Durov renaming it (to 'Gram') and deepening Telegram integration with a path to monetization — making it a fundamentally-backed alt rather than pure narrative.
data_point🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: mediumTONTelegramPavel DurovGram
So what: Distinguishes catalyst-driven alts (TON) from thinner narrative plays (NEAR) when picking longs.
“ton's outperformed over the last little bit because Pavle is renaming it Graham he's going to be integrating it more into telegram” ▶ 17:07
Recommended hedge structure: express bearish-BTC views as pair trades (short Bitcoin / long outperforming alts) rather than outright shorts to capture upside while protecting downside amid high dispersion.
framework🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: highBitcoinaltcoinspair protocol
So what: Pair trades are the prescribed vehicle for this dispersion regime; reduces directional market risk.
“if you're nervous about Bitcoin going down, just put them on as pair trades. Go short Bitcoin, go long these assets.” ▶ 17:56
Specific pair trade: long XMR / short ZEC — Monero is up 10% vs Zcash today and likely has another ~50% to go, since XMR offers privacy and will be quantum-resistant far before BTC.
prediction🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: mediumXMRMoneroZECZcash
So what: Concrete relative-value idea with a target (~50% more XMR/ZEC upside) on the privacy/quantum theme.
“I think that XMR is probably it's up 10% against ZEC today. I think it probably has like another 50% to go against ZEC.” ▶ 18:17
Specific pair trade: long IBIT / short MSTR (notional-neutral) — easiest to execute on Robinhood since collateralizing with on-chain BTC is harder; bets the MSTR NAV premium converts to a discount.
prediction🗣 Jonah Vanbergconviction: highIBITMSTRMicroStrategyRobinhood
So what: A flagged, trackable trade to capture MSTR premium compression vs spot BTC exposure.
“long IBIT short MSTR... on Robin Hood. That that's probably the trade” ▶ 19:46
Narrative rotation into equities: software, healthcare, robotics, energy 8
Crypto traders should apply their narrative/flow frameworks to equities: in a retail-driven bull, buy under-allocated or heavily-shorted sectors — which is exactly why software ripped 25% as shorts got blown out.
framework🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: highsoftwareequities
So what: A transferable edge: hunt heavily-shorted sectors for squeeze longs in a flow-driven market.
“look for the heavily shorted sectors and buy them because if retail is driving the market and flows are driving the market” ▶ 22:11
After software's 25% short-squeeze, shorting software is now an attractive entry because software businesses will be heavily impaired by AI over the next three years — a mega-trend.
contrarian🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: highsoftwareAI
So what: Use the squeeze as a higher-level entry to short a structurally AI-disrupted sector.
“shorting software here, that's a mega trend... over the next 3 years, software businesses are going to be heavily impacted by AI” ▶ 22:47
Worldcoin, up 35% today, is likely a short entry on the same logic of fading parabolic narrative pops.
prediction🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: mediumWorldcoinWorld Coin
So what: A specific short candidate flagged off an extreme one-day move.
“World Coin is probably an entry right now because it's up 35% today. Maybe you want to start building a short there.” ▶ 23:13
Healthcare/biotech is the most overlooked, underperforming sector that benefits massively from AI (drug development), making it Avi's next research focus — e.g. Novo Nordisk down ~44% in 2025 to a 4-year-low PE of ~14.
thesis🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: mediumhealthcarebiotechNovo NordiskEli LillyAI
So what: Rotation candidate: beaten-down pharma with AI tailwinds and cheap multiples.
“healthcare actually benefits massively from AI. Drug development benefits massively from AI.” ▶ 31:26
Health insurers are an underappreciated AI winner: as new drugs (e.g. a just-announced cholesterol cure) cut long-term care costs, the cost of servicing long-dated contracts falls — e.g. Oscar Health is down 50% from 2025 highs despite guiding to large 2026 growth.
thesis🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: mediumOscar HealthUnitedHealthinsuranceAI
So what: Second-order AI play: long beaten-down managed-care insurers on falling future claims costs.
“insurance companies I think are actually going to be huge winners of the AI revolution specifically because as health care outcomes improve cost comes down” ▶ 32:12
Robotics is set to be a 'massive mega bubble' with almost no listed access today except Robbo Strategy (a stock, not a token); Avi is scaling into robotics and space on the next drawdown.
prediction🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: mediumroboticsRobbo Strategyspace
So what: Early positioning in a thinly-accessible bubble theme; accumulate on dips.
“I do think robotics is going to be a pretty massive mega bubble and there kind of no ways to get access to it right now except for Robbo strategy” ▶ 47:06
On the next market scare Avi plans to scale into space and robotics, buy Mag 7 (calling them screaming buys if they fall another 20%), buy less memory and more energy, and buy less Intel than he did during the Iran war.
thesis🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: highMag 7Intelenergyroboticsspacememory
So what: A concrete dip-buying shopping list and rotation (out of memory, into energy/robotics/space).
“I'm scaling into robotics and I'm probably buying less memory and more energy” ▶ 47:44
Uranium is a long-term energy thesis; after distribution, the level to 'load the boat' again is ~42.
prediction🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: mediumuraniumenergy
So what: A defined buy level for uranium on the energy/AI-power demand theme.
“if we if we get down to a 42 level, that's, you know, load load the boat there again” ▶ 40:27
Macro crosscurrents: AI supply wall, Iran/oil, the Fed 6
Up to ~$3 trillion of new AI 'supply' is about to hit the market when OpenAI and Anthropic come public, which will definitionally dampen the rally because part of today's bid is just people wanting AI exposure they currently can't access directly.
causal🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: highOpenAIAnthropicAIMag 7IPO
So what: A coming supply event is a structural headwind/top risk; expect a slowdown around those IPOs, possibly after one final push.
“we're about to have once open AAI and Anthropic come on, maybe up to $3 trillion of additional supply for the AI thesis that comes on the market” ▶ 38:16
The 'market tops on a massive AI supply event' view is consensus among smart allocators but NOT priced or understood by the retail majority who just want AI exposure — so the supply effect can still bite.
contrarian🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: mediumAIOpenAIAnthropic
So what: The non-consensus retail blind spot means the supply shock retains downside power despite being 'known'.
“while that may be true among smart traders and allocators, I don't think that's true among the vast majority of people that are trading in this market” ▶ 38:05
Oil is rising again on Iran escalation (Iranian drones damaged Kuwait's airport, fresh US strikes on Qeshm Island, talks breaking down), a threat vector markets have largely forgotten and which is more dangerous now than in April given the weaker, weak-hand market.
thesis🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: highoilIranIsraelLebanoncrude
So what: Re-escalation risk is underpriced; an oil spike into a fragile market is the key tail risk to hedge.
“the world of markets has forgotten about Iran but that's another that's another threat vector” ▶ 40:53
Jonah's biggest position is the bet that the world will not 'stock out' of oil into a depression; he concedes his timing on the war's duration has been terrible but the expression of the trade has been fantastic as equities melted up.
thesis🗣 Jonah Vanbergconviction: highoilcrudeequitiesIran
So what: Conviction that no global oil stock-out occurs underwrites his equity-bullish stance; the risk is a $200 crude scenario.
“we are not going to stock out of oil globally and allow this to persist into a you know dire economic depression scenario” ▶ 42:52
An actual global oil stock-out sending crude to ~$200 would crash equities 30-40% — 'sayonara mag 7, sayonara Dell, sayonara memory' — because oil sits far below AI tokens on the hierarchy of needs.
prediction🗣 Jonah Vanbergconviction: highoilcrudeMag 7Dellmemory
So what: Frames the asymmetric downside: the AI trade is conditional on cheap energy; an oil shock dominates everything.
“you can bet your boots that the stock market will trade down 30 40% if there's like an actual global oil stock out and the price of a barrel of crude goes to 200 bucks” ▶ 43:21
Trump publicly threatening Fed-pick Kevin Warsh ('you better do a good job'), plus a reportedly tense Trump-Netanyahu call, add political/policy uncertainty that compounds inflation and consumption data as rally headwinds.
data_point🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: mediumTrumpKevin WarshFederal ReserveNetanyahuinflation
So what: Fed-independence and geopolitical noise are additional reasons to expect a near-term slowdown.
“Trump already attacking Kevin Worsh... He's like, "You better you better do a good job."” ▶ 38:47
Trading frameworks: holder analysis, lead-lag, and AI-as-tool 6
The single most important daily exercise is determining who holds each asset and why: ideal longs are those held by deep-value investors who won't sell even on a 5x but that will eventually hit a narrative — exactly how memory played out.
framework🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: highmemorydeep value
So what: A reusable selection rule: prefer assets with strong-hand ownership and a latent narrative catalyst.
“the optimal way to to to to trade is to hold is to hold an asset that you think is going to hit a narrative at some point in the future but right now is being held by deep value investors” ▶ 46:27
Actionable AI workflow: find the physical/fundamental leading indicator (e.g. a DDR4 index) for the sector you care about, map its lead-lag to the target basket, and sanity-check with your own brain to avoid overfitting.
framework🗣 Jonah Vanbergconviction: highAIDDR4DRAM ExchangeBloomberg
So what: A repeatable research process to surface mispriced sector plays using commodity lead indicators.
“I would try to find the commodity that represents the leading indicator for the sector or the asset or the basket of assets that you care about” ▶ 34:46
AI is far more useful when connected to proprietary/live data than when reasoning from training data alone — the claim is you source ~10x as many opportunities per hour with data-connected AI.
data_point🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: highAI1000x terminalClaudeChatGPT
So what: Edge increasingly comes from data-connected AI tooling for idea sourcing and backtesting.
“if your AI is connected to proprietary data sources, you source 10 times as many opportunities per hour” ▶ 30:32
Beware 'step 1: AI, step 2: ?, step 3: profit' — an unstructured prompt about a euphoric thesis will get the AI to self-reinforce your bias; you must guide it and verify the data yourself.
framework🗣 Jonah Vanbergconviction: highAI
So what: Risk-management caution on AI-driven research: structure prompts and human-check outputs to avoid confirmation bias.
“an unstructured prompt about, you know, a euphoric future in pharma stocks, the AI will just self-reinforce your ideas and tell you to buy the ball” ▶ 33:41
Crypto remains the most retail-democratized, inefficient market — retail can touch instruments TradFi desks cannot — so the edge advice is to 'delete the word crypto', call yourself a trader, and extend the same skills to equities.
thesis🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: highcryptoequitiesTradFi
So what: Identity/skill reframing: crypto-trained narrative and flow skills transfer to equities where retail now has an edge.
“delete the word crypto and just just branch out in other things where you can where you can be a retail trader” ▶ 26:43
Crypto-protocol employees affiliated with fading L1s (Hedera/HBAR, Polkadot, Cardano) should quit and pivot to AI or to crypto products users actually want, because the asset class has bifurcated against the old majors.
contrarian🗣 Avi Felmanconviction: highHBARHederaPolkadotCardanoAI
So what: Career/capital signal: legacy L1 protocols are viewed as structurally losing; rotate human and financial capital out.
“If you work in crypto, especially if you work for a protocol like, let's just say, HAR, Polka Dot, Cardano... quit your job.” ▶ 0:34

Peter H. Diamandis 3 episode(s) · 131 insights

The Vatican’s Stance on AI, The Mass Tech Layoffs, and China’s 2027 Moon Flyby | EP #259108 min46 insights2026-05-30
▶ Watch on YouTube
Diamandis and co-hosts (Salim Ismail, Dave Blundin, Alex Wissner-Gross) survey the AI frontier-lab race (OpenAI/Anthropic/Google/xAI), exploding token demand and revenue, the shift from layoffs to a hiring freeze plus solopreneur boom, and a possible Tesla–SpaceX merger — arguing the four-horse AI race lifts all incumbents while compute control becomes the decisive moat.
Frontier-lab competitive dynamics & talent · 8AI revenue, token economics & enterprise spend · 7Labor market: hiring freeze, not apocalypse · 7Solopreneur boom & small-team org model · 5AI forecasting & decision automation · 3AI regulation & the speed-over-safety pivot · 4Space economy: SpaceX, Starlink & a possible Tesla merger · 8Mega-cap framing & macro themes · 4
Frontier-lab competitive dynamics & talent 8
On the new DeepSWE coding benchmark, GPT-5.5 scored 70% vs Claude Opus 4.7 at 54%, with Gemini, Kimi and DeepSeek all below 32% — a sharp capability cliff between the top two labs and everyone else.
data_point🗣 host (Peter Diamandis)conviction: highOpenAIGPT-5.5AnthropicClaude Opus 4.7Google GeminiKimiDeepSeekData Curve
So what: Coding capability is now the primary competitive axis among labs; the GPT-5.5 lead and the >32% cliff signal a widening two-horse frontier with weak monetizable differentiation for laggards.
“GPT 5.5 scored 70%... Claude Opus 4.7 scored 54%. And then there's everybody else. It's a massive cliff” ▶ 0:25
Anthropic just hired Shane Longpre (MIT) and Tobin South (Stanford), and the hosts argue OpenAI has lost mojo amid lawsuits and defections, giving Anthropic momentum in the talent war despite OpenAI's recent raise.
thesis🗣 guest (Dave Blundin, investor)conviction: highAnthropicOpenAIDario AmodeiShane LongpreTobin South
So what: Top researchers gravitating to Anthropic (perceived safety/positioning edge) is a leading indicator of where frontier capability — and enterprise revenue — accrues next.
“Shane Long Pre from MIT and Tobin South from Stanford just joined Anthropic... OpenAI has lost a lot of its mojo in the lawsuit, in the defection of a lot of key people” ▶ 30:37
Opus 4.7 burns roughly twice as many tokens as GPT-5.5 to reach the same or slightly worse result, making Anthropic's model ~2x more expensive per task — yet that verbosity is itself inflating Anthropic's revenue per user.
data_point🗣 guest (Dave Blundin, investor)conviction: highAnthropicClaude Opus 4.7OpenAIGPT-5.5
So what: Token-verbosity is a hidden pricing lever: Anthropic can effectively double revenue-per-user by model behavior alone, but it raises enterprise cost-per-task and a switching incentive to OpenAI.
“it burns twice as many tokens as GPT 5.5... it's twice as expensive to get to the same result” ▶ 32:58
The hosts frame the frontier as a 'four-horse race' (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, xAI) that incumbents are unlikely to lose to a dark horse, with the most likely path for newcomers being a perfect algorithm or world-models breakthrough that then reduces to who controls the most compute.
framework🗣 guest (Alex Wissner-Gross, polymath)conviction: mediumOpenAIAnthropicGoogle DeepMindxAINoam BrownCerebrasNvidia
So what: If model weights matter less and inference/reasoning compute matters more, the investable edge shifts down the stack to compute owners and challenger chips (e.g., Cerebras) rather than model IP.
“maybe the model weights don't matter that much anymore. Maybe it's all about the compute for reasoning and inference time” ▶ 50:22
Rather than a single winner, the hosts argue all major frontier players keep getting bigger because AI is an expanding market (the future of all humanity), not a fixed-share market like phones or laptops — 'a rising tsunami lifts all boats.'
contrarian🗣 guest (Dave Blundin, investor)conviction: highOpenAIAnthropicGooglexAI
So what: Argues against picking one AI winner; supports a basket/index exposure to all frontier incumbents rather than a concentrated bet.
“it's very likely that all players that are in the middle of this get bigger and bigger and bigger. So, we're just debating who's going to get the biggest of the big” ▶ 51:21
Anthropic raised enterprise prices while OpenAI offers tiered pricing and Google's Gemini Flash undercuts rivals by 50–80%, setting up an explicit performance-vs-cost segmentation across the frontier.
data_point🗣 host (Peter Diamandis)conviction: mediumAnthropicOpenAIGoogleGemini Flash
So what: Pricing is diverging by strategy — Anthropic premium, Google cost-leader — so margin and share will hinge on which segment (premium task quality vs commodity volume) grows fastest.
“Gemini flash undercut everyone by 50 to 80%” ▶ 47:14
Coding benchmarks like DeepSWE will saturate within months because models benchmax; durable spread now requires hand-coded, held-out evals, implying benchmark-based moats are short-lived.
prediction🗣 guest (Alex Wissner-Gross, polymath)conviction: highData CurveDeepSWEOpenAIAnthropic
So what: Investors should discount any current benchmark lead as transient; capability gaps between top labs compress fast, weakening single-model differentiation theses.
“This too will saturate... probably in the next few months” ▶ 0:29
Ilya Sutskever's venture is reportedly building a proprietary-trading hedge fund (per public reporting), which the hosts read as a cash-flow engine to fund compute for recursive self-improvement — not necessarily abandoning safe superintelligence.
anecdote🗣 guest (Alex Wissner-Gross, polymath)conviction: mediumIlya SutskeverLeopold AschenbrennerSafe Superintelligence
So what: An AI lab self-funding via prop trading signals a new model where capital generation and compute accumulation, not product revenue, bootstrap frontier R&D — relevant to how SSI/Leopold-style entities scale.
“any machine that quietly generates huge amounts of profit can then be used to buy lots and lots of compute which can then be used for self-improvement” ▶ 49:11
AI revenue, token economics & enterprise spend 7
OpenAI did $5.7B in a single quarter, ChatGPT reached ~$95M... (800M+) weekly active users — more than Instagram — and its Codex coding agent has 2M users and is becoming a real revenue engine.
data_point🗣 host (Peter Diamandis)conviction: highOpenAIChatGPTCodexInstagram
So what: OpenAI's rapid pivot from consumer to coding/enterprise revenue (Codex at 2M users) validates coding as the near-term monetization wedge for frontier labs.
“OpenAI just did $5.7 billion in a single quarter... Their coding agent, Codeex, has 2 million users” ▶ 42:12
Joseph Jacks (OSS Capital) projects Anthropic could surpass Alphabet's total revenue by 2028 and grow from ~$9B to potentially $2 trillion by 2030 — which the hosts call the fastest wealth creation in human history.
prediction🗣 host (Peter Diamandis)conviction: mediumAnthropicAlphabetJoseph JacksOSS Capital
So what: Even directionally, this implies an Anthropic IPO/valuation re-rate of historic scale; sets an anchor for how aggressively private AI valuations may be marked.
“Anthropic could surpass Alphabet's total revenue by 2028... from $9 billion in revenue to potentially two trillion by 2030” ▶ 42:47
Jevons paradox in action: AI token prices fell ~75% since late 2024 (from ~$2 to ~$0.50 per million tokens) while monthly demand exploded from near-zero to ~25 trillion tokens, with demand understated because capacity is sold out.
framework🗣 host (Peter Diamandis)conviction: highAI computeNvidia
So what: Falling unit cost is expanding, not shrinking, total AI spend — a structurally bullish demand signal for compute/inference providers and a reason capacity (GPUs) stays the binding constraint.
“the price of AI tokens has dropped 75%... we've gone basically from 0 to 25 trillion tokens per month” ▶ 38:11
Gartner projects inference on a trillion-parameter LLM will cost 90% less by 2030 than in 2025, even as model capability rises orders of magnitude.
data_point🗣 host (Peter Diamandis)conviction: mediumGartnerAI inference
So what: Steep inference cost decline supports the Jevons demand thesis and makes per-token pricing a poor long-term valuation metric for AI economics.
“inference on a trillion uh parameter LLM will cost 90% less uh by 2030 than it did in 2025” ▶ 41:28
Because AI subscriptions are an 'amorphous' product, labs can raise revenue instantly (more verbose models) or throttle token-generation rate to lower delivered value and raise margins — so enterprises should lock multi-year compute contracts now before capacity sells out.
thesis🗣 guest (Dave Blundin, investor)conviction: highAnthropicOpenAIenterprise compute
So what: Labs hold a unilateral pricing/throttling lever over enterprise buyers; this is an actionable procurement signal (reserve compute, fixed contracts) and a margin tailwind for the labs.
“companies should be signing long-term contracts... You got to figure out your 5year strategy now before everything is sold out” ▶ 44:09
The deeper technical shift is from generalist reasoning agents to generalist tool-using/codegen coding agents, with Codex likely becoming OpenAI's mainline baseline model/agent offering.
thesis🗣 guest (Alex Wissner-Gross, polymath)conviction: mediumOpenAICodex
So what: If coding agents become the default product surface, evaluate AI vendors on agentic tool-use reliability rather than raw chat quality.
“codeex I I think codeex becomes probably the mainline agent/model that openai offers... that becomes the new baseline” ▶ 44:47
In software, generation is becoming abundant/commodity (anyone can build at near-zero cost) so the moat shifts to taste, domain expertise and verification of generated output — though the hosts doubt verification is a durable long-term edge.
framework🗣 guest (Salim Ismail / Alex Wissner-Gross)conviction: mediumsoftware industry
So what: Devalues pure code-production businesses; favors firms whose edge is proprietary domain data, design taste, or distribution rather than the ability to write software.
“software is becoming a commodity... your taste as a creator is becoming the moat” ▶ 33:36
Labor market: hiring freeze, not apocalypse 7
~134,000 tech workers were laid off in the first 5 months of the year (worst March for tech layoffs since the pandemic), and 99% of CEOs expect AI-driven layoffs within two years per Mercer's global talent trends report.
data_point🗣 host (Peter Diamandis)conviction: highMercertech sectorCloudflareMeta
So what: Quantifies the layoff wave and near-universal CEO expectation of more — a headline risk for labor-sensitive sectors even if AI is partly a scapegoat.
“we've had 143 134,000 tech workers have been laid off... 99% of CEOs expect AIdriven layoffs in the next two years” ▶ 59:08
Nvidia's Jensen Huang calls AI-blamed layoffs a 'lazy narrative,' arguing CEOs blame AI to sound smart while the underlying driver is a hiring freeze and bad strategy, not mass AI displacement.
contrarian🗣 host (Peter Diamandis)conviction: highNvidiaJensen Huang
So what: Reframes the AI-jobs narrative: the labor signal is frozen hiring (esp. new grads), not broad automation — relevant for reading macro employment data and avoiding overstating AI displacement.
“calling this a lazy narrative. He says, CEOs are uh blaming AI just to sound smart” ▶ 59:30
Sam Altman walked back his white-collar 'job apocalypse' warning, now saying he doesn't think there will be that kind of apocalypse — a reversal the hosts tie to OpenAI and Anthropic both heading toward trillion-dollar IPOs.
causal🗣 host (Peter Diamandis)conviction: mediumOpenAISam AltmanAnthropicDario Amodei
So what: Pre-IPO labs have incentive to soften disruption rhetoric to limit regulatory/public backlash; read leadership messaging on job loss as partly narrative-managed ahead of listings.
“the CEO of OpenAI who spent last year warning about the mass white collar displacement now says... I don't think we're going to have the kind of job apocalypse” ▶ 1:00:04
The Dallas Fed (Jan report) found employment decline correlated with AI exposure only among younger workers; older workers in high-exposure jobs showed no significant decline — consistent with a hiring freeze hitting new grads, not mass layoffs.
data_point🗣 guest (Alex Wissner-Gross, polymath)conviction: highDallas Fedlabor marketcollege graduates
So what: The labor pain concentrates at entry-level/new-grad hiring; watch youth unemployment and graduate hiring as the truest near-term AI labor indicator.
“employment de uh decline correlated with AI exposure only in the younger workers. Older workers... showed no significant decline” ▶ 1:02:44
Blundin says AI labs will now actively try to PREVENT mass job loss by focusing constrained GPUs on coding and greenfield value creation rather than automating artists/every job away — making the 2026–2030 disruption window deliberately narrow.
thesis🗣 guest (Dave Blundin, investor)conviction: mediumAI labsGPU supply
So what: If GPU scarcity plus deliberate lab choices steer automation toward greenfield rather than displacement, the feared 2026–2030 'pitchforks' scenario may be softer than consensus fears.
“the AI companies with their massive new fundings are going to actually actively try to prevent job loss” ▶ 1:01:34
At Vesemark (where Blundin is chairman), ~400 white-collar workers doing account reconciliation/back-office: a year ago he expected ~half the jobs to go; now automation flows to margin and they're keeping everyone — but the real effect is no new hiring.
anecdote🗣 guest (Dave Blundin, investor/chairman)conviction: highVesemarkback-office automation
So what: Ground-truth from an operator: AI shows up as expanding margins and a hiring freeze rather than headcount cuts — a template for how white-collar AI adoption hits P&Ls.
“Now we're having no trouble automating things, but it's all becoming margin and we're keeping everybody... where it's really really hitting everyone is in no new hiring” ▶ 1:02:26
College computer-science enrollment has peaked and is declining while mechanical/biological engineering enrollment is skyrocketing, as students retool toward physical fields tied to data-center buildout and AI-driven medical research.
data_point🗣 guest (Dave Blundin, investor)conviction: mediumhigher educationengineeringdata centers
So what: A leading talent-supply signal favoring physical/infrastructure engineering over software — supportive of data-center construction and hard-tech labor demand.
“computer science peaked and is now coming down... engineering as a whole... mechanical and biological is still skyrocketing” ▶ 1:05:36
Solopreneur boom & small-team org model 5
Per a16z data, AI solo founders doubled last quarter from ~1,500 to ~3,000 (up from near-zero three years ago) and non-AI solo founders topped ~5,000 — AI tooling is enabling single-person company formation at scale.
data_point🗣 host (Peter Diamandis)conviction: higha16zAndreessen Horowitzstartups
So what: A structural surge in solo/AI-enabled company formation reshapes where new value (and jobs) originate; relevant to seed-stage venture deployment and SMB software demand.
“AI solar found solo founders... has doubled in the last quarter from 1500 to 3,000” ▶ 1:06:22
Ismail cites U.S. startup formation up ~25% year-over-year, the U.S. now has ~6x more startups than Europe, and notes 100% of net new jobs over the past 50 years came from startups/early-stage firms while big firms shed headcount.
data_point🗣 guest (Salim Ismail)conviction: highU.S. economyEuropestartupsOpenEXO
So what: Reinforces a U.S.-vs-Europe entrepreneurship gap and locates job creation in new-firm formation — bullish on U.S. early-stage ecosystem vs European incumbents.
“the US now has six times more startups in Europe... 100% of new jobs have come from startups and early stage companies” ▶ 1:03:43
OpenEXO's organizational-singularity estimate: companies can be run with ~20% of the prior headcount on average, while ~5–6x more companies get created — a net restructuring of the firm toward many tiny, highly-connected units.
prediction🗣 guest (Salim Ismail)conviction: mediumOpenEXOAnthropicClaude
So what: If true, enterprise software and services TAM fragments toward many micro-firms; favors tools priced per-company/seat-light and self-serve over large-enterprise sales motions.
“you should be able to run a company about 20% of the people on average than you did before. But we're going to create five or six times more companies” ▶ 1:04:30
75% of successful companies now come through an incubator/accelerator (vs ~6% when Blundin started investing) — 'solo' founders are legally single-person but embedded in dense accelerator ecosystems.
data_point🗣 guest (Dave Blundin, investor)conviction: mediumacceleratorsincubatorsventure capital
So what: Accelerator participation has become the dominant path to startup success — a screening signal for venture sourcing and for where deal flow concentrates.
“if you look at the data on successful companies 75% of them now are coming through some kind of an incubator or accelerator program” ▶ 1:09:09
Ismail argues coordination overhead dooms large firms ('easier to build a feature than to have the meeting about it'); big companies devolve into platforms, then ecosystems, with small teams structurally outperforming — universities and healthcare cited as bloated-admin examples.
framework🗣 guest (Salim Ismail)conviction: highlarge enterprisesuniversitieshealthcare industry
So what: Thesis that bureaucratic incumbents (esp. admin-heavy universities, healthcare) face an 'intelligence asteroid'; a short-the-bloat / long-small-teams structural view.
“The big dinosaur the category of big companies will probably evaporate... small teams will always outperform big teams” ▶ 1:08:21
AI forecasting & decision automation 3
DeepMind's 'Green Tree' system reached parity with human super-forecasters (top 2% predictors, per Tetlock ~30% more accurate than CIA analysts with classified intel) on March 15th — with major implications for finance, insurance and governance.
data_point🗣 host (Peter Diamandis)conviction: highGoogle DeepMindGreen TreePhilip TetlockForecastBench
So what: Machine super-forecasting at human-best level threatens the alpha of discretionary hedge funds and the actuarial basis of insurance once outcomes become predictable.
“AI hit parody with the super forecasters for the first time... The implications for finance, insurance, and governance are massive” ▶ 52:02
Blundin's edge thesis: the real unlock is LLMs quantifying unstructured data (research reports), giving AI forecasters access to ~10^4–10^6x more information than any human stock picker — so even if less brilliant per-domain, capacity wins; traditional hedge funds and fixed-outcome insurance models break.
thesis🗣 guest (Dave Blundin, investor)conviction: highhedge fundsinsuranceWellingtonFidelity
So what: Directly bearish on discretionary asset managers (Wellington/Fidelity-style) and on insurers whose models assume unpredictability; bullish on AI-native forecasting/quant.
“the ability to assimilate... a million times more information than any human stock picker... it's going to outperform” ▶ 53:26
Despite AI forecasting parity, the durable edge for the next 1–5 years is human-plus-many-agents: Blundin runs ~170 agents that still make 'really stupid' choices, so human-in-the-loop oversight remains critical (chess analogy: centaur teams beat both).
framework🗣 guest (Dave Blundin, investor)conviction: highAI agentshuman-in-the-loop
So what: Argues the near-term winning operating model is hybrid human+agent workflows, not full autonomy — a practical signal for how to deploy agentic AI in investing/operations now.
“the human and the AI working together are far far better than either one by itself. And so there's a window here to take advantage of that” ▶ 54:58
AI regulation & the speed-over-safety pivot 4
A White House executive order to let government review AI models pre-release (technically voluntary) was killed hours before signing after Elon Musk, Zuckerberg and David Sacks called it 'doomer regulation' and a slippery slope to mandatory licensing; Trump pulled it.
data_point🗣 host (Peter Diamandis)conviction: highWhite HouseTrumpElon MuskMark ZuckerbergDavid Sacks
So what: The anti-regulation coalition's political win signals a durable light-touch U.S. AI regime — reducing compliance overhang for frontier labs and favoring fast model release cadence.
“they had a signing ceremony all planned... and it got killed like hours before it was supposed to happen” ▶ 21:49
The US–China frontier-model gap is estimated at roughly 3–8 months, so a proposed 90-day pre-release government review equals potentially the entire US lead — driving the 'speed over safety' choice in the AI race.
causal🗣 guest (Alex Wissner-Gross, polymath)conviction: highUnited StatesChinafrontier models
So what: Frames AI regulation as a national-security race metric; any US slowdown directly cedes the China gap, anchoring policy toward minimal friction.
≈ “estimates that they could be as close as 3 months and as far behind as 8 months... 90 days... that's the time difference potentially between the US and China” paraphrase ▶ 25:24
Model release cadence has compressed from ~2 years to ~1 year to ~1 month (Anthropic now increments monthly), so whatever administration is in office now is effectively the one governing AI's trajectory — making 2-year election cycles irrelevant to AI policy.
prediction🗣 guest (Alex Wissner-Gross, polymath)conviction: highAnthropicWhite House
So what: Monthly model cadence means regulatory and competitive timelines are measured in weeks; investors and policymakers should ignore election-cycle thinking for AI exposure.
“Anthropic increments now are once per month... And they're going to get shorter” ▶ 26:11
The hosts argue linear/committee regulation cannot keep pace with exponential AI ('software speed, not fax speed'); the durable model is adaptive governance — real-time audits, sandboxes, disclosure — and the industry prefers self-regulation, citing the Asilomar P1–P4 biosafety precedent (40 years, no major accident).
framework🗣 guest (Salim Ismail)conviction: highAI regulationAsilomarbiotech
So what: Predicts industry self-regulation and AI-assisted oversight over statutory bodies — relevant to which compliance vendors and governance frameworks gain traction.
“You cannot have uh linear regulation of an exponential technology... You need guardrails that move at software speed, not committees that... move at like fax speed” ▶ 25:01
Space economy: SpaceX, Starlink & a possible Tesla merger 8
SpaceX flew Starship V3 (new rocket, Raptor 3 engines at 250–280 tons thrust, +20% vs prior) carrying ~97,000 lbs to near orbit — nearly double the Space Shuttle — and deployed real Starlink prototypes ('Dodger Dog' v3 satellites) on its first flight, treating rockets like iterative software.
data_point🗣 host (Peter Diamandis)conviction: highSpaceXStarship V3Raptor 3Starlink
So what: Starship's rapid iteration and payload leap underpin the launch-cost collapse thesis that drives the entire orbital-compute and satellite-economy investment case.
“It carried 97,000 lbs to near Earth orbit... that almost doubled the space shuttle carrying capacity” ▶ 1:19:20
Diamandis puts the probability of a Tesla–SpaceX merger within a year at ~100% (vs Kalshi prediction market ~50/50), envisioning a ~$4T entity initially that he believes could become the first $10T and reach ~$100T in five years; full disclosure he's a SpaceX/xAI investor.
prediction🗣 host (Peter Diamandis)conviction: highTeslaSpaceXxAIKalshiOptimus
So what: A Tesla–SpaceX combination would create a mega-cap spanning EVs, energy, robots, launch and satellites; the host's conviction vs market 50/50 flags a potential mispricing/merger-arb thesis.
“showing a 50/50 odds that Tesla and SpaceX merge within the next year. I I personally put it at 100%” ▶ 1:24:39
Mechanics of the likely merger: SpaceX (where Musk holds ~85% voting via 10:1 super-voting shares) would acquire Tesla (where his shares are 1-vote, ~20%), rolling Tesla's one-vote shares into SpaceX so Musk retains ~60–80% control; the deal becomes more appetizing if Tesla's valuation falls relative to post-IPO SpaceX.
framework🗣 guest (Alex Wissner-Gross / Dave Blundin)conviction: mediumTeslaSpaceXElon Musk
So what: Spells out the governance arithmetic and a specific arbitrage: a falling Tesla price actually makes the merger easier, an unusual setup for merger-arb positioning.
“if Tesla's market value were to materially depreciate in the next year relative to SpaceX's post IPO valuation, that might... make a SpaceX acquisition of Tesla good deal more appetizing” ▶ 1:27:48
SpaceX's 'Stargaze' program leverages Starlink's onboard cameras across altitudes to image Earth and track orbital debris/objects and share that data — which Wissner-Gross argues could have a more dramatic civilizational impact than Starlink connectivity itself.
thesis🗣 guest (Alex Wissner-Gross, polymath)conviction: mediumSpaceXStarlinkStargaze
So what: An under-appreciated SpaceX data/surveillance asset (space situational awareness + Earth imaging) that could open new markets beyond bandwidth — worth watching as an option on the constellation.
“it would be an ironic, but maybe not super surprising outcome if Stargaze ends up having a more dramatic impact on civilization than even just Starlink connectivity” ▶ 1:21:32
Diamandis cites SpaceX's Philip (private missions) that Starship orbital flights will be offered at ~$20M (vs $20M–$75M historical Soyuz seats) with 1,000 m^3 of volume (~3x the ISS habitable volume) — and frames a long-run path toward radically cheaper access to orbit.
data_point🗣 host (Peter Diamandis)conviction: mediumSpaceXStarshipSoyuzSpace Adventures
So what: Concrete near-term human-spaceflight pricing ($20M/flight) defines the addressable private-astronaut market and the cost curve enabling a broader orbital economy.
“on the Starship... he's going to be offering flights to orbit... The price is going to come down to 20 million a flight” ▶ 1:33:49
Starlink announced gigabit lunar connectivity plans (radio plus laser links), and the hosts see an emerging interplanetary-internet 'fabric' of orbital compute/bandwidth nodes in LEO, cislunar and L1/L2 — Wissner-Gross also cites Starlink AI compute in sun-synchronous orbit.
thesis🗣 guest (Alex Wissner-Gross, polymath)conviction: mediumStarlinkSpaceXorbital computeVint Cerf
So what: Orbital/space-based compute and an interplanetary network are moving from concept to roadmap — a long-dated theme tying space buildout to the AI compute race (cf. Musk's 'compute in space' bet).
“It starts to create the beginnings of a fabric of an interplanetary internet” ▶ 1:37:00
China is expected to send a crewed lunar flyby in 2027 (per NASA administrator Jared Isaacman), ending US exclusivity in human lunar spaceflight, with the US targeting a lunar South Pole landing in 2028 — a competitive dynamic the hosts say helps keep NASA funded.
data_point🗣 host (Peter Diamandis)conviction: highChinaNASAJared IsaacmanArtemis
So what: A 2027 Chinese crewed flyby vs 2028 US South Pole landing sets a space-race catalyst calendar that supports sustained NASA/Artemis budget and contractor demand.
“The next time the world tunes in to watch astronauts fly around the moon... they will be tyonauts” ▶ 1:38:57
Chun Wang — co-founder of a major Chinese Bitcoin mining pool controlling ~11% of Bitcoin's hash rate — has booked the first private SpaceX Starship Mars flyby (a ~2-year mission) after commanding the polar Fram2 mission.
anecdote🗣 host (Peter Diamandis)conviction: mediumSpaceXChun WangBitcoinMars
So what: Crypto-derived wealth is now funding pioneering private interplanetary missions, illustrating new buyer pools for high-end SpaceX flights and the early commercial Mars market.
“Cryptobillionaire books SpaceX first private Mars flyby... controls 11% of Bitcoin's hash rate” ▶ 1:30:32
Mega-cap framing & macro themes 4
Wissner-Gross coins 'Magna Mopa/Mobsta' for the 11 companies closest to the AI singularity's 'innermost loop': Microsoft, Amazon, Google, Nvidia, Apple, Meta, OpenAI, Broadcom, SpaceX, Tesla, Anthropic — with OpenAI, SpaceX and Anthropic all expected to IPO over the coming months.
framework🗣 guest (Alex Wissner-Gross, polymath)conviction: mediumMicrosoftAmazonGoogleNvidiaAppleMetaOpenAIBroadcomSpaceXTeslaAnthropic
So what: An explicit basket of the 11 'innermost loop' AI/compute/space/autonomy mega-caps — a candidate thematic index (the hosts note people are trying to build ETFs off it).
“Magna Mopa are the 11 companies... Microsoft, Amazon, Google... Nvidia... Apple, Meta, OpenAI, Broadcom, SpaceX, Tesla, Anthropic” ▶ 1:42:49
The hosts flag OpenAI, SpaceX and Anthropic all heading toward IPOs over the next few months — a concentrated near-term pipeline of trillion-dollar-scale AI/space listings.
prediction🗣 guest (Alex Wissner-Gross, polymath)conviction: mediumOpenAISpaceXAnthropic
