Macro Voices · Erik Townsend 3 episode(s) · 111 insights

MacroVoices #534 Dr. Pippa Malmgren: Superpower War or Superpower Hug?110 min48 insights2026-05-28
▶ Watch on YouTube
With the Strait of Hormuz effectively closed for ~3 months over Iran's 441 kg of 60%-enriched uranium, Malmgren frames the standoff as one cell of a US-China-Russia 'Rubik's cube' grand bargain and stays optimistic (energy shock accelerates a molecules-to-atoms nuclear transition), while Bianco and Townsend argue there is no forcing mechanism, drones have neutralized naval power, and $150-200 oil is plausible within 60 days if the strait stays shut.
Hormuz / Iran nuclear standoff and the deal dynamics · 6Superpower grand bargain (US-China-Russia Rubik's cube) · 7Oil supply, demand destruction and price targets · 6Why markets are calm — and why that may be wrong · 4Modern warfare: cheap drones neutralize naval power · 3Molecules to atoms — the nuclear/energy transition trade · 7AI, robotics and the cost-collapse economy · 9Cross-asset positioning: FX, gold, rates, equities · 6
Hormuz / Iran nuclear standoff and the deal dynamics 6
As of late May 2026 the Strait of Hormuz has been effectively closed for ~3 months, running below 10% of normal traffic, with the dispute centered on Iran's refusal to surrender its 60%-enriched uranium.
data_point🗣 Erik Townsend (host)conviction: highStrait of HormuzIrancrude oiluranium
So what: A multi-month physical supply chokepoint is the dominant macro tail risk; traders should watch traffic-volume data, not headline 'deal imminent' noise.
“the Straight of Hormuz is still effectively closed. A few dozen ships got through this week, but we're still well below 10% of normal traffic levels.” ▶ 0:43
Neither Trump nor Iran has moved one inch on the central issue: Iran's Supreme Leader reiterated last Thursday the 60% uranium cannot leave Iran under any circumstance, so 'hours away from a deal' talk is propaganda.
contrarian🗣 Erik Townsend (host)conviction: highIranTrumpuraniumIAEA
So what: The 'imminent deal' narrative driving oil down ~10% is unsupported; a long-vol or buy-the-dip-oil stance is favored over fading the risk.
“All of this talk about how we're just hours away from a deal... It's all a bunch of It's propaganda. It's not real.” ▶ 1:37:23
Iran is a 'threshold state' holding 441 kg of 60%-enriched uranium (enough for up to 9-10 bombs), having done ~99% of the separative work; going from 60% to 90% would take only a few weeks IF operable centrifuges survived Operation Midnight Hammer.
framework🗣 Erik Townsend (host)conviction: highIranuraniumOperation Midnight HammerTulsi Gabbard
So what: Reframes the binary 'do they have a weapon' debate: the enriched material, not weaponization, is the binding constraint that keeps the conflict unresolved.
“Iran has done 99% of the enrichment activity necessary... getting it from 60% to 90% would only take a few weeks with operating centrifuges.” ▶ 1:33:21
Malmgren argues the Iran conflict is not a state-to-state negotiation but a 'price negotiation' with leaderless IRGC stragglers competing to be the one who shakes hands with the US, making a deal more likely and incentivizing them to hold out for a higher bid.
thesis🗣 Dr. Pippa Malmgren (guest, geopolitical economist)conviction: highIranIRGCTrump
So what: If correct, resolution comes via cash buyouts plus episodic military threats rather than formal diplomacy — a messier, headline-driven path that sustains oil volatility.
“we're not actually in a state negotiation anymore. We're in a price negotiation.” ▶ 23:48
Malmgren's contrarian read: Trump wants physical possession of the uranium less to stop a bomb than to forensically prove its origin is American — material she says he believes was supplied to Iran under Clinton/Obama/Biden-era deals.
contrarian🗣 Dr. Pippa Malmgren (guest)conviction: mediumTrumpuraniumObamaClintonBidenUSAID
So what: Implies the US may not accept any down-blending compromise and insists on custody, lengthening the standoff and the oil supply risk.
“the real reason the president wants the uranium is because he wants to prove definitively the origin of that uranium... his belief is that it is American” ▶ 13:57
Bianco says physically retrieving Iran's uranium could take until next year because it is buried under hundreds of feet of rock and would first require building a landing strip for heavy excavation equipment — so an MOU changes nothing.
causal🗣 Jim Bianco (guest, Bianco Research founder)conviction: highIranuraniumBianco Research
So what: Removes the 'quick handover' resolution path markets are pricing; supports a structurally longer crisis and higher-for-longer oil.
“the first step in getting the nuclear material might very well be to send in the engineers to build a landing strip... that might not be till next year.” ▶ 1:12:36
Superpower grand bargain (US-China-Russia Rubik's cube) 7
Malmgren's master framework: the US, China and Russia are negotiating all outstanding geopolitical issues simultaneously as one 'Rubik's cube' — Taiwan, Cuba, Venezuela, Iran and Ukraine cannot be solved independently.
framework🗣 Dr. Pippa Malmgren (guest)conviction: highUSChinaRussiaTaiwanCubaVenezuelaIranUkraine
So what: Investors should price geopolitical events as linked nodes in a single negotiation, not isolated shocks — a deal in one theater signals movement in others.
“this is a kind of Rubik's cube approach where you say you can't fix any one of them independently of the others.” ▶ 8:33
Malmgren frames the superpower choice as 'Star Wars vs Star Trek' — outright war is impossible given nuclear arsenals, so China is clearly signaling it prefers cooperation ('war with problems'), making de-escalation the base case.
thesis🗣 Dr. Pippa Malmgren (guest)conviction: highUSChina
So what: Underpins her risk-on, recession-skeptic stance; a credible cooperative endgame caps the geopolitical risk premium in equities.
“China is absolutely signaling that it prefers Star Trek. No question.” ▶ 9:55
The implicit US-China deal: the US accepts Taiwan is in China's sphere and Cuba/Venezuela in America's, conditioned on China economically liberalizing toward a 'Taiwan model' and shifting from 'made in China' to 'owned by China' brands.
thesis🗣 Dr. Pippa Malmgren (guest)conviction: highUSChinaTaiwanCubaVenezuela
So what: Implies reduced Taiwan-invasion tail risk and a US board vetting Chinese firms for market access — relevant for China brand/FDI and Taiwan-exposed semis.
“China needs to move from a world where everything's stamped made in China to a world where they're stamped owned by China.” ▶ 10:20
China flipped from Iran's sponsor to pressuring it because closing Hormuz hurt China most, so the US and China are now jointly setting reopening conditions — evidenced by the first transiting ships being Chinese oil vessels.
causal🗣 Dr. Pippa Malmgren (guest)conviction: highChinaIranStrait of HormuzXi JinpingTrump
So what: Chinese-flagged vessels getting priority transit is a tradable tell that a US-China side deal exists; watch vessel-flag composition of Hormuz traffic.
“notice that the first ships that came through are all Chinese oil carrying vessels.” ▶ 13:15
Malmgren claims Cuba is flipping: the CIA director flew to Havana the day Trump and Xi met, with the US 'dictating terms' (offering ~$100M cash to a fuel-starved Cuba) rather than doing diplomacy.
anecdote🗣 Dr. Pippa Malmgren (guest)conviction: mediumCubaCIATrumpXi JinpingRubio
So what: A Cuba/Venezuela realignment into the US sphere would reshape Western Hemisphere energy and sanctions flows (e.g., Venezuelan crude to China).
“Did you notice that the head of the CIA flew into Havana on the very day that Trump and Xi met?” ▶ 16:40
Russia, like China, is open to a swap and will prioritize Ukraine over Iran, so Iran is treated as a 'done deal' and Ukraine is the last and hardest piece of the Rubik's cube to resolve.
prediction🗣 Dr. Pippa Malmgren (guest)conviction: mediumRussiaUkraineIranChina
So what: Ukraine resolution is the key remaining catalyst; an Iran 'solution' may not arrive before Ukraine terms are set, keeping energy risk live.
“if they have a choice between Iran and Ukraine, they're going to focus on Ukraine.” ▶ 15:53
Malmgren says US strategy toward adversaries is 'conversion, not regime change' — creating conditions favorable enough that actors abandon terrorism for a place in the world economy, citing Syria's new leader's rehabilitation.
framework🗣 Dr. Pippa Malmgren (guest)conviction: highUSSyriaIranAbraham Accords
So what: A 'conversion' playbook (cash + market access) implies faster normalization of pariah states than military models — reduces long-run geopolitical risk premia.
“The strategy is not regime change. The strategy is conversion.” ▶ 12:02
Oil supply, demand destruction and price targets 6
Roughly 13 million bpd of Persian Gulf production is shut in and the US lacks the spare capacity to replace it, so ~10 million barrels of demand must be destroyed unless the strait reopens quickly.
data_point🗣 Erik Townsend (host)conviction: highcrude oilPersian GulfUS shaleSaudi Arabia
So what: Quantifies the supply gap; absent reopening, only sharply higher prices clear the market — bullish skew for crude.
“we've got about 13 million barrels per day of production shutins in the Persian Gulf region as a result of the closing of the Straight of Hormuz.” ▶ 38:48
Most analysts see $150-200 oil within ~30-60 days if Hormuz stays shut, as that's the price needed to destroy 10-15 million barrels of demand; world consumes ~105 mb/d vs ~90-95 mb/d supply capacity.
prediction🗣 Erik Townsend (host) / Jim Bianco (guest)conviction: highcrude oilWTI
So what: Defines the bull case price band and the structural supply-demand mismatch; the only durable fix Bianco names is more supply, not subsidies.
“if this goes on for another month... most analysts are saying that puts us squarely at $150 to $200 oil” ▶ 1:38:23
The world is currently bridging the gap by drawing down inventories (SPR and private), but Bianco says that runway ends around June/July, after which real demand destruction via higher prices becomes unavoidable.
causal🗣 Jim Bianco (guest)conviction: highcrude oilSPRoil inventories
So what: Gives a concrete timing trigger (June/July inventory exhaustion) for when the oil price spike could begin in earnest.
“oil inventories are being run down quite a bit... there's a finite limit... better measured as like June or July before we need to get resupplied.” ▶ 1:18:33
Contrarian view from Dr. Anas Alhajji: demand destruction needed is only ~3 million barrels (not 10-15M), thanks to price-driven demand decline already underway and Saudi East-West pipeline workarounds via Bab-el-Mandeb.
contrarian🗣 Erik Townsend (host) citing Dr. Anas Alhajjiconviction: mediumcrude oilSaudi ArabiaEast-West pipelineBab-el-Mandeb
So what: Even the most bullish-on-supply analyst agrees prices rise if the closure persists — the debate is magnitude, not direction.
“He thinks it's more like 3 million barrels... even if you take Honest's view, he's agreeing with me that if this goes on for another month, oil prices go up.” ▶ 1:40:33
Bianco warns Trump's proposed gas-tax holiday and subsidies would backfire: handing consumers money to pay higher prices forces prices even higher to achieve the needed demand destruction.
causal🗣 Jim Bianco (guest)conviction: highcrude oilTrumpgas tax
So what: Policy response itself could amplify the oil spike; a subsidy announcement is a contrarian bullish signal for crude, not bearish.
“if we're going to try and subsidize high oil prices, we're just going to make it worse in the long run. The fix is more supply.” ▶ 1:21:30
Patrick Ceresna stays a structural buyer of oil dips, arguing that even with a resolution, markets stay tight through an inventory-replenishment cycle and crude has room to trade back toward $100+.
thesis🗣 Patrick Ceresna (co-host)conviction: mediumcrude oilWTI
So what: Frames current ~10% selloff as a dip-buying opportunity; replenishment demand provides a floor under prices post-resolution.
“oil has every opportunity to trade back toward 100 plus. So, I'm using this weakness to be buying dips.” ▶ 1:42:01
Why markets are calm — and why that may be wrong 4
Bianco's behavioral thesis: markets refuse to react because investors who 'sold the bottom' on COVID, 9% inflation, SVB, and Liberation Day are now conditioned to dismiss every warning ('I'm not going to fall for that again').
framework🗣 Jim Bianco (guest)conviction: highS&P 500crude oil
So what: Complacency is a recency-bias artifact; like 2007 subprime, the danger is markets staring down genuinely bad news until it abruptly catches up.
“they were warned... and they sold the bottom of the market... Now they're being warned... And they're saying, Uh-huh. I'm not going to fall for that again.” ▶ 1:17:46
Louis-Vincent Gave's brutal explanation (relayed by Townsend): the market isn't crashing because any mass starvation from the energy crisis would occur in countries that don't affect Nvidia's earnings; resources go to the highest bidder.
contrarian🗣 Erik Townsend (host) quoting Louis Gaveconviction: mediumNvidiaS&P 500crude oil
So what: Equity calm reflects that pain is externalized to poor importers; US/China-centric indices can stay elevated even amid a global energy shock.
“if millions of people are going to starve to death as a result of this crisis, they won't be in countries that affect Nvidia's earnings.” ▶ 42:54
Malmgren argues markets are calm because so many nations are aligned and moving toward resolution from an advanced (not from-scratch) starting point, so the leaderless Iranian holdouts cannot hold out long.
thesis🗣 Dr. Pippa Malmgren (guest)conviction: highS&P 500IranChina
So what: Bull case for risk assets rests on multilateral alignment; if that alignment visibly cracks, the complacency trade unwinds.
Bianco notes the dominant equity driver remains AI — including upcoming IPOs of SpaceX, Anthropic and possibly OpenAI — which will keep markets looking past the oil squeeze at least near-term.
thesis🗣 Jim Bianco (guest)conviction: highAISpaceXAnthropicOpenAIsemiconductors
So what: The AI narrative is the structural bid; an oil shock competes with but does not yet displace it as the controlling market story.
“the dominant theme in the stock market right now is still AI... the coming IPOs of SpaceX and anthropic and possibly open AI.” ▶ 1:20:20
Modern warfare: cheap drones neutralize naval power 3
Bianco's core 'no forcing mechanism' thesis: cheap attritable drones have turned war upside down — Houthis closed Bab-el-Mandeb and defeated the French/British navies, and the same dynamic keeps Hormuz shut.
thesis🗣 Jim Bianco (guest)conviction: highdronesHouthisBab-el-MandebStrait of HormuzRoyal Navy
So what: If naval force can't reopen the strait, the crisis lacks a military off-ramp — structurally bullish for oil duration risk and defense-tech investment.
“these cheapraitable drones defeated the French and the British navy. We can't get the straight of Hermoose open.” ▶ 1:13:42
Bianco's drone math: ~100 cheap drones beat a $3B US destroyer's 96 vertically-launched missiles because the last few always get through; this is why the US, UK, France, Russia and even China cannot force outcomes.
framework🗣 Jim Bianco (guest)conviction: highdronesUS Navydefense contractorsChinaTaiwan
So what: Implies China cannot reliably invade Taiwan and saturation-drone defense is the new arms race — bullish for counter-drone/cheap-munition defense names over legacy platforms.
“I only need a hundred of them... the average US destroyer, which costs $3 billion, has 96 vertically launched missiles. And even if they're 100%, my last four get through.” ▶ 1:14:08
Operation 'Protect Freedom' collapsed after two days: three US destroyers needed 100 aircraft flying 24/7 (F-35 at $100k/hour, ~$100M spent), two destroyers exhausted their missile magazines, and Gulf states revoked airbase access.
anecdote🗣 Jim Bianco (guest)conviction: highUS NavyF-35Strait of HormuzGulf states
So what: Concrete evidence the US cannot sustain a defensive shield over the strait — magazine depth and cost are the binding constraints favoring a prolonged closure.
“two of them had to come back out because they ran out of missiles... that effectively ended Operation Protect Freedom after two days.” ▶ 1:15:00
Molecules to atoms — the nuclear/energy transition trade 7
Malmgren argues the US is content to let Hormuz stay closed because it forces the world to buy US oil/gas and petroleum byproducts, and the US can sustain that role for about two years.
thesis🗣 Dr. Pippa Malmgren (guest)conviction: highUScrude oilnatural gashelium
So what: Reframes the US as a net beneficiary of the closure — bullish US energy exports and capex; explains White House non-panic.
“the United States is perfectly fine with the straight remaining closed because this forces the whole world to buy these molecules... from America.” ▶ 31:10
Malmgren cites the helium scare as proof supply chains shift fast: after IRGC hit Qatari plants, the world's largest helium reserve in Texas filled the gap and SK Hynix/Samsung signed Texas helium deals within ~3 weeks.
anecdote🗣 Dr. Pippa Malmgren (guest)conviction: highheliumTexassemiconductorsSK HynixSamsungQatar
So what: Supply-chain reroutability tempers shortage panics; US-located inputs (helium, energy) gain pricing power and volume during Gulf disruptions.
“the largest helium reserve on Earth is in Texas. They're just going to buy from Texas.” ▶ 32:00
Malmgren's structural thesis: the US is steering the world from 'molecules to atoms' — toward small modular reactors using TRISO fuel (poppy-seed pellets, meltdown-proof), with companies like Valar Atomics, Aalo and others in Austin.
thesis🗣 Dr. Pippa Malmgren (guest)conviction: highnuclearsmall modular reactorsTRISO fuelValar AtomicsAalouranium
So what: Core long-term energy-transition trade: SMRs, advanced nuclear fuel and uranium fuel cycle as the structural beneficiary of the oil shock.
“we now can put a star in a box literally... a box that's literally half the size of a car.” ▶ 32:58
Malmgren claims an Austin startup built a small modular reactor with under 300 people in under 365 days, debunking the 'nuclear means $30B and 30 years' view — AI-driven simulation collapses development timelines.
contrarian🗣 Dr. Pippa Malmgren (guest)conviction: highnuclearsmall modular reactorsAITexas
So what: If nuclear build timelines compress dramatically, SMR/nuclear equities are mispriced to the upside; watch for accelerated regulatory approvals.
“They have built a small modular reactor with less than 300 people in less than 365 days.” ▶ 36:43
The 'Genesis Mission' — a White House initiative to declassify and run AI across all national-lab data (Los Alamos, Lawrence Livermore, Argonne) — is positioned to unleash world-shaking energy/tech breakthroughs.
thesis🗣 Dr. Pippa Malmgren (guest)conviction: mediumGenesis Missionnational labsAInuclear fusionLos AlamosLawrence LivermoreArgonne
So what: A policy catalyst to watch for breakthrough-tech commercialization (fusion, advanced energy); could re-rate energy-tech and AI-compute names.
“We're going to run AI over all the data, and we're going to connect the dots within the labs and across the labs.” ▶ 36:02
Ceresna's trade-of-the-week expresses the nuclear theme via a URA (Global X Uranium ETF) Jan-15-2027 bull call spread: buy $60 call (~$5.70), sell $70 call (~$3.50), ~$2.20 net debit, ~3.5:1 reward-to-risk, with URA near $49.58.
prediction🗣 Patrick Ceresna (co-host)conviction: mediumURAGlobal X Uranium ETFuraniumnuclear
So what: A concrete, defined-risk options structure to play the long-dated nuclear/uranium re-rating without absorbing full equity volatility.
“buying the $60 call for about $5.70 and selling the $70 call for about $3.50, creating a $10 wide bull call spread for a net debit of roughly $2.20.” ▶ 1:27:03
Malmgren says fertilizer fears are being neutralized by new petroleum-free methods like green ammonia, reinforcing that the oil shock is accelerating technological substitution away from hydrocarbons.
thesis🗣 Dr. Pippa Malmgren (guest)conviction: mediumgreen ammoniafertilizercrude oil
So what: Substitution dampens the duration of commodity shortages; green-ammonia and non-hydrocarbon input tech are second-order beneficiaries of high oil.
“we're developing all these new forms of things like green ammonia that don't even use any petroleum product.” ▶ 45:07
AI, robotics and the cost-collapse economy 9
Malmgren's macro driver: debt burdens for the US, Europe, Britain and China are so massive that the only escape is a 'whole new level of production' via AI and robotics — distinct from incremental productivity.
framework🗣 Dr. Pippa Malmgren (guest)conviction: highAIroboticssovereign debtUSChina
So what: Frames AI/robotics as a fiscal necessity, not just a tech cycle — implies sustained policy and capital support for the AI buildout regardless of valuation.
“the debt burden for all the major players... is so massive that a regular human being who needs to sleep at night can't outrun this thing.” ▶ 48:13
Malmgren reframes the AI-jobs panic as a confusion of 'jobs and tasks with income and standard of living' — robots doing tasks (like washing machines already do) frees humans for higher-value problem solving as supply costs collapse.
framework🗣 Dr. Pippa Malmgren (guest)conviction: highAIroboticslabor market
So what: Contrarian to mass-unemployment fears; supports a deflationary-abundance thesis where cost of living falls faster than wages — bullish real consumption.
“we're confusing jobs and tasks with income and standard of living and the disconnect is happening.”
Malmgren points to ICON's 3D-printed homes in Austin (three-bed with Tesla Powerwall, selling power back to grid) as evidence housing+energy costs are collapsing toward a ~$100k home that 'pays its own mortgage.'
anecdote🗣 Dr. Pippa Malmgren (guest)conviction: mediumICON3D printingTesla PowerwallhousingAustin
So what: Cost-collapse thesis spans housing and distributed energy; 3D-construction and distributed-solar/storage are structural disinflation plays.
“your 3D printed home is paying the mortgage on your behalf.” ▶ 50:40
Malmgren flags emerging energy frontiers — wireless energy transmission and space-based solar power (Japan recently made a breakthrough) — as paths to grid-independent, near-free power.
thesis🗣 Dr. Pippa Malmgren (guest)conviction: lowspace-based solar powerwireless power transmissionJapanenergy
So what: Early-stage but worth monitoring as optionality in the energy-abundance thesis; Japanese space-solar progress is a concrete watch item.
“we're making big advances in some other new forms of energy like wireless transmission of energy, space-based solar power. Japan has just made a big breakthrough there.” ▶ 50:48
Malmgren reaffirms she has argued since 2021 that 'we are in World War III' — beginning with Russia cutting Arctic subsea cables as a precursor to the Ukraine invasion — a global conflict only now being recognized and resolved.
framework🗣 Dr. Pippa Malmgren (guest)conviction: highRussiaUkrainesubsea cableshybrid warfare
So what: If the current conflict is the tail-end of a multi-year hybrid war, de-escalation (not escalation) is the forward path — supporting her risk-on stance.
≈ “I've been arguing since 2021... I think we are now in World War III... now I think it's beginning to be resolved.” paraphrase ▶ 56:22
Malmgren says 12-15 senior US nuclear/materials scientists (and younger Chinese scientists) have disappeared or died in statistically unlikely ways with little investigation, possibly tied to a fight over who controls soon-to-be-declassified nuclear tech.
anecdote🗣 Dr. Pippa Malmgren (guest)conviction: mediumnuclear scientistsUSChinaAir Force Research LabGenesis Mission
So what: Speculative but signals high-stakes contest over breakthrough energy/defense IP; watch national-lab declassification as a geopolitical and sector catalyst.
“12 and 15 of the most senior American... nuclear scientists... have literally just disappeared or suddenly been killed in very odd ways” ▶ 59:42
Malmgren's contrarian patent angle: the UAP/UFO declassification may be a strategy to void private patents — under US law, technology of non-human origin can't be patented — stripping the military-industrial complex's IP monopoly.
contrarian🗣 Dr. Pippa Malmgren (guest)conviction: lowUAPdefense contractorspatentsmilitary-industrial complex
So what: Even as a 'ruse,' it's a framework for shifting tech IP into public domain and cutting defense costs — bearish for legacy-defense IP rents.
“under US law, if the origin of a technology is not human, then the patents are not valid.” ▶ 1:03:18
Malmgren ties the declassification push to Trump's broader cost-cutting strategy — telling defense firms to stop optimizing for buybacks/profits and pharma to stop charging Americans 3x global drug prices.
thesis🗣 Dr. Pippa Malmgren (guest)conviction: mediumdefense contractorspharmaTrumpbuybacks
So what: Signals margin/pricing-power compression risk for defense primes and pharma if the administration follows through — a sector headwind to monitor.
“You guys have been optimizing for sure buybacks and for profits... this is going to end.” ▶ 1:04:21
Malmgren positions herself as 'the most optimistic economist on the planet,' arguing the convergence of AI, robotics and new energy is 'bigger than the industrial revolution' and dismissing recession fears.
thesis🗣 Dr. Pippa Malmgren (guest)conviction: highAIroboticsnucleareconomy
So what: Sets the bullish macro frame against which the oil/geopolitical bears (Townsend, Bianco) argue — a useful barometer of the optimist case.
“I may be the most optimistic economist on the planet at the moment.” ▶ 1:07:42
Cross-asset positioning: FX, gold, rates, equities 6
Ceresna sees the US dollar (DXY ~99.50) set to challenge ~100.50 range-top, driven less by US strength than by euro/yen weakness as energy-import-dependent Japan and Europe eventually reprice the prolonged closure.
prediction🗣 Patrick Ceresna / Erik Townsendconviction: mediumUS dollarDXYEURJPYJapanEurope
So what: Watch EUR below 1.16 as the trigger; a sustained Hormuz closure is a bullish-USD, bearish-EUR/JPY energy-terms-of-trade trade.
“I'm watching that euro breakdown below 116 as a... first kind of warning that... a dollar move may be underway.” ▶ 1:31:49
Townsend expects gold (testing 200-DMA ~$4,400) to break lower toward the early-March $4,100 low and possibly below if oil spikes — a $150-200 oil scenario could even drag gold toward $3,000 before an eventual post-resolution bull market.
prediction🗣 Erik Townsend (host)conviction: mediumgold200-day moving average
So what: Counterintuitive near-term bearish gold call tied to oil/liquidity stress; the post-reopening setup is framed as a major gold buying opportunity.
“I think we're very likely going to retest that $4,100 low from early March and maybe even take it out to the downside” ▶ 1:43:06
Ceresna notes the entire precious-metals complex (gold, silver false breakout, platinum/palladium at lows, stressed miners) is showing distributive price action with no money flow — vulnerable to further downside until a mid-summer catalyst.
data_point🗣 Patrick Ceresna (co-host)conviction: mediumgoldsilverplatinumpalladiumgold miners
So what: Broad PM-complex distribution argues against bottom-fishing now; flag July/August for a potential turn catalyst.
“the entire precious metals market is showing that distribution... it's not being supported and that makes it relatively vulnerable for further distribution.” ▶ 1:44:11
Bianco warns a new Fed chair (on the job under a week, last public speech a year ago, presumed dovish) cutting into a >4% CPI print risks a bond-market revolt — the market is pricing rate hikes, and stimulus could backfire by pushing yields higher.
causal🗣 Jim Bianco (guest)conviction: highFederal ReserveFed chairCPIbonds10-year Treasury
So what: If the White House forces cuts amid 4%+ inflation, the bond vigilante response (higher long yields) is the pain trade — bearish duration.
≈ “if the administration wants to scream Fed cut rates, the problem... is that the market is not there for the Fed to cut rates and it could actually backfire.” paraphrase ▶ 1:23:26
Ceresna highlights dangerously narrow equity breadth: the S&P at all-time highs (~7520) with only ~50% participation versus a typical 70-80%, making the AI/semiconductor-led rally vulnerable to either rotation or violent mean reversion.
data_point🗣 Patrick Ceresna (co-host)conviction: highS&P 500semiconductorsAIhealthcaredefense contractorsfinancials
So what: Narrow breadth is the key risk to the rally; watch whether profit-taking in semis rotates into healthcare/defense/financials or triggers a broad selloff.
≈ “Rarely do we see a market that is trading at 52-week highs for such a prolonged period with the breath of the market not participating.” paraphrase ▶ 1:29:15
Ceresna keeps the 10-year Treasury yield (~4.51%) biased higher, arguing an unresolved Hormuz situation stresses oil, keeps inflation elevated, and likely pushes the 10-year back toward last month's highs.
prediction🗣 Patrick Ceresna (co-host)conviction: medium10-year Treasuryyieldsinflationcrude oil
So what: Oil-driven inflation keeps upward pressure on long yields; recent yield relief is likely short-lived absent an Iran resolution.
“it's very likely here that we could see the 10-year yield just come right back up toward the highs from last month.” ▶ 1:46:38
MacroVoices #535 Michael Every: NAFTA and NAPTHA – Warcraft & Fartcraft97 min41 insights2026-06-04
▶ Watch on YouTube
Set against an ongoing Strait of Hormuz blockade and shut-in of 13–15 mb/d of oil, Rabobank's Michael Every argues the Iran crisis is one front in a broader US pivot from economic policy to 'economic statecraft' — weaponizing Fed swap lines, stablecoins, the SPR and a possible North American energy bloc — while commodity analyst Rory Johnston explains why oil has stayed near $97–98 Brent (a ~50% collapse in Chinese crude imports masking record-pace inventory draws) and warns of a $150–200 spike once buffers exhaust.
Economic statecraft as the new policy regime · 5Fed under Warsh: balance sheet, supply-side QE, sector-specific rates · 4Iran/Hormuz scenarios and the NAPTHA energy-bloc thesis · 8Oil market mechanics: why prices stayed near $97–98 · 9Oil price forecasts and the spike trigger · 4Inflation/deflation, gold, and the macro tail · 4Market wrap: levels, FX, rates and the industrial-rebuild trade · 7
Economic statecraft as the new policy regime 5
The US has shifted from 'economic policy' to 'economic statecraft,' openly using energy, swap lines, stablecoins and supply chains as tools of national strategy, which means an independent inflation-targeting Fed is logically incompatible with this regime.
thesis🗣 Michael Every, Rabobank global strategistconviction: highUnited StatesFederal ReserveScott BessentDonald Trump
So what: Investors should expect policy (incl. Fed) to be subordinated to geopolitical/industrial goals rather than 2% CPI, reshaping how rates, FX and sector liquidity are set.
“you cannot have an independent central bank which is trying to target 2% CPI ... and not have joined up thinking across every element of state and government towards the national security goals” ▶ 14:50
Kevin Warsh's statement that Fed independence does not apply to dollar swap lines because they are a geopolitical transaction signals the Fed itself is moving into the economic-statecraft field, with swap lines becoming quid-pro-quo tools of the presidency/Treasury rather than neutral technocratic liquidity.
causal🗣 Michael Everyconviction: highKevin WarshFederal ReserveTreasuryswap lines
So what: Dollar liquidity access could become politically allocated; watch which non-traditional allies (e.g. UAE) get Fed backstops as a tell on US alignment.
≈ “he alluded to the fact that he thought Fed independence didn't apply to them because they were a geopolitical transaction” paraphrase ▶ 16:52
The Euro-dollar system gave the US power but de-industrialized it because global demand for dollars favors holding US financial assets over productive assets; this is a closed loop in which the dollar killed the industry that propped up the military that propped up the rules-based order.
framework🗣 Michael Everyconviction: highUS dollarEuro-dollar systemUS manufacturingrules-based order
So what: Frames the entire policy pivot: Washington wants to keep dollar dominance while reversing its de-industrializing side effects — bullish physical economy, ambivalent for pure financial-asset inflation.
“the US dollar system killed the system the rules-based order which actually props up. So it's a closed loop” ▶ 35:49
Stablecoins are intended to create lower US interest rates and suck Euro-dollars out of the global economy, replacing them with digital tokens in a 'buy-one-get-one-free' fiscal mechanism that also sorts allies from non-allies.
thesis🗣 Michael Everyconviction: highstablecoinsUS dollarTreasuryChina
So what: Countries that accept US stablecoins, tariff China and split tech supply chains become the aligned bloc; stablecoin adoption becomes a geopolitical sorting signal and a source of US fiscal breathing room.
“there's a sorting effect, there's a reorganizational effect, there's a buy one get one free and there's fiscal breeding room through all of that” ▶ 37:02
Central banks as currently constituted are demand-management institutions that cannot handle structural supply-side shocks; the better tool for an energy shock is vast strategic reserves of products (as China uses), not raising or cutting aggregate borrowing costs with long lags.
framework🗣 Michael Everyconviction: highFederal Reservecentral banksstrategic petroleum reservesChina
So what: Implies policy will reach for physical interventions (SPR, export controls, swap lines) over rate moves; rate-cut/hike bets premised on classic Fed reaction functions may misfire in a supply shock.
“central banks understand supply side or structural supply side shocks like a fish understands a bicycle” ▶ 10:53
Fed under Warsh: balance sheet, supply-side QE, sector-specific rates 4
Warsh is publicly arguing AI will be very deflationary, creating room for rate cuts even as an oil-driven inflation shock looms — a contradictory backdrop that makes the rate path very hard to call.
prediction🗣 Michael Everyconviction: mediumKevin WarshFederal ReserveAIinflation
So what: Rates could be cut into an energy spike on AI-deflation logic; positions assuming a clean hawkish response to oil inflation are at risk.
Warsh wants to reduce the Fed balance sheet at the same time Trump plans to expand the Pentagon budget by $500 billion next year — a circle that may be squared by redirecting QE-style balance-sheet support from financial assets to the physical economy.
prediction🗣 Michael Everyconviction: mediumKevin WarshFederal ReservePentagonQE
So what: Suggests 'physical-economy QE' — liquidity steered to mines, ports, shipbuilding and defense supply chains — rather than broad asset-price support; a regime shift many market participants are not positioned for.
“Walsh is openly talking about trying to reduce the Fed balance sheet ... at the same time Trump is talking about expanding the Pentagon budget next year by $500 billion” ▶ 42:21
The Fed may move from one-size-fits-all policy to looking at which sectors of the economy need higher or lower rates, providing targeted balance-sheet support to strategic physical sectors rather than all favored financial sectors.
prediction🗣 Michael Everyconviction: mediumFederal Reserveindustrial policy
So what: Extra liquidity that flows to some sectors but not the usual favored ones overturns a 1–2 generation assumption of broad liquidity support; sector selection becomes paramount.
“extra liquidity that goes to some sectors but it doesn't flow to all the favored ones which is what an entire generation if not two of financial market participants have grown up expecting as normal” ▶ 44:35
The framing is 'World of Warcraft' financed by 'financial fartcraft' — using financialization to fund rearmament and re-industrialization, implying a structurally different liquidity paradigm.
framework🗣 Michael Everyconviction: mediumdefensere-industrializationfinancialization
So what: Captures the thesis in one image: liquidity directed at war-economy buildout (defense, mining, shipbuilding) over consumer/financial sectors.
≈ “using what I also call financial fart which is just financialization to try and prepare for a World of Warcraft” paraphrase
Iran/Hormuz scenarios and the NAPTHA energy-bloc thesis 8
The Strait of Hormuz remains effectively closed and Trump has floated lifting the US blockade of Iran 'as soon as Labor Day' (~95 days out, September), signaling a strategy of waiting Iran out rather than near-term resolution.
data_point🗣 host (Erik Townsend)conviction: highStrait of HormuzIranDonald Trump
So what: A multi-month blockade horizon means inventory draws continue, raising the probability of a delayed but sharp oil spike into Q3.
“the US blockade of Iran could be lifted as soon as Labor Day ... That's 95 days from now” ▶ 0:52
Every floats a 'NAPTHA' scenario (North American Petroleum and Hydrocarbons Trading Hub Association): the US forms a closed energy loop with Mexico, Canada, Venezuela, Guyana, Gulf allies and key Asian refiners, fragmenting global energy markets so the bloc survives a crisis while everyone else faces much higher prices.
thesis🗣 Michael Everyconviction: mediumUnited StatesMexicoCanadaVenezuelaGuyanaUSMCA
So what: A coercive energy bloc would entrench regional price divergence and reward North American/aligned producers and refiners while penalizing import-dependent regions — a structural trade in energy fragmentation.
≈ “instead of having NAFTA, you have NAPTHA, which is the North American Petroleum and Hydrocarbons Trading Hub Association” paraphrase
Canada's rhetoric flipped from PM Carney favoring a 'new world order' with China in January to seeking a 'new partnership with the US to make America great again' last week, illustrating how energy coercion can rapidly realign even daggers-drawn NATO allies.
anecdote🗣 Michael Everyconviction: mediumCanadaMark CarneyUnited StatesChina
So what: Watch allied rhetoric shifts as a real-time gauge of US statecraft leverage; realignment can happen fast under energy pressure.
“from ... we're in favor of a new world order working with China ... to last week saying we want to form a new partnership with the US to make America great again” ▶ 23:46
A US 'taco' (retreat from the Middle East, ceding Hormuz to an Iranian nexus) would be a '1956 Suez moment' that drops the US from great power to merely a power, shattering its global image and forcing a reassessment of US dollar and US-asset bullishness.
contrarian🗣 Michael Everyconviction: mediumUnited StatesIranUS dollarUS assets
So what: A geostrategic retreat is a tail risk that would structurally undermine the dollar and US-asset premium even if the US remains a hyperpower.
“I've referred to it as a 1956 crisis for the US in the way that the UK and France experienced one. And it dropped them from being great powers to just being powers” ▶ 40:02
Resolution could ironically come from China working with the US against its own ally Iran, once Beijing concludes the damage to China from the crisis exceeds the value of keeping Iran as an ally.
prediction🗣 Michael Everyconviction: lowChinaIranUnited States
So what: A China-US tacit deal against Iran is an under-priced bullish-for-resolution scenario; watch for rumors before mainstream reporting.
China was offered Fed-style backstop dynamics indirectly while the US extended swap lines to the UAE — a cash-rich, dollar-pegged, large-energy state that has now left OPEC/OPEC+ — signaling swap lines are being repurposed for new commodity-producing allies, not just the old Western/BOJ club.
causal🗣 Michael Everyconviction: highUAEFederal Reserveswap linesOPECOPEC+
So what: The UAE leaving OPEC+ plus a US swap line marks a realignment of energy and dollar plumbing; track which producers get backstops as the new alliance map.
“swap lines ... have now been offered to the UAE which ... has a currency pegged to the dollar and has an awful lot of energy and is no longer in OPEC and OPEC plus” ▶ 22:30
China stopped exporting refined products and fertilizer at the crisis onset, then resumed exports roughly an hour after the UAE left OPEC/OPEC+, suggesting China is reacting to shifting geopolitical 'tectonic plates' and positioning to offer product/RMB swap lines to stay ahead.
causal🗣 Michael Everyconviction: mediumChinaUAEOPECfertilizerrefined products
So what: China may emerge as a rival energy backstop via RMB swap lines; export-policy reversals are a tell on Beijing's strategic intent.
A closure of the Bab-el-Mandeb strait (Red Sea) — well within the capability of the Houthis and Iran's proxies — would be the big curveball that rapidly accelerates an oil spike, since oil blocked at Hormuz is currently pipelined to the Red Sea and exits there.
causal🗣 host (Erik Townsend)conviction: mediumBab-el-MandebRed SeaHouthisIranSaudi Arabia
So what: Bab-el-Mandeb closure is a discrete catalyst to watch; it would remove the main workaround for Hormuz and spike prices quickly.
Oil market mechanics: why prices stayed near $97–98 9
13–15 million barrels per day of oil production has been shut in for lack of export capacity, making this the core supply crisis; clearing stranded ships and restarting shut-in production after any deal will take weeks to months, not days.
data_point🗣 Rory Johnston, Commodity Context founderconviction: highcrude oilStrait of HormuzPersian Gulf
So what: Even a peace deal does not flip supply back on quickly; the multi-month restart lag underpins a structural floor and delayed normalization.
“this is the 13 to 15 million barrels of oil ... that has been shut in for lack of any export capacity” ▶ 48:48
Iran releasing trapped tankers (out of ~2,000 vessels / ~800 large merchant ships in the Gulf) is effectively Iran tapping a strategic petroleum reserve, moving 'dead' produced oil into commercial inventory at a draw of 1–3 mb/d, but this does not fix the underlying shut-in production deficit.
framework🗣 Rory Johnstonconviction: highIrancrude oil tankersStrait of Hormuz
So what: Ship crossings are a finite buffer being drained, not a supply fix; once the trapped-oil stockpile depletes, the real deficit reasserts.
“more ships means more SPR oil coming from one stock ... that's not accessible to the market to a broader stock that's accessible to the market” ▶ 48:30
Chinese crude imports have collapsed roughly 50% — from ~12 mb/d seaborne pre-war to ~6 mb/d (heading lower in weekly data), which alone offsets about half the entire Hormuz supply shock and exceeds the combined SPR injections of every other country on earth.
data_point🗣 Rory Johnstonconviction: highChinacrude oil importsSPR
So what: China is now the single most important swing factor balancing the oil market; the durability of this import cut is the key variable for the price path.
“Chinese crude oil imports have fallen by roughly 50% relative to prewar levels ... That's half of the entire supply shock from Hormuz” ▶ 56:09
China's import collapse is not demand destruction (mobility, trucking, congestion and flight data show no collapse) and visible inventories actually rose then drew only modestly; the leading theory is China is drawing on invisible underground/non-satellite-visible inventories.
causal🗣 Rory Johnstonconviction: mediumChinaKplercrude oil inventories
So what: A huge piece of the supply-demand model is unobservable; once China's hidden buffers exhaust and imports rebound with Hormuz still closed, that's the trigger for the next leg higher.
“you are seeing injections of up until now invisible inventories or nonvisible statistically inventories ... Underground inventories can't be seen” ▶ 58:30
China's refining runs collapsed because pump-price caps held retail gasoline up only ~30% versus a global doubling, so feedstock costs exploded while product prices were capped, driving refining margins deeply negative — which also explains China's ban/limits on refined-product exports.
causal🗣 Rory Johnstonconviction: highChinarefining marginsgasolinerefined products
So what: China's domestic price caps, not weak demand, drove the refining cut; this distorts global product flows and shields Chinese activity, masking the true tightness.
“the cost of your feed stock has exploded while the cost of the product you sell has been capped. So your margins have imploded to deeply negative levels” ▶ 1:03:57
The strong Chinese 'demand' seen pre-war (and back in 2023) was likely strategic stockpiling of refined products — possibly pre-positioned for a Taiwan contingency — not real demand, implying the pre-war global surplus was larger than appreciated.
contrarian🗣 Rory Johnstonconviction: mediumChinaTaiwanrefined productsoil demand
So what: Reassesses the global oil balance: apparent Chinese demand overstated true consumption, meaning structural demand is weaker and China is a more potent emergency backstop than OPEC.
“some of the strong Chinese demand we were seeing likely wasn't real demand at all, but was rather stockpiling” ▶ 1:00:16
Despite muted prices, oil inventories are being drawn down at the fastest pace of any crisis on record; in any other year/under any other president markets would already be far more panicked.
data_point🗣 Rory Johnstonconviction: highcrude oil inventoriesBrent
So what: The calm price ($98 Brent high) masks record-pace depletion; the disconnect is the setup for a violent re-rating once buffers visibly hit bottoms.
“We are now drawing that down at the fastest pace of any crisis on record” ▶ 54:52
The historical link between inventory draws and futures-market panic has been 'neutered' by Trump's Truth Social posts, which can knock $20+ off crude in 10 minutes and scared speculators out — but physical rebalancing, unlike speculators, is immune to tweets.
framework🗣 host (Erik Townsend) and Rory Johnstonconviction: highDonald TrumpTruth Socialcrude oilspeculators
So what: Tweet-driven suppression is a temporary cap; when physical traders (not speculators) drive the price, jawboning fails and the spike becomes 'unstoppable.'
“all the truth social posts in the world won't make a bit of difference” ▶ 1:09:07
Commodity markets balance supply and demand in real time and are not forward-looking, so prices won't spike until buffers are actually exhausted — mirroring early-COVID when crude rose for a month before the real-time crash.
framework🗣 host (Erik Townsend)conviction: highcrude oilCOVIDcommodity markets
So what: Don't expect oil to pre-price the shortage; the move comes only when physical balances break, likely end-June into July per Johnston.
“they have to balance supply and demand in real time ... it wasn't until they actually cancelled all the flights ... that ... the crash was dramatic” ▶ 1:07:00
Oil price forecasts and the spike trigger 4
Johnston sees ~$200 as the temporary demand-rationing level if Hormuz stays closed, not a new normal; he expects ~$150 if the blockade persists through Labor Day, and says current ~$97 pricing is 'still bullish' relative to fundamentals.
prediction🗣 Rory Johnstonconviction: mediumcrude oilBrentStrait of Hormuz
So what: Asymmetric upside: a closed strait implies $150–200 spikes to ration demand; current prices under-price the tail.
“200 is essentially what I see as if we do not open hormos. That is the level that prices would need to rise to temporarily ration demand” ▶ 54:04
Even the most bearish credible oil veterans (e.g. Ole Hansen sees a cap '~$200', Dr. Anas Alhajji argues only ~3–4 mb/d net deficit not 13, with oil below $100 in 2027 but possibly still rising in 2026) are not calling oil a short — no qualified physical-oil expert thinks it's 'no big deal.'
data_point🗣 host (Erik Townsend)conviction: highOle HansenAnas Alhajjicrude oil
So what: The bull/bear range among experts runs from $150 to catastrophic; the consensus floor of credible views still points up, not down.
The key spike trigger is when Chinese imports stop falling and rebound (delta shifting from loosening to tightening) while Hormuz stays closed and EIA/high-frequency inventories rip below trailing seasonal bottoms — Johnston flags end-June into July.
prediction🗣 Rory Johnstonconviction: mediumChinaEIAcrude oil inventoriesStrait of Hormuz
So what: Concrete watch-list: rising Chinese imports + sub-seasonal EIA inventories = the signal to position for the next leg higher in oil.
“when the delta and the impulse shifts from loosening back to tightening. I think that's probably when we do that next rip higher” ▶ 1:11:04
If the strait stays closed through Labor Day, hundreds of millions to a billion-plus barrels of additional inventory will be drawn, and even China's hidden 'black hole SPR' can't be big enough to prevent substantially higher prices.
prediction🗣 Rory Johnstonconviction: highcrude oilChinaSPRStrait of Hormuz
So what: Time does NOT favor price suppression; a long blockade makes a major spike close to inevitable on Johnston's framework.
“if this is truly still going through the end of summer, then yeah, I I I don't see a way to avoid substantially higher prices” ▶ 1:14:28
Inflation/deflation, gold, and the macro tail 4
Mike Green argues the oil crisis will be deflationary and force aggressive Fed cuts because while $150 oil is inflationary, $500 oil is deflationary (demand destruction) — a level Every calls 'a heart attack on the screen.'
contrarian🗣 host (Erik Townsend), citing Mike Greenconviction: mediumMike Greencrude oilFederal Reserveinflation
So what: A sufficiently large oil shock flips from inflationary to deflationary, scrambling the rate outlook; positioning for a clean inflation trade is risky at extreme oil levels.
“Can't you see that $500 oil is deflationary?” ▶ 9:31
Gold has fallen despite bombs falling — breaking the usual war/gold correlation — partly because Middle Eastern holders found gold hard to flee with: they sold gold for dollars to move capital abroad, and few want to re-buy heavy metal on the other side.
causal🗣 Michael Everyconviction: lowgoldUS dollarMiddle East
So what: A capital-flight mechanism (not just oil-inflation) explains gold weakness; the broken war/gold correlation may persist while the conflict drives dollar demand.
“people were finding that they were having to sell gold for dollars to try and then move them abroad” ▶ 32:49
Gold is in a four-month distributive consolidation after a parabolic blowoff at the start of the year, trading below its moving averages with the 200-day (~4415) as the key support; a break would likely accelerate the move lower, with no clear catalyst for a new bull leg until possibly Q3.
data_point🗣 host (Erik Townsend) and Patrick Ceresnaconviction: mediumgold200-day moving average
So what: Watch the ~4415 200-day on gold; macro headwinds (firmer dollar, rising rates) argue against gold longs near-term and for downside acceleration if support breaks.
High gasoline prices into the November midterms (5 months out) are a headwind for Republicans, but Every argues elections are binary and enthusiasm-driven, so a loss is 'not a gimme' given Democratic redistricting and the repeated humiliation of confident election forecasters.
contrarian🗣 Michael Everyconviction: mediummidterm electionsRepublicansDemocratsgasoline
So what: Don't over-price a clean Republican midterm loss on gasoline alone; political outcomes (and thus policy continuity) are more uncertain than consensus assumes.
Market wrap: levels, FX, rates and the industrial-rebuild trade 7
The S&P 500 (~7553) just put in an eight-days-up, one-day-down rally — a magnitude seen only four times in ~20 years, ~20% trough-to-peak in two months — leaving it deeply overbought with poor asymmetry for pressing higher; hedging or reducing exposure is the logical move.
data_point🗣 host (Erik Townsend) and Patrick Ceresnaconviction: highS&P 500
So what: A rare overbought condition argues for hedges/reduced risk; an oil break above $100 disrupting bonds is the candidate catalyst for profit-taking.
“there's only four times we've seen a rally of this magnitude ... a very poor asymmetry at this moment for pressing the market higher” ▶ 1:21:09
The DXY (~99.5) is attempting to break out of a one-month May range, with the euro at ~116 the key driver; deteriorating European conditions around next week's ECB meeting could weaken the euro and let the dollar rally, with further upside if Iran re-escalates to full-scale conflict.
prediction🗣 host (Erik Townsend) and Patrick Ceresnaconviction: mediumUS dollar indexeuroECBIran
So what: Watch the euro at 116 and the ECB; a euro breakdown or Iran escalation are the two paths to a dollar breakout.
The 10-year Treasury yield (~4.48%) sees the path of least resistance toward a 5% handle, as the oil-up/inflation-expectations-up/yields-up correlation of the last 2–3 months likely reasserts if oil turns higher; no bond-buying opportunity yet.
prediction🗣 host (Erik Townsend) and Patrick Ceresnaconviction: medium10-year Treasurycrude oilinflation expectations
So what: Bias is toward higher yields with oil; a 5% 10-year is a realistic target and a potential equity-market stress catalyst.
≈ “can we see a 5% handle on the 10-year yield. It's certainly at this point ... the path of least resistance” paraphrase ▶ 1:33:15
Copper at 52-week highs is the most bullish chart in commodities, trading along its highs with dips bought and clear momentum, eyeing a run at the $7 level.
data_point🗣 Patrick Ceresnaconviction: mediumcopper
So what: Copper momentum favors continuation toward $7; a relative-strength leader in the commodity complex.
Uranium hasn't participated in the broader rally and is seasonally weak until late August; a sharp Tuesday pop on the nuclear-fuels conference fully retraced, and a broad-market correction could drag uranium shares substantially lower without changing the intact long-term bull case (seasonal buying ~WNA conference Sept 7–11).
data_point🗣 host (Erik Townsend) and Patrick Ceresnaconviction: mediumuraniumWNA conference
So what: Expect a uranium soft patch into late summer; long-term bulls may get a better entry before the September seasonal buying window.
Ceresna frames oil as an asymmetric long: hundreds of millions of barrels of depleted inventory put a structural floor under crude even with a peace deal, so the downside is definable/manageable while inventory depletion catching up to the market offers large upside.
thesis🗣 Patrick Ceresnaconviction: highcrude oil
So what: The depleted-inventory floor makes long oil an asymmetric trade; even a peace deal leaves prices structurally elevated above pre-war levels.
“You can't deplete hundreds of millions of barrels of oil inventory and then make a bet that oil's returning back to the pre-war levels” ▶ 1:27:40
The trade of the week to express Every's industrial-rebuild thesis is long the PAVE ETF (~$57.24) paired with a July 17 2026 $55 protective put (~$1.25), effective cost ~$58.49, floor at $55, defining risk to ~$3.49/share (~6%) against a stretched, near-vertical equity rally.
thesis🗣 Patrick Ceresnaconviction: mediumPAVE ETFinfrastructureoptions
So what: A concrete, hedged way to play the physical-economy/economic-statecraft regime shift while protecting against a summer correction in an overbought market.
Trade of The Week - MacroVoices #53425 min22 insights2026-05-28
▶ Watch on YouTube
MacroVoices' Trade of the Week is a defined-risk bull call spread on the Global X Uranium ETF (URA) to play a structural 'molecules to atoms' energy-security shift toward nuclear. The post-game deck centers on an unresolved Iran/Strait of Hormuz crisis (no real deal because neither side will concede Iran's 60%-enriched uranium), with bullish-oil, dollar-up, gold-correction-lower, and AI-concentration views.
Trade of the Week: URA uranium bull call spread · 4Energy-security regime shift: molecules to atoms · 2Iran nuclear stalemate as the master catalyst · 4Crude oil: Strait closure and price scenarios · 4Equities: AI concentration and the rotation question · 3Dollar, gold, and rates · 5
Trade of the Week: URA uranium bull call spread 4
MacroVoices' trade of the week expresses a long-term nuclear-energy-security thesis via the Global X Uranium ETF (URA) rather than trying to trade Strait of Hormuz headlines.
thesis🗣 Patrick Ceresnaconviction: highURAGlobal X Uranium ETFuraniumnuclear powerStrait of Hormuz
So what: URA is the chosen vehicle for broad uranium-miner and fuel-cycle exposure; a defined-risk way to ride the nuclear repricing.
“rather than trying to trade the next headline around the Strait of Hormuz, this week's trade of the week is about positioning for strategic energy response, renewed interest in nuclear power, and the uranium fuel cycle” ▶ 1:16
The specific trade is a Jan 15 2027 URA bull call spread: buy the $60 call (~$5.70), sell the $70 call (~$3.50), a $10-wide spread for ~$2.20 net debit, with URA trading around $49.58 and ~232 days to expiration.
data_point🗣 Patrick Ceresnaconviction: highURAoptionsbull call spread
So what: Exact strikes, expiry and cost give a reproducible defined-risk structure on uranium upside.
“buying the $60 call for about $5.70 and selling the $70 call for about $3.50, creating a $10 wide bull call spread for a net debit of roughly $2.20” ▶ 2:00
The URA spread risks the $2.20 debit for a maximum $7.80 profit if URA closes above $70 at expiration — roughly a 3.5-to-1 reward-to-risk asymmetry.
data_point🗣 Patrick Ceresnaconviction: highURAoptions
So what: Quantifies the payoff; the appeal is asymmetric upside on the nuclear theme without full equity volatility.
“the maximum profit is $7.80 if URA is above $70 at expiration” ▶ 2:31
The trade is timed to use current short-term uranium weakness to position for a bigger turnaround later in 2026, since money is concentrated in the AI trade and ignoring uranium and gold.
thesis🗣 Patrick Ceresnaconviction: mediumuraniumgoldAI trade
So what: Frames the entry as buying neglected sectors during distribution ahead of a flows rotation.
“use this weakness to position for a a bigger turnaround later this year” ▶ 20:38
Energy-security regime shift: molecules to atoms 2
Pippa Malmgren argues the energy crisis is not about the next move in crude but a structural transition away from vulnerable hydrocarbon supply chains toward nuclear power, small modular reactors and advanced energy tech — 'from molecules to atoms.'
framework🗣 Patrick Ceresna (citing Pippa Malmgren)conviction: highPippa Malmgrennuclear powersmall modular reactorsuraniumhydrocarbons
So what: Reusable mental model: position for a multi-year energy-security re-rating of nuclear/uranium, not a short-term oil trade.
“Her phrase was essentially from molecules to atoms. Move from oil and gas dependency toward nuclear power, small modular reactors, and advanced energy technologies.” ▶ 1:05
Eric Townsend is structurally very bullish uranium long-term but warns that an oil-crisis escalation could drag the broader stock market down and hurt uranium equities in the short term.
thesis🗣 Eric Townsendconviction: highuraniumS&P 500semiconductors
So what: Long-term uranium bull case is intact, but near-term it trades as a risk asset vulnerable to an equity drawdown.
“I'm super duper bullish long-term” ▶ 19:54
Iran nuclear stalemate as the master catalyst 4
Eric Townsend says there is no real Iran deal because the conflict hinges entirely on Iran's 60%-enriched uranium, and neither the US nor Iran will concede on it — making 'hours away from a deal' talk propaganda.
contrarian🗣 Eric Townsendconviction: highIranUnited Statesuranium enrichmentPresident Trump
So what: If the deal narrative is false, risk assets pricing resolution are mispriced; watch for a concession on the enriched uranium as the true catalyst.
“All of this talk about how we're just hours away from a deal... it's all a bunch of It's propaganda. It's not real.” ▶ 12:20
The IAEA has physically verified Iran holds 441 kg of 60%-enriched uranium — about two pickup-truck loads of canisters — enough for as many as nine or ten bombs if enriched the rest of the way.
data_point🗣 Eric Townsendconviction: highIranIAEAuranium enrichment
So what: Concrete, verified figure that grounds the geopolitical tail risk driving oil and the energy-security trade.
“The IAEA has gone and physically inspected this 441 kg of 60% enriched uranium. They verified that it's there.” ▶ 9:35
Because enrichment separative work is non-linear, Iran has effectively completed ~99% of the work; moving 60% material to 90% weapons-grade would take only a few weeks if it had operable centrifuges — a deliberate 'threshold state' strategy.
framework🗣 Eric Townsendconviction: highIranuranium enrichmentcentrifugesOperation Midnight Hammer
So what: Reframes the '60% vs 90%' gap as nearly closed; explains why the standoff is unlikely to resolve quickly and keeps a geopolitical risk premium live.
“Iran has done 99% of the enrichment activity necessary in order to get enough enriched material to make several nuclear bombs” ▶ 8:18
Iran's supreme leader issued a directive (the prior Thursday) that the enriched material cannot leave Iran under any circumstance, and no Iranian official has ever signaled giving it up.
data_point🗣 Eric Townsendconviction: highIranuranium
So what: Hard evidence that a near-term resolution is improbable, supporting persistent oil/dollar risk premia.
“the supreme leader just last Thursday issued a new directive reiterating that that material cannot leave Iran under any circumstance” ▶ 12:05
Crude oil: Strait closure and price scenarios 4
Strait of Hormuz traffic is running below 10% of normal flows, and those remaining flows are almost entirely Chinese-flagged vessels, suggesting an unannounced US-China side agreement letting China move oil out for Iran.
causal🗣 Eric Townsendconviction: mediumStrait of HormuzChinaIranXi JinpingPresident Trumpcrude oil
So what: Chinese-only flows imply the West is more cut off than China, a geopolitical wedge that shapes who bears the oil shock.
“those flows that we have are almost all Chinese flagged vessels” ▶ 13:32
Most analysts say a continued month of near-zero Strait flow puts oil at $150–$200 to force the 10–15 million bbl/day of demand destruction needed to clear the market.
prediction🗣 Eric Townsendconviction: mediumcrude oilStrait of Hormuz
So what: Sets a consensus upside oil scenario; a tradable tail if the standoff persists.
“puts us squarely at $150 to $200 oil in order to create the demand destruction necessary” ▶ 14:20
Dr. Anas Alhajji offers the lone contrarian view that only ~3 million bbl (not 10–15M) of demand destruction is needed, citing demand already softening and Saudi workarounds via the East-West pipeline and Bab el-Mandeb — so oil rises but far less than feared.
contrarian🗣 Eric Townsend (citing Dr. Anas Alhajji)conviction: mediumAnas Alhajjicrude oilSaudi ArabiaEast-West pipelineBab el-Mandeb
So what: A credible counter to the $150–$200 panic case; even the bear agrees oil goes up if the crisis runs another month.
“He thinks it's more like 3 million barrels.” ▶ 15:30
Patrick Ceresna treats oil as a buy-on-dip even if the Strait is resolved, because tight markets and an inventory-replenishment cycle should drive crude back toward $100+.
thesis🗣 Patrick Ceresnaconviction: highcrude oil
So what: Frames near-term flow-driven sell-offs as buying opportunities targeting $100+ crude.
“I believe that oil has every opportunity to trade back toward 100 plus. So, I'm using this weakness to be buying dips.” ▶ 16:58
Equities: AI concentration and the rotation question 3
The S&P rally to prolonged 52-week highs is occurring with market breadth stuck near 50% (versus a typical 70-80%), making it an unusually concentrated AI/semiconductor-bubble story.
data_point🗣 Patrick Ceresnaconviction: highS&P 500semiconductorsNasdaqAI
So what: Narrow breadth at highs sets up a binary: a healthy rotation that widens breadth or a violent mean reversion.
“Rarely do we see a market that is trading at 52-week highs for such a prolonged period with the breadth of the market not participating.” ▶ 4:13
Ceresna gives bulls the benefit of the doubt that profit-taking in semiconductors rotates into healthcare, defense contractors and financials rather than triggering a broad crash.
prediction🗣 Patrick Ceresnaconviction: mediumsemiconductorshealthcaredefense contractorsfinancials
So what: Names the likely rotation beneficiaries to watch if AI leadership cools.
“will we see areas like healthcare, uh defense contractors, and financials get some of the money flow” ▶ 5:07
Townsend is unsure a bad Iran outcome causes a crash, noting the market might instead rally on expectations of government stimulus in a really bad outcome.
contrarian🗣 Eric Townsendconviction: lowS&P 500Iranfiscal stimulus
So what: Counterintuitive: a geopolitical shock could be net bullish for equities if it triggers stimulus expectations.
“the stock market might actually just celebrate expectations of government stimulus in the event of a really bad outcome” ▶ 3:42
Dollar, gold, and rates 5
Townsend expects DXY (holding ~99.50) to challenge or break the ~100.50 top of its range on the next round of Iran military escalation, but to turn south if a real deal materializes.
prediction🗣 Eric Townsendconviction: mediumUS dollarDXY

“we're holding 99.50 and the chart looks like it's set to challenge the top of the trading range, which is about 100.50 on the Dixie chart” ▶ 5:21
Ceresna sees the dollar story in the crosses: complacency on Japan and Europe could break if the crisis persists, with EUR/USD breaking below 1.16 as the first warning of a broader dollar rally driven by euro and yen weakness from energy-import stress.

Townsend expects gold to test its 200-day MA (~$4,400) but doubts it holds if the Iran standoff persists and oil rises, projecting a retest of the early-March ~$4,100 low or lower before an eventual bull market once the Strait reopens.
prediction🗣 Eric Townsendconviction: mediumgold

≈ “I'm very likely going to retest that $4,100 low from early March, and maybe even take it out to the downside for lower numbers still” paraphrase

Ceresna expects the 10-year Treasury yield to come back up toward last month's highs, because the unresolved oil crisis keeps inflation elevated and pressures bonds.
prediction🗣 Patrick Ceresnaconviction: medium10-year Treasuryinflationcrude oil

Invest Like the Best · Patrick O'Shaughnessy 2 episode(s) · 95 insights

Legendary Investor Dan Loeb on AI, Credit, & Third Point’s $25B Strategy73 min48 insights2026-05-28
▶ Watch on YouTube
Dan Loeb (Third Point, ~$25B) lays out a bullish, non-bubble thesis on AI/semis as his single largest sector exposure (Nvidia at ~12-15x forward, plus semi-cap/hyperscalers), explains how Third Point's cross-capital-structure 'fulcrum security' lens let it make Twitter and XAI its largest credit positions, and argues credit, human-behavior anomalies, and corporate transactions remain durable alpha sources even as AI commoditizes pure data work.
AI/semis positioning — the biggest book is tech · 10Macro lens — oil/geopolitics and AI dominate · 3Cross-capital-structure 'fulcrum' investing — Twitter & XAI · 4Third Point's evolution — from event-driven value to quality + credit platform · 7Where alpha survives AI — human behavior, credit, deals · 5Activism & governance · 5International & non-US opportunity set · 4Insurance/liability-side strategy · 2Mistakes, AI-short risk, and the modern analyst · 5AI as a research tool & firm process · 3
AI/semis positioning — the biggest book is tech 10
Third Point's largest sector exposure and the bulk of its hedge-fund capital is in AI semis, semi-cap equipment and hyperscalers, which Loeb calls the single most attractive sector in the market right now.
thesis🗣 Dan Loebconviction: highThird Pointsemiconductorssemi-cap equipmenthyperscalersNvidia
So what: A value-rooted, multi-strategy CIO is overweight AI infrastructure rather than taking profits; signals continued institutional support for the trade.
“Yeah, I think it's the most attractive sector right now. It's where the bulk of our capital is invested.” ▶ 43:18
Loeb argues Nvidia is a catch-up trade at roughly 12-15x forward earnings (FY27/FY28) for one of the fastest-growing companies of its size, and chose not to take profits across his semis/hyperscaler book after reviewing valuations vs growth rates.
thesis🗣 Dan Loebconviction: highNvidia
So what: Frames mega-cap AI leaders as reasonably priced on forward earnings, not bubble-valued; a low-teens multiple on a fast grower is the explicit bull case.
“there's such a catch-up trade in Nvidia at 15 times 27 12* 28 for the most dominant fastest... very fast growing company at its size” ▶ 42:36
Loeb explicitly rejects the AI-bubble comparison to the dot-com era: today's leaders fund capex off their own balance sheets and generate enormous cash, with no valuation bubble like 2000, so a bear case requires believing the capex earns no return.
contrarian🗣 Dan Loebconviction: highhyperscalersdot-com bubblecapex
So what: Counters the loudest bear thesis; the tell to watch is post-capex free cash flow and whether AI capex actually yields returns.
“it's very different from the dot bubble which we were short... you don't have the valuation bubble now on those companies that you had on... those companies back in those days” ▶ 44:19
The SOX semiconductor index is up ~40% this year, an event Loeb says he's never seen, driven by fundamentals in semis/semi-cap/memory being far stronger than the already-high expectations.
data_point🗣 Dan Loebconviction: highSOXsemiconductorsmemorysemi-cap equipment
So what: Quantifies the magnitude of the move and grounds it in earnings, not just multiple expansion.
“the socks is up 40%. I... don't think I've ever seen an event like that” ▶ 3:44
Loeb dates the semiconductor regime change to Nvidia's blowout March quarter ~3 years ago, after which semis went from being 'roadkill'/'left for dead' to the market's central trade, and says latecomers could still play catch-up.
anecdote🗣 Dan Loebconviction: highNvidiasemiconductors
So what: Frames the AI trade as still investable years in; pattern argues against assuming you've 'missed it.'
“semiconductors were kind of left for dead. They were like roadkill in the market... that all changed when Nvidia reported its March results three years ago” ▶ 3:52
Loeb uses Jensen Huang's AI 'stack' (power/energy at the base, then chips/infrastructure, then LLMs/software/applications) as his core mental model and says Third Point has played it via industrials, infrastructure and hyperscalers.
framework🗣 Dan Loebconviction: highJensen HuangNvidiaenergy/powerindustrialsLLMshyperscalers
So what: A reusable map for expressing AI exposure across the whole value chain, including unglamorous power/industrials at the base.
“starting with uh power and energy at the bottom and... chips and infrastructure and moving up through LMS and software and applications” ▶ 3:20
Loeb tracks competing AI compute ecosystems — Nvidia vs Trainium vs TPU — and their relative strength as the key driver of how value flows through the different hyperscalers.
framework🗣 Dan Loebconviction: mediumNvidiaTrainiumTPUGoogleAmazonhyperscalers
So what: Custom-silicon adoption (Trainium/TPU) vs merchant GPUs is the swing factor for hyperscaler and Nvidia relative performance.
“thinking about... the Nvidia the tranium and the TPU ecosystems and how those play out and relative strength and how that plays through the different hyperscalers”
Loeb names Nvidia, Anthropic, and 'Elon World' (Elon Musk's companies collectively) as the three most consequential companies today and uses them as his prism for understanding how AI flows through markets.
framework🗣 Dan Loebconviction: highNvidiaAnthropicElon MuskXAITeslaSpaceX
So what: Identifies which private/public entities he watches as leading indicators for the whole complex.
“the probably the three most consequential companies today. Nvidia, Anthropic, and Elon World, you know, all of his companies collectively” ▶ 4:36
Loeb cites Anthropic's revenue growth, product adoption and next-generation models as evidence that enterprise AI is 'barely scratching the surface,' placing him firmly in the AI optimist camp.
thesis🗣 Dan Loebconviction: highAnthropicenterprise software
So what: Bullish read on AI application-layer monetization runway; supports continued enterprise AI spend.
“You look at Anthropic's revenue growth and the adoption and the usefulness of its products... We're... barely scratching the surface” ▶ 44:38
Loeb argues you can no longer 'punt on tech' and focus only on industrials/consumer/healthcare; being a tech investor is now mandatory because tech is a big, compounding share of the economy that affects everything else.
thesis🗣 Dan Loebconviction: hightechnology sector
So what: A self-described non-native-tech, value-rooted manager declaring tech literacy non-optional — a signal for allocator due diligence.
“I think you have to be a tech person today. It's such a... big and growing and compounding part of the economy. It affects everything else.” ▶ 0:07
Macro lens — oil/geopolitics and AI dominate 3
Loeb says the conventional macro variables (growth, unemployment, inflation, rates, currencies, gold, crypto) are currently trumped by just two factors: oil (driven by war/geopolitics) and AI (spending/infrastructure plus its societal/economic impact).
framework🗣 Dan Loebconviction: highoilgeopoliticsAIinflationrates
So what: Tells you which macro inputs a top CIO is actually weighting; oil and AI capex are the dashboard, not the usual prints.
“all that stuff is trumped right now by two things... Where's oil and that's going to be dictated by what happens in the war and geopolitics and what's happening with AI” ▶ 2:09
Loeb is tactical over strategic and deliberately avoids minute-to-minute news, focusing only on what's relevant to the economy and his positions to avoid being driven crazy by noise.
framework🗣 Dan Loebconviction: mediumThird Point
So what: Process insight: filter the firehose to position-relevant signal; an 'essentialism' discipline applied to information.
“I try not to get too obsessed with the minute-to-minute stuff because that will drive you crazy. I try to be a little more tactical than strategic.” ▶ 1:47
Loeb invokes Eric Schmidt's ~2013 Davos warning that accelerating technological disruption is not an anomaly, arguing innovation has since 'logarithmically accelerated' and investors must adapt continuously rather than expect mean-reversion to steady growth.
causal🗣 Dan Loebconviction: highEric SchmidtAItechnology sector
So what: Frames pace of change as accelerating, not reverting — argues for adaptive strategy and against static value frameworks.
“Hold on to your seats... things are only going to accelerate from here. And he was really right.” ▶ 0:12
Cross-capital-structure 'fulcrum' investing — Twitter & XAI 4
Third Point made the Twitter financing debt its largest credit position at the time, buying at ~96-97 cents on the dollar for a ~12% yield when most credit investors were too scared to touch it, after Morgan Stanley resold the deeply underwater Musk-acquisition debt near par.
anecdote🗣 Dan Loebconviction: highTwitterXElon MuskMorgan Stanleycredit
So what: Concrete contrarian credit trade: buying hated, mispriced acquisition debt at a fat yield when underlying business value supports it.
“Most credit investors were really scared and nervous to buy that even though it was like 96 97 cents on the dollar. It was yielding around a 12% yield.” ▶ 40:24
Third Point bought XAI's debt financing when almost no credit investors would, despite no cash flow, ~$2B revenue and a ~$20B enterprise value, because its equity/private-investing knowledge gave it conviction XAI was a real business.
anecdote🗣 Dan Loebconviction: highXAIElon Muskcredit
So what: Demonstrates lending into pre-cash-flow AI businesses using equity-side conviction — a template for AI-infrastructure credit underwriting.
“there was no cash flow... $2 billion in revenues and a $20 billion uh enterprise value, but we were very comfortable that this was a real business” ▶ 40:57
Loeb's central edge is identifying the 'fulcrum security' — the part of the capital structure with the best risk/reward — using a comprehensive view across debt and equity; in the Credit Suisse/UBS deal the holdco paper had the most upside while the prefs (AT1) were wiped out.
framework🗣 Dan Loebconviction: highCredit SuisseUBSAT1 preferredholdco debtopco debt
So what: Reusable restructuring framework; in stressed deals, placement in the cap structure (holdco vs opco vs pref) determines outcome more than the directional call.
“The fulcrum there was actually the hold coat paper that had the most upside but the opco paper... also did well. The pref was wiped out.” ▶ 39:09
Loeb personally 'supersizes' specific opportunities like Twitter and XAI even though he is only the hedge-fund PM and isn't on the investment committees of Third Point's private-credit, CLO, structured-credit or high-yield businesses, each of which has its own PM.
anecdote🗣 Dan Loebconviction: mediumThird PointTwitterXAI
So what: Clarifies governance/structure of the $25B platform; CIO intervention is selective and opportunity-driven, not blanket.
“I will come in if there's like an interesting opportunity like we superersize both of those both Twitter and XAI when I got involved” ▶ 41:48
Third Point's evolution — from event-driven value to quality + credit platform 7
Third Point's roots are in event-driven/distressed credit; Loeb's early framework (from Joel Greenblatt's 'You Can Be a Stock Market Genius') exploited cheap newly created securities — spin-offs, demutualizations, privatizations, post-reorg equities — sold indiscriminately by holders, with managers who sandbagged numbers at the time their incentives were set.
framework🗣 Dan Loebconviction: highJoel Greenblattspin-offsVisaMastercardevent-driven
So what: The structural-mispricing playbook (liquidity gaps + sandbagged guidance + underoptimized subsidiaries) that Loeb says still works today.
“a new security is often created which is priced and valued at a very cheap price because of a lack of liquidity” ▶ 7:43
Loeb says investors who underperformed or didn't survive the last decade were the ones stubbornly stuck on deep-value low multiples and unwilling to move into higher-multiple, faster-growing, higher-ROIC 'quality' businesses.
causal🗣 Dan Loebconviction: highvalue investingquality investing
So what: A pointed warning that rigid low-multiple value was the losing posture; adaptability into quality/growth is framed as survival.
≈ “people who have underperformed or haven't survived the last decade... were really stuck on the idea of... deep value low multiples and being really stubborn” paraphrase ▶ 10:44
Loeb says the best opportunity today overlays the old event-driven mispricing lens with a quality/business-quality lens, citing 'The Outsiders' (capital allocators like Danaher, TransDigm) and Cunningham's 'Quality Investing' as the most influential books on his evolution.
framework🗣 Dan Loebconviction: highThe OutsidersQuality InvestingDanaherTransDigm
So what: Combining structural cheapness with durable moats/high ROIC is his stated current edge; a reading list for the approach.
