Loeb warns that last year was the worst for ostensibly high-quality compounders because AI disruption rapidly made many 'apparently high-quality' businesses less so — a regime shift that broke the buy-and-hold quality playbook.
contrarian🗣 Dan Loebconviction: highquality investingAIsoftware
So what: Even moaty compounders carry AI-obsolescence risk now; quality must be re-underwritten against disruption, not assumed durable.
“a lot of these companies that appeared to be super high quality, it's probably the worst year... because the disruption of AI, a lot of these apparently highquality companies um very rapidly became less” ▶ 12:27
Third Point is a multi-business platform of ~$25B: the flagship hedge fund grew from $3M to ~$9B (now ~30% credit), plus a ~$7B CLO business, ~$3B (≈30%) in structured/corporate credit, ~$1B insurance credit, a couple billion in asbestos liabilities, a small new private-credit unit, and a venture-capital business.
data_point🗣 Dan Loebconviction: highThird PointCLOstructured creditprivate creditventure capitalinsurance
So what: Maps the actual AUM composition; ~60% credit overall makes Third Point as much a credit shop as an equity hedge fund.
“which started at three million. It's now about $9 billion today... we have um a CLO business with about $7 billion in it” ▶ 36:23
Loeb dialed back hedge-fund risk going into 'the war' and for the first time since 2009 ran more credit than equity exposure, before quickly rotating risk back up to a more-equity-than-credit stance.
data_point🗣 Dan Loebconviction: highThird Pointcreditequities
So what: Shows real-time defensive rotation into credit at stress and back into equity — the optionality of a multi-asset book in practice.
“for the first time since 2009 we did have more credit exposure than equity but we very quickly have taken that back up” ▶ 36:58
Loeb describes the hedge fund's typical equity posture as roughly 110% long / 30-40% short, though it moves around substantially with the risk environment.
data_point🗣 Dan Loebconviction: mediumThird Pointlong/short equity
So what: Concrete gross/net framing of the flagship's equity book for sizing the directional bet behind his AI bullishness.
“our equity book is generically around 110 long by 30 or 40 short but that can be all over the place” ▶ 36:44
Where alpha survives AI — human behavior, credit, deals 5
Loeb argues the durable edge for fundamental investors is exploiting forced behavior by quants, CTAs and pod shops whose risk metrics force selling into declines — irrational for long-term holders but baked into those models — creating persistent anomalies.
causal🗣 Dan Loebconviction: highquantsCTAsmulti-strategy podsfundamental investing
So what: Structural-flow alpha: fade forced de-grossing/stop-outs; the proliferation of risk-managed strategies manufactures the dislocations to trade against.
≈ “They have... risk metrics which have forced selling on the way down. And so they do the opposite of what it might be rational” paraphrase
Loeb cites repeated 'good-numbers, falling-stock' episodes (Nvidia after a monster quarter, Micron up ~80% revenue, Meta years ago) as proof that expectations getting too high causes sell-offs on strong prints — the 'Wile E. Coyote' moment when there's no marginal buyer left.
framework🗣 Dan Loebconviction: highNvidiaMicronMeta
So what: Trade the expectations gap, not the fundamentals; positioning/exhaustion of buyers can tank a stock on great results.
“stock went up was like wy coyote. There was no one else to buy the stock. get tanked” ▶ 21:24
Loeb predicts AI will not eliminate the capital allocator's job for the next couple of years because companies still need to raise money, securities must change hands, and a human interface is needed; a fully AI-managed capital system is highly unlikely.
prediction🗣 Dan Loebconviction: mediumAIcapital markets
So what: Tempers AI-replaces-investors fears; near-term, intermediation and human judgment persist as moats.
“I think it's highly unlikely that there will be a completely... AI managed capital system” ▶ 18:41
Loeb argues AI cannot do private equity, creditors'-committee work, restructurings or high-touch private credit because deals require human negotiation — so the private and stressed-credit end of the continuum is structurally protected from automation.
thesis🗣 Dan Loebconviction: highprivate equityprivate creditrestructuringAI
So what: Reinforces why Loeb leans into credit/private markets as AI-proof alpha; the harder-to-automate the process, the more durable the edge.
“It's hard to imagine the computers sitting on a creditor's committee and working through the capital structure and being able to transact.” ▶ 23:27
Loeb invokes 'there's nothing new under the sun' (Reminiscences of a Stock Operator / Ecclesiastes): hysteria, bubbles, panics and the extremes of human nature won't be removed by AI, and may even get re-encoded under labels like 'risk management.'
framework🗣 Dan Loebconviction: highReminiscences of a Stock Operatorbehavioral finance
So what: Behavioral cycles remain the bedrock alpha source even in an AI-driven market; AI may amplify, not dampen, herding.
“the thing that... doesn't change is... hysteras... bubbles... panics and just the extremes of human nature both optimistically and pessimistically” ▶ 19:48
Activism & governance 5
Loeb insists boards' primary duty is shareholder value and criticizes the Business Roundtable's stakeholder pivot as a distraction; he says good treatment of communities/employees/products is part of — not in conflict with — creating shareholder value.
thesis🗣 Dan Loebconviction: highBusiness Roundtablecorporate governanceMilton FriedmanWarren Buffett
So what: A clear shareholder-primacy stance from an activist; signals which boards he'd view as drifting from fiduciary duty.
≈ “the business roundt said we're no longer going to... say that... the board's responsibility is primarily to drive shareholder value... I think it was a distraction” paraphrase
Loeb says the main source of bad governance is boards letting loyalty to an underperforming CEO override their duty to shareholders, since boards set strategy and accountability but don't run companies.
framework🗣 Dan Loebconviction: highcorporate governanceboardsCEO
So what: Defines the activist trigger: CEO-board capture; watch for boards too deferential to weak management.
“they let their loyalty or relationship to a CEO who's not up to the job overshadow their duty to shareholders. That's probably the main thing” ▶ 28:30
Loeb identifies social pressure via writing and PR as activism's most effective lever — alongside financial (bids) and legal (proxy contests, litigation, information requests) levers — because public letters shake up boards and rally other shareholders.
framework🗣 Dan Loebconviction: highactivismThird Point
So what: Explains the strategic role of Loeb's famous letters; reputational pressure can move outcomes without a proxy fight.
“Social pressure is actually a very effective way and I think the best way to... put social pressure on a company is through writing and PR efforts” ▶ 30:42
Loeb confirms his activism often targets high-status companies/CEOs not earning that status; in Sotheby's ('Sabes') Third Point bought 9.9%, replaced a CEO lacking art/collector depth with MSG's Tad Smith, cleaned up operations, and the company was ultimately sold.
anecdote🗣 Dan Loebconviction: highSotheby'sTad SmithMSGThird Point
So what: Illustrates the 'status gap' screen and the operational/CEO-change activist playbook that produced a realized exit.
“We brought in guy named Tad Smith who was terrific from MSG... cleaned up the operations, improved the technology and then... they sold the company.” ▶ 33:55
Loeb says classic 'fix the bad management' activism mostly survives in the sub-$2B market cap space, but Third Point now prefers backing already-great management over turnarounds, because badly-run companies tend to have ten times more hidden problems ('a morass').
thesis🗣 Dan Loebconviction: mediumsmall capsactivismThird Point
So what: Signals a strategic shift away from deep turnaround activism toward 'quality + cheering on good management'; small-cap mispricing is where pure activism remains.
“we'd much rather invest in... a great company with awesome management... than... find something that but for... mismanagement the company would be worth a lot more” ▶ 34:49
International & non-US opportunity set 4
Loeb is more bullish on Korea, Taiwan and Japan as hunting grounds for great companies, sees Israel as a niche market with a top-performing portfolio holding despite the war, and views European local-economy-dependent businesses as challenged by regulation and an anti-capitalist attitude.
thesis🗣 Dan Loebconviction: mediumJapanKoreaTaiwanIsraelEuropeRolls-RoyceASML
So what: Geographic tilt: Asia ex-China and select Israel over domestic-Europe; he still owns export/quality European names like Rolls-Royce and ASML.
“there's a lot going on in Korea, Taiwan, Japan. I'm probably more bullish on them... The European markets are... just tough right now given the regulatory environment” ▶ 46:57
Loeb says Japanese activism is hard and slow — Third Point's Sony stake (once ~7%) took ~5 years to bear fruit — but the government wants reform while entrenched management resists; Japan is now breaking up cross-shareholdings and penalizing companies trading below book value.
anecdote🗣 Dan Loebconviction: highSonyJapanKaz Hiraicross-shareholdings
So what: Japan reform (ROIC focus, below-book penalties, unwinding cross-holdings) is a real, government-backed tailwind for value/activist plays — just slow.
“They're breaking up some of these cross shareholdings. They're... penalizing companies that trade at discounts to book value.”
Loeb helped seed Japan's corporate-governance reform by co-writing an AEI paper (with Larry Lindsey and Niall Ferguson) urging Abe's 'three arrows' to add a fourth focus on corporate governance and return on invested capital, which was picked up by the WSJ and adopted.
anecdote🗣 Dan Loebconviction: highJapanLarry LindseyNiall FergusonAEIWall Street JournalROIC
So what: Shows the policy origins of Japan's ROIC/governance push that now underpins the multi-year Japan re-rating trade.
“my suggestion was that they needed to include corporate governance and in particular a focus on return on invested capital as part of their three arrow strategy” ▶ 50:53
Loeb argues Middle East states — Bahrain, UAE, Saudi, Morocco, Azerbaijan — have become better US allies than NATO with far higher growth rates and faster technology adoption, and calls the region the most vibrant and interesting part of the world to invest.
thesis🗣 Dan Loebconviction: mediumMiddle EastUAESaudi ArabiaBahrainMoroccoNATO
So what: Flags Gulf/MENA growth and tech adoption as an emerging allocation theme tied to geopolitical realignment.
“who would have thought... Bahrain, the Emirates, Saudi, Morocco, Azar Bjan would be better allies to the US than NATO?” ▶ 1:08:39
Insurance/liability-side strategy 2
Loeb says the original Third Point Re thesis (2010 Bermuda reinsurer investing float in the hedge fund — a barbell with treasuries for tax deferral and leverage, modeled on Greenlight Re at 140% of book) failed because P&C reinsurance turned sharply for the worse; the fix was to pivot to plain-vanilla annuities.
anecdote🗣 Dan Loebconviction: highThird Point ReGreenlight RereinsuranceannuitiesKelsoPine Brook
So what: Candid post-mortem on hedge-fund-reinsurer 'float' model; annuities + credit, not P&C + equity float, is the durable structure.
“we had the right idea, but we had the... wrong insurance vehicle. We were doing PNC insurance. We really should have just done plain vanilla annuities” ▶ 57:25
Third Point merged its reinsurer into the UK closed-end fund Third Point Offshore Investors, redomiciled it Guernsey-to-Cayman, and repurposed it from a hedge-fund feeder into an insurance company that will write reinsurance and primary annuities, with the money managed in private/structured credit, whole-loan mortgages, real-estate direct lending and IG corporates.
data_point🗣 Dan Loebconviction: highThird Point Offshore Investorsannuitiesstructured creditwhole-loan mortgagesprivate credit
So what: Maps Third Point's Apollo/Athene-style permanent-capital build; annuity liabilities feed a credit-heavy asset engine plus equity in junior structured tranches and growth equity.
“we repurposed it from a closed end investor in my hedge fund into an insurance company... third point manages the money in private credit, structured credit, whole loan mortgages” ▶ 58:24
Mistakes, AI-short risk, and the modern analyst 5
Loeb calls FTX his hardest lesson — it looked great and blockchain-verifiable with good co-investors, but wasn't what it seemed; Third Point now checks bank balances as basic diligence, and Loeb notes SBF's venture picks (Cursor, Anthropic, Solana) showed a genuine nose for value.
anecdote🗣 Dan Loebconviction: highFTXSBFCursorAnthropicSolanadue diligence
So what: Diligence lesson — verify cash/bank balances on private deals; also a backhanded signal on the quality of the FTX/Alameda venture book.
“I will say that now our due diligence process, we definitely like check bank balances and do like the most basic due diligence” ▶ 1:00:01
Loeb says Third Point's recent mistakes came from being too confident that AI wouldn't disrupt certain info-services businesses or that incumbents' proprietary data was protected, even as it made good short bets on other AI-disrupted names; he expects an eventual shakeout with some 'phoenixes' rising.
contrarian🗣 Dan Loebconviction: mediuminfo-servicesAI disruptionshort selling
So what: Warning that 'proprietary data moat' is a weaker defense against AI than assumed; info-services incumbents are vulnerable longs and crowded-short candidates.
“we thought we knew better that AI wasn't really going to... affect this part of the infoservices business or these guys had... proprietary information” ▶ 1:00:50
Loeb says the great analyst today is a 'junior Gavin Baker' who deeply understands an industry and the nuances of a technology — not the 1990s archetype who could fast-build models and decode complex restructurings (as he once did with the Drexel Burnham bankruptcy claims).
framework🗣 Dan Loebconviction: highGavin BakerDrexel Burnhamanalysts
So what: Hiring/skill thesis: domain and technology depth beats financial-engineering speed in the AI era — relevant for talent and research-edge assessment.
“now it's somebody who's like a Gavin Baker type, a junior Gavin Baker, somebody who understands a company or understands an industry and understands the nuances of a technology” ▶ 1:06:35
Loeb cites Casey's General Stores as a top performer that looked like a tech stock because it was really a pizza chain masquerading as a convenience-store chain — surfaced by an analyst who went to Texas and ate the pizza, exemplifying gumshoe insight over data.
anecdote🗣 Dan Loebconviction: mediumCasey's General Stores
So what: Reframes a 'boring' retailer as a high-margin food business — the kind of non-obvious reclassification that drives mispricing alpha.
“they were not a convenience store chain... they were a pizza chain... masquerading as convenience stores” ▶ 1:06:57
Loeb says Danaher was his most instructive investment — a ~4-year winner that taught him how the Danaher Business System (Kaizen continuous improvement, accountability where underperformance is 'celebrated' because fixable, shedding low-quality units for higher-ROIC ones) creates durable quality; he sold it but recently re-entered small after the selloff.
anecdote🗣 Dan Loebconviction: highDanaherDBSLarry CulpTransDigmIngersoll Randquality investing
So what: A template for spotting operating-system-driven compounders; the recent small re-entry signals renewed (cautious) interest after weakness.
“what they have is a whole system of implementing improvement across the organization” ▶ 55:13
AI as a research tool & firm process 3
Loeb is mandating firm-wide AI adoption — hiring native computer scientists and a system integrator to coach the team, with some analysts running agents overnight burning huge token volumes — framing Claude as a tool that makes individuals autonomous self-improvers who get back proportional to what they put in.
framework🗣 Dan Loebconviction: highThird PointClaudeAI agentssystem integrators
So what: A bellwether hedge fund operationalizing AI in research workflow; signals rising buy-side AI/token spend and a coaching/integration org model.
“some people are running agents overnight and using tons and tons of tokens. Other people are... probably more like me, just using it more for queries” ▶ 1:02:40
Loeb positions Third Point as more optimistic / less doom-prone than peers on AI, expecting it to create net jobs and opportunities, and says the firm's true differentiator is the ability to default into credit investing during stressed times — a comfort few equity-rooted peers share.
thesis🗣 Dan Loebconviction: highThird PointAIcredit
So what: The stated edge is optionality — pivot to credit in a real credit cycle; relevant if a long-absent (post-2020) credit cycle finally arrives.
“the differentiator for us is that we can always... default into credit investing... I'm very comfortable investing in... incredibly stressed times” ▶ 1:03:30
Loeb says credit can't be done by tourists — it requires standing relationships across the ~$1.5T high-yield, ~$1.5T broadly-syndicated-loan and ~$6T structured-credit markets — which is why Third Point built out CLO and structured-credit franchises rather than dabbling when opportunities arise.
framework🗣 Dan Loebconviction: highhigh yieldleveraged loansstructured creditCLOThird Point
So what: Sizes the credit opportunity set and explains the relationship moat; you must be permanently wired in to capture dislocations.
“these are not markets that lend themselves to... tourism when the opportunities really come up. We're already there with the relationships” ▶ 1:04:50
Uber CEO on AI, Autonomous Vehicles, and the Future of Transportation71 min47 insights2026-06-03
▶ Watch on YouTube
Dara Khosrowshahi lays out Uber's AV strategy as a supply-aggregation play: partner with (not build) 30+ AV makers, own the demand and the fleet-ops/financing/insurance ecosystem, and treat AV as 'another trillion dollar marketplace.' He shares hard unit-economics (AVs on Uber's network run 30%+ busier; >$10B FCF on >10B trips; Uber One at 50M members growing 50%; Reserve at >$5B run-rate) and frames capital allocation as organic growth and AV commitments first, buybacks second.
AV strategy: supply aggregation over fleet ownership · 5AV unit economics and cost-down trajectory · 5Manufacturing, OEMs, and China · 3Drones and delivery · 3Regional AV rollout and regulation · 4AI internal adoption, cost, and the token economics squeeze · 5Platform, membership, and cross-sell economics · 5Supply-first growth model and underpenetrated markets · 3New verticals: hotels, travel, and on-demand-to-planned · 4Interface shift: from apps to agentic, personalized UX · 3Capital allocation and financial profile · 4Leadership, culture, and mental models · 3
AV strategy: supply aggregation over fleet ownership 5
Uber's AV thesis is to be the demand aggregator and go-to-market layer for digital-driver makers rather than build its own driver, partnering with 30+ AV companies including Waymo, Nuro, Lucid, Nvidia, Wayve, Wabi, and Pony.ai.
thesis🗣 Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)conviction: highUberUBERWaymoNuroLucidNvidiaWayvePony.aiautonomous vehicles
So what: Frames UBER as a picks-and-shovels demand layer that wins regardless of which AV maker leads, de-risking the AV transition for the equity.
“we've got over 30 partnerships now with incredible uh partners like a Whimo to a neuro and lucid to an Nvidia” ▶ 24:20
Khosrowshahi argues the AV market will mirror foundation models — no single winner, many players plus open-source/smaller models — so an aggregator that secures the most supply is structurally advantaged.
framework🗣 Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)conviction: highUberautonomous vehiclesfoundation models
So what: Implies AV economics won't concentrate into one vertically integrated owner, supporting the bull case for a neutral demand platform like UBER.
“just like you're seeing in the foundation model space there isn't going to be a single winner there are going to be many players” ▶ 24:39
Uber is building the full AV support ecosystem — depots, charging, fleet partners, financing, and autonomous insurance — and is actively collecting street data to feed partners' models so demand is instant at launch.
thesis🗣 Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)conviction: highUberautonomous vehiclesfleet operationsinsurance
So what: These services create switching costs and capture margin beyond a pure marketplace take-rate, widening UBER's AV moat.
“we're building the entire ecosystem so someone who builds a driver can get those cars in the road and then uh we have all the supporting infrastructure” ▶ 25:32
The single thing Uber controls that could cause it to lose the AV opportunity is failing to secure access to AV supply — which is why it has a partnership with 'basically every AV provider' across mobility, delivery, and freight.
thesis🗣 Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)conviction: highUberautonomous vehiclesfreight
So what: Identifies supply exclusivity/breadth as the key competitive battleground to monitor for UBER's AV thesis.
“Access to supply and that's why we are you know we have a partnership with basically every AV provider whether it's in mobility or delivery or even freight” ▶ 32:01
Khosrowshahi frames Uber–Waymo as 'coexistence' rather than pure competition, analogizing to OTAs working with hotels/airlines who also build direct channels but need incremental demand to fill capacity.
framework🗣 Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)conviction: highUberWaymoOTAMarriottDelta
So what: Suggests even AV makers with direct apps will keep cars on Uber's network for utilization, blunting the 'Waymo disintermediates Uber' bear case.
“It's a coexistence that frankly I'm quite used to coming from the travel business” ▶ 28:20
AV unit economics and cost-down trajectory 5
AVs operating on Uber's network are 30%+ busier than AVs not on the network, measured in trips and revenue per vehicle per day, materially improving ROI on expensive AV hardware.
data_point🗣 Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)conviction: highUberautonomous vehicles
So what: A concrete utilization edge Uber can sell to AV fleet owners; the 30% delta is the core ROI argument for putting cars on UBER vs. running them solo.
“AVs that are on our network are 30% or more busy than let's say one PVs who aren't using our network” ▶ 25:55
Khosrowshahi expects AV hardware cost to fall ~30-40% per generation, citing a Lucid/Nuro midsize vehicle Uber is co-developing at roughly $60-70K, which he says is low enough to drop transportation cost for the mass market.
data_point🗣 Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)conviction: highLucidNuroUberautonomous vehicles
So what: Quantifies the AV cost curve; the $60-70K per-vehicle target is a checkpoint for whether AV ride economics can scale beyond premium urban use.
“the lucid midsize that they are building for us and neuro together it would be 60 $70,000 car” ▶ 32:56
Each AV vehicle drives roughly three to four times what a human driver does, so a single AV's effective throughput is substantially higher than a human-operated car.
data_point🗣 Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)conviction: highautonomous vehiclesUber
So what: Implies far fewer vehicles are needed to serve demand, a double-edged dynamic that could constrain manufacturing scale needed to drive unit costs down.
“Each of these vehicles probably drives three to four times what a human does” ▶ 34:56
Khosrowshahi invokes the original Uber experience — the market was sized to the taxi market but Uber became multiple times larger — to predict AV will similarly expand the total transportation market as cost falls and demand rises elastically.
framework🗣 Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)conviction: highUberautonomous vehiclestaxi market
So what: Supports a TAM-expansion narrative for UBER under AV rather than cannibalization of existing rides.
“When Uber was introduced early on, people were sizing the market based on the taxi market. We're multiple times bigger than the taxi market.” ▶ 33:16
Khosrowshahi sizes the AV opportunity as 'another trillion dollar marketplace' on top of Uber's existing business.
prediction🗣 Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)conviction: highUberautonomous vehicles
So what: Anchors the long-term bull TAM for UBER's AV optionality.
“We think it's another trillion dollar marketplace.” ▶ 32:40
Manufacturing, OEMs, and China 3
Traditional OEMs are now investing in L4-ready systems because they see Level-4 autonomy as much closer than they thought; Khosrowshahi expects legacy automakers to reach it in 2-4 years.
prediction🗣 Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)conviction: highOEMsautonomous vehiclesL4 autonomy
So what: Signals AV supply will broaden from startups to legacy auto over a 2-4 year window, a timeline for AV-exposed auto and supplier names.
“I think you'll see the traditional players get there over the next, you know, 2 to four years” ▶ 34:42
Khosrowshahi says Chinese manufacturing capability and bill-of-materials cost for AVs is 'unrivaled,' and a low-cost Foxconn-style AV contract manufacturer exists in China while the West is still working on one.
contrarian🗣 Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)conviction: highChinaFoxconnautonomous vehiclesOEMs
So what: Flags a Western AV-manufacturing cost gap vs. China — a strategic and geopolitical risk/opportunity for Western AV scaling and a potential cost disadvantage.
“The the Chinese capabilities in terms of manufacturing both in terms of quality and cost at this point is unrivaled.” ▶ 35:33
AV production is currently in the hundreds-to-thousands of vehicles and must ramp through tens of thousands to hundreds of thousands before costs fully come down.
data_point🗣 Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)conviction: mediumautonomous vehiclesOEMs
So what: Sets the scale milestones to watch before AV unit economics reach mass-market thresholds.
“right now the cars are being manufactured in the hundreds and the thousands. It'll get to the tens of thousands. It'll get to the hundreds of thousands.” ▶ 34:49
Drones and delivery 3
The binding constraint on delivery drones is battery density — the battery's ability to lift itself plus payload while meeting range and recharging needs — not regulation or software.
causal🗣 Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)conviction: highdronesZiplineJobybatteries
So what: Points investors to battery-density progress (not air-traffic rules) as the gating metric for drone-delivery economics.
“The biggest issue is just um battery density and the ability of essentially battery to lift itself and then lift the payload” ▶ 36:07
Khosrowshahi expects food and grocery drone delivery to hit real scale over the next two to five years, initially costing more than human delivery, eventually cutting delivery times to 10-15 minutes from 25-30.
prediction🗣 Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)conviction: mediumdronesUber Eatsdelivery
So what: A 2-5 year horizon and an explicit 'costs more first' caveat temper near-term drone-delivery economics for UBER and delivery peers.
“drones for food and other grocery are going to start hitting real scale over the next two to five years” ▶ 36:25
Khosrowshahi singles out Zipline as an interesting company and notes Joby is building passenger drones, while saying drones won't be a big part of daily life two years out but will normalize over 5-10 years.
anecdote🗣 Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)conviction: mediumZiplineJobydrones
So what: Names specific drone players worth tracking and dampens near-term hype with a 5-10 year normalization timeline.
Regional AV rollout and regulation 4
The Middle East (Abu Dhabi, Dubai, Saudi Arabia) is moving fastest on AVs with entrepreneurial regulators and no-safety-driver services live today, while New York and Boston will take longer.
data_point🗣 Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)conviction: highAbu DhabiDubaiSaudi Arabiaautonomous vehiclesNew YorkBoston
So what: Regulatory posture, not technology, dictates AV geography; Gulf and Sun Belt markets lead, mapping where AV revenue scales first.
≈ “Middle East is going fast... they are on it. The regulators are very entrepreneurial.” paraphrase ▶ 37:17
Uber expects to launch commercial robotaxi pilots in Europe, including London, before the end of the year, as European regulators and OEMs move to avoid being left behind.
prediction🗣 Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)conviction: highUberLondonEuropeautonomous vehiclesOEMs
So what: A near-term, datable catalyst (London robotaxi pilot by year-end) for UBER's AV narrative in Europe.
“I think we will have pilots for example in London before the end of the year” ▶ 38:02
In Austin and Atlanta, where Uber runs two large Waymo partnerships, human drivers are making more money and driver sign-ups are increasing, suggesting AVs are adding incremental demand rather than displacing drivers early.
data_point🗣 Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)conviction: highAustinAtlantaWaymoUberdrivers
So what: Early evidence against the 'AVs gut driver supply/earnings' thesis; a key data point to track as AV penetration deepens.
“drivers in Austin and Atlanta where we have two big partnerships with Whimo, drivers on the Uber platform, they're they're making more money” ▶ 31:19
Khosrowshahi's biggest premortem risk for the AV opportunity is public/regulatory backlash — AI and AVs are unpopular with the general public over job and cost fears — requiring Uber to move at 'the pace society is prepared for.'
contrarian🗣 Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)conviction: highautonomous vehiclesAIregulatorsUber
So what: Names social/regulatory acceptance — not technology — as the top tail risk to the AV thesis for the whole industry.
“how unpopular it is with the general public... otherwise there will be a backlash” ▶ 30:25
AI internal adoption, cost, and the token economics squeeze 5
Uber blew through its entire annual AI budget in a single quarter, forcing it to meter headcount increases as AI-boosted engineer throughput rises.
data_point🗣 Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)conviction: highUberAIOpenAIAnthropic
So what: Concrete signal that frontier-model inference costs are large and real; AI productivity is being traded against slower hiring — a margin and opex dynamic to watch.
“We blew through our AI budget in a quarter, you know, for the whole year essentially.” ▶ 19:08
Uber's AI playbook is to explore with expensive frontier models (OpenAI, Claude) then migrate scaled experiences to cheaper, more token-efficient or open-source models to control cost.
framework🗣 Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)conviction: highUberOpenAIAnthropicopen sourceAI
So what: A reusable 'explore expensive, scale cheap' framework; bullish for cheaper/open-source inference demand once use cases productionize.
“we're using the more expensive models to explore... once we scale some of these experiences... we'll look to bring in more efficient models that are more efficient on a token basis or open source” ▶ 20:14
Uber engineers in India are now driving 10x the code commits they used to by using autonomous agents, with AI uptake appearing unpredictably across the org.
data_point🗣 Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)conviction: highUberAIIndiaautonomous agents
So what: A tangible developer-productivity data point supporting the AI-coding thesis and the case for slower engineering headcount growth.
“we've got devs in India who all of a sudden are driving 10x the code commits that they used to” ▶ 18:11
Khosrowshahi pushes teams to rebuild processes from first principles with AI rather than merely optimizing 20-30% of an existing process, arguing ground-up redesign is where the real value is.
framework🗣 Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)conviction: highUberAI
So what: Distinguishes incremental AI gains from transformational ones — a lens for judging which companies will actually re-rate from AI.
≈ “to rebuild it from the ground up, that's much harder... I'm coming in and really pushing the teams to think about processes from a first principles standpoint” paraphrase ▶ 17:43
Uber's feed and search models are now ~10,000x bigger than its older models, enabling universal search across rides/eats/grocery and predicting a rider's destination ~75% of the time as a one-tap interaction.
data_point🗣 Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)conviction: highUberAIsearchmachine learning
So what: Quantifies how larger models drive conversion/utilization; the 75% destination-prediction stat is a concrete monetization lever for UBER.
“today we can guess when you take an Uber ride. 3/4 of the time we can guess where you're going and it's just a one tap interaction” ▶ 21:54
Platform, membership, and cross-sell economics 5
Uber One has 50 million members growing 50% year-on-year and is positioned 'like Netflix' — more content for the same price (mobility discounts, surge protection, free delivery, 10% back on hotels).
data_point🗣 Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)conviction: highUber OneUberNetflix
So what: Membership scale and growth rate are a core retention/margin engine; the 50M/50% figures are key KPIs for the UBER bull case.
“we have a Uber 1 membership program, 50 million members now, growing 50% year-on-year” ▶ 39:53
Uber One members are unprofitable in their first year — Uber loses money on a member's early transactions — but become more profitable over years 2-4, mirroring Amazon Prime's variable-cost membership economics.
framework🗣 Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)conviction: highUber OneAmazon PrimeUber
So what: Explains why membership growth can depress near-term margins while building long-term LTV — important for reading UBER's reported profitability.
“the first year of membership is a year where we lose money on you, but we're going to make money on you 2, three, four years from now” ▶ 45:49
About 13% of Uber Eats bookings now come from the mobility business, giving Uber Eats a structural free-customer-acquisition advantage over pure-play delivery competitors.
data_point🗣 Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)conviction: highUber EatsUberdelivery
So what: Quantifies the cross-platform CAC advantage that lets Uber Eats win #1 internationally with higher margins than single-product rivals like DoorDash.
“about 13% of Eats bookings are actually coming from the mobility business” ▶ 39:31
Uber's cross-platform flywheel is its structural edge: more services drive more interaction and retention, letting it reach #1 in Eats internationally with higher margins than single-product competitors.
thesis🗣 Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)conviction: highUberUber EatsDoorDash
So what: The multi-product retention loop is the durable competitive advantage underpinning UBER's margin and share gains vs. mono-line peers.
“the platform for us is a structural advantage we have versus other model line players. We get a bunch of free customers uh from our mobility business.” ▶ 39:43
Uber has only signed up an estimated 40-50% of the serviceable restaurants/merchants in cities where it operates, leaving substantial selection-driven growth runway in Eats.
data_point🗣 Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)conviction: mediumUber EatsUber
So what: Implies meaningful merchant-selection upside remains in core markets, a tailwind for Eats GBV growth.
“probably signed up around 40 to 50% of the restaurants and or merchants that can be on our platform” ▶ 38:53
Supply-first growth model and underpenetrated markets 3
Uber is fundamentally a supply company — recruiting drivers, merchants, and couriers first, after which demand follows — the inverse of the demand-first model Khosrowshahi ran at Expedia.
framework🗣 Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)conviction: highUberExpedia
So what: Reframes UBER's growth engine around supply density; investors should track supply acquisition (drivers/merchants) as the leading indicator.
“we are a supply company... the first thing we do is actually go out and recruit drivers... and then just the demand shows up” ▶ 22:44
A significant growth vector is expanding supply beyond top cities into sparse suburban and smaller markets — the 'next 50' and 'next 200' cities rather than the top 10.
thesis🗣 Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)conviction: highUbersuburban markets
So what: Geographic density expansion into less-penetrated markets is a concrete, underappreciated growth lever for UBER.
“Not the top 10 cities in a country but the next 50 cities, the next 200 cities” ▶ 23:25
Khosrowshahi emphasizes drivers/couriers/merchants experience P95 bugs weekly (vs. monthly for consumers) because they're on the app 6-10 hours a day, so supplier-side reliability is a higher-stakes quality lever than consumer UX.
framework🗣 Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)conviction: mediumUberdriverscouriers
So what: Supplier retention hinges on tail-latency reliability; an operational edge that compounds supply density and the marketplace moat.
“A P95 bug is happening every single week for a driver who's on the app, you know, 6 hours a day” ▶ 42:06
New verticals: hotels, travel, and on-demand-to-planned 4
Uber is entering hotels via an Expedia supply deal, passing the vast majority of the economics back to Uber One members (10% off hotels, 20% off 10,000 hotels) to deepen platform integration rather than maximize take.
thesis🗣 Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)conviction: highUberExpediaUber Onehotels
So what: Hotels are a member-retention play, not a margin grab; signals Uber will keep using new verticals to feed Uber One LTV.
“we happened to make a deal with Expedia, my my old company, and we're essentially giving the vast majority of the economics of that deal back to Uber 1 members” ▶ 49:01
Uber Reserve has scaled to over $5 billion run-rate from nothing 5-6 years ago by re-engineering the backend to 99%+ pre-booked reliability, proving riders will pay more for reliability and that Uber can move its brand from on-demand to planned.
data_point🗣 Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)conviction: highUberUber Reserve
So what: A $5B+ run-rate validates the pre-commit/planned-demand expansion that underpins the travel/hotels push — a tangible monetization proof point.
“reserve now is over $5 billion run rate. Um it didn't exist you know five six years ago” ▶ 51:31
Uber completed 1.5 billion trips for people outside their home city last year and ~15% of all trips are to/from airports, letting it identify travelers and justify the travel/hotels expansion.
data_point🗣 Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)conviction: highUberairportstravel
So what: The traveler-identification data (1.5B out-of-city trips, 15% airport mix) is the strategic rationale and asset behind UBER's move up the travel stack.
“we completed 1 and a.5 billion trips for people outside of their home city. About 15% of our trips are to and from airports.” ▶ 47:50
Khosrowshahi sees an idealized travel end-state where Uber auto-orchestrates the full trip (pre-booked airport rides, hotel arrival heads-up, phone-as-room-key) by reading flight/email data, turning Uber into an end-to-end in-market travel layer.
prediction🗣 Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)conviction: mediumUberhotelstravel
So what: Outlines a long-term travel-superapp ambition that, if realized, materially expands UBER's wallet share per traveler.
Interface shift: from apps to agentic, personalized UX 3
Khosrowshahi predicts inbound user interaction will move increasingly away from apps toward unstructured, conversational AI in ~7 years, while apps persist for outbound info (maps, car tracking) better served visually.
prediction🗣 Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)conviction: mediumUberAIagents
So what: Signals a UX paradigm shift toward agentic interfaces; relevant for how aggregators retain users when conversation replaces app surfaces.
“the inbound interaction is going to be much more unstructured and AI makes it possible” ▶ 55:22
AI lets Uber personalize its UI per user/context for the first time, breaking from the historical model of optimizing one interface to the population average.
framework🗣 Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)conviction: highUberAI
So what: Per-user UI personalization is a new conversion lever; a differentiator for scaled platforms with rich first-party data like UBER.
“Our booking experience on Uber may not be optimized to you exactly, but it's optimized to overall averages... Now, we're going to personalize it.” ▶ 55:35
Khosrowshahi reversed his prior view that marketing only acquires users and product surfaces features; storytelling-led marketing (Uber Teens, Reserve, grocery) is now essential to drive cross-service awareness and lifetime value.
anecdote🗣 Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)conviction: mediumUberUber Teensmarketing
So what: Marketing-driven cross-sell awareness is a recognized growth lever for multi-product platforms — relevant to UBER's services-attach rate.
“The goal isn't to get you coffee on that ride. The goal is to make you understand that you can get coffee on Uber” ▶ 53:24
Capital allocation and financial profile 4
Uber generates over $10 billion in free cash flow but on well over 10 billion trips a year, which Khosrowshahi stresses makes it a low-margin business that must drive efficiency to lower rider prices and raise earner pay.
data_point🗣 Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)conviction: highUberfree cash flow
So what: Frames UBER as high-FCF but thin-margin per trip; efficiency gains flow to price/earnings rather than margin, shaping how to value the cash flows.
“We've got over um $10 billion in in free cash flow, which is great, but it's on well over 10 billion trips a year... we are not a high margin business.” ▶ 19:45
Khosrowshahi prioritizes capital allocation as organic growth and AV commitments first, buybacks second — explicitly 'somewhere in between' Amazon (reinvest) and Apple (return cash).
thesis🗣 Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)conviction: highUberAmazonApplebuybacks
So what: Sets expectations that UBER will fund AV/growth ahead of buybacks despite large authorizations — important for shareholders modeling capital returns.
“I prioritize growth. I prioritize innovation over buybacks. Uh if you're building the company right, you'll do both.”
Uber will make capital commitments to tens of thousands of AV vehicles but plans to financialize them (keep them off-balance-sheet via financing), exemplified by a newly announced ~$1 billion Santander financing line for EV and AV fleets.
data_point🗣 Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)conviction: highUberSantanderautonomous vehiclesEV fleets
So what: Shows Uber funding AV scale via partner financing rather than its own balance sheet — limiting capital intensity while still developing the market.
“we just announced the deal with Santaare for example to finance both EVs and AVs” ▶ 1:05:10
Uber Eats grew from under $1 billion in gross bookings when Khosrowshahi joined to over $100 billion now, evidence of the organic-compounding playbook (costs growing slower than revenue) he prioritizes.
data_point🗣 Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)conviction: highUber EatsUber
So what: A ~100x GBV scale-up validates the organic-investment-first strategy and the compounding thesis for the platform.
“Uber Eats when I joined was uh doing under a billion dollars in gross bookings. That's over, you know, over 100 billion now.” ▶ 1:04:00
Leadership, culture, and mental models 3
Khosrowshahi argues AI has accelerated the rate of internal change in companies by ~5x, and that companies comfortable with constant change will adapt while rigid 'we do things a certain way' firms will struggle.
causal🗣 Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)conviction: highAIUber
So what: A framework for assessing which incumbents will win or lose in the AI era based on organizational adaptability, not just tech access.
“with AI now, the rate of change inside companies... has accelerated by 5x” ▶ 1:02:17
Khosrowshahi uses a 'companies are organisms that evolve by mutating' framework, actively seeking out internal 'troublemakers' and random interactions because firms with single processes and information flows die.
framework🗣 Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)conviction: highUbermanagement
So what: A reusable mental model for organizational resilience and innovation — a lens for evaluating management quality in high-change environments.
“every company is like an organism... organisms evolve by mutating. And companies that don't mutate... those are the companies that die.” ▶ 1:01:36
Khosrowshahi's core decision-making lesson from Barry Diller is to get unfiltered 'ground truth' from source material, because edge comes from the P95/20% outliers, not the processed average that everyone reacts to identically.
framework🗣 Dara Khosrowshahi (Uber CEO)conviction: highBarry DillerUber
So what: An investing-adjacent edge framework: differentiated decisions come from unprocessed tail data, not consensus averages.
“it's the filtering that gets the edge out of the story... It's often the edge that gives you an edge. It's not the the the average.” ▶ 57:00

Unchained · Laura Shin 2 episode(s) · 85 insights

Ethereum's Boomer Era? Apostates, EF Drama, and Solana Fights Back - The Chopping Block65 min43 insights2026-05-28
▶ Watch on YouTube
Dragonfly's Chopping Block hosts (Haseeb, Tarun, Tom) debate Bankless co-founder David Hoffman and ETH/Solana apostate Max Resnick on why ETH the asset has plateaued, the wave of Ethereum Foundation departures, ETH's 'Microsoft of crypto' boomer-utility trajectory, the case for a second/for-profit foundation, and whether Solana or Ethereum wins the $500T tokenization race — with a Solana post-quantum vs Ethereum scaling fight.
ETH-the-asset re-rating thesis is exhausted · 4Ethereum Foundation exodus and the 'who's in charge' problem · 7Ethereum as the 'Microsoft of crypto' — boomer utility · 6Second foundation / for-profit advocate for ETH · 5Competitive landscape: Solana, Base, Tempo, stablecoin chains · 6Strong crypto vs weak crypto regime shift · 4Tokenization race: $500T of securities on-chain · 7Quantum threat: Solana vs Ethereum architecture · 4
ETH-the-asset re-rating thesis is exhausted 4
David Hoffman (Bankless co-founder) sold his last ETH because the 2019-2020 'ETH is money' thesis has largely played out and he sees it as hard for ETH to be re-rated up or down from here — a capital opportunity-cost decision, not a bear call.
thesis🗣 David Hoffman (co-founder Bankless, Superstate)conviction: highETHEthereumBanklessBitcoin
So what: A former top ETH proselytizer rotating fully out signals the marginal long-term ETH bull is capitulating on the 'monetary premium' trade; watch for ETH/BTC continuing to drift.
“I think it's going to be hard for ETH to get re-rated up or down in any direction.” ▶ 4:17
ETH faces structural barriers to value capture: layer-two margins run at ~98%, and the 'fat app' thesis is displacing the 'fat protocol' thesis, so the L1 captures little of the value flowing through its ecosystem.
framework🗣 David Hoffmanconviction: highETHEthereum L1Layer 2sfat protocol thesisfat app thesis
So what: If L2s keep ~98% of margin and apps capture the rest, ETH-the-token is structurally starved of cash flow — undermining any DCF-style valuation and favoring L2/app tokens over the base asset.
“layer two margins are at 98% for example. You know, the fat app thesis is displacing the fat protocol thesis.” ▶ 6:01
Because Ethereum services its ecosystem at cost (nonprofit-like, no take rate), ETH can only be re-rated as 'money' — but that requires strong startup-minded leadership to push it, which David argues the EF lacks.
causal🗣 David Hoffmanconviction: highEthereum FoundationETH
So what: The bull case for ETH now rests entirely on a 'monetary premium' narrative that needs an aggressive promoter; absent one, the asset lacks a catalyst.
“It services everything to its ecosystem at cost... for Ether the money to be like the thesis be fully realized, it would needed to have maintained like a huge market dominance” ▶ 6:21
David frames ETH as being 'at the end of the sigmoid' — plateauing rather than collapsing — explicitly not a doom call but a low-gradient, no-momentum state.
framework🗣 David Hoffmanconviction: mediumETH
So what: Reframes the bear case as 'dead money / range-bound' rather than 'going to zero' — relevant for option sellers and pair traders rather than outright shorts.
“you're at the end of the the sigmoid. You're not at like a high gradient part. You're like, "Okay, we're plateauing."” ▶ 27:06
Ethereum Foundation exodus and the 'who's in charge' problem 7
A large 2025-26 wave of senior EF departures (Tomas as co-ED after ~11 months; Barnaby and Tim Beiko as protocol cluster co-leads; Carl Beekhuizen after 7 yrs; Julian Ma; Josh Stark after 7 yrs; Trent Van Epps; Pablo Vourt; Alex Stokes on sabbatical; Dankrad earlier to Tempo) has been read as institutional decay.
data_point🗣 Haseeb Qureshi (Dragonfly, host)conviction: highEthereum FoundationTim BeikoCarl BeekhuizenJosh StarkDankradTempoAlex Stokes
So what: The departing names were the 'aggressive/pro-growth' faction — their exit suggests the EF's internal balance has tilted toward slow, credibly-neutral stewardship, reducing odds of a pro-ETH-price pivot.
Vitalik publicly reframed the EF as explicitly NOT the 'number-go-up org,' said its job is to make Ethereum 'impressive,' noted the EF holds only ~16 basis points of ETH supply, and said other orgs should step up as the central node.
data_point🗣 Haseeb (paraphrasing Vitalik)conviction: highVitalik ButerinEthereum FoundationETH
So what: Official disavowal of price-stewardship removes any expectation the EF will market or shill ETH — a structural headwind for the monetary-premium narrative.
“the EF is not the number go up org. That is not our job. That has never been our job... our job is to make Ethereum impressive.” ▶ 9:17
The EF's ETH holdings have fallen from ~8.3% of supply to ~16 basis points, and with no revenue it will be forced to keep cutting ('DOGE happening out of necessity') and effectively dissolve over the next few years.
data_point🗣 Haseebconviction: highEthereum FoundationETH
So what: A shrinking, revenue-less EF means client/protocol development funding (much routed through EF grants to firms like Nethermind) is at structural risk, raising centralization-of-funding questions.
“they went from 8.3% of the ether supply to now down to 16 basis points” ▶ 38:49
Max Resnick argues the EF, after accidentally creating a ~$400B asset, has gone 'totally risk off' — prioritizing not nuking the asset over technical ambition, and views some past upgrades (e.g. EIP-4844) as already value-destructive.
thesis🗣 Max Resnick (ETH/Solana apostate, technologist)conviction: highEthereum FoundationVitalik ButerinEIP-4844ETH
So what: If leadership is optimizing for capital preservation over technical edge, expect conservative, slow upgrades — bearish for catching the tokenization wave on the L1.
“they're basically totally risk off. Preserve the value of the asset as much as they can and not try to take any risk.” ▶ 13:23
David's 'conspiratorial' take: part of the EF's DNA is actively adverse to growth because Vitalik doesn't want the responsibility/baggage of a large Ethereum economy — a psychological resistance to economy-maximizing behavior.
contrarian🗣 David Hoffmanconviction: mediumVitalik ButerinEthereum Foundation
So what: Implies the missing ETH catalyst is cultural/founder-driven, not fixable by org charts — so a leadership 'fix' is unlikely without Vitalik stepping back.
“part of the EF DNA is actually adverse to growth and Vitalik truly just doesn't want the responsibility” ▶ 13:53
Tarun's signal for ossification: no new blood — the same aging people at the same events — and Ethereum has bled talent faster than the broader (already shrinking) crypto talent market.
framework🗣 Tarun Chitra (Gauntlet)conviction: mediumEthereumGauntlet
So what: 'Follow the talent' as a leading indicator: declining developer/founder inflow precedes ecosystem decline, a metric to track vs Solana/Tempo.
“There's no new blood. That's... the real signal... Ethereum has bled more talent than the already fast slowing crypto talent market” ▶ 16:09
Tarun pushes back that the departures are over-attributed to personalities: since the highs ETH's performance hasn't been that different from Solana's, and the real driver is a broad 'L1 drag' where every L1 is down a lot.
contrarian🗣 Tarun Chitraconviction: mediumETHSolanaLayer 1s
So what: Suggests ETH underperformance is a beta/sector story, not idiosyncratic — argues against ETH-specific shorts and for an L1-basket view.
“since the highs, it's not performance has not been that different than Solana's... there's just been an L1 drag.” ▶ 17:18
Ethereum as the 'Microsoft of crypto' — boomer utility 6
Haseeb's framework: Ethereum is the 'Microsoft of crypto' — old, enterprise-beloved, deeply embedded, slow, uncool, never first to integrate, but extremely valuable and not going anywhere — likely to succeed despite mediocre execution (a Steve Ballmer era).
framework🗣 Haseeb Qureshiconviction: highEthereumMicrosoftETH
So what: Reframes ETH as a durable, Lindy 'utility' holding rather than a growth asset — supports a low-conviction long for survivability/trust premium, not for outperformance.
“Ethereum has become the Microsoft of crypto... it's very safe. It moves very slowly. It's kind of lame... but it's not going anywhere and it's really valuable.” ▶ 25:35
Haseeb's core valuation bet: the long-run blockchain premium accrues not to performance or deal-count but to being the biggest, most-trusted chain — the dimension Ethereum dominates.
thesis🗣 Haseeb Qureshiconviction: highEthereumblockchains
So what: If 'trust/Lindy' is the moat, ETH's incumbency outweighs Solana's speed — a bull thesis for ETH as settlement layer for high-value assets.
“The thing that they're competing on is just being the biggest, most trusted chain. And I... think that's where the premium is going to come from over the long run.” ▶ 36:36
Tarun/Tom argue 'dApps as the final story' and number-go-up messaging is fundamentally uninspiring to young builders, so even if ETH ripped back to $5,000 it wouldn't attract new entrepreneurs.
causal🗣 Tarun Chitra / Tomconviction: mediumEthereumTom LeeETH
So what: Price recovery alone won't fix the talent-pipeline problem; bullish ETH narratives that rely on 'price fixes everything' are flawed.
“dApps are the final story... such a... uninspiring story to someone who's 19... You're selling the olds. You're not selling the youngs.” ▶ 17:56
The bear-on-multiples view: if Ethereum's users become businesses with low equity/income ratios and PE ratios of ~6, ETH compresses to a DTCC-like utility multiple rather than a 'future of the internet' growth multiple.
framework🗣 Tarun Chitraconviction: mediumEthereumDTCCETH
So what: Frames the valuation ceiling: being the rails for low-margin enterprises caps ETH's multiple — a quantitative anchor for the 'utility, not equity' thesis.
“you're kind of in established boring business. You're a utility at that point.” ▶ 25:26
David counters that the EF has little explanatory power for ETH's price: despite a week of EF defections and his public ETH sale, ETH barely moved — so 'crypto Twitter intelligentsia' sentiment is not where the marginal buyer is.
contrarian🗣 Haseeb / Davidconviction: highEthereum FoundationETHCrypto Twitter
So what: If the marginal ETH buyer ignores CT sentiment (possibly pricing other things like Venice), then ETH-bearish social narratives may be poor short signals.
“Twitter is not pricing ETH... whatever Twitter thinks about ETH is just not where the marginal buyer is coming from.” ▶ 27:20
The Microsoft analogy implies an eventual Satya Nadella-style renaissance — but the panel agrees there is no equivalent talent pipeline inside Ethereum, since there's no for-profit ladder to groom and pay a future leader.
causal🗣 Tom / Haseebconviction: mediumEthereumMicrosoftSatya NadellaSteve Ballmer
So what: The 'it'll recover like Microsoft did' bull case has a hole: no internal career structure to produce the turnaround leader, weakening the long-term ETH bull thesis.
“There is no Satya Nadella joining the ranks of Ethereum core developers today who will be ready to assume the reins in five years” ▶ 40:12
Second foundation / for-profit advocate for ETH 5
People at the EF are actively working on a 'second foundation' — a for-profit, startup-minded, shareholder-interest entity (akin to a revived Enterprise Ethereum Alliance) — to be the 'number-go-up' advocate the EF refuses to be.
prediction🗣 David Hoffmanconviction: mediumEthereum FoundationEnterprise Ethereum AllianceBitmainDeFi founders
So what: Watch for the launch of a for-profit ETH advocacy org; its legitimacy (EF endorsement + Bitmain backing) could become the catalyst the ETH-money thesis needs.
“what's missing is just like a for-profit startup-minded entity who wants to like be a rallying cry for shareholder interest.” ▶ 31:30
A second foundation likely fails on its own because institutional counterparties only meet 'whoever is in charge of EF #1' — moral authority and the head-of-EF title are non-transferable (the 'soybean deal' / China-trip analogy).
framework🗣 Max Resnick / Haseebconviction: highEthereum FoundationSolana FoundationTomas
So what: Casts doubt on the second-foundation fix; the real BD power stays with the EF's title-holder, so spinning out adoption can't easily work.
“people want to be meeting with the person who's in charge... I want to talk to whoever's at of Ethereum Foundation 1 cuz that's where the real power is.” ▶ 34:24
David's fear: Vitalik's outsized sway means he can't simultaneously run Ethereum and credibly empower a differently-minded for-profit entity (the Bob Iger/Disney board-control parallel — real power sits with whoever holds the board seats, i.e. Vitalik and Aya).
causal🗣 David Hoffmanconviction: mediumVitalik ButerinAya MiyaguchiEthereum Foundation
So what: Governance concentration is the binding constraint on any pro-growth pivot; ETH catalysts require Vitalik to actively cede control, which the panel doubts.
“it would require Vitalik not having absolute governance over whoever this person is.”
The panel's most-likely positive scenario for ETH: Bitmain takes a far more activist role and pairs with the forthcoming second foundation to provide the pro-growth muscle the EF lacks.
prediction🗣 David Hoffman / Haseebconviction: mediumBitmainEthereum FoundationETH
So what: Bitmain (described as a ~$10B-ish entity) becoming an ETH activist is a concrete, watchable catalyst — track its public stance and capital commitments.
≈ “Bitmain takes a far more activist role and works well and pairs well with this second foundation that's on its way.” paraphrase
A precedent exists for self-organizing spiritual leadership: Michael Saylor became 'Mr. Bitcoin' purely via financial gravitational force, showing a dominant capital holder can become a chain's de facto promoter without official sanction.
framework🗣 Haseeb Qureshiconviction: highMichael SaylorBitcoinStrategy
So what: Implies a large ETH treasury/whale could spontaneously fill the leadership vacuum — a reason not to fully write off the ETH-money narrative.
“Saylor has a stronger gravitational force than anybody else in Bitcoin... the idea that like this can't happen on its own... is just insufficiently imaginative.” ▶ 48:26
Competitive landscape: Solana, Base, Tempo, stablecoin chains 6
Haseeb argues the feared 'ETH killers' have all underperformed expectations: a year and a half on, neither Base nor Solana nor Tempo has eaten Ethereum's lunch, and Ethereum still holds the vast majority of TVL.
contrarian🗣 Haseeb Qureshiconviction: highEthereumSolanaBaseTempoTVL
So what: The 'ETH is dead' competitive narrative has repeatedly failed to play out — a reason to fade extreme ETH-bearishness on competitive grounds.
“do you see Base eating Ethereum alive? Do you see Solana eating Ethereum alive?... all those things didn't really happen. Ethereum is still massive, still has vast majority of the TVL.” ▶ 22:52
As a VC, Haseeb says Solana's developer/founder market share peaked ~18 months ago and has clearly crested — though new builders aren't going to Ethereum mainnet either, but to EVM-compatible alternatives.
data_point🗣 Haseeb Qureshiconviction: highSolanaEthereumEVM
So what: Counters the 'Solana is winning devs' narrative; founder flow is fragmenting to EVM L2s/alt-chains, not consolidating on either leader — bearish for a clean ETH-vs-SOL binary.
“Solana was all-time high probably a year and a half ago... it's clearly crested in terms of the market share of people building on Solana.” ▶ 23:32
Max counters that on Gauntlet's developer metrics Solana has been winning vs Ethereum (and Base has done poorly), creating an explicit data disagreement on Solana's developer trajectory.
data_point🗣 Max Resnickconviction: mediumSolanaEthereumBase
So what: The developer-share data is genuinely contested between practitioners — a metric to independently verify rather than take on narrative.
“on the developer side, Ethereum has been losing to Solana over the past... Solana has been doing pretty well on our developer metrics.” ▶ 23:04
The stablecoin-chain threat to ETH has largely fizzled: Stable, Plasma, Arc and Taproot mostly fell flat and aren't regaining momentum, so stablecoin and tokenized-RWA activity isn't migrating off Ethereum soon.
data_point🗣 Tomconviction: mediumStablePlasmaArcTaprootstablecoinsRWAEthereum
So what: Removes a near-term bear catalyst (stablecoin-chain disintermediation), supporting ETH's incumbency in stablecoin/RWA settlement.
“we had stable and plasma and arc and taproot. And those have really mostly fallen flat.” ▶ 28:26
Tom names Hyperliquid as a 'real' threat on the DeFi side — distinct from the failed stablecoin chains — singling it out as the credible competitive risk to watch.
thesis🗣 Tomconviction: mediumHyperliquidDeFi
So what: Hyperliquid flagged as the one genuinely threatening DeFi competitor; warrants positioning attention vs other 'fake-metric' rivals.
“the hyper liquid threat... is kind of real” ▶ 28:49
Etherealize is cited as the model for breaking EF orthodoxy — an unapologetically enterprise-/Wall-Street-focused org willing to make decentralization compromises, which gains share automatically as the EF weakens.
framework🗣 Haseeb Qureshiconviction: mediumEtherealizeEthereum FoundationWall Street
So what: As the EF recedes, enterprise-aligned orgs like Etherealize become a larger share of Ethereum's footprint — a structural tilt toward 'weak crypto' adoption rails.
“Etherealize is we are for enterprises, period, full stop, end of story... We're here to actually get Wall Street on chain.” ▶ 46:32
Strong crypto vs weak crypto regime shift 4
David's 'strong vs weak crypto' framework: 'strong crypto' (DeFi, NFTs, cypherpunk, rewriting finance) — Ethereum's native habitat — is at its weakest, while 'weak crypto' (blockchain as a back-end efficiency upgrade for big financial institutions) is thriving.
framework🗣 David Hoffmanconviction: highDeFiNFTsEthereumcypherpunk
So what: A regime shift away from ETH's core 'strong crypto' use case toward enterprise rails reframes which assets/chains capture the next cycle — favoring infrastructure plays over speculative DeFi.
“Weak crypto is doing really well right now. Strong crypto, not so well. And... DeFi especially, which is Ethereum's core driver, is the weakest it's ever been” ▶ 20:30
David ties ETH's peak monetary moment to the COVID lockdown (everyone stuck inside, forced online, money loose), implying ETH's 'money' premium was a special macro condition unlikely to repeat at the same scale.
causal🗣 David Hoffmanconviction: mediumETHCOVIDBitcoin
So what: If ETH's monetary premium was a one-off macro artifact, betting on a repeat 'ETH is money' regime is betting on conditions that may not recur.
“when was ETH the most money it ever was? It was when everyone was stuck inside during COVID under lockdown” ▶ 21:08
Haseeb disputes that DeFi is at its weakest ever — citing FTX's 2022 collapse as a far weaker moment, with TVL, trading volumes and on-chain flows having ground up robustly over multiple years despite the last 6 months down.
contrarian🗣 Haseeb Qureshiconviction: highDeFiFTXTVL
So what: Direct rebuttal to the DeFi-decline narrative; if TVL/volumes are structurally higher than post-FTX, ETH's core driver is healthier than headlines suggest.
“we were FTX exploded like 3 years ago... TVL like on chain and DeFi look stronger if you zoom out than they have in a long long time.” ▶ 21:45
Max dismisses headline stablecoin and chain volume metrics (on both Ethereum and Solana) as 'fake' — largely exchange-to-exchange deposit-address transfers, not genuine economic activity; only AUM is real.
contrarian🗣 Max Resnickconviction: highstablecoinsEthereumSolanaAUM
So what: If on-chain volume metrics are inflated wash/transfer flow, valuation and dominance comparisons based on them are unreliable — anchor on AUM instead.
“Stable coin volume, fake. It's just it's like totally fake... it's people transferring money from one exchange deposit address to another.” ▶ 29:07
Tokenization race: $500T of securities on-chain 7
Max's Solana bull case: ~$500T of global securities are coming on-chain, regulators (SEC) want it on real decentralized chains (not centralized systems), leaving effectively two viable venues — Ethereum or Solana — and Ethereum lacks the scale to host the global financial system today.
thesis🗣 Max Resnickconviction: highSolanaEthereumSECDTCCsecurities/RWA
So what: Positions Solana as the scalable winner of the tokenization wave if Ethereum can't scale its L1 in time — the core ETH-vs-SOL trade thesis.
“there are basically two options in terms of public blockchains that you would want to go on... Ethereum and Solana, and Ethereum doesn't have enough scale to accommodate the global financial system at this time.” ▶ 56:59
Max sees a major missed opportunity: with regulatory clarity arriving, no one is aggressively lobbying Washington, the DTCC and transfer agents to actually push securities on-chain — a coordination/BD gap, not a tech gap.
thesis🗣 Max Resnickconviction: highDTCCSECtransfer agentssecurities/RWA
So what: Whoever does the regulatory/BD legwork ('pound on the door') could capture the tokenization flow — an execution edge available to either chain's advocates.
“it's a really big missed opportunity to not have people pounding down the door in Washington and at the DTCC and at the transfer agents” ▶ 10:53
David's risk scenario: if everyone is ready to put assets on-chain (DTCC, etc.) but Ethereum says it won't be scaled enough until ~2031, that flow defects to Solana, Hyperledger or elsewhere — a genuine risk he acknowledges.
causal🗣 Haseeb (scenario) / David (concession)conviction: mediumEthereumSolanaHyperledgerDTCC
So what: Scaling timeline is the pivotal variable: if ETH L1 lags institutional demand, it permanently cedes the RWA flow — the single biggest bear catalyst named.
“based on our current rate of increasing the gas limit, we'll be ready for you in... 2031... And then they just choose to go somewhere else, like Solana or Hyperledger” ▶ 51:58
It's not even given that institutions use public chains at all: Nasdaq and NYSE are building their own internal blockchains, and DTCC (charging ~6 cents/transaction, ~$1.5B/yr revenue) may run its own chain rather than picking Ethereum or Solana.
contrarian🗣 Tom / Haseebconviction: mediumNasdaqNYSEDTCCEthereumSolana
So what: The entire ETH-vs-SOL tokenization thesis may be moot if incumbents run private chains — a tail risk that undercuts both bull cases.
“Nasdaq and the New York Stock Exchange both are doing their own internal blockchains... Why are is it coming on the public chain? Do we even know that?” ▶ 52:57
Even the DTCC's own economics (~$1.5B/yr revenue) wouldn't justify Ethereum's valuation — implying the tokenization narrative alone can't underwrite ETH's market cap, Haseeb concedes.
data_point🗣 Tom / Haseebconviction: mediumDTCCEthereumETH
So what: Quantifies the gap between RWA fee potential and ETH's valuation — a sober anchor against over-pricing the tokenization story.
“they charge 6 cents a transaction at the DTCC... and they make 1.5 billion dollars a year... But that's still not really enough to justify Ethereum's valuation, right?” ▶ 53:18
David's 'make it up on scale' thesis: blockchains should forego fees today (vs the ~2% 'highway robbery' of 2021, which only worked due to no interoperability and trapped capital) to win volume, then earn a tiny rate on a huge base — and if scale never comes, the whole blockchain thesis was wrong.
framework🗣 David Hoffmanconviction: highEthereumblockchainsMEV
So what: Justifies near-zero L1 take rates as strategy, not weakness — but explicitly makes ETH's value contingent on blockchains becoming 'one of the main things in the world' within ~15 years.
“it's a classic you make it up on scale... If blockchains don't get there, then we kind of lost. We were kind of wrong about the thesis” ▶ 50:15
Tom Lee's framing that 'stablecoins are the ChatGPT of crypto' (and stablecoins live on Ethereum) is mocked by the panel as a pure-adoption, non-technical comparison — though they concede it coincided with ETH running to all-time highs.
anecdote🗣 Tom / Haseebconviction: lowTom LeestablecoinsChatGPTEthereum
So what: The dominant retail/institutional bull narrative for ETH is now 'stablecoins on Ethereum' — note it as the marketing line moving price even if technically thin.
“Stable coins are the chat GPT of crypto.” ▶ 54:18
Quantum threat: Solana vs Ethereum architecture 4
Max argues post-quantum is a far smaller problem for Solana than Ethereum: Solana has abundant bandwidth (100 Gbit NICs) and will use a smaller signature scheme (Falcon, ~8x smaller), whereas Ethereum's solo-staker Raspberry-Pi target plus ~4,000-byte signatures per transaction/vote chokes its already low-bandwidth network.
thesis🗣 Max Resnickconviction: highSolanaEthereumFalcon signaturesJustin Drakequantum computing
So what: Frames quantum as an asymmetric architectural risk that hurts Ethereum's decentralization model more than Solana's — a long-dated differentiator for the ETH-vs-SOL debate.
“For Ethereum, they have a solo staker Raspberry Pi that can only do so many bits per second... we're going to use a smaller signature, Falcon... eight times smaller than that and we have way more bandwidth.” ▶ 59:07
Max claims Solana's post-quantum design (Falcon, ~600-byte signatures vs 32 today) costs only a ~2x constant factor in bandwidth — offset by bandwidth doubling every ~2 years — with no end-user performance impact and still reaching ~1M TPS; Solana won't flip the switch until quantum is a real signal, likely within 5 years.
prediction🗣 Max Resnickconviction: highSolanaFalcon signaturesquantum computing
So what: If credible, the quantum migration is a non-event for Solana performance — neutralizing a feared 'obvious bad thing' overhang on SOL.
≈ “it's a factor of like two. It's like ultimately a constant factor and bandwidth is increasing by factor of two every two years... maybe it will change the timeline by two years” paraphrase
Max contends Ethereum's million-validator design (a '10-year-old poor architecture decision') is a post-quantum liability — a MacBook can only aggregate ~800 signatures/second, so aggregating a million validator signatures requires heavy compute.
causal🗣 Max Resnickconviction: highEthereumvalidatorssignature aggregationquantum computing
So what: Pinpoints Ethereum's large validator set as a specific, quantifiable scaling/quantum bottleneck — a technical bear point distinct from leadership/narrative concerns.
“they have a million validators on ETH cuz they shot themselves in the foot with a very poor architecture decision like 10 years ago. And a MacBook can... aggregate 800 signatures in 1 second.” ▶ 1:03:10
Tarun is sanguine on quantum compute cost: once a post-quantum standard is chosen, CPU vendors will optimize it to a single instruction (as happened when Intel's SHA SIMD opcode made SHA complaints vanish), though he concedes quantum may kill the cheap Raspberry-Pi validator.
framework🗣 Tarun Chitraconviction: mediumquantum computingIntelSHARaspberry Pi
So what: Suggests the compute side of quantum migration is a solved-by-progress problem; the real casualty is ultra-cheap home staking, a decentralization (not performance) concern.
“once a standard is chosen, like every CPU provider is just going to make it a one-click... the way that like SHA was optimized away”
Is DeFi Unsafe? Saylor's Sale, ETH Brain Drain & Hyperliquid - The Chopping Block62 min42 insights2026-06-04
▶ Watch on YouTube
Chopping Block panel (Tom, Tarun, Joe Lubin, host Steve) reads a 6% crypto down-day off Saylor's belated 25-BTC sale disclosure as a DAT death-spiral scare, argues Ether DATs are safer than Bitcoin DATs because ETH is a yield-bearing/staked asset, dissects the Ethereum Foundation 'second foundation' splintering, and frames AI-driven attackers temporarily out-running defenders as a 1-2 year DeFi danger window before a formally-verified hardening. Also: ETH's quantum migration roadmap as a differentiator, and Hyperliquid's distribution-flywheel moat over rival perp/RFQ venues.
Saylor sale & DAT death-spiral risk · 5Ether DAT thesis & ETH yield mechanics · 2Ethereum Foundation splintering & 'second foundation' · 4Quantum threat & ETH post-quantum roadmap · 5AI attackers vs defenders & the DeFi danger window · 14Privacy protocols & legal attack vectors · 2Hyperliquid moat, perps regulation & competition · 7Extended Ethereum: ZK composability & institutional rails · 3
Saylor sale & DAT death-spiral risk 5
MicroStrategy's 6% single-day stock drop was triggered not by Saylor selling 25 BTC (a tiny amount sold months earlier at ~$77K BTC) but by the delayed disclosure of that sale, which markets read as the lone perma-buyer signaling willingness to capitulate.
causal🗣 host (Steve)conviction: highMichael SaylorMicroStrategyStrategyBitcoin
So what: Watch disclosure timing on DAT treasuries; the market punishes the signal far more than the size, so any forced-seller optics around MSTR can cascade into broad crypto beta.
“Bitcoin was at 77K when he actually sold it. So market felt a lot calmer at the time that he made the sale. Now it's just the disclosure of the sale that has caused markets to just quickly leg down.” ▶ 4:56
Strategy owes roughly $1.4 billion a year in cash dividends on its STRC ('Stretch') preferred but holds only about $700 million cash, so it must raise cash via dilutive new-share issuance, fueling death-spiral fears.
data_point🗣 host (Steve)conviction: mediumStrategyMicroStrategySTRCMichael Saylor
So what: The structural cash gap means MSTR is a forced equity issuer; monitor share count and STRC coverage as the real solvency tell, not BTC sales.
“Sailor owes something like 1.4 billion a year, I think, in cash dividends from Stretch from STRC ... I think they have like 700 million cash. So the cash has to come up somehow.” ▶ 3:46
A Bitcoin DAT is more dangerous than an Ether DAT because BTC is not a productive asset, forcing BTC-DATs into coupon-paying leveraged structures that may require selling the underlying token, whereas staked-ETH treasuries generate native yield.
thesis🗣 Joe Lubinconviction: highBitcoinEthereumSharplinkBMNRDAT
So what: Within the DAT trade, prefer yield-bearing-token treasuries (ETH/Sharplink/BMNR) over BTC-DATs that must service coupons by liquidating; the asset's productivity is the survival variable.
“maybe a Bitcoin DAT is potentially more dangerous uh because it's not a productive asset in the same way that uh an Etherdat Sharplink and BMNR are.” ▶ 2:44
A token-holding vehicle with 10 layers of preferred stacked over a constantly-diluted common share resembles an algorithmic stablecoin 'with too many steps' — an unstable equilibrium whose blow-up probability rises as the common stock falls.
contrarian🗣 Tarunconviction: mediumMicroStrategySTRCDATconvertibles
So what: Treat multi-tranche preferred DAT structures as reflexive/path-dependent: short interest or hedges should scale up as common equity weakens, since fragility compounds on the downside.
“it does feel a little like algorithmic stable coin with too many steps to me ... it's an event whose probability seems higher the lower the common stock goes” ▶ 5:51
The proper lesson from the punished 25-BTC sale is that selling spot to recapitalize is the wrong playbook for an over-collateralized BTC treasury — the disciplined move is to never repeat it, not to read it as a routine liquidity test.
thesis🗣 host (Steve)conviction: mediumMicroStrategyMichael SaylorBitcoin
So what: Future MSTR recapitalizations are likely to route through equity/preferred issuance rather than BTC sales, capping forced spot-selling pressure but increasing dilution risk.
Ether DAT thesis & ETH yield mechanics 2
Ether as a DAT reserve asset yields ~3% risk-free via staking, allows additional yield 'sleeves' on top, carries no leverage, and represents essentially permanent long-term capital that can fund ecosystem growth.
thesis🗣 Joe Lubinconviction: highEthereumETHSharplinkstaking
So what: The staking-yield base case is the bull argument for ETH treasuries as durable buyers/holders; track whether DAT ETH is staked (permanent capital) vs. liquid (potential sell pressure).
“our token Ether um yields uh approximately 3% risk-free yield we can layer on sleeves on top of that Virtually all of our tokens have been staked when we buy them.” ▶ 6:46
Sharplink and BMNR are in active discussions to jointly become 'decentralized commercialization engines' for Ethereum, with Consensys, positioning ETH-DAT permanent capital as funders of ecosystem stewards rather than passive holders.
anecdote🗣 Joe Lubinconviction: mediumSharplinkBMNRConsensysEthereum
So what: ETH DATs may evolve from treasury vehicles into ecosystem capital allocators; an emerging Sharplink/BMNR/Consensys axis could become the commercial counterweight to the Ethereum Foundation.
Ethereum Foundation splintering & 'second foundation' 4
The Ethereum Foundation is deliberately shrinking its budget and externalizing functions, reframing itself per Vitalik as 'one node' that sets the north star and focuses on 'crops' (core protocol components) and the cypherpunk vision rather than business development.
thesis🗣 Joe Lubinconviction: highEthereum FoundationVitalik ButerinBastianTomasz
So what: EF departures should be read as planned decentralization of roles, not collapse; expect a constellation of new stewarding orgs to absorb scaling, usability and commercialization work.
“we should be thinking of the EF as one node, a major node, a node that sets the north star. Uh focus on the cipher punk vision, focus on crops.”
A separate body of elite core developers will spin up its own organization focused on platform scalability, usability and 'number go up' for ETH, potentially funded by permanent-capital holders (i.e., ETH DATs) with EF blessing — a de facto 'second foundation' the panel avoids naming as such.
prediction🗣 Joe Lubinconviction: mediumEthereum FoundationETHcore developers
So what: A new ETH-aligned dev/commercial org is imminent ('will announce themselves soon'); its funding by DATs links ETH price performance to protocol development capacity.
“There may be some financial support from organizations that have uh long-term permanent capital, for instance.” ▶ 12:23
