The EF deliberately avoids the credible-neutrality-eroding 'bizdev / pay-to-play' model used by rival ecosystems, accepting that conflicts of interest between business development and researchers are why a credibly-neutral platform needs credibly-neutral funding nodes.
framework🗣 Joe Lubinconviction: highEthereum Foundationcredible neutrality
So what: Ethereum's refusal to run pay-to-play bizdev is a structural difference vs. faster-moving L1s; it trades short-term adoption speed for long-term neutrality, a key bull/bear fault line for ETH.
“What we see in other ecosystems that don't value credible neutrality quite as much ... is this group of bisdev people. Uh there's some pay-to-play stuff.” ▶ 10:48
The EF no longer says the original foundation will eventually dissolve; it is now 'saving its money for the long term,' a quiet shift toward indefinite existence whose runway is implicitly levered to the ETH price.
causal🗣 panelconviction: mediumEthereum FoundationETH
So what: EF longevity is now an ETH-price call; a higher ETH treasury extends its runway, tightening the reflexive loop between token price and Ethereum's governance durability.
“No, they're saving their money for the long term.” ▶ 16:29
Quantum threat & ETH post-quantum roadmap 5
Justin Drake calls a 50% probability of quantum 'Q-day' (breaking conventional crypto) by 2032 and 10% by 2030, and dismisses NIST's 2035 recommended transition date as 'a joke that should be discounted entirely.'
prediction🗣 host (Steve)conviction: highJustin DrakeNISTquantum computingEthereum
So what: Frontier researchers see quantum risk as far nearer than official timelines; assets with credible post-quantum roadmaps may earn a durable premium years before Q-day.
“he thinks there's a 50% chance by 2032, a 10% chance by 2030 ... NIST ... date of 2035 ... to be a joke that should be discounted entirely.” ▶ 20:46
Ethereum plans to migrate to post-quantum cryptography by 2029 via a 'lean VM' construction that rips out BLS signatures, KZG aggregation and ECDSA signatures in favor of hash-based cryptography.
prediction🗣 host (Steve)conviction: highEthereumJustin DrakeBLSKZGECDSAhash-based cryptography
So what: ETH's concrete 2029 post-quantum timeline is a differentiator vs. chains without one; the three required migrations are also execution risk to watch.
“ETH is going to migrate to postquantum crypto by 2029. Um, there's going to be this uh lean VM construction that rips out all of the BLS signatures.” ▶ 22:21
ETH/BTC functions as a market-implied 'quantum index' (per a Nick Carter tweet) — part of why ETH is the least-worst major is the market pricing in its quantum roadmap.
framework🗣 Tarunconviction: mediumETH/BTCNick CarterBitcoinEthereumquantum computing
So what: Use ETH/BTC as a proxy gauge for the market's perceived quantum timeline; accelerating quantum news could be an ETH/BTC tailwind independent of broader risk sentiment.
“Nick Carter has some old tweet that's ETHBTC is the quantum index.” ▶ 22:43
Google published only a ZK proof (not the circuit) that it had a much smaller/faster Shor's-algorithm circuit for breaking ECDSA, after which crowdsourced amateurs using LLM subscriptions rederived and then improved on it by 12-13% in circuit size within days, using the ZK proof as a reinforcement-learning target.
data_point🗣 host (Steve)conviction: highGoogleECDSAShor's algorithmzero-knowledge proofquantum computing
So what: AI is accelerating cryptanalysis faster than feared; the gating factor on this style of progress is compute cost, so subsidized free AI compute is itself a security-timeline variable.
“Like since the last two days it actually went to like 12% better 13% better ... they use the ZK proof as almost like a RL environment.” ▶ 19:52
AI accelerates building hardened/post-quantum-ready software far more than it accelerates building physical quantum computers, because hardening is a software problem while quantum hardware remains 'very engineering heavy.'
framework🗣 Tarunconviction: mediumAIquantum computingEthereum
So what: The defense (post-quantum migration) may outpace the offense (working quantum machines), reducing the practical window of exposure for chains that move early.
“it does seem like the problem of creating hardened clients is more accelerated by AI than the problem of actually building quantum computers” ▶ 23:17
AI attackers vs defenders & the DeFi danger window 14
OpenZeppelin founder Manuel Araoz publicly declared all of DeFi now unsafe and told friends and family to withdraw everything — a notable warning given his security credentials.
contrarian🗣 host (Steve)conviction: highManuel AraozOpenZeppelinDeFi
So what: A top security figure is signaling near-term DeFi tail risk; warrants raising the bar on protocol risk budgeting and avoiding under-audited/long-tail contracts during the transition.
“Manuel, he tweeted that he believes all of DeFi is now unsafe and he's recommending that all of his friends and family withdraw everything from DeFi.” ▶ 25:25
DeFi is entering a roughly one-to-two-year window where AI-armed attackers learn faster than defenders, framed as a Stackelberg security game where the defender usually eventually wins but suffers a long losing streak first.
framework🗣 Tarunconviction: highDeFiAIsmart contract security
So what: Size DeFi exposure for a defined 1-2 year elevated-hack regime; the edge is in protocols with frontier-grade security spend that adopt AI tooling as fast as attackers.
≈ “there's this horrible period of like cat-and-mouse games ... the attacker has this like long period where they like ... it's like win-win win win” paraphrase ▶ 26:06
The hacker-as-consultant model (whitehats extract a ~10% 'consulting fee' and the industry hardens) only works if lessons are socialized industry-wide; catastrophic full-loss hacks like Ronin charge 'the consulting fee for the entire industry.'
framework🗣 Joe Lubin / Tarunconviction: highRoninDeFiwhite hat hackers
So what: Protocols that don't share security lessons (or get fully drained) impose negative externalities on the whole sector; cross-protocol disclosure norms are the key risk mitigant to monitor.
“this works well if the consulting fee is 10%. Right? But if we're talking about like a Ronin hack situation, the consulting fee is everything.” ▶ 28:01
ThorChain has been shut off for two weeks after a hack and, when Zelic's V12 AI scanner found further vulnerabilities and asked to be paid, ThorChain refused — so the researchers threatened to publicly disclose all the bugs since the chain was already down.
anecdote🗣 Tarunconviction: highThorChainZelicV12
So what: Concrete evidence that security lessons are NOT socialized uniformly; under-resourced protocols that stiff researchers risk full public-disclosure detonation — a red flag for due diligence.
“Thor Chain was like, "No, we're not going to pay you anything." They said, "Oh, okay. We'll just disclose them then because like you're down anyway."” ▶ 28:52
Composability is the core structural problem in DeFi because it makes insurance hard: a composable product has many inputs (bridge, oracle, RWA issuer signature), so the missing innovation is distributing loss-coverage cost across the whole supply chain rather than pinning it at the single end-point that got hit.
framework🗣 Tarunconviction: highDeFicomposabilityinsuranceoraclesbridgesRWA
So what: Watch for distributed/supply-chain insurance primitives as the next DeFi innovation wave; today's end-point-only coverage is structurally mispriced.
“the main problem with DeFi right now is composability. It makes insurance very hard ... how do you distribute that type of cost across the system as opposed to just like trying to like fix it at one place” ▶ 30:57
By analogy to the late-19th-century railroad boom and Great Depression — an era of private bank-issued dollars and fraudulent bonds — DeFi's blow-ups will birth large insurers and an FDIC-equivalent that drains demand for uninsured 'private money,' the maturation path for the sector.
framework🗣 Tarunconviction: mediumDeFistablecoinsFDICinsurance
So what: The maturation trade is in the insurers and coverage layers that emerge post-crisis; uninsured protocols/stablecoins may see capital flight toward insured equivalents over time.
“the FDIC insurance offered by the government effectively like moved all like no one wanted to hold the private money anymore” ▶ 30:42
Defenders will lag attackers because effectively wielding AI security tooling takes longer than weaponizing it; attackers exploit the cracks between protocols and social engineering, while best-resourced defenders eventually catch up but only the well-funded ones get there fast.
thesis🗣 Tarun / hostconviction: highAIDeFismart contract security
So what: Security spend and AI-tooling adoption speed become a primary protocol-selection filter; under-funded protocols are the soft targets to underweight.
“the defenders being able to use it effectively will take longer than the attackers” ▶ 38:28
Formal verification ('correct by construction') is presented as the only durable escape from the multiplying 'multi-dimensional Swiss cheese' of patch-based security, with Ethereum framed as an anti-fragile system that will squeeze out legacy bugs and move to formally-verifiable software.
thesis🗣 Joe Lubin / Tarunconviction: mediumEthereumformal verificationsmart contract security
So what: Long-term, formally-verified protocols may command a safety premium; the migration to formal verification is the structural bull case for surviving DeFi after the danger window.
“the formal verification is the strongest argument for ... correct by construction as opposed to ... the Swiss cheese ... multi-dimensional Swiss cheese” ▶ 0:01
Mythos-class AI vulnerability finders only reliably work when pointed at a known target; aimed at the Linux kernel or a browser with no target they 'zip around like a mosquito,' so generalized autonomous exploitation is harder than the hype implies.
contrarian🗣 Tarunconviction: mediumMythosAILinux kernelsmart contract security
So what: Tempers the most extreme 'AI breaks everything' fears; targeted attacks (where the payoff is known) are the near-term threat, not open-ended kernel-wide zero-day discovery.
“If you just point it at the Linux kernel or at a browser, it's going to ... just zip around in circles like a mosquito and like not find anything that actually is is reliable.” ▶ 22:09
Polymarket prices Mythos-quality AI security models at ~70% to be released by end of summer, putting frontier vulnerability-finding (and black-hat) capability into ordinary hands on that timeline.
data_point🗣 host (Steve)conviction: mediumMythosPolymarketAI
So what: A near-dated market-implied catalyst for the attacker-side capability jump; protocols should assume commoditized AI exploitation tooling by late summer.
“mythos now if you look on poly market it's like 70% to be released by the end of summer.” ▶ 37:02
Old closed-source and non-Linux codebases relying on security-by-obscurity are the next big soft targets as AI makes decompiling binaries far easier, and nobody has economic incentive to maintain that legacy code.
thesis🗣 Tomconviction: mediumclosed source softwareAILinux
So what: The biggest AI-security blowups may land in legacy closed-source software (and the firms relying on it), not open-source crypto; a cross-sector risk to watch beyond DeFi.
“I'm I'm more worried about these old closed source code bases now that hey, it's you know getting much much easier to decompile these binaries.” ▶ 43:19
Open-source blockchains may reach a more impenetrable end-state first ('Valhalla') because their attacks are public and force coverage, while closed-source systems will bleed quietly for years as owners deem auditing too expensive — citing Mozilla's overconfident claim of being 100% vulnerability-free by year-end despite auditing little of its codebase.
thesis🗣 Tarunconviction: mediumMozillaFirefoxopen sourceblockchainsMythos
So what: Contrarian bull case: public-attack-surface open-source crypto could end up safer than closed enterprise software, inverting the usual DeFi-is-risky framing over a multi-year horizon.
“the optimism for blockchains and open sources, they're going to be the first ones to reach that Valhalla hopefully” ▶ 42:21
Blockchains are structurally bad at fast patching — upgrades require slow, discursive validator coordination ('we all argue in a discord') — illustrated by SUI being down ~48 hours across three cascading patch-induced failures, a pattern expected to worsen with AI-discovered bugs.
causal🗣 Tom / hostconviction: mediumSUIblockchainssmart contract security
So what: Slow patch cadence is a structural vulnerability multiplier under AI-speed attacks; chains with weak upgrade governance are higher-risk during the danger window.
“this week there was a story about SUI that uh SUI was like down for 48 hours ... down three times in a row ... a sort of cascade of failures” ▶ 36:28
North Korea's outsized success as a crypto attacker is unexplained by necessity alone (many poor states want the capability but only NK pulls it off); in a world where everyone has frontier AI, the binding constraint on offensive capability shifts from talent to money.
framework🗣 Tarunconviction: mediumNorth Koreacyber securityAI
So what: Democratized frontier AI broadens the attacker base from a few well-resourced states to anyone with capital, raising baseline DeFi attack frequency structurally.
“in a world where everybody has access to frontier intelligence, then the limiting factor really is just money.” ▶ 39:26
Privacy protocols & legal attack vectors 2
Circle froze a confidential-USDC (cUSDC) contract on Zama/Overnight Finance after a federal restraining order over a ~$15M treasury, demonstrating that a privacy overlay is only as private/censorship-resistant as its freezable underlying collateral.
causal🗣 host (Steve) / panelconviction: highCircleUSDCZamaOvernight FinanceFHE
So what: Privacy built as an overlay on freezable stablecoins inherits the issuer's freeze risk; only privacy 'built in' at the base layer avoids this weak point — a design distinction for evaluating privacy protocols.
“well when the underlying collateral is freezable ... For a privacy that's the that's the underlying weak point.” ▶ 50:29
An activist/corporate-raider investor (via a fund called Patagon) bought a distressed protocol's tokens and used a federal court restraining order to try to claw back assets — a repeat tactic signaling that 'the long arm of the law' is now a live attack vector against DeFi protocols.
thesis🗣 host (Steve)conviction: mediumPatagonOvernight FinanceCircleDeFi
So what: Litigation-driven asset clawbacks are emerging as a DeFi-native attack/activist strategy; protocols and their token holders face a new legal-coercion risk distinct from code exploits.
“buying like a distressed asset and then pushing for a court order to try to get some assets returned to the underlying investment” ▶ 49:15
Hyperliquid moat, perps regulation & competition 7
Hyperliquid crested $75, reaching a ~$50B+ fully-diluted valuation, flipped Solana on FDV, and is now the #9 most valuable crypto asset — one of the only majors rising while the market sells off.
data_point🗣 host (Steve)conviction: highHyperliquidHYPESolana
So what: Hyperliquid's relative-strength leadership marks it as the cycle's momentum asset; its FDV flip of Solana is a notable ranking shift worth tracking for rotation flows.
“it crested $75, which brings it to a fully diluted valuation of what is it, 50 something billion ... it recently in FTV terms flipped Salana. It is now number nine”
The CFTC issued perps guidance and approved the first US-domestic perpetual (a BTC perp on Kalshi, not Coinbase), opening the door for US perp products — though equities and likely SEC-controlled assets remain excluded.
data_point🗣 host (Steve)conviction: highCFTCKalshiCoinbaseSECperpetuals
So what: US on-shore perps are now regulatorily live via the CFTC; Kalshi's first-mover approval and the CFTC-vs-SEC jurisdiction line define which venues and assets can compete domestically.
“the uh CFTC recently issued some per guidance and approved the first US per product from KHI of all people.” ▶ 51:18
The panel predicts Hyperliquid will come to America by creating a regulated, CFTC-licensed, KYC'd US entity alongside its offshore product — a Polymarket-style onshore/offshore split — because it is the perp venue most often mentioned in Congress.
prediction🗣 Joe Lubin / Drewconviction: mediumHyperliquidCFTCPolymarket
So what: A US-regulated Hyperliquid entity is the panel's base case; its competitiveness as a necessarily-centralized onshore product is the open question for the bull thesis.
“It means it's coming to America at some point ... they're going to create a regulated US entity that CFTC get the CFTC license and KYC's customers domestically.” ▶ 52:19
Hyperliquid's uncopied moat is distribution — order flow routed via MetaMask/Phantom plus permissionless market creation through HIP-3 (and soon HIP-4) — an L1-grade reach achieved without an L1's coordination overhead, plus Polymarket-style 'earned media' from people watching its markets.
thesis🗣 Drewconviction: highHyperliquidMetaMaskPhantomHIP-3HIP-4Polymarket
So what: Distribution, not tech, is Hyperliquid's defensible edge; rivals must replicate the distribution flywheel (which competitors are 'racing' to do) to threaten it — the key competitive metric to track.
≈ “the key innovation for Hyperlid that no one has copied is distribution ... I think no one in crypto has ever pulled off that type of distribution play.” paraphrase
Hyperliquid's strength is its 'unbundled exchange' — partitioning an exchange into distributors (order-flow/quasi-prime-brokers) and permissionless market creators — whereas HyperEVM has 'failed in a lot of ways' because no one wanted the full programmable platform.
contrarian🗣 Drew / Tarunconviction: highHyperliquidHyperEVMHIP-3
So what: Bet on Hyperliquid's market-specific order-book model, not its general-purpose EVM ambitions; entrepreneurs are increasingly building HIP-3/HIP-4 markets and skipping HyperEVM.
“I think that's arguably why Hype EVM has failed in a lot of ways is like no one wanted the full programmable thing” ▶ 57:16
Hyperliquid faces intensifying perp/RFQ competition from Lighter (the only perp on a Delaware Corp, with token issued on the Delaware Corp), Ostium, Variational, and incumbents like ICE/NYSE that are tooling up by extending Ethereum technology.
thesis🗣 Joe Lubin / Tomconviction: mediumLighterOstiumVariationalICENYSEHyperliquidRFQ
So what: Watch the RFQ space and Lighter's regulatory-friendly Delaware structure as the credible challengers; TradFi entrants (ICE/NYSE) building on Ethereum tech are the structural long-term threat to crypto-native perp venues.
≈ “light lighter uh like great osteium ... variational as well ... There's a little company called ICE uh New York Stock Exchange ... they're actually building or extending Ethereum technology” paraphrase ▶ 53:08
Perps and prediction markets are framed as a casino-like 'entertainment layer' that financially onboards a largely illiterate global public, who can use AI to understand each instrument — a mass-adoption funnel for decentralized finance.
framework🗣 Joe Lubinconviction: mediumperpetualsprediction marketsPolymarketHyperliquid
So what: The simpler/gamified derivative products (perps, prediction markets) are the user-acquisition wedge for on-chain finance; adoption growth may come through this entertainment layer rather than complex DeFi.
“it's a bit more like a casino ... but to the extent that it can in draw people in uh and get them learning things” ▶ 53:59
Extended Ethereum: ZK composability & institutional rails 3
Consensys is building an 'extended metropolitan Ethereum' architecture using ZK proofs to enable atomic, bridge-free composability across L1, L2s, Linea networks and ~200 private permissioned Besu networks (including Citi's token services), aggregating proofs into L1/L2 blocks to move tokens without bridges.
thesis🗣 Joe Lubinconviction: highConsensysEthereumLineaBesuCitizero-knowledge proofsGnosis
So what: Bridge-free ZK composability targets the largest DeFi attack surface (bridges); if it works, it materially de-risks cross-chain value transfer and pulls private institutional networks into the ETH economy.
“We can have tokens move across these networks without the need for bridges because you're just doing zero knowledge proofs ... that is a essentially bridge-free architecture that removes a lot of the vulnerabilities.” ▶ 33:26
Consensys contributed Besu (execution client) to the Hyperledger Foundation and Linea (its L2) to Linux Foundation Decentralized Trust, with ~100 well-announced and ~100 quieter businesses built on Besu being brought 'into the Ethereum economy proper.'
anecdote🗣 Joe Lubinconviction: highConsensysBesuLineaHyperledgerLinux FoundationEthereum
So what: A real institutional/permissioned pipeline (~200 Besu networks, Citi) is being linked into public Ethereum — a concrete enterprise-adoption channel underpinning the ETH bull case beyond retail DeFi.
“There are 100 wellannounced projects that have built businesses on Basu. There's another hundred uh not so wellannounced. Uh we can bring all of those into the Ethereum economy proper.” ▶ 1:01:43
ETH is the 'least worst' major in the current selloff — ETH/BTC rising while ETH falls in USD — making it attractive only as a pair trade, not for outright holders, with its quantum roadmap and institutional rails cited as relative-strength drivers.
thesis🗣 panelconviction: mediumETHBitcoinETH/BTC
So what: Express any ETH conviction as a relative-value ETH/BTC pair rather than directional long during the drawdown; the differentiators (quantum, institutional adoption) are relative not absolute tailwinds.
“of the majors, Ethereum is the least worst ... Bullish for those of you who are doing the pair trade. For anyone else who's just holding ETH, it's a painful painful day.” ▶ 1:27

Top Traders Unplugged 2 episode(s) · 82 insights

Are Markets Becoming Living Systems? | Systematic Investor | Ep. 40291 min39 insights2026-05-31
▶ Watch on YouTube
Richard Brennan argues markets are complex adaptive systems whose behavior is set by their 'agent ecology', and that the 20-year rise of price-insensitive passive capital has diluted the balancing (convergent) agents while leaving the reinforcing (divergent) ones intact, structurally hardening the trend/cascade architecture and making markets more inelastic. He presents agent-based-model evidence that a ~25% minority of price-conditioned 'chartists' triggers a phase transition into the fat-tailed, memory-laden, volatility-clustering fingerprint seen across 68 markets and 40 years.
Passive ownership has restructured the agent ecology · 6Markets as complex adaptive systems, not equilibrium clocks · 5The endogenous engine and reflexivity · 4Feedback architecture: reinforcing vs balancing · 3Market inelasticity and the flow multiplier · 3Agent-based-model evidence and the 25% phase transition · 8Concentration, fragility and the 'extinction-level event' · 5Risk management: variance as control, correlation as diagnostic · 2Market snapshot and CTA positioning, May 2026 · 3
Passive ownership has restructured the agent ecology 6
Passive vehicles hold roughly 60% of US equity fund assets, and once benchmark-hugging pensions, sovereign wealth funds and endowments are included, the price-insensitive share of US equity ownership is meaningfully higher than 60%.
data_point🗣 Richard Brennanconviction: highpassive fundsETFsUS equitiespension fundssovereign wealth fundsendowments
So what: If most ownership no longer reacts to price, the marginal active dollar sets prices in a much thinner pool — a structural reason to expect larger directional moves.
“the most commonly cited figure puts passive at around about 60% of US equity fund assets” ▶ 3:49
The single biggest structural change in global markets over two decades is not algos, HFT or mega-cap dominance — those are downstream effects — but the transfer of ownership from price-sensitive to price-insensitive agents.
thesis🗣 Richard Brennanconviction: highpassive fundsHFTalgorithmic tradingmega-caps
So what: Reframes what to monitor: watch the price-insensitive ownership share, not the usual narratives, as the driver of market fragility.
“The structural change is the transfer of a growing share of market ownership from price sensitive agents to price insensitive ones.” ▶ 22:16
Passive is price-insensitive by design: it buys index constituents at any price in index proportion, does not buy more when cheap or less when expensive, and therefore performs no price discovery.
framework🗣 Richard Brennanconviction: highpassive fundsindex funds
So what: Explains why valuations can stretch indefinitely in index names — there is no built-in seller of the overpriced.
“Price is not an input into its decision function.” ▶ 13:17
Passive index funds belong on the reinforcing/divergent side of markets because cap-weighted inflows buy more of whatever is already largest, amplifying the existing distribution rather than fading it.
causal🗣 Richard Brennanconviction: highpassive fundsS&P 500index inclusion
So what: Implies passive inflows mechanically entrench winners and concentration, a tailwind for momentum and mega-cap leadership.
“Passive joins that divergent side because it's it's flows buys more of what is already large.” ▶ 19:40
Passive does not eliminate balancing agents (value investors, counter-trend traders, market makers still exist) but dilutes their proportional weight, thinning the balancing feedback that used to cap reinforcing loops.
thesis🗣 Richard Brennanconviction: highvalue investorscounter-trend tradersmarket makerspassive funds
So what: Trends should run further before meeting resistance — the brake, not the accelerator, is what has weakened.
“what has been diluted is the proportional weight of the agents conditioned on price relative to value” ▶ 1:05:04
The individual-level rationality of owning low-cost index funds and the system-level harm of price-insensitivity are separate questions with different answers; millions of sensible individual choices can change market structure in ways no individual intended.
framework🗣 Richard Brennanconviction: highindex fundspassive funds
So what: Defuses the 'passive is fine' rebuttal — the systemic fragility argument stands even if indexing is correct for the saver.
“Index funds can be a sensible choice for the individual and the aggregate consequence of millions of those sensible choices can have changed the structural behavior in markets” ▶ 15:31
Markets as complex adaptive systems, not equilibrium clocks 5
Markets are complex adaptive systems whose prices, trends and cascades emerge from interactions among agents holding partial models — not a reflection of any underlying economy or fundamental value.
framework🗣 Richard Brennanconviction: highcomplex adaptive systems
So what: Shifts the key analytical question from 'what is fair value' to 'who are the agents and how do they interact'.
“A market is constituted by the interactions of the population of agents, each holding a partial model of the situation” ▶ 9:01
The market is an autonomous system that merely correlates with — rather than reflects — the economy, the firm and the banking system; fundamental value lives in a separate system that need not agree with the market.
contrarian🗣 Richard Brennanconviction: highfundamental valuecentral banksmacroeconomy
So what: Justifies ignoring fundamentals as a price anchor and explains persistent valuation overshoots like April's moves.
“to say the market should reflect fundamental value is to assume a binding correlation between two autonomous systems that, on evidence, don't always agree” ▶ 10:39
The equilibrium/efficient-markets model treats markets as clocks (additive parts that don't observe each other) when they are flocks (agents that imitate, panic and anchor to each other), making classical finance a category error.
contrarian🗣 Richard Brennanconviction: highefficient market hypothesisequilibrium modelMBA finance
So what: Models built on equilibrium assumptions systematically underestimate tail risk and trend persistence.
“It's treating a flock as if it were a clock” ▶ 47:50
The equilibrium model survived because it is convenient — it produces spreadsheet numbers to value securities and convince investors — not because it is correct; the honest alternative is a hard research program nobody wants.
causal🗣 Richard Brennanconviction: highequilibrium model
So what: Suggests an enduring edge for practitioners who abandon equilibrium framing while institutions stay anchored to it.
“the equilibrium model has survived because it's convenient, not because it's right” ▶ 49:10
Emergent market structures — trends, crashes, volatility clustering and concentration in a handful of names — are real and structural, not anomalies or coincidences, like a starling murmuration that self-organizes with no central control.
framework🗣 Richard Brennanconviction: hightrend followingvolatility clusteringconcentration
So what: Treating trends/cascades as durable features rather than aberrations supports systematically trading them.
“trends are not anomalies, cascades are not aberrations, concentration is not coincidence”
The endogenous engine and reflexivity 4
Microstructure research (Bouchaud, Gabaix, Cohen) finds most price movement cannot be attributed to identifiable news — prices move on no-news days and out of proportion to the news that does arrive.
data_point🗣 Richard Brennanconviction: highJean-Philippe BouchaudXavier Gabaixmicrostructure research
So what: Undercuts news-driven trading narratives; the size of a move tells you more about internal positioning than about the catalyst.
“The vast majority of price movement can't be attributed to identifiable news.” ▶ 51:41
Most price movement is endogenous — the system processing itself in a loop where a breakout draws momentum buyers, which alters volatility, which triggers vol-targeting and rebalancing, which forces further price adjustment.
framework🗣 Richard Brennanconviction: highmomentum fundsvolatility targetingrebalancing
So what: Maps the mechanical chain that sustains cascades — useful for anticipating second-order forced flows after an initial move.
“Markets don't simply reflect the world, they process themselves.” ▶ 53:32
News is a trigger, not a driver: the match starts the fire but its shape and extent depend on the dryness of the wood (the sensitized feedback architecture), so a sensitized system can produce arbitrarily large moves from small sparks.
framework🗣 Richard Brennanconviction: highvolatilityfeedback loops
So what: Position for outsized moves when the system is 'dry' (calm, leveraged, concentrated), regardless of catalyst size.
“The fire's shape and extent depends on the dryness of the wood, not the size of the match.” ▶ 50:28
Soros's reflexivity is the operational mechanism of the endogenous engine: price-sensitive agents' reactions reshape the market, which reshapes the next observation and reaction in a continuous recursive loop.
framework🗣 Richard Brennanconviction: highGeorge Sorosreflexivity
So what: Explains why trends persist past any catalyst and why mean-reversion models fail in reflexive regimes.
“their reactions reshape the markets which reshape the next observation which reshape the next reaction” ▶ 54:59
Feedback architecture: reinforcing vs balancing 3
Market behavior is governed by two feedback modes — reinforcing (momentum, stop-losses, vol-targeting, index inclusion, dealer delta-hedging, passive inflows) that amplifies moves, and balancing (value buyers, overpriced-asset sellers, market makers, counter-trend) that absorbs them.
framework🗣 Richard Brennanconviction: highmomentumstop-lossesvolatility targetingoptions dealersvalue investorsmarket makers
So what: A practical taxonomy: gauge which mode dominates to judge whether a market is set to trend, range or break.
“When reinforcing loops dominate, the system becomes directional in trends form. When balancing loops dominate, the system becomes range bound.” ▶ 1:02:24
Divergent traders (trend followers, momentum) ride moves while convergent traders (value, mean-reversion, counter-trend) fade them; passive sits on the divergent/reinforcing side, so the reinforcing camp has grown while the convergent camp has been diluted.
framework🗣 Richard Brennanconviction: hightrend followersmomentum tradersmean-reversionvalue investorspassive funds
So what: The net shift toward reinforcing capital is a structural tailwind for trend-following returns.
“Reinforcing feedback comes from divergent traders and from passive long only investors.” ▶ 19:32
Phase transitions in volatility occur when influence shifts rapidly between reinforcing and balancing loops — the market 'breathes' between trending and range-bound states.
framework🗣 Richard Brennanconviction: highvolatilityregime change
So what: Watch for rapid handoffs between the two regimes as the precursor to volatility surges.
“When the influence shifts rapidly from one to the other, phase transitions occur and volatility surges.” ▶ 1:02:32
Market inelasticity and the flow multiplier 3
Because price-insensitive holders own the bulk of the float, active flow must find counterparties in a structurally thin price-sensitive pool, so each unit of flow moves price more — markets have become structurally less elastic.
causal🗣 Richard Brennanconviction: highinelastic markets hypothesispassive fundsfloat
So what: Expect outsized, non-linear price impact from modest active flows — sizing and liquidity assumptions from 20 years ago are now too generous.
“fewer price sensitive players in the active mix means more violent price action per unit of flow, not less” ▶ 1:08:58
Research (Gabaix, Koijen; Brennan also cites Caban/Kogan and Bouchaud) finds an inelasticity multiplier around 5 — $1 of net buying can add roughly $5 to aggregate market cap and $1 of selling destroy ~$5 — with literature estimates ranging from ~3:1 to ~7:1.
data_point🗣 Richard Brennanconviction: mediumXavier GabaixRalph Koijeninelastic markets hypothesis
So what: A 3x-7x flow multiplier means flow-of-funds and forced-rebalancing analysis is more predictive of price than fundamentals.
“$1 of net buying can increase aggregate market capitalization by approximately $5.” ▶ 1:08:05
Inelasticity ensures the trends the feedback architecture produces are larger than the flows that created them — the move exceeds the cause.
causal🗣 Richard Brennanconviction: hightrendsinelasticity
So what: Reinforces convexity in trend payoffs: the structural amplification favors persistent directional positioning.
“The inner elasticity ensures that the trends that produces are larger than the flows that created them.” ▶ 1:09:15
Agent-based-model evidence and the 25% phase transition 8
In a minimal two-agent model (price-conditioned 'chartists' vs value-based 'fundamentalists'), 0% chartists reproduces textbook efficient markets: Hurst ~0.54, negative excess kurtosis (-0.2), thinner-than-normal tails, zero five-sigma events.
data_point🗣 Richard Brennanconviction: highagent-based modelHurst exponentkurtosisefficient markets
So what: Shows the Gaussian/efficient world only exists with zero reinforcing feedback — a world that has never actually existed.
“Every assumption of efficient markets held in this world of zero chartists.” ▶ 1:14:59
At ~20-25% chartists the simulated market undergoes a sudden phase transition: kurtosis erupts, Hurst leaps above 0.85, and fat tails, memory and volatility clustering appear all at once — a modest minority of reinforcing agents flips the whole system.
data_point🗣 Richard Brennanconviction: highagent-based modelphase transitionHurst exponentvolatility clustering
So what: Real markets sit far above the 25% threshold (stop-losses, margin calls, vol-targeting, options hedging are all feedback), so the fat-tailed regime is the permanent state.
“we only needed to put 25% chartists who are price sensitive working in the reinforcing direction to create this phase transition” ▶ 1:15:59
The feedback-on simulation became statistically indistinguishable from 68 real futures markets spanning 8 asset classes, 6 continents and 41 years — feedback alone, with no news, fundamentals, central banks or geopolitics, reproduces the real-market fingerprint.
data_point🗣 Richard Brennanconviction: highfutures marketsagent-based model
So what: Strong (causal-experiment-grade) evidence that internal feedback, not macro narrative, is the central driver of market statistics.
“feedback is sufficient to generate the statistical signatures we observe” ▶ 1:18:46
Across 1985-2025 rolling 5-year windows, the Hurst exponent never fell to the 0.5 random-walk baseline, fluctuating between 0.62 and 0.82 and surviving Black Monday, electronic trading, the GFC, HFT and the COVID crash.
data_point🗣 Richard Brennanconviction: highHurst exponentBlack MondayGFCCOVID crashHFT
So what: Trend persistence is a continuous 40-year structural feature, not a fading anomaly — supporting strategic, not tactical, allocation to trend.
“the Hurst exponent never once fell to the random walk baseline of 0.5. It fluctuated between 0.62 and 0.82” ▶ 1:20:36
Trend following is not exploiting a temporary inefficiency that might close; it is harvesting a 40-year structural feature, and with passive diluting balancing agents the architecture is hardening — same fingerprint with more violence per unit of flow than 20 years ago.
thesis🗣 Richard Brennanconviction: hightrend followingpassive funds
So what: Core bullish case for the CTA/trend space: the edge is structural and strengthening, not decaying.
“the architecture is not weakening, it's hardening”
The world's 68 futures markets behave as one permanently coupled feedback engine, not 68 independent systems — the fraction sharing the majority feedback sign averaged ~0.6 (vs ~0.55 if independent) and reached 0.82 in the most coordinated regimes.
data_point🗣 Richard Brennanconviction: highfutures marketscross-market coupling
So what: Cross-asset diversification is weaker than it appears; correlated systematic models can synchronize drawdowns and crowd the same trades.
“They are one permanently coupled feedback engine with different surface manifestations in different asset classes” ▶ 1:24:17
The cross-market coupling is caused by practitioners deploying the same correlated models across many of the same markets, so a buy signal in one market tends to coincide with buy signals in others.
causal🗣 Richard Brennanconviction: highsystematic fundstrend followingdiversification
So what: Crowding risk: the diversification trend followers rely on is itself the source of synchronized, correlated positioning.
“Many of the same investors apply the same models across many of the same markets. That's what's creating the coupling.” ▶ 1:24:36
The coupling tilts toward positive feedback (trends emerge) during crisis episodes and toward negative feedback (oscillation) during QE-suppressed regimes, but the coupling itself never disappears.
causal🗣 Richard Brennanconviction: mediumquantitative easingcrisis regimestrends
So what: QE/liquidity-suppression regimes are the structural headwind for trend; crisis regimes the tailwind — watch policy stance as a regime signal.
“during quantitative easing suppressed regimes, the system collectively tilts towards negative feedback and oscillation emerges” ▶ 1:25:29
Concentration, fragility and the 'extinction-level event' 5
Loss of ecological diversity is dangerous: when strategies concentrate into a calm ('calm/car') regime and lever up via structured products to extract return from low vol, the system builds hidden risk and becomes fragile.
framework🗣 Richard Brennanconviction: highstructured productsvolatilityleverage
So what: Periods of strategy homogeneity plus leverage are the setup for non-linear breakouts — monitor crowding and embedded leverage in low-vol regimes.
“the market is actually building up risk in that car environment because it's getting a concentration of strategy in it” ▶ 26:30
A small deviation out of an over-concentrated, levered calm regime produces non-linear amplification and a massive breakout because the suppressed risk uncoils all at once.
causal🗣 Richard Brennanconviction: highvolatilitytail riskleverage
So what: Suppressed-vol regimes are not safe; they are coiled springs — favors long-convexity/tail and trend positioning into the breakout.
“you suddenly get this non-linear amplification and and this massive breakout that occurs out of that because the risk was building up”
The crowd into passive sets up a theoretical 'extinction-level event' — a massive uncoiling when everyone-passive meets a regime explosion — consistent with how complex adaptive markets cycle through growth-growth-extinction phases.
prediction🗣 Richard Brennanconviction: mediumpassive fundscomplex adaptive systemstail risk
So what: Flags a fat-tail crash scenario embedded in passive dominance — a reason to hold crash protection against an index unwind.
“What happens when suddenly they're they're met with this this explosion out of of a regime or a behavior that satisfies. You suddenly get this massive uncoiling” ▶ 28:23
Multi-strat 'pod shops' grew by trading against trend followers and suppressing breakouts, but when markets do break out they end up on the wrong side and amplify the eventual move.
anecdote🗣 host (Niels Kaastrup-Larsen)conviction: mediummulti-strategy fundspod shopstrend followers
So what: The convergent flows that cap volatility today can become accelerants in a breakout — second-order fuel for large trends.
“when the markets then do break out, they find themselves on the wrong side of that trade and then that actually amplifies the the the outcome” ▶ 27:54
Demography is the main structural force working against passive; the headwind and the eventual tailwind from a passive unwind may fall on different generations of investors.
thesis🗣 host (Niels Kaastrup-Larsen)conviction: lowpassive fundsdemographics
So what: Frames passive dominance as cyclical over a generational horizon — relevant for very long-horizon allocators expecting a reversal.
“what works against passive it's to some degree is demography” ▶ 28:57
Risk management: variance as control, correlation as diagnostic 2
A trend-following engine runs on variance, not correlation: positions are sized by Average True Range (ATR), so when an instrument's variance rises, position size shrinks automatically with no discretion before the next bar prints.
framework🗣 Richard Brennanconviction: highATRaverage true rangetrend followingvolatility targeting
So what: Counters the critique that trend ignores single-instrument stress — variance-based sizing is the built-in within-instrument risk control.
“every position, for example, in my program is sized by average true range, ATR, which is a direct measure of the instrument's own variance” ▶ 39:47
Correlation is the diagnostic (it tells you what the system is doing across instruments) and variance is the control (it tells you how to size, when to cut, where to back off) — a trend follower needs both at different levels of the architecture.
framework🗣 Richard Brennanconviction: highcorrelationvariancerisk management
So what: A clean mental model for separating portfolio-level diagnosis from position-level risk control.
≈ “The system tells you the weather and variance tells you whether to put on another coat right now.” paraphrase
Market snapshot and CTA positioning, May 2026 3
SocGen's spring 2026 investor sentiment survey shows discretionary global macro most in demand at 59%, with CTA trend far down at 29% interest and CTA non-trend at 28%; multi-strategy systematic fell to its lowest ranking of the last five surveys.
data_point🗣 host (Niels Kaastrup-Larsen)conviction: highSocGenCTA trenddiscretionary global macromulti-strategy systematic
So what: Low survey interest in trend can be a contrarian positive given the structural case; declining demand for multi-strat signals waning pod-shop popularity.
“with 59% respondents, it's discretionary global macro” ▶ 32:03
Through May 2026 trend was modestly positive: SocGen CTA index +0.72% in May / +11% YTD, SocGen Trend +1.07% / +11.31% YTD, while MSCI World +3.8% in May, the S&P 500 +4.43% (~+10.38% YTD, near all-time highs) and the US aggregate bond index roughly flat.
data_point🗣 host (Niels Kaastrup-Larsen)conviction: highSocGen CTA indexSocGen TrendMSCI WorldS&P 500US aggregate bond index
So what: Equities outpacing trend in May with bonds flat frames the relative-performance backdrop for systematic allocators.
“SocGen trend up 1.07 up 11.31 percent”
The TTU trend barometer fell to 39% at month-end (a level untouched for a while), and oil was down more than 5% over the prior week, signaling near-term weakening in the trend environment.
data_point🗣 host (Niels Kaastrup-Larsen)conviction: mediumTTU trend barometeroiltrend following
So what: A falling short-term trend barometer is a leading indicator of choppier conditions ahead for trend strategies.
“trend barometer update it doesn't look good at coming to the end of the month. It's down to 39%” ▶ 33:48
The Space Economy Is No Longer Science Fiction | Ideas Lab | Ep.4961 min43 insights2026-05-28
▶ Watch on YouTube
Historian Rainer Zitelmann argues the space economy has already shifted from state-led to private 'space capitalism'—SpaceX alone out-launched every nation in 2025 and cut launch costs ~95% via reusable rockets—but that the next leg (Mars/Moon settlement, asteroid mining) is gated by the absence of private property rights in space. He frames the imminent ~$70B+ SpaceX IPO as the catalyst that makes the thesis investable, while flagging ETFs over stock-picking and SpaceX's heavy key-man dependence on Elon Musk.
The privatization thesis: state-led to space capitalism · 3Cost-plus vs. fixed-price service model (the SpaceX edge) · 3Launch economics and reusability · 5Market dominance data points (2025) · 4Property rights as the gating constraint · 6Asteroid mining: timeline, economics, and in-space use · 7Space tourism: a luxury-to-mass adoption curve · 3Investing the thesis: SpaceX IPO, ETFs, and adjacencies · 9Why now: the missing incentive and US-China space race · 3
The privatization thesis: state-led to space capitalism 3
The space industry has flipped from being a government monopoly to a privately-led 'space capitalism' era driven by billionaire-founded companies, making it investable rather than a state activity.
thesis🗣 Rainer Zitelmann (guest, author)conviction: highSpaceXBlue OriginElon MuskJeff BezosNASA
So what: Frames space as a new private-equity/equity sector rather than a defense-budget line item; the central premise behind every named investment idea in the episode.
“today it's you see it's the time of private as I call it space capitalism and this is what the book is about” ▶ 5:37
Government-led space success (Apollo) was a historical exception driven by Cold War prestige, not a repeatable model, because once the incentive disappeared the program shrank fivefold and stopped.
framework🗣 Rainer Zitelmann (guest, author)conviction: highNASAApollo programSoviet Union
So what: Implies state space agencies will chronically underperform private firms absent an existential incentive—bearish on legacy cost-plus primes, bullish on commercial operators.
≈ “But in the moment when when there was the American flag on the moon, there was no incentives. They gone five times smaller and then stopped” paraphrase ▶ 41:00
The core driver of cost and performance in space is the incentive structure of contracts, not technology—'everything is about incentives.'
framework🗣 Rainer Zitelmann (guest, author)conviction: highNASASpaceX
So what: A lens for screening space exposure: favor fixed-price, profit-on-efficiency operators over cost-reimbursed contractors.
“everything is about incentives and there were no incentives to do it” ▶ 0:30
Cost-plus vs. fixed-price service model (the SpaceX edge) 3
NASA's legacy 'cost-plus' contracts (cost + ~8-10% margin) structurally incentivized contractors to inflate costs, because a higher cost base mechanically produced a larger absolute profit.
causal🗣 Rainer Zitelmann (guest, author)conviction: highNASABoeingRockwellcost-plus contracts
So what: Explains why legacy primes are high-cost and slow; a durable structural disadvantage versus fixed-price commercial competitors.
“Our our best product was our overhead” ▶ 16:05
SpaceX sells a fixed-price 'service' (like FedEx/UPS) rather than selling rockets, which flips the incentive so that lower internal cost equals higher profit—driving relentless cost reduction.
causal🗣 Rainer Zitelmann (guest, author)conviction: highSpaceXNASAFedExUPS
So what: The fixed-price service model is the moat; investors should look for it as the marker of a viable commercial space operator.
“lower costs would mean would mean more profit. And before with this cost plus program, higher costs mean more profit” ▶ 17:44
Even during Apollo, NASA never built its own rockets—private firms like Boeing and Rockwell did—but under micromanagement so tight NASA dictated which employees to hire.
data_point🗣 Rainer Zitelmann (guest, author)conviction: highNASABoeingRockwell
So what: Contextualizes that the shift is about contract structure and autonomy, not merely 'public vs private'—the variable that matters is operational control.
Launch economics and reusability 5
SpaceX has cut the self-cost of putting 1 kilogram into space by roughly 95% versus the Space Shuttle, primarily by pioneering reusable rockets.
data_point🗣 Rainer Zitelmann (guest, author)conviction: highSpaceXFalcon 9Space Shuttle
So what: A 95% cost collapse is the unlock for every downstream space business model (satellites, tourism, data centers); the key metric to track for the sector's TAM.
“he reduced it by 95% 95%. This is his self self cost”
Musk claims he can cut launch costs a further 90% from current levels, implying another order-of-magnitude reduction ahead.
prediction🗣 Rainer Zitelmann (guest, author, citing Musk)conviction: mediumElon MuskSpaceX
So what: If realized, further 90% cuts would open business cases (in-space manufacturing, mass tourism) that are uneconomic today—a forward catalyst for the whole stack.
“From this point, I will reduce it even 90% more” ▶ 20:14
A single Falcon 9 has been reused 34 times, analogized to not throwing away an airliner after one LA-to-NY flight; reusability is the dominant cost lever.
data_point🗣 Rainer Zitelmann (guest, author)conviction: highFalcon 9SpaceX
So what: Reuse cadence per booster is a tangible KPI for evaluating any launch competitor's cost competitiveness.
“The same rocket was used now 34 times” ▶ 18:37
No state-owned space agency—US, China, Russia, or Europe—has succeeded in building a reusable rocket; only two private firms have, with Bezos achieving it ~10 years after Musk.
contrarian🗣 Rainer Zitelmann (guest, author)conviction: highSpaceXBlue OriginElon MuskJeff BezosChinaRussiaESA
So what: Reusability capability is a near-duopoly (SpaceX, Blue Origin)—a structural competitive advantage and pricing power for those two.
“today no state uh owned uh space agency, not in China, not in Russia, not in Europe, and not in the United States was able to build such a reusable rocket”
As reusability commoditizes hardware, fuel/propellant becomes the next-largest cost component, pointing to the next area for cost innovation.
framework🗣 host (Kevin Coldiron) / guestconviction: mediumSpaceX
So what: Investors should watch propellant cost and in-space fuel sourcing (e.g., asteroid/Mars water) as the next margin frontier after launch reuse.
Market dominance data points (2025) 4
Of 324 rocket launches worldwide last year, the majority were SpaceX; as a single private company it out-launched every nation, ahead of China's 88 and Europe's 8.
data_point🗣 Rainer Zitelmann (guest, author)conviction: highSpaceXChinaEurope
So what: Quantifies SpaceX's near-monopoly on global launch—an extraordinary moat supporting a premium IPO valuation.
“if SpaceX were a country it would be number one far before China that comes with 88 launches” ▶ 22:28
Of ~15,000 active satellites in orbit, ~10,000 are SpaceX Starlink—roughly two-thirds of all operational satellites are owned by one private company.
data_point🗣 Rainer Zitelmann (guest, author)conviction: highSpaceXStarlink
So what: Starlink's orbital dominance underpins a recurring-revenue connectivity business that is likely the core of SpaceX's IPO valuation.
“we have today 15,000 active satellites in orbit. From this 15,000 uh 10,000 Starlink”
Rocket Lab, founded by Peter Beck (originally New Zealand, now US-based), achieved 21 launches last year at 100% success—about three times Europe's total—showing viable non-SpaceX private operators exist.
data_point🗣 Rainer Zitelmann (guest, author)conviction: highRocket LabPeter BeckEurope
So what: Names a specific publicly-investable launch alternative to SpaceX with a strong execution record; a way to get listed-equity space exposure pre-SpaceX-IPO.
“they had 21 rocket launches last year, 100% successful” ▶ 23:11
Europe is structurally far behind in launch (only 8 launches last year, ~5% of SpaceX's output despite being an entire continent of spacefaring nations).
data_point🗣 Rainer Zitelmann (guest, author)conviction: highEuropeSpaceX
So what: Bearish signal on European state launch programs; competitive opportunity skews to US (and US-domiciled) private players.
Property rights as the gating constraint 6
The 1967 Outer Space Treaty (Article 2) clearly bars nations from claiming ownership of celestial bodies, but is silent on private companies—creating legal ambiguity that is bad for investors.
framework🗣 Rainer Zitelmann (guest, author)conviction: highOuter Space Treaty 1967Article 2
So what: Property-rights uncertainty is the single biggest non-technical risk capping space-economy valuations; resolution would be a major re-rating catalyst.
“The only thing that is really clear is that it's not clear” ▶ 27:47
The 1984 Moon Agreement is effectively irrelevant because only ~20 countries signed it and no spacefaring nation did; it is dismissed as a 'socialist' common-good concept.
data_point🗣 Rainer Zitelmann (guest, author)conviction: highMoon Agreement 1984
So what: Investors can ignore the Moon Agreement; the binding legal framework is the Outer Space Treaty.
Large-scale Mars/Moon settlement (whether Musk's 1 million or Zubrin's 50,000 people) cannot be government-funded and is economically impossible without private property rights, because no economy on Earth has ever functioned without them.
thesis🗣 Rainer Zitelmann (guest, author)conviction: highElon MuskRobert ZubrinMars SocietyMarsMoon
So what: Settlement-scale space economy is contingent on a legal regime that does not yet exist—long-dated optionality, not near-term cash flow.
“It's not possible without private property because on Earth there is no system that works without private property” ▶ 29:06
A proposed mechanism: whoever can reach and develop territory in space (e.g., a Singapore-sized parcel of Mars) could claim ownership via a homesteading-style 'develop-it-to-keep-it' rule, then float it on the stock exchange as a REIT.
framework🗣 Rainer Zitelmann (guest, author)conviction: mediumMarsREITElon MuskSingapore
So what: If such a regime emerges, space land becomes a securitizable real-estate asset class—shareholders could 'own Mars' via listed vehicles.
“he should bring it to the stock exchange. Like a real real estate investment trust REIT and then who owns Mars? Every shareholder” ▶ 32:09
Enforcement of space property is grounded on Earth, not in space: a country that legalizes space ownership (the guest floats Milei's Argentina) could attract operators via regulatory arbitrage.
causal🗣 Rainer Zitelmann (guest, author)conviction: lowJavier MileiArgentinaElon MuskUnited States
So what: Jurisdictional competition over space law is a potential wildcard; the first nation to grant clear private space title could capture domicile of space firms.
≈ “maybe Javier Milei will make then better space law and then Elon Musk goes there” paraphrase
Tradable Mars/Moon land claims would likely spawn speculative pre-settlement trading driven by appreciation expectations, analogous to Bitcoin or equities.
prediction🗣 Rainer Zitelmann (guest, author)conviction: mediumMarsMoonBitcoin
So what: Anticipates a speculative space-land market emerging before any physical presence—an early, high-risk asset class to watch.
≈ “People will start to buy and and and sell only because they think they hope the same when you buy Bitcoin or stocks” paraphrase ▶ 34:31
Asteroid mining: timeline, economics, and in-space use 7
Asteroid mining will not happen in the next 5 years but is expected within 10-20 years, contingent on property-rights clarity.
prediction🗣 Rainer Zitelmann (guest, author)conviction: mediumasteroid mining
So what: Sets a concrete decade-plus horizon—asteroid-mining plays are long-dated venture bets, not near-term revenue.
≈ “I don't think that it will happen in the next 5 years... In the next 10 to 20 years it will happen” paraphrase ▶ 43:57
The bigger value of asteroid/space mining is using resources in space (water for propulsion and life support, materials for in-space construction) rather than returning them to Earth, since launching mass from Earth is costly even with cheaper rockets.
contrarian🗣 Rainer Zitelmann (guest, author)conviction: highasteroid miningElon Muskwaterpropulsion
So what: Reframes the asteroid-mining TAM around in-space supply chains (propellant depots, in-space data centers) rather than Earth commodity imports—different set of beneficiaries.
“most of the this what you do with asteroid mining is not for use on Earth... but for using to use it in space” ▶ 46:37
Only platinum-group metals (PGMs) and gold are economic to return to Earth; these are the exception, not the rule, for asteroid-mining returns.
data_point🗣 Rainer Zitelmann (guest, author)conviction: mediumplatinumPGM groupgoldasteroid mining
So what: Narrows the Earth-bound commodity case to PGMs/gold—relevant for sizing any 'space metals to Earth' thesis.
There are an estimated 700,000 to 1.7 million asteroids, mostly between Mars and Jupiter, but ~40,000 near-Earth asteroids are the practically reachable targets.
data_point🗣 Rainer Zitelmann (guest, author)conviction: mediumnear-Earth asteroidsMarsJupiter
So what: Defines the addressable target set; near-Earth asteroids are where any early mining venture economics will be built.
Asteroids are often loose 'rubble piles' held by weak gravity rather than solid rock, so landing on and mining them risks breaking them apart—a hard technical/operational constraint.
data_point🗣 host (Kevin Coldiron) / guestconviction: mediumasteroid mining
So what: A real engineering risk that tempers near-term asteroid-mining optimism and favors patient capital.
US executive orders under Obama and Trump established that resources extracted in space belong to the extractor and do not violate Outer Space Treaty Article 2—but this is only a 'halfway' solution because you still cannot own the land.
data_point🗣 Rainer Zitelmann (guest, author)conviction: highObamaTrumpTed CruzMarco RubioOuter Space Treaty
So what: Resource-extraction rights exist but land title does not, leaving financing hard—the regulatory gap investors must monitor.
The two first-generation asteroid-mining companies (lobbied for by Ted Cruz and Marco Rubio) ultimately failed, showing the sector is harder and earlier-stage than hype suggested.
anecdote🗣 Rainer Zitelmann (guest, author)conviction: highTed CruzMarco Rubioasteroid mining
So what: Cautionary precedent: first-wave asteroid miners went bust—underwrite this theme with deep skepticism on timing.
“these two companies failed in the end” ▶ 47:31
Space tourism: a luxury-to-mass adoption curve 3
Space tourism is bifurcated: a ~$300,000 'cheap' suborbital few-minute ride (Blue Origin, Virgin Galactic) versus ~$40-50 million orbital/ISS trips affordable only to billionaires.
data_point🗣 Rainer Zitelmann (guest, author)conviction: highBlue OriginVirgin GalacticJeff BezosRichard BransonInternational Space Station
So what: Concrete current price points define the tourism market's two tiers and the cost gap that must close for mass adoption.
“the other version to go for example to the International Space Station... This is I think today it would cost like 40 or 50 million” ▶ 48:38
All transformative innovations (windows, cars, flight, safaris) began as luxuries for the ultra-rich who financed them, then cost-curved down to mass affordability—space tourism will follow the same path.
framework🗣 Rainer Zitelmann (guest, author)conviction: highspace tourism
So what: An adoption-curve mental model for sizing space-tourism's long-run TAM; today's billionaire pricing is the early-adopter phase, not the ceiling.
“all innovation in history were first for very rich people... in the end it becomes cheaper for for everyone” ▶ 49:40
Jared Isaacman—billionaire, owner of the largest private fighter-jet collection, and a SpaceX space tourist who paid ~$40M—is cited as the new NASA administrator, signaling commercial-space alignment at the top of the agency.
data_point🗣 Rainer Zitelmann (guest, author)conviction: mediumJared IsaacmanNASASpaceX
So what: A commercially-aligned NASA leadership is a tailwind for private contractors and against legacy cost-plus models.
Investing the thesis: SpaceX IPO, ETFs, and adjacencies 9
The SpaceX IPO—expected in June, targeting a raise of more than $70 billion—would be by far the largest IPO in history (vs. the prior record of ~$29 billion), validating the space economy as real and investable.
prediction🗣 Rainer Zitelmann (guest, author)conviction: highSpaceXElon Musk
So what: A near-term, dateable catalyst (June) and the single biggest liquidity event in the sector—central to any space-economy positioning timeline.
“he tries to raise more more than 70 billion dollars. And this is by far the biggest IPO in history because so far the biggest IPO they launched 29 billion” ▶ 57:41
SpaceX has the potential to become the largest company in the world given its current launch and satellite monopoly, but it is not cheap (compared to Tesla's elevated earnings multiples).
thesis🗣 Rainer Zitelmann (guest, author)conviction: mediumSpaceXTeslaElon Musk
So what: Frames SpaceX as a high-upside but richly-valued IPO; valuation discipline matters despite the monopoly narrative.
“They have the opportunity to be the biggest company in the world, maybe... but of course on the other side it's not cheap” ▶ 55:18
The dominant risk to SpaceX is key-man dependence on Elon Musk—'what if he's going to die or have a heart attack'—a concentration risk that could impair the whole thesis.
contrarian🗣 Rainer Zitelmann (guest, author)conviction: mediumElon MuskSpaceX
So what: Explicit key-man risk flag—position-sizing and hedging consideration for any concentrated SpaceX bet.
≈ “it depends a lot maybe on Elon Musk. So, it's this is the risk. What is if he's uh going to die or going to have a heart attack” paraphrase
The guest prefers passive space ETFs (he notes roughly seven or eight listed) over stock-picking, citing statistics that few retail investors successfully pick stocks.
thesis🗣 Rainer Zitelmann (guest, author)conviction: highspace ETFs
So what: Endorses diversified ETF exposure as the prudent way to play an early, high-dispersion theme rather than concentrating in single names.
“I'm more this what you mentioned, passive ETF... there are not so many uh layman people who are really successful with stock picking” ▶ 54:46
Beyond launch primes (SpaceX, Blue Origin), secondary 'picks-and-shovels' industries—component and raw-material suppliers—will benefit from space-economy growth.
framework🗣 host (Kevin Coldiron) / guestconviction: mediumSpaceXBlue Origin
So what: Points investors toward supply-chain adjacencies as a lower-profile way to gain space exposure.
Planet Labs (SF/Berlin-based) and Rocket Lab are flagged as early space equities; Planet Labs is partnering with Google on in-space data centers and operates ~200 satellites.
data_point🗣 Rainer Zitelmann (guest, author)conviction: mediumPlanet LabsRocket LabGooglePeter Beck
So what: Names specific listed/early companies and an emerging in-space-data-center theme—concrete watchlist items beyond SpaceX.
“they work together with with Google for data center in space” ▶ 54:23
In-space data centers are an emerging use case being pursued by both Musk and Planet Labs/Google, creating demand for in-space-sourced materials and power.
prediction🗣 Rainer Zitelmann (guest, author)conviction: mediumElon MuskPlanet LabsGoogledata centers
So what: Links the space thesis to the AI/compute demand cycle—orbital data centers could become a second-order beneficiary of both trends.
An investment-bank forecast (possibly Bank of America/Merrill Lynch) from ~3-4 years ago that the world's first trillionaire would be a space entrepreneur is now plausibly on track, with Musk's net worth cited near $840 billion and an IPO likely to push him past $1 trillion.
prediction🗣 Rainer Zitelmann (guest, author)conviction: mediumElon MuskBank of AmericaMerrill LynchSpaceX
So what: A once-derided forecast nearing reality signals the magnitude of value the market is ascribing to private space—momentum context for the IPO.
“After the IPO definitely he will be the first trillionaire and the prediction was correct” ▶ 56:45
Macro/geopolitical shocks (Iran, unpredictable US policy moves) could derail the timing of the SpaceX IPO regardless of the company's fundamentals.
causal🗣 Rainer Zitelmann (guest, author)conviction: mediumSpaceXIranTrump
So what: IPO timing is exposed to exogenous macro risk—event-driven traders should not treat the June window as certain.
“what happens with Iran or what does Trump tomorrow two things no one can predict” ▶ 57:21
Why now: the missing incentive and US-China space race 3
There are no technical 'showstoppers' to reaching Mars (per NASA Ames' Harry Jones, who lists ~8 huge but solvable problems); the binding constraint is economic incentive, not engineering.
causal🗣 Rainer Zitelmann (guest, author, citing Harry Jones)conviction: highHarry JonesNASA AmesMars
So what: Reframes Mars as an economics/financing problem rather than a tech-risk problem—shifting the analytical focus to business models and property rights.
“There are no technical showstoppers for our way to Mars” ▶ 40:18
A new US-China space race is reviving national-prestige incentives reminiscent of the 1960s US-Soviet competition, but prestige alone is insufficient—economic incentives are needed to sustain progress.
framework🗣 Rainer Zitelmann (guest, author)conviction: highChinaUnited StatesSoviet Union
So what: Geopolitical competition provides a tailwind (budgets, urgency) but the durable driver is commercial economics—favoring private operators over time.
“now it's China and the United States. It's again about national prestige, but I think this is not enough” ▶ 51:50
Wernher von Braun himself argued post-Apollo that future space efforts must make economic sense—an early endorsement of the commercial-incentive thesis from the program's chief architect.
anecdote🗣 Rainer Zitelmann (guest, author, citing von Braun)conviction: mediumWernher von BraunApollo programNASA
So what: Historical validation that the economics-first framing predates today's private-space era; supports the structural thesis.
“We we should make it in a way that it makes economical in economic sense” ▶ 41:41

Real Vision · Raoul Pal 2 episode(s) · 78 insights

Why Hyperliquid Is the "Gold Standard" | REKT Vision with @blocmates58 min38 insights2026-06-01
▶ Watch on YouTube
Grant of Blocmates explains his early (2023) Hyperliquid thesis: a purpose-built, product-market-fit DEX with a near-fair launch that has become arguably the most profitable business per employee on the planet, with FDV ~$62B closing in on BNB. He extends the 'equal and opposite reaction' framework to the privacy-coin narrative (Zcash/Monero), is sharply critical of Ethereum's lack of direction versus Solana/Tempo, and is bullish on stablecoin/agentic micropayments while warning retail will get wrecked on the coming OpenAI/Anthropic/SpaceX IPOs.
Hyperliquid thesis & origin · 4Hyperliquid valuation, revenue & competitive position · 8Tokenomics & the VC-vs-fair-launch model · 3Privacy-coin narrative (Zcash, Monero) · 5Ethereum critique vs Solana/Tempo · 6Near Protocol & AI-chain narrative · 2AI agents, stablecoins & micropayments · 4AI private valuations & IPO risk · 3AI cost & infrastructure constraints · 3
Hyperliquid thesis & origin 4
Hyperliquid was identified as an early winner by Blocmates in 2023 specifically because it was a purpose-built, single-use-case chain rather than another general-purpose blockspace L1, which they judged to be hitting saturation.
thesis🗣 Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)conviction: highHyperliquidCosmosBlocmates
So what: The winning bet was on application-specific chains with real product-market fit over generic L1s — a template for screening future early-stage crypto teams.
“we felt like the demand for that general purpose block space was kind of hitting like a plateau, a s saturation and we thought well what's the again what's the equal and opposite reaction to that so purpose-built” ▶ 7:41
Grant's framework for picking early-stage crypto winners is that the team must be doing something new, novel and unique rather than replicating a well-trodden playbook, because copycat projects hit a law of diminishing returns.
framework🗣 Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)conviction: highHyperliquid
So what: A repeatable filter for early-stage crypto allocation: weight novelty and founder conviction over me-too execution.
“It has to be new. It has to be novel. It has to be unique and people have to be thinking a little bit outside the box” ▶ 6:50
Hyperliquid is described as a best-in-class product whose go-to-market — galvanizing a community and giving a large share of token supply to the market rather than defending an inflated price — was as important to its success as the technology.
thesis🗣 Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)conviction: highHyperliquidJeff (Hyperliquid founder)
So what: Product quality alone is insufficient; investors should weight token distribution design and community alignment as core to durable value.
“obviously the product's killer. It's like it's best-in-class product, but then that also has to match up with how you go to market” ▶ 8:33
Hyperliquid's team was able to self-fund because they ran a very successful market-making business before launching, letting them avoid VC funding and offer a near-fair launch — a precondition Grant notes is hard to replicate without being 'giga-rich' already.
causal🗣 Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)conviction: highHyperliquid
So what: The fair-launch model is gated by founders' pre-existing wealth, so it will stay rare — scarcity that makes genuine fair launches a premium signal.
“I think the Hyperlid team had a very very successful market making business before this” ▶ 13:32
Hyperliquid valuation, revenue & competitive position 8
Hyperliquid's FDV was ~$62 billion at the time of recording (HYPE ~$65), with Grant noting only USDC and XRP (about 2x higher) ranked above it and that it is closing in on BNB.
data_point🗣 Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)conviction: highHyperliquidHYPEUSDCXRPBNB
So what: Sets concrete valuation anchors; a 'flippening' of BNB is the next watchable milestone for HYPE holders.
“just looking at like FDV it's at what's it 62 billion FDV. I think you've got only USDC higher XRP which is 2x from here. Um yeah it's closing in on BMB” ▶ 16:29
Grant expects HYPE catching BNB to become a self-fulfilling prophecy and warns that take-profit selling is likely as the two market caps/FDVs converge, given Jeff was in an early Binance Accelerator cohort and the rivalry is now head-to-head.
prediction🗣 Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)conviction: mediumHyperliquidHYPEBNBBinanceJeff (Hyperliquid founder)
So what: Traders should anticipate profit-taking resistance as HYPE's FDV approaches BNB's, even if the long-term narrative is bullish.
≈ “people will probably have take profit targets as those uh market caps and FDVS converge” paraphrase ▶ 17:08
Grant calls Hyperliquid arguably the most profitable business on the planet on a revenue-per-employee basis, reportedly run by only 12-15 people.
data_point🗣 Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)conviction: highHyperliquid
So what: Extreme revenue-per-head is the fundamental underpinning the bull case; a key metric to track versus traditional exchanges.
“if not the most profitable business on the planet per revenue per per employee” ▶ 17:17
Hyperliquid has surpassed Coinbase's volume and competes with Binance's daily volume, and the head of the NYSE reportedly called it bigger than the NASDAQ despite running on ~12-15 people.
data_point🗣 Grant (guest, Blocmates founder); hostconviction: highHyperliquidCoinbaseBinanceNYSENASDAQ
So what: A decentralized exchange now rivals incumbent global venues on volume — a structural threat to centralized exchange revenue.
“the the the head of New York Stock Exchange basically saying it's bigger than the NASDAQ and it's running on like 12 to 15 people” ▶ 15:46
Hyperliquid added a third revenue stream via a deal with Coinbase and Circle, on top of its DEX and the perps product (which now lets users trade oil, gold and silver).
data_point🗣 hostconviction: mediumHyperliquidCoinbaseCircle
So what: Revenue diversification beyond crypto perps (into commodities and a Coinbase/Circle partnership) broadens the addressable market and de-risks the cash flow.
“recently they came they have that deal with Coinbase and Circle which you know just essentially adds a third revenue stream to them” ▶ 14:26
Matt Hougan of Bitwise frames Hyperliquid as a 'super app' targeting the $600 trillion global asset market rather than the $3 trillion crypto economy.
thesis🗣 host (citing Matt Hougan, Bitwise)conviction: mediumHyperliquidMatt HouganBitwise
So what: If the TAM reframing holds, current FDV may still be early relative to a $600T target — the core long-term bull case.
“Hyperlquid is not a crypto app. It's a super app. It's not targeting the $3 trillion crypto economy. It's targeting the $600 trillion global asset market.” ▶ 14:43
Grant frames HYPE's appeal via an analogy: it is as if Base were an L1 that took 97% of Coinbase trading fees and used them to buy back and burn the token — implying aggressive fee-driven value accrual.
framework🗣 host / Grantconviction: highHyperliquidHYPEBaseCoinbase
So what: The buyback-and-burn fee mechanism is the value-accrual engine; investors should track fee capture and burn rate as the key fundamental.
≈ “what if Bass was just an L1 and they said... we're going to take 97% of our fees and buy back and burn base. Like, how bullish would people be on that token? Like, that's literally hype.” paraphrase ▶ 17:48
Grant argues markets are mispricing high-revenue crypto assets, noting that on fundamentals Hyperliquid should rank top-five or even top-three, while low-revenue tokens persist 'for no apparent reason.'
contrarian🗣 Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)conviction: mediumHyperliquid
So what: Implies further re-rating upside for revenue-generating tokens versus zombie tokens as the market matures toward fundamentals.
“on paper you would expect to see it in top five, top maybe even three. But, you know, thinking too logically sometimes can get you killed” ▶ 17:33
Tokenomics & the VC-vs-fair-launch model 3
Grant argues the VC low-float/high-FDV token model broke retail trust: tokens launched at 3-10% float after private price discovery, leaving buyers defending unrealistic valuations that consistently bled lower ('VC coins').
causal🗣 Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)conviction: highVC tokens
So what: Low-float/high-FDV launches are structurally bearish for late buyers; screen new listings for float and FDV before participating.
“you'd get a token come to market with, I don't know, three to maybe 10% float supply if you were lucky. Um, price discovery had already happened.” ▶ 11:37
Grant frames the market reaction to VC coins as two opposite extremes: pump.fun-style instant $2 token launches (too far the other way) versus the more pragmatic Hyperliquid path of building a genuinely demanded product with fair, non-gatekept access.
framework🗣 Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)conviction: highHyperliquidpump.funVC tokens
So what: Positions fair-launch product-led tokens as the durable middle path between extractive VC launches and worthless memecoins.
“One of them was pumped up fun [pump.fun]... And the and the more ethical and more like pragmatic response to it was something like hyperlquid.” ▶ 12:13
Grant highlights Megaeth's KPI-based vesting — where the team only vests on hitting tangible milestones — as a step in the right direction for aligning founders with token holders.
data_point🗣 Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)conviction: mediumMegaeth
So what: Milestone-gated vesting is an emerging structure worth watching as a sign of founder alignment in new launches.
“I think what Mega Reef [Megaeth] did was pretty interesting where it was like KPI based vesting. So, nobody's going to eat unless we hit like actual tangible milestones.” ▶ 13:42
Privacy-coin narrative (Zcash, Monero) 5
Grant argues privacy is the next major crypto narrative — potentially bigger than anything before it — because the 'institutions are coming' Bitcoin thesis is now played out and privacy is the last asset class people can buy into on ideological grounds.
thesis🗣 Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)conviction: highBitcoinZcashMoneroprivacy coins
So what: Positions privacy coins as the next thematic rotation for ideologically-driven capital; an early-cycle narrative to accumulate.
“the next battle which I think is going to be much larger than anything that's came before it is privacy” ▶ 19:21
Grant says the privacy narrative has been building since late Q3 2025 and is a sustained, multi-catalyst theme rather than a flash-in-the-pan, driven by ZK, FHE and TEE tech maturing alongside rising geopolitical tension.
thesis🗣 Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)conviction: highprivacy coinsZKFHETEE
So what: A durable narrative with converging tech and macro tailwinds is more investable than a single-catalyst pump; tracks back to late Q3 2025 as the start.
“this has kind of been ticking over since Q late Q3 2025 and is continuing to be one of the main stairs” ▶ 22:49
Grant discloses he holds a position in Zcash and would like to see it outperform, citing a major late-2024 (November) upgrade around shielded transactions and a strong mobile-first wallet, while declining to rank privacy coins on tech.
anecdote🗣 Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)conviction: mediumZcashMonero
So what: A named position with a stated catalyst (shielded transactions + mobile wallet) — note the disclosed bias when weighing his view.
“from like put my investor head on, I'd probably like to see Zcash do a little bit better, but that's only because I've got a bias because I've got a position in it.” ▶ 21:32
Old-guard Bitcoin cypherpunks including the Winklevoss twins and Balaji are reportedly rotating capital into Zcash, signaling smart-money interest in privacy coins.
data_point🗣 hostconviction: mediumZcashBitcoinWinklevossBalaji
So what: Named smart-money flows into Zcash are a leading indicator that retail typically follows later.
