“We're seeing some of the old Bitcoin like cipher funks... moving from Bitcoin into Zcash... Winkle VI, Bellagi, all of them.” ▶ 18:15
Grant's broader framework is that real wealth is made buying narratives at the lows in bear markets (e.g. Solana at $8-10 last cycle) before they hit CNBC/CNN, not after mainstream coverage.
framework🗣 Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)conviction: highSolanaprivacy coins
So what: Reinforces accumulating emerging narratives (like privacy) early rather than chasing headline-driven tops.
“thinking about maybe Salana last cycle 8 to10... that's where the true meat is actually made it's not when you start seeing it all on CNN CNBC” ▶ 23:37
Ethereum critique vs Solana/Tempo 6
Grant is sharply critical of Ethereum, arguing it has lost direction through constant pivots, flip-flopping ideas and leadership changes, and was wrong to be dragged into a throughput battle with Solana it can never win.
contrarian🗣 Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)conviction: highEthereumSolanaVitalik Buterin
So what: A bearish structural read on ETH relative to faster, more organized chains; weigh against consensus 'ETH near and dear' sentiment.
“I think they've been dragged into a throughput conversation with Salana and other highly performing pragmatic chains which which they're never going to win.”
Grant warns that if Ethereum keeps selling its treasury at the current rate against a roadmap to 2030, it could run out of funds by 2027 — sending mixed signals to the market.
prediction🗣 Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)conviction: mediumEthereum
So what: A concrete treasury-runway risk (2027) that, if real, undermines ETH's long-horizon roadmap credibility — a fundamental to monitor.
“if you keep selling your treasury at the rate you're going to go, you're going to run out of funds by 2027, right?” ▶ 27:10
Grant frames Ethereum's core problem as ideology-versus-pragmatism: ETH is the maximally-decentralized ideological chain, but the market clearly prefers pragmatic, demand-driven chains like Solana and Hyperliquid.
framework🗣 Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)conviction: highEthereumSolanaHyperliquid
So what: Implies capital rotation toward pragmatic high-throughput chains; the ideology premium is no longer rewarded by price.
“you've got this like ideology versus pragmatism battle going on and the market's shown you which one it prefers” ▶ 31:00
Grant flags Tempo — backed by Stripe with $500M behind it, Paradigm involved, and reportedly poaching lead Ethereum researchers — as a serious new competitor with built-in Stripe distribution.
data_point🗣 Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)conviction: highTempoStripeParadigmEthereum
So what: A well-funded, distribution-rich Stripe-backed chain poaching ETH talent is a competitive threat worth tracking as it launches.
“the likes of Tempo which is backed by Stripe and has been porting some of the Ethereum like lead Ethereum researchers... it's got $500 million behind it and paradigm involved” ▶ 26:30
Grant argues Ethereum erred by de-emphasizing DeFi — the financial-primitives use case that originally put it on the map — and should fix its DeFi 'house' first before pursuing artistic and social pursuits.
causal🗣 Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)conviction: highEthereumDeFi
So what: Suggests the durable value of any smart-contract chain is permissionless financial access; chains that lean into DeFi distribution win.
≈ “I also don't like the fact that they've basically done everything they can not to promote what happened in the DeFi sector, which basically put them on the map.” paraphrase
Grant contends Solana's organizational advantage (clear leadership, north star, execution) decisively beats Ethereum, and dismisses the 'Solana is insufficiently decentralized' critique as weak.
contrarian🗣 Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)conviction: highSolanaEthereum
So what: Centralized, well-led execution is treated as a feature not a bug — a bull case for SOL versus ETH's diffuse governance.
“who's steering the ship, who has the grand vision, what is the north star... If you put them side by side, it's night and day.” ▶ 30:08
Near Protocol & AI-chain narrative 2
Grant calls Near Intents and Near's just-rolled-out confidential transactions two of the most underrated products in crypto, citing strong revenue and excellent UX, and notes Near appears fully vested with no large unlocks pending.
thesis🗣 Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)conviction: mediumNear ProtocolNear Intents
So what: A multi-tailwind (AI + privacy + no supply overhang) liquid bet; fully-vested supply removes a common overhang risk.
“they've got two products that are probably the most two underrated products in the full industry and one of them is Near Intents... the revenue on that thing is absolutely nuts” ▶ 32:33
Grant says Near is capturing multiple tailwinds at once (AI work, intents, confidential transactions, no supply overhang), making it easily justifiable as a liquid open-market purchase.
thesis🗣 Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)conviction: mediumNear ProtocolAI
So what: Stacked tailwinds plus clean tokenomics make NEAR a higher-conviction liquid allocation than single-narrative plays.
“any liquid funds very very easily justifiable if you want to go and purchase some near in the open market” ▶ 34:25
AI agents, stablecoins & micropayments 4
Grant predicts subscription pricing dies in favor of headless, pay-per-use micropayments executed by agents pinging APIs and webhooks from chat interfaces (Telegram, WhatsApp, ChatGPT, Claude), naming X402 and Tempo's MPPP as early rails.
prediction🗣 Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)conviction: highX402TempoMPPPstablecoinsAI agents
So what: Micropayment/agentic-payment rails are an early, explosive opportunity; businesses enabling pay-per-use get first-mover advantage.
“the idea of paying for something per month or annually is going to be gone. I think it's going to be pay per use. It's going to be microtransactions.” ▶ 42:36
Grant predicts the next ~100 million on-chain users will be AI agents rather than new humans, making programmable money at the stablecoin level a highly interesting investment theme.
prediction🗣 Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)conviction: highAI agentsstablecoins
So what: If agents drive the next user wave, stablecoin and agent-payment infrastructure is the pick-and-shovel play, not human-onboarding apps.
“the next 100 million users that are operating particularly on chain are definitely going to be agents as opposed to the new cohort of humans” ▶ 54:00
Grant dismisses the argument that AI agents will spin up their own chains and bypass existing ones, reasoning that human operators control agents and will route them down the path of least resistance using already-built infrastructure.
contrarian🗣 Grant / hostconviction: mediumAI agentsblockchains
So what: Supports incumbent-chain value accrual from the agentic economy rather than a disruptive new-chain thesis.
“it's the human operator that's controlling it... they're going to go the path of least resistance and with infrastructure already built” ▶ 41:41
Grant argues regulation and over-bureaucracy in personalized medicine will, over the next 5-10 years, cause more deaths than it prevents, because AI models can readily map DNA/RNA and design patient-specific antibodies (e.g. CAR-T-style therapy).
contrarian🗣 Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)conviction: highpersonalized medicineAnthropicClaudeAI
So what: A bullish, contrarian read on AI-driven personalized medicine and a bet that breakthroughs happen outside heavy regulatory jurisdictions.
“I think regulation in the next 5 10 years is going to cause more deaths than it's actually going to save people.” ▶ 49:52
AI private valuations & IPO risk 3
Grant agrees retail will likely get wrecked on the coming AI IPOs, arguing companies like OpenAI, Anthropic and SpaceX stay private longer because they have near-infinite access to private capital (e.g. Stripe-style secondary buyers, an SF house listed for ~$8M in Anthropic exposure instead of cash).
prediction🗣 Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)conviction: mediumOpenAIAnthropicSpaceXStripe
So what: Implies the public-market entry point for these names will be late-cycle and unfavorable for retail; the gains accrue privately.
“once that does run dry, retailer stupid is the is the is the thought.” ▶ 38:34
Grant frames the AI valuation question against Nvidia at ~$5 trillion, arguing there may still be upside but that allocating at trillion-dollar private valuations forces tiny relative returns versus going higher up the stack into consumer, defense tech, robotics, quantum or photonics.
framework🗣 Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)conviction: mediumNvidiaOpenAIAnthropicdefense techroboticsquantumphotonics
So what: Suggests rotating risk capital toward earlier-stage adjacent narratives (defense, robotics, quantum, photonics) rather than mega-cap AI at peak valuations.
“you got to be putting minimill size in at a trillion dollar valuation if you wanted to decent return compared to... going a little bit higher up the stack” ▶ 37:58
Grant warns the AI IPO wave will create a huge liquidity suck as these mega-caps go public, and that Elon Musk's cult-like following lets him take almost anything public — with SpaceX likely going first.
prediction🗣 Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)conviction: mediumSpaceXOpenAIAnthropicElon MuskTesla
So what: A large liquidity drain from AI IPOs could pressure other risk assets (including crypto) as capital rotates into the new listings.
“I think it might be quite scary when and I a huge liquidity suck when when these things start going live” ▶ 39:03
AI cost & infrastructure constraints 3
Grant warns AI usage costs are currently subsidized and risk becoming unaffordable until inference costs fall, with the GPU bottleneck (Nvidia reportedly sold out to 2028) and an electricity/power bottleneck for giga data centers both unresolved.
causal🗣 host / Grantconviction: mediumNvidiaGPUsAI inferencedata centersenergy
So what: Power and GPU supply are the binding constraints on the agentic economy; energy and compute infrastructure are the durable investment chokepoints.
“It's not just Nvidia, you know, priced out till 2028 to get their GPUs into people's hands. It's also where you get the electricity” ▶ 44:27
Grant (and the host) report defaulting away from open-source agent frameworks like Open Claw because OpenAI and Anthropic ship native features (scheduled jobs, mobile/terminal interfaces) overnight, making third-party tooling redundant and not worth the debugging time.
anecdote🗣 Grant / hostconviction: mediumOpen ClawOpenAIAnthropicClaudeCodex
So what: Platform incumbents are absorbing open-source agent tooling features, compressing the moat for independent agent wrappers — a competitive risk for that category.
“OpenAI and Anthropic and just turn on features overnight... it became kind of like redundant to the point of am I spending more time trying to fix this” ▶ 46:33
Grant sees media as an increasingly strong business vertical in the AI era because distribution, credibility and reputation can't be instantly created by AI models, citing OpenAI's reported acquisition of a media property as evidence incumbents must buy that trust.
thesis🗣 Grant (guest, Blocmates founder)conviction: highmediaOpenAIBlocmates
So what: Human-led media with distribution and credibility becomes a scarce, acquirable asset as AI commoditizes content — a counter-cyclical bet.
“reputation can't just be immediately created by any AI model and they need to be able to purchase that” ▶ 4:54
How Crypto and TradFi Are Starting to Merge | Moulik Nagesh of Binance54 min40 insights2026-05-31
▶ Watch on YouTube
Binance macro research lead Moulik Nagesh argues this crypto cycle is defined by institutionalization and regulatory clarity that are structurally lowering volatility, and by a two-way convergence of crypto and TradFi rails — ETFs and tokenization pulling crypto onto TradFi rails, while stablecoins, tokenized assets, and L1 specialization onboard liquidity on-chain. He flags stablecoins (especially B2B payments) as the dominant real use case, the Clarity Act and yield as the key regulatory swing factor, and rising quantum and DeFi-exploit risks as new premia to be priced in.
Institutionalization & structurally lower volatility · 4Bitcoin as a maturing macro asset class · 4ETF expansion up the risk curve · 3L1 consolidation & specialization · 4Crypto-TradFi convergence (two-way) · 7Stablecoins as the dominant real use case · 424/7 markets, perps & price discovery · 3AI agents on crypto rails · 2On-chain debt, credit & money markets · 2Regulation as the primary catalyst · 3Emerging risks to price in (quantum & DeFi exploits) · 3Token valuation gap & what TradFi misunderstands · 1
Institutionalization & structurally lower volatility 4
Crypto's buyer base has shifted from retail-driven to institution-driven, making demand more mandate-based and structurally lowering volatility versus prior cycles' peak-to-trough swings.
thesis🗣 Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)conviction: highBinanceBitcoininstitutional investorsretail
So what: Lower realized vol and stickier flows change the risk/return profile and option pricing for crypto; trend-following and vol-selling strategies may need recalibrating.
“the size of demand uh a large proportion of it is coming in from institutional side” ▶ 0:19
During the recent ~30-40% market drawdown, Bitcoin ETF assets under management had a much smaller marginal drop than spot price, evidencing that institutional ETF capital is stickier and less reactive than retail.
data_point🗣 Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)conviction: highBitcoin ETFsBitcoin
So what: ETF AUM resilience in selloffs is a tradable signal that the marginal holder base has changed; dip-buying ETF holders dampen downside cascades.
“we saw the market drop by around uh 30 or 40 odd percent. Um but at the same time um the kind of the asset under management for ETFs uh had a much kind of smaller marginal based drop” ▶ 10:18
Bitcoin dominance is rising because institutions prefer the harder, more mature large-cap asset, while reactiveness and speculation have concentrated into altcoins.
causal🗣 Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)conviction: mediumBitcoinBitcoin dominancealtcoins
So what: Persistent BTC dominance argues against a broad indiscriminate alt-season; alt exposure should be selective and fundamentals-driven.
Altcoin valuation is undergoing a 'return to fundamentals' and has become far more selective than prior narrative-driven cycles, which Nagesh frames as the key altcoin focus point for the rest of the year.
thesis🗣 Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)conviction: mediumaltcoins
So what: Favor altcoins with real fee/revenue and value accrual over narrative tokens; the dispersion between 'fundamental' and 'narrative' alts should widen.
“there's a massive kind of drive towards a return toward fundamentals in terms of how we value altcoins” ▶ 5:31
Bitcoin as a maturing macro asset class 4
Bitcoin is being reframed from a speculative/inflation-hedge narrative into a macro asset that provides enhanced risk-adjusted returns with regime-dependent correlation.
framework🗣 Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)conviction: highBitcoin
So what: Treating BTC as a regime-dependent risk-adjusted return enhancer (not a constant hedge) changes how it should be sized and timed in a portfolio.
“Bitcoin being this sort of macrobased asset that provides enhanced risk adjusted returns um and it correlation is very much regime dependent” ▶ 4:45
Asset managers are driving toward roughly a 2.5% Bitcoin inclusion in the traditional 60/40 portfolio.
data_point🗣 Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)conviction: mediumBitcoinasset managers60/40 portfolio
So what: A ~2.5% allocation across large 60/40 mandates implies a sizable structural bid; quantify the addressable AUM to gauge incremental demand.
“there's this drive for a 2.5% inclusion or so so on and we're seeing that drive really much um kind of come and take effect from various different kind of asset managers” ▶ 7:48
Bitcoin behaves as a short-term risk-off reactor during geopolitical stress but acts as a semi-diversifier versus equities over longer horizons due to its distinct supply/cycle structure.
framework🗣 Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)conviction: mediumBitcoinequities
So what: Horizon matters: short-term BTC won't hedge an equity shock, but it can still add long-run diversification — relevant for hedging vs. allocation decisions.
Bitcoin has never operated through a high-interest-rate or geopolitically volatile regime before, so its asset-class role remains unproven and its correlation profile will keep evolving with the next macro regime.
contrarian🗣 Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)conviction: mediumBitcoininterest rates
So what: Backtests of BTC as a hedge are built on a zero-rate sample; don't over-trust historical correlation stats when sizing for a new rate regime.
“We haven't kind of seen Bitcoin where we've sort of had like high interest rate environment. it was always where interest was pretty much uh near kind of like zero” ▶ 8:58
ETF expansion up the risk curve 3
After Bitcoin and Ethereum ETFs, Solana ETFs have been approved and there is a push for a BNB ETF, with ETF-ization spreading to L1s that have strong value accrual, revenue, utility and fees.
prediction🗣 Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)conviction: highSolanaBNBEthereumBitcoinETFs
So what: L1 tokens with real fee revenue are the next ETF candidates; front-running ETF approval of revenue-generating L1s could capture an institutional access premium.
“we've already seen kind of Salana based ETFs being approved. Um there's kind of a push for BNB based as well” ▶ 11:57
Institutions accumulating L1 native tokens for ETFs evaluate them on fundamentals — value accrual, network activity, fee collection (revenue), and supply-side token emissions/inflation.
framework🗣 Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)conviction: highL1 tokensinstitutional investors
So what: Screen L1 tokens like equities: fee revenue and net emissions (supply dilution) become the institutional valuation inputs to watch.
Institutional token valuation frameworks are still nascent, so expect more token-level investor relations, transparency, and standardized classifications of how tokens accrue value going forward.
prediction🗣 Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)conviction: mediumtokensinstitutional investors
So what: Protocols that build TradFi-style IR and disclosure early may win the institutional allocation race; a coming standardization wave for token valuation.
L1 consolidation & specialization 4
General-purpose L1s are consolidating into specialized roles: Ethereum as the settlement/security/liquidity layer, BNB Chain as a retail distribution funnel from CEX to DeFi, and Solana as a high-throughput trading/UX layer.
framework🗣 Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)conviction: highEthereumBNB ChainSolanaL1s
So what: Bet on chains that own a defensible niche rather than 'ETH-killer' generalists; valuation should hinge on the specialized flow each chain captures.
“Ethereum, I think that's now naturally looked at more as a settlement based layer... BNB chain... providing a very good funnel for retail... Salana... towards just like trading uh volume and then uh throughput” ▶ 13:40
Application-specific and specialized chains are emerging — Hyperliquid's own chain, Unichain, and stablecoin-focused chains like Arc and Tempo — confirming the move away from general-purpose L1s.
data_point🗣 Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)conviction: highHyperliquidUnichainArcTempostablecoin chains
So what: App-specific and stablecoin chains are a distinct emerging investable category; watch Arc/Tempo and Unichain as proxies for the specialization thesis.
“whether it's hyperl with their own chain um we have kind of uni chain on that side and then uh more recently stable coinbased change uh in terms of like arc and and tempo” ▶ 14:44
Binance/BNB Chain's strongest moat is the flow between centralized-exchange and decentralized-exchange liquidity, with Binance Alpha giving early asset and price discovery for tokens before they hit centralized exchanges.
thesis🗣 Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)conviction: highBinanceBNB ChainBinance Alpha
So what: A CEX-DEX distribution funnel is an underrated L1 moat; BNB's value accrual may track Binance's ability to route liquidity and discovery to its chain.
“the strongest moat there... is sort of the flow between a centralized exchange liquidity as well as decentralized exchange” ▶ 17:45
Fragmentation and cross-chain bridges create poor UX and security risk, which combined with liquidity gravitating to fewer venues makes consolidation the natural endpoint for the L1 landscape over 3-5 years.
prediction🗣 Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)conviction: highL1sbridgesDeFi
So what: Expect liquidity (and value) to concentrate in fewer specialized chains; bridge-dependent and long-tail generalist L1s are structurally disadvantaged.
Crypto-TradFi convergence (two-way) 7
Convergence is happening from both directions: crypto built on TradFi rails (ETFs, yield-on-Bitcoin products, crypto-backed mortgages) and TradFi built on crypto rails (stablecoins, payment rails, tokenization).
framework🗣 Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)conviction: highETFsstablecoinstokenizationBitcoin
So what: Map opportunities on both vectors; crypto-backed mortgages and yield-on-BTC products are early TradFi-rail use cases to watch.
“crypto being built on traded rails and then we're also seeing being built on crypto rails” ▶ 22:59
Tokenization is expanding into on-chain equities, commodities, and notably pre-IPO/private-market tokenized assets, giving retail-accessible exposure to previously gated private markets.
thesis🗣 Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)conviction: mediumtokenizationtokenized equitiespre-IPO assetsprivate markets
So what: Tokenized pre-IPO/private-market access is a new, fast-growing product frontier; an opening for platforms that can source and structure private deals on-chain.
“a space which very kind of new and and recent is um preipo based um tokenized assets um so providing exposure towards private market based assets” ▶ 23:46
A financial 'super app' is emerging, led by neobanks, fintechs, and crypto exchanges because they are best positioned across payments and multiple asset classes, while traditional institutions lag waiting for regulation.
prediction🗣 Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)conviction: mediumneobanksfintechscrypto exchangessuper app
So what: Neobanks/fintechs and crypto exchanges (not legacy banks) are the likely super-app winners; position toward platforms aggregating payments plus multi-asset access.
“the advent of a super app uh emerge... driven by a lot of kind of near banks, fintex as well as cryptobased exchanges” ▶ 24:05
Convergence is access- and UX-based, not valuation convergence — tokens and equities will remain valued by fundamentally different mechanisms even when held in the same app.
framework🗣 Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)conviction: hightokensequities
So what: Don't assume tokenized assets get re-rated like equities; the valuation gap (tokenomics vs. cash flows) persists and is itself a source of edge.
“this is all very much an access based convergence as well as a UX based one... when it comes towards the asset classes itself um they will remain different” ▶ 24:42
Crypto is driving convergence faster than TradFi (crypto-to-TradFi) because it operates more quickly and nimbly, evidenced by strong growth in stablecoins, tokenization, and crypto exchanges listing TradFi assets.
causal🗣 Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)conviction: highstablecoinstokenizationcrypto exchangesTradFi
So what: First-mover advantage in tokenized-TradFi-asset listings favors crypto-native venues; volume is already migrating there.
“crypto can operate a lot more let's say quicker and and more nimly um it's been crypto to trady” ▶ 25:21
Crypto exchanges listing tokenized TradFi assets generate strong volume by offering access during off-market and weekend hours, letting investors react to news and geopolitics outside traditional sessions.
data_point🗣 Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)conviction: mediumcrypto exchangestokenized stocks
So what: Weekend/off-hours tokenized-equity trading is a real volume driver; an edge for venues offering 24/7 access to TradFi assets.
Crypto and TradFi will split convergence by their existing edge: crypto leads consumer-to-consumer and merchant payments, while TradFi leads B2B and corporate-treasury use cases.
framework🗣 Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)conviction: mediumcrypto platformsTradFistablecoins
So what: Segment exposure by use case: crypto-native players for retail/merchant payments, TradFi rails for B2B/treasury — they won't simply out-execute each other.
Stablecoins as the dominant real use case 4
Stablecoins are the strongest, most front-and-center crypto use case and the primary bridge between TradFi and crypto rails, with payments (B2B and consumer-to-business) the biggest growth area.
thesis🗣 Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)conviction: highstablecoinspayments
So what: Stablecoin payment rails are the clearest 'real' adoption vector; exposure to issuers and payment infrastructure captures the highest-conviction use case.
“The biggest one which I think is is very kind of front and center is stable coins” ▶ 30:32
Stablecoin trading-pair volume still dominates usage, so investors must use adjusted-volume metrics that strip out trading to isolate genuine payment, B2B, commerce, and cross-border remittance flows.
framework🗣 Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)conviction: highstablecoinscross-border remittance
So what: Headline stablecoin volume is misleading; track adjusted/use-case-segmented volume to separate real adoption signal from trading noise.
“the the trading volume side um, that dominates stable coin usage... there's a lot more kind of metrics that are coming about in terms of adjusted um volume” ▶ 38:43
Stablecoin use cases differ sharply by market: in the US they're financial infrastructure for payment-processing efficiency, while in emerging markets (notably South America) they drive payments and 'digital dollarization' to hedge high local-currency inflation.
framework🗣 Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)conviction: highstablecoinsUSDemerging marketsSouth America
So what: Geographic segmentation matters: EM digital-dollarization demand is a distinct, inflation-driven growth driver separate from US institutional adoption.
“in some markets is also seen as sort of um digital dollarization uh... especially in South American countries seen as as a way to get exposure to the dollar” ▶ 31:57
The key second-order effect of stablecoin payment/treasury adoption is onboarding liquidity on-chain, which then naturally pulls that liquidity toward yield, DeFi, and broader crypto markets.
causal🗣 Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)conviction: highstablecoinsDeFion-chain liquidity
So what: Stablecoin growth is a leading indicator of future DeFi/crypto demand; payment liquidity is a funnel into on-chain yield and asset markets.
“it's it's essentially onboarding a lot of liquidity on chain... more propensity for a lot of this like new liquidity that's come from let's say payment based rles to eventually flow towards um cryptobased markets” ▶ 0:49
24/7 markets, perps & price discovery 3
Hyperliquid and crypto perpetual markets demonstrated faster risk-off repricing during recent geopolitical tension than traditional markets, with that move later reflected in standard asset classes.
data_point🗣 Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)conviction: highHyperliquidperpetuals24/7 markets
So what: 24/7 on-chain perps can lead TradFi repricing during off-hours shocks; they're a real-time sentiment/forward indicator for weekend gap risk.
Stock exchanges including NASDAQ (and S&P discussing 24/7 on-chain perps) are moving toward tokenizing equities and offering broader 24/7 access, adopting the always-on model crypto pioneered.
prediction🗣 Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)conviction: mediumNASDAQS&Pstock exchangestokenized stocks
So what: TradFi exchanges adopting tokenization/24-7 validates crypto infrastructure; watch NASDAQ tokenization moves as a convergence catalyst.
“We're seeing um NASDAQ and kind of um the stock exchanges themselves wanting to tokenize” ▶ 34:47
The main risk of fast 24/7 tokenized markets is immature price discovery — tokenized on-chain prices can diverge from equity-market prices until more liquidity enters.
causal🗣 Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)conviction: hightokenized stocksprice discoveryliquidity
So what: Tokenized-vs-spot price gaps create arbitrage but also execution risk; thin on-chain liquidity is the binding constraint on these markets today.
“the main risk tends to be price discovery and liquidity... there's still some disparities in terms of... the price... quoted on uh especially onchain like tokeniz based... versus uh what ends up being on... equity based markets” ▶ 35:02
AI agents on crypto rails 2
AI agents are an emerging demand layer that rely on crypto rails because traditional bank accounts' KYC/regulatory requirements make it hard for agents to transact, so stablecoins and wallet-based products become their execution infrastructure.
thesis🗣 Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)conviction: mediumAI agentsagentic economystablecoinscrypto wallets
So what: The agentic economy is a structural new demand source for stablecoins/wallet infra; an investable theme as agent adoption scales.
“the use case of stable coins and and wallet based um products come into play where agents can essentially execute” ▶ 36:48
Agent adoption follows a phased path — first a co-pilot/assist phase across commerce, trading, and business use cases, then a transaction-execution phase that drives real stablecoin/payment-rail usage.
framework🗣 Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)conviction: mediumAI agentsstablecoins
So what: Stablecoin demand from agents is back-loaded to the execution phase; track the co-pilot-to-execution transition as the real adoption trigger.
On-chain debt, credit & money markets 2
Tokenizing debt and private credit mirrors tokenizing equities — needing issuer frameworks, off-chain-to-on-chain data rails, and security — but requires rebuilding institutions' legacy operating processes and integrations on-chain, a slow buildout.
framework🗣 Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)conviction: mediumprivate credittokenizationdebtmoney markets
So what: On-chain private credit is early-stage infrastructure; the bottleneck is replicating legacy institutional processes, favoring infra builders over speculation.
“thousands and thousands of legacy based integrations. So all of that will essentially need to be catered for” ▶ 41:24
On-chain borrowing/money markets are less mature than stablecoin holding, and as rates rose the spread between DeFi yield and the risk-free Treasury rate compressed, with strong demand for yield but weak demand for on-chain borrowing.
data_point🗣 Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)conviction: highDeFi yieldTreasury yieldmoney marketsstablecoins
So what: Compressed DeFi-vs-Treasury spread plus weak borrow demand signals thin on-chain credit demand; lending-protocol revenue may stay pressured until rates fall or use cases mature.
“the risk-f free rate which is treasury based yield um and these days given interest rates have kind of essentially gone up um that that spread has has decreased over time” ▶ 50:34
Regulation as the primary catalyst 3
Regulatory trends are the single biggest crypto catalyst this year, with the Clarity Act the current focus — debated mainly around whether stablecoin issuers and crypto entities can pass yield through to retail users.
thesis🗣 Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)conviction: highClarity Actregulationstablecoinsyield
So what: Stablecoin yield pass-through is the regulatory swing factor; a favorable Clarity Act outcome could unlock a major new retail yield demand wave.
“the current one in focus is is... the clarity act. Um that's something which uh currently is in kind of undergoing debate mainly from a yield perspective” ▶ 42:25
The GENIUS Act's passage last year was the first major catalyst that drove stablecoin adoption and volume, and Nagesh expects tokenization and other use cases to follow the same regulation-then-adoption pattern.
data_point🗣 Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)conviction: highGENIUS Actstablecoinstokenizationregulation
So what: Use regulatory passage as a forward catalyst playbook: GENIUS → stablecoins suggests the next act could similarly inflect tokenization adoption.
“once the genius act was essentially uh approved... that's essentially the first major catalyst that drove the stable coin uh based kind of adoption and volume” ▶ 43:53
Regulatory passage repricing is immediate in markets (and in crypto-rail public equities) but real product adoption lags as a structured, use-case-dependent buildout — so the 'rails free-flowing next day' expectation is wrong.
framework🗣 Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)conviction: highregulationClarity Actcrypto equities
So what: Trade the price reaction to regulatory news quickly, but the adoption ramp is a 1-2 year structured process — separate the pricing trade from the fundamentals trade.
“it wouldn't be kind of immediate per se, but I I think we'll see it as a natural kind of progression... what is probably more immediate in terms of reaction is is what will get reflected in terms of... how crypto markets are valued” ▶ 45:48
Emerging risks to price in (quantum & DeFi exploits) 3
Quantum risk has accelerated as an industry concern this year, partly due to a Google research paper, and a 'quantum-resistance progress' risk premium will start being priced into Bitcoin and general-purpose L1s.
prediction🗣 Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)conviction: mediumquantum riskBitcoinL1sGoogle
So what: Chains' progress toward quantum resistance may become a valuation differentiator; lagging chains could carry a discount as the premium gets priced.
“this risk will start being priced in uh towards how far um a particular uh whether it's Bitcoin or um a layer one... they are in the progression of becoming quantum resistant” ▶ 47:52
April saw one of the largest months of DeFi exploits since early last year by incident count, triggering on-chain liquidity outflows, falling total value locked, and price hits to DeFi assets.
data_point🗣 Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)conviction: highDeFiexploitstotal value locked
So what: Exploit-driven TVL outflows directly hit DeFi token prices; security-incident frequency is a tradable risk signal for the DeFi sector.
“we've seen kind of April um have one of the largest kind of like uh months in terms of um exploits um since um we say early last year” ▶ 48:53
The industry is debating whether DeFi yield correctly prices exploit risk, with proposed mitigations including market-based recovery funds, higher risk-adjusted yields, and on-chain insurance.
thesis🗣 Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)conviction: mediumDeFi yieldinsurancerecovery funds
So what: On-chain insurance and recovery-fund models are an emerging investable response to DeFi risk; current DeFi yields may be underpricing tail risk.
“whether uh you know defy yield itself um it's not pricing this risk uh correctly and whether yield needs to maybe more attractive” ▶ 49:49
Token valuation gap & what TradFi misunderstands 1
Nagesh's key message to TradFi is that tokens are valued on fundamentally different demand- and supply-side dynamics (tokenomics, emissions, protocol role) than equities' cash-flow/earnings-call metrics, and investors should keep tokenomics front and center.
framework🗣 Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)conviction: hightokenstokenomicsequities
So what: Applying equity cash-flow valuation to tokens is a category error; supply-side emissions and protocol token-role analysis are essential for valuing crypto assets.
“keep tokconomics front and center... understanding the role of a token uh in a particular protocol... value cruel is essentially done... both from a demand side as well as a supply side” ▶ 53:07

Forward Guidance · Blockworks 2 episode(s) · 76 insights

How To Trade The AI Productivity Boom | Weekly Roundup50 min39 insights2026-05-29
▶ Watch on YouTube
Three macro/crypto traders argue the AI productivity boom is real but policy-managed: the Fed won't hike despite Taylor-rule signals, negative real rates are 'here for good,' and the market is a centralized, vol-suppressed melt-up with extreme call-skew that they'd rather watch than chase. They are structurally bearish crypto (broken token value-capture), wary of an Iran oil-supply stagflation shock, and positioned for a sector rotation out of semis rather than an outright crash.
Fed policy & negative real rates regime · 5Vol mechanics, positioning & how to trade the melt-up · 7AI productivity boom: real but socially destabilizing · 4Crypto bear case & broken token value-capture · 7Iran oil shock, consumer health & stagflation risk · 5K-shaped economy, passive flows & market structure · 5Thematic trading frameworks & process discipline · 6
Fed policy & negative real rates regime 5
Taylor-rule and DB R-star models prescribe rate HIKES given Fed funds is below policy-rule levels, but the panel does not expect the Fed to hike at all because this is 'wartime policy' managing a massive deficit and ~$1 trillion of interest expense.
thesis🗣 host (Tyler)conviction: highFederal ReserveTaylor RuleDeutsche BankR-starFed funds rate
So what: Curve is starting to price a first hike by end-2026; fading that and positioning for continued financial repression / no-hike is the base case.
“by the end of 2026, we might even get the first hike” ▶ 1:57
The real policy stance is far more stimulative than rate cuts alone: suppressing oil prices, suppressing yields, yen/dollar currency management and balance-sheet/RMP support of the long end together raise the odds an energy price shock seeps from headline into core inflation.
causal🗣 guest, former gold-fund PMconviction: highoilyenUS dollarlong-end Treasuriescore inflationPowell
So what: Watch core inflation pass-through; the trade is removal of stimulus, not hikes — but until that happens stimulus keeps risk assets bid.
“all of the other stimulative measures they're doing like suppressing oil prices, uh, suppressing yields, currency manipulation with the yen and dollar, all of that stuff is stimulative” ▶ 2:35
Inflation has run above the Fed's 2% target for 60+ months, yet Powell has been 'unbelievably dovish' and balance-sheet support of the long end has been more stimulative than cuts would have been.
data_point🗣 guest, former gold-fund PMconviction: highFederal ReservePowellinflation
So what: Persistent above-target inflation plus dovish bias supports owning real/productive assets over cash and bonds.
“inflation's been above the Fed's target for 60 plus months” ▶ 3:22
The long-term policy endgame is to fiscally grow out of the debt with negative real rates 'here for good,' implying the market is correctly front-running a deliberate inflate-away regime.
thesis🗣 host (Tyler)conviction: highreal ratesfiscal deficitUS debt
So what: Negative real rates argue for staying long hard/productive assets and short duration / cash as a structural posture.
“we're going to fiscally grow our way out of this and negative real rates are going to be here for good” ▶ 5:06
Mention of Kevin Warsh as 'slightly more hawkish' is offset by Trump-appointee pressure, so even a leadership change is unlikely to deliver materially tighter policy.
thesis🗣 guest, former gold-fund PMconviction: mediumKevin WarshTrumpFederal ReserveScott Bessent
So what: Don't position for a hawkish Fed regime change; personnel signaling on footprint-reduction has not translated into action.
“I get that Worsh is maybe slightly more hawkish, but then you have the offsetting factor of the Trump appointee and pressure” ▶ 3:29
Vol mechanics, positioning & how to trade the melt-up 7
VIX is sub-16 after a spike near 40, and the panel refuses to add risk into euphoric grind-up tape with extreme positioning, preferring to wait for better prices.
thesis🗣 host (Tyler)conviction: highVIXS&P 500
So what: Tactical traders should treat sub-16 VIX + euphoria as a reason to harvest, not chase; mean-reversion risk is rising.
“I hate taking risk when things grind up every day and the VIX is, you know, sub 16 after being at what was it like 40” ▶ 5:26
Big semiconductor single-stock call/put skew sits in the 98th percentile — investors are aggressively bidding calls with essentially no downside protection bought, the mirror image of the fear seen during the Iran shock.
data_point🗣 host (Tyler)conviction: highsemiconductorsoptions skewIran
So what: 98th-percentile call skew with no hedges flags an asymmetric short-term downside; cheap puts make defined-risk hedges attractive.
“It's in like the 98th percentile meaning like people are really bidding up calls to to puts. There's no downside protection being bought.” ▶ 16:30
Low VIX is structurally manufactured: retail/momentum players go 'balls long' single-stock calls (raising single-stock vol) while quant funds short index vol as a hedge, and falling implied correlation lets systematic funds keep buying what's working.
framework🗣 host (Tyler)conviction: highVIXimplied correlationdispersion tradequant fundsretail
So what: The dispersion/low-correlation regime is self-reinforcing but can violently unwind like the 2024 carry trade — a key risk-trigger to monitor.
“the quant funds are shorting you know index volatility. So that's why we see you know the VIX is so low is that's that's their hedge.” ▶ 15:44
S&P one-month 25-delta put/call skew is in the 4th percentile (puts extraordinarily cheap vs calls), per a Clear Street contact.
data_point🗣 host (Tyler)conviction: highS&P 50025-delta skewClear Street
So what: 4th-percentile put pricing makes index downside protection unusually cheap — a low-cost hedge or tail trade.
“it's in 25 delta implied V spread for puts relative to calls in the fourth percentile, which is like super super cheap on on the put side” ▶ 27:03
Retail using Robinhood to buy YOLO call options is now a primary driver of gamma squeezes because pensions/endowments/boomer holders have 'choked the float' of the equity market.
causal🗣 host (Tyler)conviction: mediumRobinhoodretailpension fundsendowmentsgamma squeeze
So what: Thin float plus retail call-buying means dealer gamma dynamics, not fundamentals, drive marginal price action — trade the flows.
“they're figuring out how to use Robin Hood and and basically buy call options to artificially create gamma squeezes” ▶ 25:54
The panel is not outright bearish but expects a 'massive sectoral rotation' / baton-pass out of semis rather than an index crash, because credit spreads are too tight for a 'volmageddon.'
prediction🗣 host (Tyler)conviction: mediumsemiconductorscredit spreadssoftwareSnowflake
So what: Position for intra-market rotation (rotate out of semis, watch squeezes elsewhere) over the next few weeks rather than net-short the index.
“there will be a baton passing at some point over the next few weeks I think from semis and that doesn't even need to be market down” ▶ 48:38
Snowflake-driven short squeeze in software is cited as a template for how deleveraging in one frothy sector can spark squeezes elsewhere.
anecdote🗣 guest, former gold-fund PMconviction: mediumSnowflakesoftware sector
So what: Single-name squeezes can cascade across crowded sectors — watch for follow-on squeezes when one frothy name deleverages.
“we saw a lot of the software stuff like snowflake caused a big squeeze in in software” ▶ 49:10
AI productivity boom: real but socially destabilizing 4
The AI productivity boom is judged to be real and 'working,' creating monstrous earnings winners, but it is breaking the social contract during a brutal transition for displaced workers.
thesis🗣 guest, former gold-fund PMconviction: highAIMarc Andreessenproductivity
So what: Stay long the AI/productivity earnings winners while pricing rising political-backlash risk as a second-order tail.
“we're seeing a massive productivity boom... the one piece where I think it's failing is it's not it's breaking the social contract” ▶ 6:04
If the dislocation isn't addressed politically, the likely outcome is AOC/Mamdani-style left-populist redistributive policy rather than hands-off conservative governance — a structural risk to asset-holders.
prediction🗣 guest, former gold-fund PMconviction: mediumAOCMamdaniredistributionpolitics
So what: Long-horizon investors should price in higher future taxes/redistribution and political risk to concentrated capital.
“you're probably more likely with the AOC and Mumanis than you are with you know handsoff LZ fair uh conservative government” ▶ 6:42
A speaker openly entertains that AI could be so productive (hypothetical ~15% national GDP growth) that it ushers in an entirely new economy in which a store-of-value asset like Bitcoin loses its purpose.
contrarian🗣 host (Tyler)conviction: exploratoryAIGDPBitcoinstore of value
So what: A genuinely transformative AI-growth scenario is bearish the macro case for non-yielding store-of-value assets — a contrarian risk to the Bitcoin thesis.
“what is the role of crypto if you're you have national GDP P growth of 15%. Do you need a store of value?” ▶ 10:01
There is 'a clear bid for productive assets,' which is exactly why non-productive/no-cashflow tokens are being left behind.
framework🗣 guest (Felix/Quinn)conviction: highproductive assetscrypto tokensequities
So what: Favor cash-flowing productive assets over speculative non-yielding ones in this regime; it explains crypto underperformance.
“there's there's a clear bid for productive assets right now” ▶ 10:38
Crypto bear case & broken token value-capture 7
The only parts of crypto leaning into the 'productive asset' theme — stablecoin adoption and payment rails — have no investable token, so usage rises while token prices capture no value (e.g., Ethereum network usage up, token price decoupled).
thesis🗣 guest (Felix/Quinn)conviction: highstablecoinsEthereumpayment rails
So what: Adoption is not a buy signal for the underlying token; the edge is in the rails/usage, not ETH or most L1 tokens.
“you see more and more people using Ethereum for example the network but it really does not correlate to the to the price of their token” ▶ 11:06
Bankless co-founder David Hoffman, an OG ETH believer, sold his Ethereum this week, calling the network fine but the asset itself 'uninvestable' — a capitulation signal among long-time holders.
anecdote🗣 guest (Felix/Quinn)conviction: highDavid HoffmanBanklessEthereum
So what: OG holders capitulating on ETH is either a deepening bear signal or a sentiment-washout marker to watch for a bottom.
“David Hoffman from Bank list recently sold his Ethereum this week and he's like I'm bullish on the network... the the the asset itself is uninvestable” ▶ 11:19
Crypto Treasury companies ('DATs') bought roughly $15 billion of Bitcoin and ETH this year, yet both assets are down massively YTD — characterized as financial engineering propping up artificial demand and scamming retail.
data_point🗣 guest, former gold-fund PMconviction: highBitcoinEthereumDATscrypto treasury companies
So what: Massive corporate buying failing to lift price signals weak underlying liquidity — a structural red flag for BTC/ETH near-term.
“these guys have bought 15 trillion 15 billion dollars of Bitcoin and ETH this year, and both assets are down massively year to date” ▶ 11:50
Because equities are propped by vol-suppression, passive flows and the systematic bid, crypto lacks the same support mechanism and is therefore in a bear market with poor under-the-surface liquidity.
causal🗣 guest, former gold-fund PMconviction: highBitcoincryptopassive flowsequities
So what: Crypto is the wrong vehicle for this macro regime; the systematic/passive bid that supports stocks doesn't exist for it.
“it makes sense that crypto is lagging... it's just not the right market environment and macro conditions for it to do well” ▶ 12:19
Analogy: AI infrastructure is draining capital from crypto the way productive Silicon Valley tech sucked capital out of overlevered, oversupplied gold miners after gold's 2011-12 blowoff top.
framework🗣 guest, former gold-fund PMconviction: mediumgold minersAI infrastructurecryptoSilicon Valley tech
So what: Capital rotates to the fastest-growing productive theme; crypto may keep bleeding allocation to AI infra until growth rates converge.
“I'm almost wondering if we're watching the same thing happening in, you know, AI infrastructure to crypto” ▶ 13:20
The administration used crypto as election leverage but has delivered little beyond drone-financing talk, ultimately backing the centralized banking industry over decentralization.
thesis🗣 guest, former gold-fund PMconviction: mediumcryptobanking industryTrump administration
So what: Don't underwrite a friendly-regulation crypto tailwind from this administration; policy favors incumbent banks.
“it's clear the administration used crypto as a a leverage to, you know, for the for the election. And he hasn't they haven't really done anything” ▶ 13:43
99% of the token landscape is dismissed as 'nothing useful,' with even the majors corrupted by Ponzi-like Treasury-company market structure.
contrarian🗣 guest, former gold-fund PMconviction: highaltcoinsBitcoinEthereumDATs
So what: Broad altcoin exposure is viewed as worthless; concentration risk in even BTC/ETH from DAT structure is a hazard.
“there's nothing useful uh in 99% of the token landscape” ▶ 11:38
Iran oil shock, consumer health & stagflation risk 5
US crude inventories have fallen below the 5-year range and the SPR is being drawn toward zero to suppress oil prices, removing the buffer that made the Iran/Strait-of-Hormuz shock manageable so far.
data_point🗣 host (Tyler)conviction: highSPRoil inventoriesIranStrait of Hormuz
So what: With buffers exhausted, oil-price suppression becomes harder — rising stagflation/oil-spike risk over coming months.
“we're like like we're below the 5year range in inventories. I think I think now it gets a bit more real” ▶ 18:42
Personal incomes are trending negative for the first time since 2022 and consumers are tapping savings to absorb the energy shock; historically negative real income episodes coincided with growth shocks or recessions like 2008.
data_point🗣 host (Tyler)conviction: highpersonal incomesavings ratePCEconsumer
So what: Negative income momentum plus energy shock skews toward a stagflationary growth scare — bearish the consumer and cyclicals.
“incomes are actually negative for like the first time since 2022... it's it's during times of either growth shocks or outright recessions like '08” ▶ 20:25
Consumer buffers (inventories, SPR, savings, and the big-beautiful-bill tax refunds) are all running out over the next couple months, so absent an Iran resolution things get 'pretty hairy' in a stagflationary way.
prediction🗣 host (Tyler)conviction: hightax refundsconsumerIranstagflation
So what: Sets a near-term clock: monitor the next 1-2 months and an Iran deal as the key catalyst for a stagflation outcome.
“all that stuff is running out over the next couple months. And so it's like we need that resolution or... things are going to get pretty hairy” ▶ 21:14
Trump is expected to cut an Iran deal — likely 'worse than Obama's' — because he can't risk another months-long conflict with elevated gas prices four months before the midterms; today's headline was a classic trial balloon.
prediction🗣 guest, former gold-fund PMconviction: highTrumpIranmidtermsoil
So what: A de-escalation deal would relieve oil and risk; fade headline-driven oil spikes as politically-pressured resolution looms.
“I think Trump's going to cut a deal that will probably end up being worse than Obama's... we're four months away from the midterms” ▶ 21:28
Energy supply shocks are a 'nightmare' for central banks because they push headline inflation up at the same time real incomes turn negative — the exact bind constraining the Fed now.
framework🗣 host (Tyler)conviction: highFederal Reserveenergyinflationincomes
So what: This bind keeps the Fed paralyzed (neither hiking nor easing decisively), reinforcing the no-hike, repression base case.
≈ “energy supply shocks are such a nightmare for central banks is that you have the supply shock in terms of headline inflation going higher... incomes are actually negative” paraphrase ▶ 20:13
K-shaped economy, passive flows & market structure 5
The K-shaped divergence is framed as a deliberate policy choice: propping equities and the long end via manipulation comes at the direct expense of Main Street, the opposite of letting market pain slow inflation and enable rate cuts that help lower-income households.
causal🗣 guest, former gold-fund PMconviction: highK-shaped economyMain StreetScott Bessentequities
So what: Policy that favors asset-holders entrenches asset inflation but builds political backlash — long assets short-term, hedge the regime long-term.
“It is at the direct like literal direct expense of main street small business lower and middle inome class people” ▶ 22:18
Mike Green's passive-investing thesis: the shift from defined-benefit pensions (managed with duration/liability hedging that kept markets rational) to defined-contribution plans turned every worker into a price-insensitive passive ETF buyer, making multi-trillion-dollar flows that no longer care about valuation.
framework🗣 guest (Felix/Quinn)conviction: highMike Greenpassive investingdefined benefitdefined contributionETFs
So what: Price-insensitive passive flows are the structural bid under equities — explains valuation indifference and the melt-up's durability.
“we basically manded every mandated every employee to become their own stock picker... They just buy passive ETFs every every paycheck” ▶ 32:36
Inherited/wealthy capital compounds far better because it can withstand drawdowns, while lower-income investors can't take risk and fall further behind — a self-reinforcing erosion of the social contract.
framework🗣 guest (Felix/Quinn) citing Mike Greenconviction: mediumMike Greenwealth inequalitydrawdowns
So what: Structural inequality dynamic underpins both the redistribution/political risk and the retail-leverage behavior driving markets.
“your ability to compound that wealth is like exponentially better because you can deal with draw downs” ▶ 33:33
Priced out of the centralized, asset-inflated system, younger investors are forced into high-risk behavior — 40x leverage on Hyperliquid and YOLO call options — as the only way to keep up.
thesis🗣 host (Tyler)conviction: mediumHyperliquidretail leveragecall options
So what: Explains the structural growth of leveraged retail flows and gamma dynamics; a fragility that can unwind violently.
“I'm going to hyperlquid and yoloing 40x leverage or I'm going into call options. It's the only way you can stay up” ▶ 27:49
Peter Thiel relocating to Argentina (on top of New Zealand bunkers) is read as an early, high-conviction signal of a US secular decline/socialism shift worth respecting rather than fading, given Thiel's track record.
anecdote🗣 host (Tyler)conviction: mediumPeter ThielArgentinaUnited States
So what: A prescient insider exiting US risk is a soft signal to watch for a deeper secular shift in US asset/political risk.
“I do recognize him as a very powerful signal early to these secular shifts... that's more of like a signal of of the beginning” ▶ 35:22
Thematic trading frameworks & process discipline 6
Mark Hart's thematic-valuation formula scores assets on access, awareness, patina (gravitas), TAM/right-tail growth, and use-as-collateral; new 'concentric circles of adoption' (ETF launch, custody solutions, ability to borrow against it) open fresh investor pools and signal entry/exit.
framework🗣 guest, former gold-fund PM (ex-Mark Hart)conviction: highMark HartBitcoinETFscustodycollateral
So what: A reusable lens: buy when an access/custody/collateral change widens the buyer base; sell when regulation restricts access.
“he calls them concentric circles of adoption when you have access open up like take Bitcoin... like an an ETF, right? That's an access change” ▶ 44:56
Regulatory or access restrictions (e.g., a Clarity Act outcome that limits access) are red flags most fundamental investors ignore because they only watch earnings — and they signal it's time to get out.
framework🗣 guest, former gold-fund PMconviction: highClarity Actregulationfundamental investors
So what: Track access/custody/collateral changes as leading exit signals, separate from earnings analysis.
“those things are like red flags that most fundamental investors don't think about. They're just like, 'Oh, did the earnings go up?'” ▶ 46:33
Best risk-adjusted entries into high-beta thematic trades come from buying pullbacks into a moving average within a multi-year bull market — before the theme reaches the front page of the WSJ — which removes stress and lets you play with house money.
framework🗣 guest (Felix/Quinn)conviction: hightechnical analysismoving averagethematic investing
So what: Entry quality dominates in volatile high-beta themes; chasing breakouts in fear yields poor entries and whipsaws.
“buying this thing that's pulled back into a moving average in a multi-year bull market that's, you know, not yet the front page of the Wall Street Journal” ▶ 47:01
Cash is a position and the discipline to sit out low-conviction, heavy-policy-influence environments — pressing hard only when edge is clear — beats forcing trades, since most P&L is made in short stretches.
framework🗣 guest (Felix/Quinn)conviction: highcashrisk managementtrading discipline
So what: Process edge: hold cash and wait for clear setups rather than overtrading a policy-distorted tape.
“cash is a position and if things don't make sense to you... I'm not going to force it” ▶ 36:32
The panel separates trades by horizon — 1-3 month macro swings (e.g., a bearish Mag 7 trade, bullish metals) vs. 2-year secular holds (solar, semis) — using different trackers/accounts so short-term tactics don't sabotage long-term theses.
framework🗣 guest, former gold-fund PMconviction: highMagnificent 7metalssolarsemiconductors
So what: Book-segmentation by horizon prevents over-trading conviction themes — a practical risk-management structure.
“the majority of my my trades and views and positions are one to three month swings... that's very different from like, hey, I'm super bullish this trend for two years” ▶ 42:17
Named missed/winning thematic trades cited: Rocket Lab (called weekly for two years), Bitcoin-miners pivoting to AI data centers (recorded an hour on it in late 2024), Nebius (Leo's new biggest position), AI memory stocks, and SpaceX with an IPO ~4 months out as a hype catalyst.
data_point🗣 host (Tyler)conviction: mediumRocket LabNebiusSpaceXBitcoin minersAI data centersAI memory
So what: Concrete AI/space themes flagged as the productive-asset winners; SpaceX pre-IPO hype and Nebius/AI-memory are watchlist names.
“today Leo pulled his new biggest position was Nebius... you have the SpaceX IPO four months out... there's going to be hype going into it” ▶ 40:01
Why This Economy Refuses To Break | David Cervantes47 min37 insights2026-06-03
▶ Watch on YouTube
David Cervantes (Pine Brook Capital) argues the US economy can't fall into recession while ~$1T of AI capex and World War II-style 6-7% fiscal deficits pump money through it; he flips from 'none-and-done' on cuts to expecting rate hikes to be priced in as inflation broadens beyond oil, while staying constructive on equities and bullish on Korea.
AI capex + fiscal deficits as the dominant macro driver · 5Profit margins, productivity boom, and manufacturing restocking · 6Consumer resilience and the stealth wealth transfer · 6No recession: deficits, labor market, and immigration mechanics · 3Inflation broadening and the pivot to rate hikes · 6Fed credibility, Warsh, and the goalpost-moving risk · 3Rates, term premium, bonds and equities coexisting · 4Energy/oil risk and the Korea trade · 4
AI capex + fiscal deficits as the dominant macro driver 5
The biggest macro driver is the AI buildout at roughly $1 trillion of capex that keeps rising, and combined with large government deficits it creates a 'pile of money gushing through the economy' that makes recession very hard.
thesis🗣 David Cervantes, Pine Brook Capitalconviction: highAI capexhyperscalersUS fiscal deficits
So what: As long as AI capex + deficits keep flowing, fade recession calls and stay long risk; watch capex guidance and deficit trajectory as the key macro tell.
“between the government deficits and the AI build. I mean, there's just a pile of money gushing through the economy” ▶ 0:10
The AI buildout has moved from being funded by hyperscaler free cash flow to tapping debt markets (Dec-Feb) and now to equity issuance, signaling the first phase of self-funding is exhausted.
framework🗣 host (Felix)conviction: highhyperscalersGoogledebt marketsequity markets
So what: The funding-source progression (cash flow -> debt -> equity) is a maturity gauge for the AI capex cycle; equity issuance marks a more capital-intensive, dilution-sensitive phase.
Google issuing an ~$80 billion equity raise to fund its AI buildout is read as a sign equity valuations are rich enough that dilution is an attractive funding source.
data_point🗣 host (Felix)conviction: mediumGoogleAI capexequity dilution
So what: Mega-cap equity raises for capex signal both rich valuations and escalating funding needs; watch whether other hyperscalers follow into dilution.
Unlike prior exuberance episodes driven by P/E expansion, this cycle is driven by earnings-expectations expansion, but there is a risk of a 'bubble in earnings expectations' that may not pan out in time to fund the next leg of AI capex given ~5-year obsolescence cycles.
contrarian🗣 David Cervantes, Pine Brook Capitalconviction: mediumAI capexhyperscalersearnings expectationsobsolescence cycle
So what: The key risk isn't valuation per se but whether AI earnings arrive fast enough to fund a ~5-year replacement cycle before assets go obsolete; a multi-year tail risk to monitor.
“will they pan out in time to fund the next step of the uh AI buildout? Meaning, you there's obsolescence that's kind of factor factored into these plans” ▶ 4:53
Public deficits are private-sector surpluses, so massive government spending mechanically flows into private balance sheets regardless of distributional concerns, which are 'irrelevant' from a macro-aggregate standpoint.
framework🗣 David Cervantes, Pine Brook Capitalconviction: highUS fiscal deficitsprivate sectorsectoral balances
So what: Use the sectoral-balances lens to expect deficit spending to support corporate profits and consumption; distributional debates are political noise for asset allocation.
“public deficits are private sector uh surpluses. So, one way or another, that money is finding its way through the economy” ▶ 0:22
Profit margins, productivity boom, and manufacturing restocking 6
Profit-margin expansion, not headline earnings, is the key signal to watch; margins reflect the underlying economics of what firms are doing with AI, so as long as margins expand or hold, the equity trade stays on.
framework🗣 David Cervantes, Pine Brook Capitalconviction: highcorporate profit marginsS&P 500 earningsAI capex
So what: Track profit-margin trajectory over top-line/bottom-line growth as the cleaner forward signal for whether to stay long equities.
“the bigger thing I'm focused on versus earnings is profit margin expansion”
The US is in its first procyclical productivity boom since the 1990s, a 'perfect storm' of positive factors now manifesting in expanding profit margins.
thesis🗣 David Cervantes, Pine Brook Capitalconviction: highUS productivityprofit margins1990s analog
So what: A 1990s-style productivity boom can sustain elevated margins and equity multiples for years; frames the bull case as structural, not just cyclical.
“This is this is the first um procyclical productivity boom that we've had since the 90s” ▶ 7:20
The US manufacturing renaissance is driven by a post-COVID inventory restocking cycle (an echo of prior bullwhip supply shocks), not by the AI buildout, since AI inputs are supply-constrained.
contrarian🗣 David Cervantes, Pine Brook Capitalconviction: highmanufacturing PMIISMinventory restockingAI capextariffs
So what: Don't attribute strong manufacturing PMI (54) to AI; it's a restocking impulse that may fade, so size manufacturing-cyclical exposure accordingly.
“I don't think the driver is the AI buildout... what's been driving the the the industrial manufacturing renaissance... is really a restocking cycle” ▶ 8:38
Manufacturing PMI printed 54 with several months of meaningful expansion, indicating an accelerating industrial impulse.
data_point🗣 host (Felix)conviction: highmanufacturing PMIISM
So what: A 54 PMI confirms expansion; pairs with the restocking thesis as a watch item for whether the industrial impulse persists or rolls over.
Supply chains are structurally shifting from 'optimization' (just-in-time) to 'resiliency' (buffer stocks), a permanent feature-not-a-bug that raises working-capital and inventory requirements.
framework🗣 David Cervantes, Pine Brook Capitalconviction: highsupply chainsjust-in-time inventoryworking capital
So what: Structurally higher inventory/working-capital needs support sustained goods demand but can pressure margins over time; a durable shift favoring inventory-heavy and resilience plays.
“now there's a shift from from optimization to resiliency... I I think it's kind of a for now a permanent thing” ▶ 11:29
The resiliency shift has a long-term cost: working capital tied up in buffer stocks and a non-optimized capital structure will eventually eat into profitability, though not yet at a scale that runs 'super redundant' operations.
causal🗣 David Cervantes, Pine Brook Capitalconviction: mediumworking capitalprofit marginssupply chains
So what: A latent headwind to the margin-expansion thesis; watch for the point where resiliency spending starts visibly compressing margins.
Consumer resilience and the stealth wealth transfer 6
US consumption is propped up by a 'three-legged stool': (1) boomers stealth-funding adult children's lifestyles, (2) the wealth effect from a 300%+ S&P rally since 2009, and (3) 40-60% of the population having no mortgage.
framework🗣 David Cervantes, Pine Brook Capitalconviction: highUS consumerboomer wealth transferwealth effectS&P 500mortgages
So what: Official income/savings data understate consumer capacity; fade bearish-consumer calls built only on wage and savings-rate weakness.
“this three-legged bar stool of of of that's proconumption. whether it's the boomer parents helping out, it's the wealth effect, and a lot of people just don't have a mortgage anymore” ▶ 17:56
A hard-to-document 'stealth boomer wealth transfer' funds upper-income consumption (vacations, childcare) and stays off official data partly because gift-tax limits (~$13k) discourage reporting.
thesis🗣 David Cervantes, Pine Brook Capitalconviction: mediumboomer wealth transferUS consumergift tax
So what: Explains the gap between weak measured income and strong spending; this off-the-books support is a reason the upper-K consumer is more durable than data implies.
“this kind of stealth um and very hard to document uh wealth transfer”
The wealth effect dominates the income flow: as net worth rises via equities and 401ks, the upper-50% save less and spend more, making the low savings rate misread rather than a fragility signal.
causal🗣 David Cervantes, Pine Brook Capitalconviction: highwealth effectsavings rate401kUS consumer
So what: A sub-3% savings rate may be a wealth-effect feature, not a warning; ties consumer strength directly to equity-market levels, creating reflexive support that can reverse on a drawdown.
Recent PCE data showed the savings rate falling below 3% (near a multi-decade low) with lackluster income growth and consumption running above incomes.
data_point🗣 host (Felix)conviction: highPCEsavings rateconsumer spending
So what: Sets up the bear case (unsustainable spending) that Cervantes counters with the wealth/transfer thesis; the savings-rate floor is a key fragility metric to monitor.
Credit card delinquency rates are accelerating meaningfully, a counter-signal to the resilient-consumer narrative.
data_point🗣 host (Felix)conviction: mediumcredit card delinquenciesUS consumercredit
So what: The lower-income consumer crack to watch; rising delinquencies are the tail risk to the upper-K-driven consumption thesis.
High-end experiential consumption (e.g., $300-400+ Sphere shows in Las Vegas) illustrates a cohort with ample spending capacity, evidence that discretionary demand is concentrated in the wealthy but robust.
anecdote🗣 David Cervantes, Pine Brook Capitalconviction: mediumSphere (Las Vegas)experiential consumptionhigh-end consumer
So what: Premium/experiential providers with pricing power and scarcity are beneficiaries of concentrated high-end spend; a sector tilt within consumer discretionary.
No recession: deficits, labor market, and immigration mechanics 3
Today's 6-7% deficits are 'World War II-style' (vs. ~3-4% during the Reagan buildout), making it very hard to push the economy into recession as that money is force-fed through it.
thesis🗣 David Cervantes, Pine Brook Capitalconviction: highUS fiscal deficitsWWII analogReagan era
So what: Frames deficits as a structural anti-recession backstop; supports staying invested and treating growth scares as buying opportunities.
“These are World War II style deficits... now we're looking at six 7%. And we haven't had that since World War II” ▶ 23:14
The economy has weathered successive shocks (2022 rate shock, tariff war, the kinetic war and oil-price shock) relatively well, and the current oil shock 'doesn't seem to be even that much of a shock anymore.'
thesis🗣 David Cervantes, Pine Brook Capitalconviction: highrate shock 2022tariffsoil price shockgeopolitical risk
So what: Track record of absorbing shocks argues against pricing large shock-driven downside; shocks remain the main residual recession risk.
The President's immigration policies shrink the labor force, mechanically pushing the unemployment rate down, so the NBER-relevant labor-market decline needed to date a recession simply won't materialize; unemployment could fall to 4% by year-end.
causal🗣 David Cervantes, Pine Brook Capitalconviction: highimmigration policyunemployment rateNBERlabor force
So what: A falling unemployment rate is structurally incompatible with a recession call; immigration-driven labor supply contraction is the key mechanism keeping U-rate low.
“by h shrinking the labor market, you kind of mechanically put downward pressure on the unemployment rate” ▶ 24:54
Inflation broadening and the pivot to rate hikes 6
Cervantes called 'none-and-done' on March 1st - no rate cuts by year-end - because the inflationary impulse was broadening even before the oil shock, with January running 0.42% month-over-month two months before the invasion.
prediction🗣 David Cervantes, Pine Brook Capitalconviction: highFederal ReserveinflationCPI/PCErate cuts
So what: An early, correct out-of-consensus call; position for higher-for-longer or hikes rather than the cuts that were priced in January.
“back March 1st I called none you know none and done... we were not going to have any uh rate cuts um you know by year end” ▶ 27:04
The disinflation of 2024-25 was largely housing/rent-driven, but the 'low-hanging fruit' of falling rents has been harvested, so future rent disinflation will be much slower - removing a key disinflationary support.
causal🗣 David Cervantes, Pine Brook Capitalconviction: highshelter/rent CPIdisinflationinflation
So what: With shelter disinflation exhausted, headline disinflation stalls; supports the hawkish/no-cuts thesis and higher rate path.
“we've kind of harvested the lowhanging fruit of a decline in rents” ▶ 29:12
With inflation broadening and unemployment falling, wage pressure becomes 'another nail in the disinflationary coffin' as a tight labor market lets workers negotiate higher pay, risking running above the non-accelerating-inflation wage rate.
causal🗣 David Cervantes, Pine Brook Capitalconviction: highwageslabor marketNAIRUinflation
So what: Wage-driven inflation is the next leg; reinforces a hawkish rates view and argues against fading hike pricing.
“another another nail in the disinflationary coffin is in fact going to become is will be coming from... upward pressure on on the wage side” ▶ 30:44
The reacceleration is broad, not just oil: the ISM prices-paid component is 'on fire' at levels not seen since 2022, so investors should not fade the rate hikes as a pure energy artifact.
data_point🗣 David Cervantes, Pine Brook Capitalconviction: highISM prices paidinflationoil
So what: Strong ISM prices-paid validates a broad inflation impulse; don't fade priced-in hikes, lean into higher-rate trades.
“if you look at the ISM numbers too the in the prices paid component of of ISM that is on fire... numbers that we haven't seen since 2022” ▶ 31:46
Cervantes expects rate hikes (or at least hawkish talk and hike pricing) to happen; the market already has one hike priced in and that pricing is likely to accelerate.
prediction🗣 David Cervantes, Pine Brook Capitalconviction: highFederal Reserverate hikesfed funds futures
So what: A genuinely contrarian rate call (hikes, not cuts); position rates exposure for hike-pricing to build through the curve.
“I don't see how hikes don't happen or at least talk of hikes when they get priced in don't don't happen” ▶ 30:58
Traditional core PCE is ~3.3% YoY; the Cleveland Fed's symmetric trimmed mean is just below 3% (high 2.9s), while the Dallas asymmetric trimmed mean reads ~2.5%.
data_point🗣 David Cervantes, Pine Brook Capitalconviction: highcore PCECleveland Fed trimmed meanDallas Fed trimmed mean
So what: Which inflation gauge the Fed adopts changes the perceived distance to target by ~80bps; a metric-choice that directly affects the rate path.
Fed credibility, Warsh, and the goalpost-moving risk 3
Incoming Fed chair Kevin Warsh is in a 'pickle' - not independent, with the President as his boss - and is trying to move the goalposts by pushing the asymmetric Dallas trimmed-mean PCE (~2.5%) over core PCE to make inflation look closer to target.
thesis🗣 David Cervantes, Pine Brook Capitalconviction: highKevin WarshFederal ReserveDallas trimmed mean PCEFed independence
So what: Metric-switching is a politically expedient dovish lever; watch for Fed credibility erosion that lifts term premium even if policy rates stay low.
“instead of being core PCE he wants to be trimmed you know Dallas trimmed mean uh PCE” ▶ 33:01
The Dallas trimmed-mean methodology is asymmetric - cutting the top ~34% of high prices and bottom ~20% of low prices - which mechanically biases the reported inflation rate lower.
framework🗣 David Cervantes, Pine Brook Capitalconviction: highDallas Fed trimmed meanPCE methodology
So what: Understanding the asymmetry exposes why a 2.5% print is artificially soft; a tell for politically motivated dovish framing.
“The the the Dallas methodology is not symmetric... It cuts off the top 34% of high prices and the bottom 20 something odd percent of low prices” ▶ 33:11
Warsh will struggle to build FOMC consensus and there's a good chance of an internal 'revolt' / hawk-dove fight, with Beth Hammock signaling the conversation shifts if inflation prints persist and Waller flipping from dovish toward hawkish.
prediction🗣 David Cervantes, Pine Brook Capitalconviction: mediumKevin WarshChristopher WallerBeth HammockFOMC
So what: A visibly divided FOMC raises policy uncertainty and term premium; Waller's hawkish pivot is a key signal that hikes are more likely.
“there's a good chance that there might be kind of a... a a revolt within the institution” ▶ 35:06
Rates, term premium, bonds and equities coexisting 4
Market expectations front-run policy, so the pivot from priced-in cuts to priced-in hikes delivers more 'bang for the buck' than the actual rate change, which is a near non-event unless it surprises (like the September 50bp surprise cut).
framework🗣 David Cervantes, Pine Brook Capitalconviction: highFederal Reserveforward guidancerate expectations
So what: Trade the repricing of expectations, not the meeting; the cuts-to-hikes pivot has already moved NPV of assets ahead of any actual hike.
“you do get more more bang for the buck u from the you know expectations front running than you do... by the time the the actual hike... comes it's kind of baked in the cake” ▶ 37:23
In the recent long-end selloff, real rates rose but term premium rose more, reflecting investor fear about the Fed's rules, reaction function, and institutional credibility rather than the level of rates itself.
causal🗣 David Cervantes, Pine Brook Capitalconviction: highterm premiumreal rates10-year TreasuryFederal Reserve credibility
So what: The bond-market risk is Fed-credibility-driven term premium, not the rate level; watch term-premium decomposition to gauge the real stress in long bonds.
“real rates did go up, but, um, term premium went up more” ▶ 38:50
Higher nominal rates can coexist with rising equities; the 10-year tagged 5% in October 2023 and markets ran higher afterward, so a 5% 10-year is not inherently bearish - what matters is the causal driver, not a 'magic number.'
contrarian🗣 David Cervantes, Pine Brook Capitalconviction: high10-year TreasuryequitiesS&P 500
So what: Don't auto-sell equities on a 5% 10-year; the 2022 'high yields = stocks down' framing is wrong this cycle if margins/growth drivers are intact.
“higher higher higher higher nominal interest rates can coexist with rising rising markets and vice versa” ▶ 41:05
The 10-year is around 4.60%, having peaked near 4.68% about three weeks earlier.
data_point🗣 David Cervantes, Pine Brook Capitalconviction: high10-year Treasury yield
So what: Reference levels for the long end; Cervantes would not get concerned even up to 5%, implying room to run higher without breaking equities.
Energy/oil risk and the Korea trade 4
Cervantes's earlier bearish oil thesis (oil above $150 by June unless the Strait was open by midsummer) was offset by gargantuan SPR releases from both China and the US, with China acting as a 'central oil banker' by cutting imports and releasing reserves starting early May.
thesis🗣 David Cervantes, Pine Brook Capitalconviction: mediumoilSPR (Strategic Petroleum Reserve)ChinaUnited StatesStrait of Hormuz
So what: SPR releases were the hidden hand capping oil; their finite nature is the key variable for the next oil move.
“the invisible hand in the market was the SPR releases” ▶ 43:35
SPRs are finite and the rundown period looks like late July to early August, when physical commodity pressure that failed to materialize in late May/June is likely to reappear - the can has only been kicked.
prediction🗣 David Cervantes, Pine Brook Capitalconviction: mediumoilSPRphysical commodity markets
So what: Watch late July-early August for an oil/inflation re-acceleration as SPR support exhausts; a potential catalyst for the broad inflation thesis.
