“that rundown period is looking like late July, uh maybe early August... We just... were just able to kick the can” ▶ 43:46
Cervantes's highest-conviction tactical trade is long Korea: cheap valuations (post-ceasefire P/E ~6), parabolic exports/semiconductor growth, a surprise pop in birth rates, and Samsung paying ~$400k bonuses - and it's still cheap despite a parabolic run.
thesis🗣 David Cervantes, Pine Brook Capitalconviction: highKorea (KOSPI)SamsungSK Hynixsemiconductors
So what: A named, high-conviction long: Korean equities (beyond just Samsung/SK Hynix, which are ~50% of the index) as a cheap, momentum-plus-fundamentals trade.
“I'm fired up on the Korea trade. I've been... very bullish at trade since early this year... that's just been the no the no-brainer trade. It's worked out.” ▶ 44:38
Samsung and SK Hynix together are about 50% of the Korean index weight, but the bull case extends to the whole economy 'on fire,' not just the semiconductor names.
data_point🗣 David Cervantes, Pine Brook Capitalconviction: highKorea (KOSPI)SamsungSK Hynix
So what: Concentration risk in the index is high, but broad-economy strength argues for index-level exposure rather than just the two semi names.

Real Vision · Raoul Pal - The journey man 2 episode(s) · 74 insights

Don’t Make This Crypto Mistake | Raoul Pal The Journey Man49 min41 insights2026-05-28
▶ Watch on YouTube
Raoul Pal lays out his full crypto investing framework: macro debasement (the 'Everything Code') plus business-cycle timing drive a network-adoption trend toward a $100T crypto market cap by ~2034, and the dominant edge is to buy-and-hold a 3-5 asset core (BTC, ETH, SOL, SUI), buy the dips, avoid leverage/FOMO/self-trading, and recycle gains into hedge funds for altcoin alpha and digital art for long-term wealth storage.
Behavioral mistakes that destroy compounding · 4The Everything Code: debasement framework · 5Liquidity & business cycle as crypto timing tools · 5Rules of engagement: the 'don't screw this up' playbook · 6GMI compounding machine: systematic dip-buying · 4Asset selection: BTC, ETH, SOL, SUI and the layer-1 thesis · 6Network-density adoption signals · 3Alt alpha, hedge funds, and the $100T thesis · 4Economic singularity & digital art as wealth store · 4
Behavioral mistakes that destroy compounding 4
Trying to time entries and exits in a network-adoption asset like Bitcoin is value-destructive; Pal's $200k Bitcoin stake from ~2012 would be worth ~$100M today had he simply held instead of selling and rebuying.
anecdote🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highBitcoin
So what: The single biggest return driver is doing nothing; over-management of a long-trend asset compounds into massive opportunity cost.
“It would have been worth a hundred million if I hadn't done anything. And that's the power of compounding. And also proof that I am a total idiot.” ▶ 6:17
Selling crypto into strength on FUD (e.g. Pal selling Bitcoin at $2,000 only to watch it rise another 10x into late 2017) is the classic error, because the chart keeps rising as a network-adoption asset.
anecdote🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highBitcoin
So what: Reacting to fear/narrative cycles caps upside; the base case for adoption assets is to stay invested through bubbles and forks.
Once an investor takes money off near a perceived top, the probability of redeploying it all back in is close to zero; they instead scale in on the way up and end up suboptimal.
framework🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highBitcoincrypto
So what: Argues against cycle-top selling; the behavioral cost of being out outweighs the timing benefit for long-term wealth builders.
“even if you time taking money off right the probability of you putting that all back in later is pretty close to zero” ▶ 27:17
Brokerage data shows the best-performing clients are dead ones, because inactivity lets the long-term log regression work in the holder's favor.
anecdote🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highBitcoin
So what: A memorable proof that inaction beats activity in trend assets; bias toward holding over trading.
“in a brokerage house, the best performing clients they've got are dead ones because people don't do anything” ▶ 18:45
The Everything Code: debasement framework 5
Global total liquidity grows ~8% annualized over full cycles (currently ~10%), and adding ~3% inflation produces an ~11% hurdle rate below which an investor's purchasing power actually shrinks each year.
framework🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highglobal liquidityinflationCPI
So what: Sets a concrete ~11% return bar; assets that don't clear it leave you poorer, justifying allocation to high-beta debasement plays.
“you'll get to an 11% hurdle rate of which if your if your if your returns aren't beating that 11% you're actually getting poorer every year” ▶ 7:59
Debasement (currency value falling versus long-term assets) is distinct from CPI inflation (currency value falling versus goods/services), and both must be summed to compute the real hurdle rate.
framework🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highinflationCPI
So what: Reframing returns against debasement, not CPI, makes asset ownership (not cash) the only way to preserve wealth.
Demographics drive debt: GDP = population growth + productivity growth + debt growth, so a shrinking labor force forces debt to keep rising to maintain GDP, which forces ongoing currency debasement.
causal🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highdemographicsGDPdebtlabor force participation
So what: The mechanical demographics-to-debt-to-debasement chain is the structural tailwind under all long-term assets including crypto.
“Debt is a function of demographics... As the labor force participation rate keeps falling, debt has to keep going up until something changes.” ▶ 9:40
US interest payments now exceed defense spending, and the government services them by issuing more debt and printing via total liquidity—like using a credit card to pay credit-card interest.
data_point🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highUS Treasuryinterest paymentsdefense spending
So what: The interest-payment 'wall' makes continued liquidity expansion structurally unavoidable, supporting the debasement-asset thesis.
“It's like getting a credit card to pay the interest on your credit card.” ▶ 11:19
Liquidity provision has shifted from Fed net liquidity to a broader 'total liquidity' measure sourced from Treasury bill issuance and the banking system (via SLR/Basel III changes and the money multiplier), per Bessent and others wanting it off the central bank.
thesis🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highFederal ReserveScott BessentUS TreasurySLRBasel IIIbanking system
So what: Watch Treasury issuance and bank regulatory changes (not just Fed) as the real liquidity signal driving risk assets.
Liquidity & business cycle as crypto timing tools 5
Bitcoin is ~87% correlated to total liquidity and the NASDAQ is ~97% correlated; Bitcoin runs hotter/colder because it carries an added network-adoption driver on top of debasement.
data_point🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highBitcoinNASDAQglobal liquidity
So what: Treat liquidity as the primary macro driver of BTC/NASDAQ; deviations from liquidity trend are noise, not regime change.
“you have a correlation of about 87%... In fact, the NASDAQ is 97% correlated” ▶ 12:11
The US total liquidity chart shows a 'liquidity gap' extending into end of 2027, but Pal expects an extended cycle or no cycle at all due to debt and capex dynamics.
prediction🗣 Raoul Palconviction: mediumglobal liquidityBitcoin
So what: Contrarian to the standard 4-year crypto cycle; if cycles flatten, dip-buying and holding beat trying to call a 2025-2027 top.
“My view is we probably have an extended cycle or even no cycle at all because of the debt dynamics and also the capex dynamics.” ▶ 11:41
The ISM business cycle drives crypto: activity and investment rise when people have more money; the ISM just printed its longest stretch below 50 in history and is now turning up, which should lift Bitcoin.
causal🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highISMBitcoinS&P 500NASDAQ
So what: A rising ISM off historic lows is a bullish setup for crypto and risk assets broadly; ISM peaks roughly mark crypto peaks.
“we in fact had the lowest period of the ISM below 50 in history” ▶ 13:36
The ETH/BTC ratio (year-on-year) is driven by the ISM: as the business cycle strengthens, ETH outperforms Bitcoin because ETH is a coordination/technology layer (block-space demand rises with activity) while Bitcoin is a store-of-value (driven by savings).
framework🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highEthereumBitcoinISMETH/BTC
So what: A rising ISM is a signal to tilt toward ETH and altcoins over BTC; analogous to small-caps and junk bonds outperforming late in cycle.
“ETH outperforms Bitcoin once the business cycle is stronger because ETH is really a coordination layer while Bitcoin is a store of value layer” ▶ 14:41
Alts have stabilized against Bitcoin recently, driven by the rising business cycle, and 'alt season' should continue to play out as the cycle breaks higher.
prediction🗣 Raoul Palconviction: mediumaltcoinsBitcoinISM
So what: Frames the coming alt outperformance as cycle-driven adoption maturation, not mere speculation—watch the ISM to time alt exposure.
Rules of engagement: the 'don't screw this up' playbook 6
Bitcoin/crypto is a logarithmic network-adoption asset following Metcalfe's law, the same log-adoption path as NASDAQ, Google, Facebook, and Amazon; viewing it on log charts removes the drama and reveals a smooth trend.
framework🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highBitcoinNASDAQGoogleFacebookAmazonMetcalfe's law
So what: Use log regression channels, not linear charts; the long-term adoption trend, not the cycle, is the real game.
Never use leverage in crypto—leverage looks heroic in a bull market but a sudden 50% drawdown (like October 2025) wipes out your stake; rule number one is never lose control of your own coins.
thesis🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highleverageBitcoin
So what: Avoiding leverage and self-custodying is the survival rule that lets you stay in the trade long enough to compound.
“Leverage makes you look like a hero in a bull market, but suddenly you have a 50% draw down... and you lose your stake at the casino.” ▶ 16:24
Hold only 3-5 main assets with proven network adoption (not hunches or chart-watching), and confine speculative bets to a ~10% 'DGEN bag' for experimentation; never FOMO into your friend's meme coin.
framework🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highmeme coinsFartcoinaltcoins
So what: A disciplined core-satellite structure (concentrated proven core + capped speculation) is the recommended portfolio shape.
“Please hold three to five assets as your main holdings... You need to have proof of network adoption.” ▶ 17:39
Practice strict self-custody and wallet hygiene: use a hardware Ledger or multisig wallet, don't connect wallets to third-party sites, use transition wallets, and avoid DeFi protocols because they can be hacked.
thesis🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highLedgermultisigDeFiself-custody
So what: Security discipline is treated as a core return driver—lost/stolen coins are an avoidable permanent loss.
“I don't really like DeFi protocols for the same reason because they can be hacked. Never lose control of your tokens.” ▶ 18:25
Expect and ignore routine volatility: 35% pullbacks 2-3x/year, 50% Bitcoin pullbacks every 2-3 years, recurring bear markets, and 80% drawdowns in smaller tokens—zoom out to lower cortisol and keep perspective.
framework🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highBitcoinaltcoins
So what: Calibrating to normal drawdown magnitudes prevents panic-selling and enables holding through the adoption trend.
“you're going to get 50% pullbacks in Bitcoin, maybe once every two or three years” ▶ 19:31
Buying the big 50% dips is essential to compounding—investors should pre-save cash specifically to deploy into large sell-offs because that is where the biggest returns are made.
thesis🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highBitcoincrypto
So what: Maintaining a dry-powder reserve for crashes is an explicit strategy; the dips, not the rallies, generate the alpha.
“buy the [dip] when you can. If you don't do that simple thing, you do not compound.”
GMI compounding machine: systematic dip-buying 4
Bitcoin is currently ~38% below fair value per Metcalfe's-law log trend, by Pal's GMI compounding-machine model.
data_point🗣 Raoul Palconviction: mediumBitcoinMetcalfe's lawGMI
So what: A specific undervaluation read (~38% below trend) framed as a buy signal at one standard deviation oversold.
“We're 38% below where fair value is according to Metaf's law right now.” ▶ 22:48
Pal's backtest: a pure buy-the-dip-and-hold strategy (no selling) compounded a $100k Bitcoin stake to ~12x ($2.6M), beating buy-and-hold (~$1.6M) and beating buy-sell strategies that take lifestyle chips at overbought bands.
data_point🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highBitcoinGMI
So what: Quantifies that never-sell dip-buying outperforms both passive holding and active profit-taking, reinforcing the hold-and-add thesis.
“I just bought every time it sold off, I'd have compounded to 12 times my money” ▶ 24:46
Investors choosing to manage the cycle can systematize it with standard-deviation bands—adding (e.g. 25% of initial stake) at oversold extremes and selling (e.g. 20% of stack) at overbought extremes, recycling sale proceeds into the next dip.
framework🗣 Raoul Palconviction: mediumBitcoinNASDAQGMI
So what: A rules-based band system removes emotion from cycle management for those who must take lifestyle chips; selling strength funds buying weakness.
The hardest unsolved problem is sourcing cash to deploy at oversold lows, because dips coincide with a weak business cycle when income and savings are squeezed—solved via overbought selling plus disciplined ongoing saving.
framework🗣 Raoul Palconviction: mediumbusiness cycle
So what: Highlights the practical liquidity trap of dip-buying and prescribes pre-funding via systematic saving and trimming.
“how do I solve to have cash when the market's down? That's not always easy.” ▶ 27:59
Asset selection: BTC, ETH, SOL, SUI and the layer-1 thesis 6
Pal's provable core of layer-1s that matter is ETH, Solana, and SUI (plus Bitcoin), with ETH most mature/Lindy, Solana less mature but adopting, and SUI early-stage, riskier, smaller allocation.
thesis🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highEthereumSolanaSUIBitcoinlayer 1
So what: A concrete four-asset core portfolio recommendation, ranked by maturity/risk for position sizing.
“the only layer ones that really matter at this stage and provably so are these three with SUI being the earlier more speculative more volatile” ▶ 31:15
ETH has the most economic and intellectual density and strongest Lindy effects (most developers, protocols, layers, DeFi, users), which is why the traditional financial system is adopting EVM/ETH/layer-1s as core rails.
thesis🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highEthereumEVMDeFiTradFi
So what: Institutional EVM adoption is a structural demand driver for ETH; density metrics justify it as the core smart-contract holding.
Layer-1s accrue value while layer-2s tend not to—an L2 built on ETH compounds ETH's value—so the protocol/infrastructure layer is where to own exposure, not the L2s on top.
framework🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highEthereumlayer 1layer 2
So what: Allocate to base-layer L1 tokens rather than L2 tokens for value accrual; L2 activity is bullish for the underlying L1.
“eth layer twos tend to not acrewue as much value as layer ones. Layer ones are the protocol layer.” ▶ 29:51
Pal values a blockchain by asking how much economic value would be destroyed if it were turned off—for ETH that's every L2, ~70% of the entire stablecoin market, all DeFi, all NFTs, plus future RWAs and finance-system rails.
framework🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highEthereumstablecoinsDeFiNFTsreal world assets
So what: A 'switch-off' valuation lens suggests ETH's intrinsic worth far exceeds its current price given dependent value.
“let's say we turned off the switch to ETH. How much economic value gets destroyed?” ▶ 30:31
Bitcoin's only real competitor is Zcash (the private version of Bitcoin), which may capture ~10% of Bitcoin's value over time while Bitcoin keeps gaining as a share of global savings, much like gold.
prediction🗣 Raoul Palconviction: mediumBitcoinZcashgold
So what: Names Zcash as the notable Bitcoin alternative and frames BTC's TAM as a rising share of global savings, not a fixed pie.
“The only competition it has is probably from Zcash, which is the private version of Bitcoin.” ▶ 31:36
Solana is fast, cheap, and secure, unlocking use cases ETH can't serve; tokens should be understood as a stake in a technology coordination network (of value, agents, identity), not as a currency.
thesis🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highSolanaEthereum
So what: Reframes L1 tokens as equity-like stakes in coordination infrastructure for the machine economy, supporting a tech-valuation approach.
“The token is just your stake in a technology network of the internet of the future.” ▶ 32:09
Network-density adoption signals 3
A winning network is measured by Metcalfe's law (active users × total value transacted), decomposed into intelligence density (programmability/developers driving applications) and economic density (stablecoin float, DeFi TVL, real value settled per daily active user).
framework🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highMetcalfe's lawstablecoinsTVLDeFi
So what: Provides a concrete, reusable due-diligence checklist for picking adoption winners over hype tokens.
A bullish maturity signal is rising TVL with stablecoins outperforming TVL, and real (de-spoofed) value settled by genuine daily active users—you must clean wash/spoof numbers to read it.
framework🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highstablecoinsTVL
So what: Stablecoin float growth relative to TVL is the early tell of network outperformance and maturity.
In the recent drawdown (SUI -80%, SOL -70%, ETH -60%), only ETH, Solana, and SUI held their economic/network density (stable TVL-per-user) while all other chains' density collapsed with price—the key adoption signal.
data_point🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highEthereumSolanaSUI
So what: Density resilience through a crash is the empirical proof Pal uses to separate maturing L1s from the rest—watch density through drawdowns.
“their economic density held their network density... There was only three chains that did that. None of the others did.” ▶ 36:25
Alt alpha, hedge funds, and the $100T thesis 4
Crypto is a ~$2.5T asset class today; following the trend rate of adoption (bank infrastructure, sovereign wealth funds buying Bitcoin, exponential stablecoins, US Treasury funding the short end via stablecoins, Clarity Act passage) implies a path to $100T by ~2034.
prediction🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highBitcoinstablecoinsUS TreasuryClarity Actsovereign wealth funds
So what: A ~40x market-cap target over ~8 years frames crypto as the largest wealth-accumulation event in history—the core bull case.
“we get to 100 trillion by, let's say, 2034... That would be the largest wealth accumulation event in all of human history.” ▶ 0:41
'Alt season' is not mere speculation but the phase where use cases mature and early-stage tokens move up the rankings (e.g. SUI going top-20 to top-10, Solana from nothing to top-10), triggered when the business cycle breaks higher.
framework🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highaltcoinsSUISolanaBitcoinISM
So what: Reframes alt outperformance as fundamental adoption maturation tied to the cycle—watch ranking ascents as confirmation.
Picking applications-layer winners (e.g. Hyperliquid 'coming out of nowhere') is 'real-time VC investing' that Pal says he can't do himself, so he built an asset-management firm to capture it.
thesis🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highHyperliquidventure capitalapplications layer
So what: Acknowledges the application layer holds the biggest gains but requires specialist VC-style selection most investors should outsource.
“this is the bit that I can't do... it's real time VC investing” ▶ 38:49
Pal's firm runs a long-only fund of ~12 of the best crypto hedge funds to capture altcoin outperformance, plus a coming market-neutral fund harvesting arbitrage across perps, swaps, futures, and DeFi at lower volatility.
thesis🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highhedge fundsperpetualsfuturesDeFimarket neutral
So what: Outsourcing alt selection to hedge funds is Pal's prescribed way to capture the 5-10-20x 'others vs Bitcoin' rerating without self-trading.
“every few years you'll suddenly see that whole others part versus Bitcoin rerating 5, 10, 20x” ▶ 47:14
Economic singularity & digital art as wealth store 4
The 'economic singularity' arrives when AI and robots add to the labor force and break the GDP formula (population + productivity + debt), changing the denominators and making much human labor—including knowledge work—approach zero economic value.
prediction🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highAIrobotsGDPlabor force
So what: Frames the largest, fastest technological transition in history—investors must position for abundance of intelligence and scarcity of the human/cultural.
“AI is the last technology humans will ever discover and it's the greatest technology we will ever discover.” ▶ 0:45
In a world of abundant intelligence, scarce stores of value become high-end real estate and human/cultural goods—especially art—because art carries the storytelling and cultural relevance that persists over time.
thesis🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highartreal estateAI
So what: Suggests reallocating long-term wealth into culturally durable assets as AI commoditizes labor and intelligence.
“Upstream of all wealth is art.” ▶ 43:08
Art appreciates with the wealth and cultural relevance of its generation—Warhol Marilyns rose from $33,000 to a high of ~$200M and a Jackson Pollock recently traded at ~$182M as baby boomers/Gen X got rich.
data_point🗣 Raoul Palconviction: mediumartAndy WarholJackson Pollock
So what: Cultural persistence plus generational wealth drives explosive art appreciation—the template Pal applies to crypto-native art.
“This is a Marilyn at auction. They started at $33,000 and now they last traded the highest Marilyn traded at $200 million.”
Crypto-native digital art (XCopy 'Last Selfie' ~$3.5M, Sam Spratt, CryptoPunks up to ~$23M) offers a leveraged bet because it is priced in ETH—if ETH 4x's and the art 5x's versus ETH, that compounds to a ~15x multiplier.
framework🗣 Raoul Palconviction: mediumdigital artXCopySam SprattCryptoPunksNFTsEthereum
So what: ETH-denominated art with cultural consensus is a stacked-leverage play on both ETH appreciation and art appreciation for long-term wealth storage.
“let's say ETH goes from $25,000 to $10,000. It's a 4x... it goes up 5x versus the price of ETH... Then you've got a 15x multiplier”
Agents: The Next Crypto Boom | Raoul Pal The Journey Man35 min33 insights2026-06-04
▶ Watch on YouTube
Raoul Pal argues an invisible, machine-speed 'agentic economy' is forming at the nexus of AI and crypto, where billions of autonomous agents transact, raise capital and trade data on blockchain rails. His actionable conclusion: own the infrastructure layer-1 tokens (ETH, Solana, SUI) because they are the only settlement substrate machines can use at scale, and crypto's total market cap is therefore vastly underpriced.
Invisible machine-speed economy thesis · 5Agents as self-replicating economic actors · 5Agents form networks and emergent intelligence · 3Crypto rails as the only machine-usable substrate · 4Tokenization of data and the identity layer · 3Layer-1 valuation and concentrated ownership thesis · 6Economic singularity and macro reframe · 5Human positioning and qualia · 2
Invisible machine-speed economy thesis 5
Raoul Pal predicts that within two years the majority of economic transactions on Earth will be executed by machines/agents, not humans, operating at a scale and speed beyond human perception.
prediction🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highAI agentsagentic economy
So what: If true, current valuation frameworks built on human transaction volumes massively understate the addressable market for crypto settlement rails.
“within two years the majority of economic transactions on Earth will be invisible to humans” ▶ 1:40
Pal frames the coming system as a 'dark economy' in which human-originated activity will be only about 5% of total economic transactions, because silicon processes information roughly a million times faster than human neurons.
framework🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highAI agents
So what: Reframes TAM for digital infrastructure around machine volume, implying order-of-magnitude upside for the rails carrying that traffic.
“It's a dark economy and we will only be about 5% of it.” ▶ 2:06
Pal argues every total-addressable-market estimate for anything digital is wrong by orders of magnitude because forecasts are anchored to human adoption curves (Facebook, OpenAI, Anthropic) rather than self-replicating agents.
contrarian🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highFacebookOpenAIAnthropicTAM
So what: Suggests demand for APIs, cloud compute, data feeds, storage and settlement is being systematically underestimated by investors.
“every TAM in everything digital in the entire planet is wrong” ▶ 7:37
Pal cites a forecast of an 80-to-1 ratio of agents to humans, implying hundreds of billions of new economic participants entering markets.
data_point🗣 Raoul Palconviction: mediumAI agents
So what: Quantifies the scale driving demand for agent identity, payment and compute infrastructure.
“many people are forecasting an 80 to1 um ratio between agents to humans” ▶ 2:38
Pal references a forecast that up to 60% of all crypto transactions could be done by agents by end of this year, but explicitly doubts that timing while expecting it by end of next year.
prediction🗣 Raoul Palconviction: mediumAI agentscrypto
So what: Watch on-chain transaction composition for the agent share as a leading indicator of the thesis playing out.
“maybe up to 60% of all crypto transactions will be done by Asians by the end of this year. Now, I don't think that's probably the case, but by the end of next year, certainly most likely” ▶ 2:51
Agents as self-replicating economic actors 5
Pal characterizes agents as self-replicating economic patterns that perceive, plan, negotiate, sign transactions, spawn sub-agents, spend budgets and earn money, scaling non-linearly as agents build their own agents.
framework🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highAI agentssub-agents
So what: Explains the compounding mechanism behind the explosive growth assumption and the demand for permissioning and authorization infrastructure.
“agents really are self-replicating economic patterns” ▶ 5:29
Pal argues agent-driven growth follows Reed's law (exponential in subgroups) rather than Metcalfe's law (users squared), producing a 'Cambrian explosion' of new financial-market participants.
framework🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highReed's lawMetcalfe's law
So what: Provides a mathematical rationale for why network-value upside on agent platforms could far exceed historical tech adoption.
“It's actually going to be Reed's law which is the square of that because of how this all works.” ▶ 7:14
Pal recounts that Yoni Assia (eToro) literally 'lost' a funded agent that may have gone rogue with its own money, illustrating real, present-day deployment of capital-controlling autonomous agents and their control risks.
anecdote🗣 Raoul Palconviction: mediumYoni AssiaeToroAI agents
So what: Flags agent custody, control and accountability as an emerging risk surface and potential product category.
“he'd actually lost one of his agents... it's gone rogue because it's got his own money” ▶ 6:20
Pal says people are already spinning up agents whose explicit objective is to make money via businesses or trading bots, handing them a wallet and instructions to learn to trade.
data_point🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highAI agentstrading bots
So what: Profit-seeking agents become a new, always-on source of on-chain trading volume and capital flows.
“Here's a wallet. Off you go.” ▶ 6:16
Pal predicts a phase transition from today's semi-autonomous 'tinkering' agents to fully autonomous agents that set their own objectives, allocate capital, build businesses and even hack websites, producing a rapid takeoff.
prediction🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highAI agentsAGI
So what: Autonomy is the key catalyst that turns the thesis from incremental to discontinuous; track autonomy milestones as the inflection.
“the next phase that's coming is when agents set their own objectives and become much more autonomous” ▶ 20:25
Agents form networks and emergent intelligence 3
Pal argues the real exponential intelligence comes not from a single agent but from groups of agents forming networks, coalitions and collective decisions.
framework🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highAI agentsagent networks
So what: Coordination/networking layers between agents (messaging, social, consensus) become an under-appreciated investable surface.
“It's the group of agents together that start operating at scale is where intelligence really really goes exponential.” ▶ 12:34
Pal cites 'Moltbook,' a social network for Claude agents spun up in a weekend that went viral with hundreds of thousands of bots inventing their own religion ('crustaparianism'), as evidence agents spontaneously form memetic coordinating cultures.
anecdote🗣 Raoul Palconviction: mediumMoltbookClaudecrustaparianismTerminal of TruthsAndy Ayrey
So what: Agent-native social/memetic platforms can drive token speculation and viral capital formation, echoing the memecoin dynamic.
“They created a religion in a week.” ▶ 12:24
Pal reframes memecoins as a working proof-of-concept of agents signaling via memetics to capture attention and raise instant capital at machine speed for opportunities that may last only minutes.
thesis🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highmemecoinsAI agents
So what: Instant agent-to-agent capital formation implies extreme, transient volatility and a new market microstructure investors must model.
“agents can raise capital from other agents for opportunities that they exploit and that opportunity may last minutes and then it can collapse the whole substrate again” ▶ 9:33
Crypto rails as the only machine-usable substrate 4
Pal argues a machine cannot use a bank because legacy rails require KYC, settle in hours/days, have a 1-cent minimum unit and cap at thousands of TPS, whereas crypto rails offer sub-second final settlement, wallet-as-identity, 18-decimal divisibility and 24/7 programmability.
causal🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highcrypto railsbanksKYCTPS
So what: Positions blockchain settlement as a structural requirement, not an option, for the agent economy.
“crypto rails are the only thing that machines can use at scale” ▶ 13:46
Pal cites SUI settling in ~200-300 milliseconds versus a 3-day cross-border bank transfer from the Cayman Islands to the US as the speed differential that makes blockchains the machine-economy substrate.
data_point🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highSUIblockchain settlement
So what: Settlement latency becomes a competitive moat for high-throughput L1s capturing agent transaction flow.
“sui is what 200 milliseconds 300 millisecond 300 milliseconds for for settlement” ▶ 13:20
Pal asserts that stable coins, DeFi, RWA, gaming, storage and identity are not really use cases for crypto but use cases for agents, all converging into one narrative where blockchain is the coordination layer for the machine economy.
thesis🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highstablecoinsDeFiRWAgamingstorageidentityblockchain
So what: Reframes fragmented crypto verticals as a single demand stack driven by agents, justifying a concentrated infrastructure bet.
“These are all things use cases for crypto. They're not. They're use cases for agents.” ▶ 16:34
Pal identifies named adoption catalysts already building agent payment rails: Coinbase and others creating agent payment rails, Google letting agents see machine-readable web pages, and Google Gemini starting to embed agents.
data_point🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highCoinbaseGoogleGeminiClaudebotHermes
So what: Concrete incumbent moves to watch as confirmation the agent-payments infrastructure layer is being built now.
“SU Coinbase um and others creating agent payment rails across everything” ▶ 3:18
Tokenization of data and the identity layer 3
Pal argues 'tokenization' will extend far beyond equities to making all data (scientific, university, climate, soil, farm) machine-readable and tradable for tokenized money in agent data marketplaces that do not yet exist.
thesis🗣 Raoul Palconviction: hightokenizationdata marketplaceshyperscalersfrontier labs
So what: Points to data-monetization marketplaces and tokenized data assets as a large greenfield investment category.
“every piece of information that humans have collected that is machine readable. It will all be tradable and it'll be monetized in tokenized money” ▶ 15:24
Pal contends every agent will need its own on-chain identity, authorization and permission system, making name services (ENS, Unstoppable/'eth', 'sol') and agent-specific wallets a massively underappreciated growth layer.
thesis🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highidentity layerENSname serviceswallets
So what: Agent identity/authorization infrastructure is flagged as a scale opportunity investors are ignoring.
“Think of the scale of the identity layer alone.” ▶ 17:35
Pal frames stablecoins as agents' bridge into the existing financial system, with agents running their own treasuries by rebalancing the cryptocurrencies they are paid in.
framework🗣 Raoul Palconviction: mediumstablecoinsagent treasuries
So what: Agent treasury management implies persistent algorithmic demand for stablecoins and on-chain FX/rebalancing.
“They'll run their own treasuries agents because they'll be rebalancing the different cryptocurrencies” ▶ 18:15
Layer-1 valuation and concentrated ownership thesis 6
Pal's baseline log-trend model takes crypto total market cap from ~$2.5 trillion to $100 trillion within 10 years on 'normal adoption' alone, and he argues the agent economy makes even that vastly underpriced.
prediction🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highcrypto total market cap
So what: Anchors a roughly 40x base-case market-cap target investors can use as a thesis benchmark.
“going from 2 and a half trillion to 100 trillion within 10 years and that's just normal adoption” ▶ 18:40
Pal distinguishes layer-1s (ETH, Solana, SUI) as the infrastructure substrate from layer-2s, which merely rent security from the L1, and from Bitcoin/Zcash, which are store-of-value layers, not the economic substrate.
framework🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highEthereumSolanaSUIlayer-2sBitcoinZcash
So what: Directs capital allocation toward base-layer L1 tokens over L2s for capturing the agent-economy value.
“The layer 1, ETH, Salana, SUI, these are the things at scale that will run our global economy” ▶ 19:34
Pal argues ETH should be valued not on cash flows but as a utility infrastructure layer; destroying ETH would wipe out NFTs, RWAs, every L2, all of DeFi and 70% of stablecoins, so it 'should be priced in trillions.'
thesis🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highEthereumETHDeFistablecoinsNFTsRWA
So what: A utility/dependency-based valuation argues current ETH pricing ignores the economic value built on top of it.
“ETH should be priced in trillions considering what I've told you about with the agent economy” ▶ 30:08
Pal predicts L1 market structure will concentrate like cloud, operating systems and mobile, leaving only two-to-five dominant winners, and for now he sees only Ethereum (plus its L2s), Solana and SUI as able to handle the agent economy.
prediction🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highEthereumSolanaSUIcloud computing
So what: Frames a concentrated, oligopolistic winner-take-most bet on three L1s rather than broad crypto exposure.
“for me it is only Ethereum and its component layer 2s etc. Salana and sui that is even able to deal with this” ▶ 28:06
Pal's central trade is to buy three major substrate-layer L1 tokens and hold for 10 years, framing it as 'humanity's pension plan' and the most obvious opportunity he has ever seen, while disclosing he sits on the SUI foundation.
thesis🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highEthereumSolanaSUIlayer-1 tokens
So what: The explicit, disclosed-conflict actionable call: concentrated long-term ownership of L1 infrastructure tokens.
“if you were just to buy three of the major tokens of the substrate layer and went to the beach in 10 years time, you'll come back from the beach on a jet” ▶ 0:36
Pal contends the agent-economy infrastructure substrate is, unlike the internet, ownable and fractionalizable by everyone globally, so anyone can own a fraction of the rails that all economic activity must coordinate on.
thesis🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highlayer-1 tokensblockchain
So what: Frames L1 tokens as democratized ownership of critical infrastructure, the core retail pitch.
“the substrate, the infrastructure, for the first time in history, unlike the internet is ownable by everybody” ▶ 27:31
Economic singularity and macro reframe 5
Pal predicts an 'economic singularity' around 2030 (window 2030-2032) when agent networks scale enough to break how the world works and make GDP as currently measured obsolete.
prediction🗣 Raoul Palconviction: higheconomic singularityGDPAGI
So what: Gives a dated catalyst horizon (~3.5-4 years) for accumulating exposure before the regime change.
“I marked it as 2030 to 2032. I think 2030 is going to be on the nose for this.” ▶ 22:55
Pal upgrades the trend-GDP formula (population + productivity + debt) to a new growth function of humans + robots + AI + debt + energy density + compute efficiency, arguing every component goes exponential.
framework🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highGDProbotsAIenergy densitycompute efficiency
So what: Offers a reusable macro model for why growth and asset returns could detach from historical trend rates.
“humans plus robots plus AI plus debt plus energy density and compute efficiency” ▶ 23:21
Pal argues collapsing energy cost (solar down ~99.99% over 30 years on a Wright's-law curve) plus rising compute efficiency drives the cost of intelligence toward collapse, making AI growth less physically constrained than robots.
causal🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highsolarWright's lawcompute efficiencyAI
So what: Falling intelligence cost is the engine behind exponential agent proliferation and the demand thesis for compute/energy.
“solar is on a rights law collapsing, it's down like 99.99% over the last 30 years” ▶ 24:30
Pal cites Anthropic going from zero to a roughly $120 billion run-rate in revenue in about a year and a half as evidence of Reed's-law compounding across the AI stack.
data_point🗣 Raoul Palconviction: mediumAnthropic
So what: A concrete revenue-velocity data point supporting the exponential-adoption claim (figure stated loosely).
“Anthropic went from zero revenue to what his run rate is what 120 billion or something stupid in revenue in a year and a half” ▶ 24:52
Pal takes the contrarian view that AI/agents will not cause demand collapse and mass unemployment; instead agents create demand for energy, compute, storage and data, producing an explosion in (mostly invisible) economic activity rather than a contraction.
contrarian🗣 Raoul Palconviction: highAI agentsUBIunemployment
So what: Bullish reframe against the AI-deflation/UBI consensus, supporting demand for energy, compute and data infrastructure.
“agents create demand... Demand goes through the roof. So we get an explosion in economic activity.” ▶ 26:57
Human positioning and qualia 2
Pal advises investors to avoid being labor an agent can replace and instead be the human it cannot, anchored in 'qualia' (subjective sensory/emotional experience) that machines may never possess.
framework🗣 Raoul Palconviction: mediumqualiaAIlabor
So what: Career/positioning guidance: lean into non-replicable human experiential value while owning the rails for upside.
“Just don't be the labor that the agent can replace. Be the human that it can't compl.” ▶ 31:19
Pal projects that as AGI and autonomous agents take over the digital and robotic/physical layers, humans shift to earning in a layer of culture, community, experiences and nature.
prediction🗣 Raoul Palconviction: mediumAGIrobotsRay Kurzweil
So what: Implies long-run value migration toward experiential/cultural sectors as machines absorb digital and physical labor.
“we make our money and our living in that world of culture community experiences nature” ▶ 22:18

No Priors: AI, Machine Learning, Tech, & Startups 2 episode(s) · 70 insights

The Rise of the Full-Stack Builder and Hyper-Leveraged Generalist with Microsoft CEO Satya Nadella42 min37 insights2026-06-04
▶ Watch on YouTube
Satya Nadella reframes Microsoft's AI strategy as an ecosystem/'harness' play rather than a single model bet, arguing the durable IP is a company's private eval plus the agent traces that let it hill-climb on swappable frontier models. He predicts SAS unbundling and rebundling, agent-driven consumption pricing displacing per-seat, agents/token-expertise moving onto corporate balance sheets, and a labor shift toward hyper-leveraged generalists and 'full-stack builders' — gated by communities and politicians seeing tangible AI benefits.
Platform strategy: ecosystem & harness over single model · 5Private eval & agent traces as the new corporate IP · 4MAI models, lineage & temporal frontier · 4Coding agents, UI rebuild & the agentic glue-work expansion · 3SAS unbundling, durability & end-of-software debate · 6AI pricing models evolution · 3Labor structure: generalists, full-stack builders & new infra roles · 4Meta-work, ambition & reconceptualizing operations · 3Data-center buildout, energy & community 'permission' · 3Verticals: healthcare & the education opportunity · 2
Platform strategy: ecosystem & harness over single model 5
Nadella is positioning Microsoft's AI offering as an ecosystem/platform that lets any company create its own AI, explicitly rejecting framing it as a single model or single platform; he judges a platform by how much value is created on top of it versus captured inside it.
framework🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: highMicrosoftAzure
So what: Signals Microsoft will compete on enablement/distribution breadth rather than model supremacy — bullish for Azure/Foundry attach, less of a pure model-vs-model trade vs OpenAI/Anthropic.
“a platform is defined by fundamentally its ability to create more value about the platform versus what's captured in the platform” ▶ 2:27
Microsoft is building its products (GitHub Copilot, security copilot, mdash, science discovery) as multi-model 'harnesses' that bind models, data and tools in a loop, and is exposing the GitHub harness in Foundry as open so customers can bring their own models, tools and context.
thesis🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: highGitHub CopilotMicrosoft FoundryAzureLlama
So what: An open multi-model harness commoditizes the model layer and entrenches Microsoft at the orchestration/tooling layer — watch Foundry adoption as the real moat metric.
“you can use your llama harness whatever or you can use the ... any harness of yours and train with your tools and multiple models and your context” ▶ 11:02
Nadella offers Microsoft's security tool mdash as existence proof that an open multi-model harness can outperform a tightly co-trained model+harness, because mdash found bugs/vulnerabilities at launch that the incumbent (referred to as 'mythos') did not.
data_point🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: highmdashMicrosoftcybersecurity
So what: Undercuts the rival-lab thesis that vertically integrating model+harness+tools is required for top performance; supports multi-model procurement strategies.
“when it launched um it found bugs or vulnerabilities that were not found by mythos ... there is existence proof” ▶ 11:31
Nadella frames Microsoft's evolution as a three-act story — operating systems, then cloud, and now the harness/eval layer — characterizing 'every company operating at the frontier with their own frontier intelligence' as the company's mission.
framework🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: highMicrosoftWindowsAzure
So what: Clarifies the strategic narrative investors should track Microsoft against: success = breadth of customers building differentiated AI on its stack, not first-party model wins.
“can everybody operate at the frontier with their frontier intelligence” ▶ 14:46
Nadella argues a successful platform builder can also run first-party products (as Windows and the cloud/SAS businesses did) without that being a limiter on others' success, contrasting with independent frontier labs whose first-party productivity/code/search products are designed to drive the majority of revenue.
contrarian🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: mediumMicrosoftWindowsOpenAIfrontier labs
So what: Implicit dig at frontier-lab business models that depend on first-party apps; investors should weigh whether labs' app revenue concentration is a vulnerability versus Microsoft's platform breadth.
“a firstparty product be it productivity or code or search drives the majority of revenue. That's a different value equation” ▶ 12:03
Private eval & agent traces as the new corporate IP 4
Nadella claims a company's private eval is becoming its single biggest piece of IP, because public benchmarks can all be maxed out and the network effects of intelligence come from learning from a few novel samples rather than from large data volumes.
thesis🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: highMicrosoftAI evals
So what: Reframes defensibility for AI-native startups and incumbents away from data scale toward proprietary evals/traces — a lens for assessing moats in AI app companies.
“every company having private eval maybe the biggest IP” ▶ 13:15
Nadella proposes an 'acid test' of control: if you can swap the underlying frontier model from A to B and still hill-climb on your private eval, you are in control; if you cannot, you are not — making an open harness essential to retaining that control.
framework🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: highAI evalsMicrosoft Foundry
So what: A concrete diligence test for whether an AI company is hostage to one model vendor; model-agnosticism becomes a valuation-relevant attribute.
“another asset test is you have an eval that's private ... can you switch it to model B and you climb up. If you can, then you're in control. If you can't, you're not in control.” ▶ 13:30
Nadella argues that company-specific agents trained on the traces between humans and agents capture previously un-bookable tacit knowledge ('the company veteran agent') and should literally go onto the corporate balance sheet as an asset.
prediction🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: mediumMicrosoft TeamsAI agentsaccounting
So what: If agent/token-expertise becomes a capitalizable asset, it reshapes how AI-heavy firms are valued and audited — a structural shift for accounting and equity analysis.
“when a company says it should in fact go onto the balance sheet is how I think about it ... human capital was never possible to go put on a balance sheet” ▶ 17:10
Both hosts and Nadella anticipate the SEC will need new accounting standards for 'token expertise'/agent assets, signaling that AI agents may become a recognized intangible on financial statements.
prediction🗣 host (Sarah Guo)conviction: exploratorySECaccountingAI agents
So what: Early signal of a potential regulatory/disclosure regime for AI assets — watch for FASB/SEC moves that could materially change reported balance sheets of AI-native firms.
“I think the SEC is going to have to have accounting standards for token uh expertise” ▶ 17:31
MAI models, lineage & temporal frontier 4
Microsoft has built an in-house frontier 'neo-lab' (MAI models) inside ~two years, emphasizing clean pre-training lineage with high data quality and aggressive ablation as a differentiator versus open-weight models that benchmark well but underperform in practice.
data_point🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: highMicrosoftMAI modelsMustafa Suleymanopen-weight models
So what: Microsoft now has its own frontier-class models, reducing dependence on OpenAI — a strategic and negotiating shift relevant to the Microsoft-OpenAI relationship.
“how the heck can a small 5D model hill climb? ... try to pursue finding that cognitive core” ▶ 4:17
Nadella describes a 'temporal frontier' technique where a top model (e.g. a GPT-class model) generates traces offline, then a much smaller ~5B reasoning model is trained on those traces and surpasses the original — demonstrated in a Land O'Lakes demo.
framework🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: highMAI modelsLand O'LakesGPTreasoning models
So what: Distillation-from-traces lets cheap small models beat frontier models on specific tasks — bearish for the assumption that frontier-model inference spend scales indefinitely, bullish for cost-down on production AI.
“you collected a bunch of traces and then you took a 5B reasoning model and achieved higher” ▶ 5:37
Nadella says the industry underestimated the real-world complexity of deploying models to deliver value, and that prepping the context layer is where 'the magic is' — the hardest lesson of the last two years.
causal🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: highMicrosoftcontext engineeringAI deployment
So what: Value capture is shifting from raw model capability to deployment/context engineering — favors companies and tools owning the context/data-prep layer.
“the amount of work you need to do to prep the context layer ... is where the magic is” ▶ 10:45
Nadella reframes the worry about 'token max' (high token consumption) as a category error: tokens should be understood as the unit of value creation at every step, implying token consumption is a feature, not a cost to minimize.
contrarian🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: highMicrosoftAI tokensAzure
So what: Reinforces the 'token economy' demand thesis underpinning hyperscaler capex — supports the bull case for sustained Azure/compute revenue growth.
“it's an artifact of us not having thought ourselves as an industry that we are using tokens to create value every step of the way” ▶ 7:22
Coding agents, UI rebuild & the agentic glue-work expansion 3
Coding has worked so well that Microsoft is having to rebuild the IDE itself, because running ~100 agent sessions transfers excessive cognitive load back to the human and chat-as-sole-artifact breaks down — driving a move to a 'canvas' UI.
data_point🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: highGitHub CopilotIDEAI agents
So what: Even in a fully agentic world, new software/UI is needed — a counter to the 'end of software/UI' thesis and an opportunity area for agent-management tooling.
“I have these 100 agent sessions ... the cognitive load it transfers back to me as a human is so excessive that now I need a new UI” ▶ 8:02
Nadella predicts that within ~6 months long-running, durable autopilot agents working overnight with delegated authority/identity will be commonplace, extending the coding-style productivity gain to judgment-heavy 'glue work', and requiring new audit tools to verify what agents did.
prediction🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: highMicrosoftAI agentsco-workautopilots
So what: Suggests near-term expansion of agent monetization from code into broad knowledge work — watch consumption growth and the emergence of agent-audit/identity tooling as new categories.
“all through night the night there was a bunch of stuff that all these autopilots that I have working on my behalf with my delegated authority ... did a bunch of work” ▶ 9:10
Nadella reports personally building production agents trivially — e.g. a long-running Foundry 'chief of staff' autopilot last week assembled from Work IQ with Rayfin-backed memory and published to Teams — as evidence end-to-end app creation is now near-effortless.
anecdote🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: highMicrosoft FoundryWork IQMicrosoft TeamsRayfinScout
So what: Demonstrates collapsing cost of bespoke internal software, pressuring point-solution SAS vendors and validating Foundry's build-anything pitch.
“I took it work IQ ... build a foundry longunning agent ... and lo and behold, it built it ... I could say publish to teams” ▶ 27:48
SAS unbundling, durability & end-of-software debate 6
Nadella argues the underlying data models, schemas and business logic (e.g. a general ledger, or PowerBI's semantic models) of legacy SAS remain valuable and stable, while the SAS challenge is to unbundle the packaged stack and rebundle it into new business models.
thesis🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: highSASPowerBIMicrosoft
So what: Suggests data-model/semantic-layer incumbents are more durable than UI-layer SAS in the agent era — a sorting lens for which software stocks survive disruption.
“the challenge of the SAS business model is we packaged one way we now have to learn how to unbundle these things and rebundle in new ways” ▶ 19:47
Nadella offers a build-vs-buy rule: always acquire/license software when the marginal cost of building and maintaining it yourself (including AI-driven security fixes that burn tokens) is higher than buying it.
framework🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: highSASAI agentscybersecurity
So what: Counters 'agent euphoria' that enterprises will rebuild everything in-house; durable SAS survives where maintenance cost favors buying — relevant to SAS revenue-durability bears/bulls.
“you should always acquire something if the marginal cost of building and maintaining ... something on your own is higher” ▶ 24:49
Nadella predicts enterprises currently in 'agent euphoria' (threatening to drop SAS vendors to rebuild internally) will largely return to buying software, but only from flexible vendors, because there will be very little tolerance for inflexible vendors.
prediction🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: mediumSASenterprise software
So what: Implies a near-term air pocket then reversion for SAS revenue; vendor flexibility (model/business-model adaptability) becomes the differentiator between winners and losers.
“there'll be very little tolerance for anybody who's inflexible uh at the vendor level” ▶ 25:40
Nadella says reaching the new SAS equilibrium requires going through one full enterprise budget cycle before the durable/non-durable split is clear.
prediction🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: mediumSASenterprise software
So what: Sets a ~12-month timing marker for when SAS disruption winners/losers will be observable — useful for sizing the duration of current uncertainty in software multiples.
“I think we have to go through one full budget cycle on this to really see the ... emergence of the equilibrium” ▶ 24:29
Nadella illustrates SAS rebundling with Work IQ exposing M365's long-captive data (email, Teams, Office, SharePoint) as a queryable database — enabling cross-app agentic tasks like turning design-meeting transcripts into a code-change plan — and claims this is a 10x value-creation expansion for M365.
data_point🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: highMicrosoft 365Work IQMicrosoft TeamsSharePointGitHub
So what: Frames M365/Work IQ as a major monetization lever via agentic usage; supports the bull case for Microsoft's productivity-suite ARPU expansion.
“with work IQ, we've exposed what is perhaps the most important database in a company that never got used as a database” ▶ 20:39
Nadella draws a historical parallel: when on-prem servers (Exchange, SharePoint, Lync) moved to cloud subscriptions, cloud server usage grew 10x-100x because customers were buying subscriptions not servers — and predicts the same multiplier as agents (not just end users) drive M365 usage, forcing re-architecture of mail/inbox serving for agents.
causal🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: highMicrosoft 365ExchangeSharePointLyncWork IQ
So what: Agent-driven usage could expand M365 consumption far beyond seat counts — a structural tailwind for Microsoft cloud/productivity revenue and infrastructure demand.
“the number of people who will use servers in the cloud is 10x, 100x ... The same thing is now happening with M365” ▶ 20:26
AI pricing models evolution 3
Nadella predicts a sequence of AI pricing models — per-user subscriptions (bundling usage into entitlements for budget certainty) persisting, followed by consumption pricing becoming the next big thing, with every per-user product gaining a consumption meter.
prediction🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: highMicrosoftGitHub CopilotSAS pricing
So what: Consumption metering layered onto seats reshapes software revenue models toward usage-elastic growth — favors vendors with metered, agent-intensive products.
“there will always be a per user but they will have to be a consumption meter” ▶ 23:58
Nadella is skeptical of outcome-based pricing as a durable model, observing that customers love it until they have an outcome and then resist sharing it 'like giving away royalty', reverting to per-user and consumption pricing.
contrarian🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: highAI pricingSAS
So what: Tempers the popular outcome-based/agentic-pricing narrative; investors should discount business plans premised on durable outcome pricing.
“most people love outcomes until they have an outcome. Because once you have an outcome, it's like giving away royalty” ▶ 22:52
Microsoft recently added per-user pricing to GitHub because GitHub Copilot's original per-user model didn't anticipate agent usage intensity — e.g. launching 10,000 agents running all day rather than interactive code-complete.
data_point🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: highGitHubGitHub Copilot
So what: Concrete example that agent intensity is forcing pricing re-architecture in real time — a template for how all per-seat AI products will adjust.
“GitHub copilot was constructed at a per user level before we understood even uh the intensity of usage of agents” ▶ 23:33
Labor structure: generalists, full-stack builders & new infra roles 4
Nadella endorses (with experimentation) the view that the ~dozen engineering specialties will collapse toward a few roles — agent managers, forward-deployed engineers, security engineers, and large-scale infrastructure engineers — with most other work absorbed into the agentic layer.
prediction🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: mediumsoftware engineeringAI agentslabor markets
So what: Structural labor shift in engineering — relevant to talent strategy, edtech, and the demand profile for forward-deployed/security/infra skills.
“It'll be people who are managing agents ... forward deployed engineers ... security engineers ... large scale infrastructure” ▶ 28:35
Nadella cites LinkedIn structurally creating a new 'full-stack builder' discipline that merges design, product management and front-end engineering into broader-scope roles while preserving each person's specialist edge.
anecdote🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: highLinkedInMicrosoftlabor markets
So what: Real example of org-chart restructuring driven by AI leverage — a leading indicator of how AI-native companies will staff and a theme for workforce/edtech investors.
“built up a new discipline called full stack builder” ▶ 29:04
Nadella argues the hyper-leveraged generalist will see the maximum returns because AI puts knowledge work and app-building 'in the same sentence', dramatically raising generalist leverage across the board.
thesis🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: highlabor marketsAI agentsgeneralists
So what: Reinforces the 'golden age for idea people with agency' thesis — bullish for small founding teams and individual leverage, a recurring No Priors investment lens.
“the generalist role is going to be the most exciting ... because the leverage of a generalist ... is where we are going to see the maximum returns” ▶ 30:12
Nadella says building the RL environment in which a reward can be learned is one of the hardest infrastructure problems — even the Excel team now needs distributed-systems/infra talent inside what was an end-user app team.
data_point🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: highMicrosoft Excelreinforcement learningdistributed systems
So what: Demand for RL-environment and distributed-systems infra talent is spreading into traditionally non-infra product teams — a hiring/skills signal and a hard-to-replicate moat.
“building the RLE in which a reward can be learned is actually one of the hardest sort of infrastructure problems” ▶ 29:41
Meta-work, ambition & reconceptualizing operations 3
Nadella's ambition framework (via Kevin Scott) distinguishes making hard things easier from the higher-leverage goal of making the impossible possible, arguing organizations lack the new conceptual model of what newly-impossible things they can now build.
framework🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: highMicrosoftKevin Scott
So what: A lens for evaluating which AI initiatives create durable value (new categories) versus incremental efficiency — informs where outsized enterprise value accrues.
“true ambition is about making the impossible possible” ▶ 0:18
Nadella reports the Azure network team reconceptualized its job from doing Azure networking to building an agentic system ('Miles') that does the networking — managing 500+ fiber operators — and asked for tokens instead of headcount to scale operations.
anecdote🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: highAzureAI agentsMicrosoft
So what: Concrete 'tokens instead of headcount' substitution inside Microsoft's own ops — a vivid signal of AI-for-labor substitution and the token-demand flywheel.
“they were saying look ... we don't need headcount we need tokens in order to be able to manage uh our operation” ▶ 33:33
Microsoft built more Azure capacity in the last 15 months than in its first 15 years, with the same team forced to make their work 'meta' to keep up with the scaling pace.
data_point🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: highAzuredata centersMicrosoft
So what: Quantifies the extraordinary acceleration of Azure capacity buildout — directly relevant to Microsoft capex trajectory and infrastructure-supply-chain demand.
“We built in the last 15 months more Azure capacity than we built in the first 15 years” ▶ 0:25
Data-center buildout, energy & community 'permission' 3
Nadella frames the AI data-center buildout as gated by 'community permission': the industry must prove tangible local benefits — lower long-term energy prices via a better grid, replenished water through closed-loop systems, construction/operations jobs, and tax base — or it won't be allowed to continue.
thesis🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: highdata centersenergyMicrosofthyperscalers
So what: Local/political acceptance is a real constraint on hyperscaler expansion — a regulatory/permitting risk factor for the entire AI-infrastructure capex cycle.
“if that is the case, then we will have permission. If it is not, we won't have permission. It's as simple as that” ▶ 36:18
Nadella invokes the historical pattern that high-energy-use endeavors that also create large societal value have fared well, betting that a token economy driving productivity, growth, broad participation and better health outcomes will justify AI's energy consumption.
causal🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: highenergydata centerstoken economy
So what: Ties AI infrastructure's social license directly to demonstrable productivity gains — if value fails to materialize broadly, the buildout faces backlash risk.
“if you use a lot of energy but also create a lot of value for society, the story has been fantastic. If you don't do that, it's not been that great” ▶ 36:43
Nadella warns the public will be skeptical of tech companies asking it to 'trust us', so the industry must deliver tangible benefits within the next 12-18 months — and notes politicians winning elections by advocating for AI as part of earning that permission.
prediction🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: highAI policyMicrosoftpolitics
So what: Sets a near-term (12-18 month) window for AI to show broad benefit or face political/social headwinds — a timeline for sentiment and policy risk in the AI trade.
“the world is going to be very skeptical of tech and tech companies that say trust us we've got it the future is going to be glorious” ▶ 0:00
Verticals: healthcare & the education opportunity 2
Nadella names healthcare (citing OpenEvidence) and education as the clearest broad-benefit AI verticals beyond tech-sector wealth creation, but says education has seen less impact than expected.
thesis🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: mediumhealthcareeducationOpenEvidenceAlpha School
So what: Flags healthcare AI (OpenEvidence) as already delivering and education as an under-penetrated opportunity — directional for AI vertical-application investing.
“there are companies like open evidence I think that is happening um education seems like another one that's an obvious good where we haven't seen as much impact as I'd expect” ▶ 40:13
Nadella suggests the next big startup success could be one that builds a new university or pedagogy that takes someone through a curriculum to economic opportunity, arguing credentials, incentives and how credentials map to employment all need to change because how people learn has shifted.
prediction🗣 Satya Nadellaconviction: mediumeducationAlpha Schoolstartups
So what: An explicit greenfield startup thesis from a hyperscaler CEO — edtech/new-credentialing ventures flagged as high-value, watch for Microsoft-adjacent investment or partnership.
“maybe the next big startup and success story could be someone who builds a new university um or a new um pedagogy” ▶ 41:40
Building an AI Guardian for Enterprise with Onyx Security CEO Maxim Bar Kogan41 min33 insights2026-05-28
▶ Watch on YouTube
Onyx Security CEO Maxim Bar Kogan argues that enterprises are adopting autonomous coding agents (Claude Code, Cowork, OpenClaw) faster than they can control them, creating a $100B+ opening for an independent 'guardian' layer that trains small, cheap models to oversee other agents — a market the foundation labs are structurally disqualified from owning.
Agent-action risk as the new security category · 4Enterprise deployment mix & adoption pace · 4Why legacy security stacks fail on agents · 4Onyx's small-model moat (cost/latency) · 4Structural moat vs. the labs · 4Mythos: cheap vulnerability discovery & controlled rollout · 4Enterprise risk posture & vendor concentration · 4Market size, interpretability bet & Israeli ecosystem · 5
Agent-action risk as the new security category 4
Enterprises now face near market-wide panic about AI security, and have shifted from a 'DLP for chatbots' (what employees type into ChatGPT) risk frame to worrying about autonomous agent actions like deleting databases or publishing secrets.
thesis🗣 host (Sarah Guo)conviction: highChatGPTOnyx SecurityDLPenterprise security
So what: Signals a fast-growing TAM for 'agent-action' security distinct from legacy DLP/endpoint vendors; watch incumbents' ability to pivot vs. new entrants.
“The consensus risk story uh two two years ago when you started the company was basically like DLP for chat bots... Now we have clearly something that is not quite panic but close to marketwide panic.” ▶ 1:21
Enterprises have no way to stop AI agent adoption and so must instead buy tooling to reduce the chance of agent actions being illegitimate or incorrect, making spend on agent-control non-discretionary.
thesis🗣 guest (Maxim Bar Kogan, CEO Onyx Security)conviction: highOnyx Securityenterprise AI
So what: Non-discretionary, adoption-driven demand implies durable spend regardless of macro; a structurally bullish setup for the category.
“they don't have any way to stop the adoption. So like they just now have to do something to reduce the chance of these agent actions being uh illegitimate or incorrect.” ▶ 0:18
Real-world incidents are already materializing: downtime caused by agents 'just doing the wrong thing,' and agents accidentally publishing code and secret tokens they weren't supposed to.
data_point🗣 guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)conviction: highAI agentsenterprise security
So what: Concrete loss events accelerate budget approval; expect publicized agent failures to be demand catalysts for guardian-layer vendors.
“down times that were caused by a just doing the wrong thing, agents accidentally publishing code and tokens uh that they weren't supposed to” ▶ 6:44
Human-in-the-loop oversight breaks down as agent actions scale 100x/1,000x/1,000,000x, forcing automated AI-on-AI oversight rather than manual review.
causal🗣 guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)conviction: highAI agentshuman-in-the-loop
So what: Validates demand for automated oversight infra and undercuts manual-review GRC tooling; the volume of agent actions is the core demand driver.
“now that you're going to have 100x, a thousandx, a millionx of these actions, h that's not going to work.”
Enterprise deployment mix & adoption pace 4
In a typical enterprise, autonomous coding agents/assistants are now over 50% of agentic deployment, low-code drag-and-drop automations ~45%, and first-party custom-built agents only ~2%.
data_point🗣 guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)conviction: highcoding agentslow-code platformsenterprise AISaaS
So what: Quantifies where agent budget actually flows; off-the-shelf autonomous coding tools dominate while in-house agent-building remains marginal — bearish for 'build your own agent' platforms.
“I would say roughly at this point over 50% is the autonomous uh coding agents and assistance in the average enterprise. Then probably 45% is is those uh uh low code automations. And the last 2% are really the first party ones” ▶ 8:51
Autonomous agents are the fastest-growing category; Claude Code grew 'like fire,' Cowork is growing even faster, and enterprises are now sanctioning OpenClaw as a legitimate tool driven by CEO-level AI mandates.
data_point🗣 guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)conviction: highClaude CodeCoworkOpenClawAnthropic
So what: Top-down CEO mandates are pulling unrestricted autonomous tools into regulated enterprises faster than controls can keep up — bullish for Anthropic seat growth and for the control layer.
“We're starting to see to our own surprise actually people adopting openclaw as a legitimate sanctioned tool in the company because the CEO is very driven to adopt AI.” ▶ 9:37
Low-code, connector-based agent platforms proved too limited to deliver real productivity gains, whereas 'unleashed' autonomous agents with fewer baked-in controls produced the crazy benefits that drove enterprise adoption.
causal🗣 guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)conviction: highlow-code platformsautonomous agents
So what: Implies the ROI lives in unconstrained agents, not safe connector platforms — a headwind for restrictive low-code automation vendors and a tailwind for capability-maximizing tools plus external guardrails.
“those platforms ended up being quite limited. So that we didn't get the productivity gains from those limited platforms.” ▶ 4:41
Anthropic's revenue is increasingly coming from enterprises paying for Claude Code to do work developers used to do.
data_point🗣 guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)conviction: mediumAnthropicClaude Codesoftware developers
So what: Reinforces labor-substitution as the monetization engine for coding agents; supports a thesis of seat-displacement revenue for Anthropic.
“tropics revenue is coming from enterprises that are paying for cloud code to do a lot of the work that developers used to do.” ▶ 5:01
Why legacy security stacks fail on agents 4
Identity/permission controls — security's first and most important lever — break for agents because users deliberately grant agents their own broad permissions to delegate diverse tasks, so there is no 'right' permission set to scope.
causal🗣 guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)conviction: highidentity securityIAMAI agents
So what: Disrupts the IAM/least-privilege playbook; identity vendors (Okta-style) can't constrain agents, opening whitespace for context-aware controls.
“with these autonomous AIs... we kind of want them to have our permissions... so suddenly our identity security software is not very useful” ▶ 11:12
Endpoint and API security tools fail on agents because they lack the context to know why an agent is acting — they can't distinguish a sanctioned database delete from a dangerous one on an unrelated task.
causal🗣 guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)conviction: highendpoint securityAPI securityClaude Code
So what: Intent/context is the missing primitive legacy EDR/API tools can't supply — a moat for vendors who model agent reasoning rather than network/endpoint signals.
“our endpoint providers or API security tools, they don't know what cloud was thinking. why is it doing what it's doing?” ▶ 12:15
A naive 'proxy with a policy engine' approach is insufficient: proxying is often not technically viable (AI runs on third-party cloud and endpoints), and seeing the data isn't the hard part — deciding whether an action is legitimate is the hard problem.
framework🗣 guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)conviction: highproxypolicy engineAI security
So what: The defensible value is the judgment/underwriting engine, not packet inspection; commoditized proxy/policy plays will struggle to differentiate.
“that's not the hard problem. The hard problem is understanding if what I should do... what is the engine that needs to underwrite these different actions” ▶ 13:35
Without purpose-built agent controls, enterprises face a forced trade-off: heavily limit agents (destroying their usefulness) or miss dangerous actions — there is no safe middle with legacy tooling.
framework🗣 guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)conviction: highAI securityenterprise AI
So what: Frames the guardian layer as the only way to keep agents both useful and safe — positions agent-control as enabling, not gating, adoption.
“you're either going to end up limiting them a lot, making them almost uh much less useful... or uh you're going to miss a lot of pretty dangerous things” ▶ 12:33
Onyx's small-model moat (cost/latency) 4
Using a full frontier agent to oversee every agent is a commercial dealbreaker: the security vendor would cost more than the AI being watched, and latency would be unacceptable.
framework🗣 guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)conviction: highClaude CodeAI securityunit economics
So what: Unit economics, not capability, decide who wins agent oversight — a vendor whose cost scales with frontier inference can't survive; cheap triage is the moat.
“if I need to run an agent for every agent you're running as your security vendor, h you're going to be paying for me more than you're paying for your AI” ▶ 15:25
Onyx's core technical bet is training very small, single-purpose models that cheaply decide when a smarter agent should take a closer look — minimizing both misses and over-escalation to balance performance against cost and latency.
thesis🗣 guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)conviction: highOnyx Securitysmall modelsspecialized model training
So what: Specialized small-model triage is the claimed moat; the defensibility hinges on the routing intuition, a recurring small-model-vs-frontier-cost theme.
“models that are just good at one thing. They're very small... they don't miss a lot of stuff and they don't call that other agent too much” ▶ 16:10
Onyx must continuously retrain its small oversight models as frontier models get smarter and adversaries (harnesses) evolve, framing the moat as a perpetual catch-up race rather than a static product.
causal🗣 guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)conviction: highfrontier modelsOnyx Security
So what: Recurring retraining cost is a structural treadmill — durable revenue but ongoing R&D burn; a risk if frontier pace outruns the small-model team.
“you need to be able to have models that are on your side that are small and effective at continuously h being able to say now is the time this is the action where I think someone should take a closer look” ▶ 16:53
The chess-master analogy: like grandmasters who play most moves intuitively and only stop to deeply calculate on critical high-risk moves, efficient oversight spends heavy intelligence only on high-risk actions.
framework🗣 host (Sarah Guo) and guestconviction: mediumcompute allocationAI oversight
So what: A reusable mental model for cost-efficient AI safety/monitoring architectures investors can use to evaluate competing approaches.
“You don't want to spend too much intelligence where you don't have to and you want to spend a lot of intelligence overwhelmingly a lot in situation where there's high risk.”
Structural moat vs. the labs 4
Buyer psychology favors an independent third party: security teams won't trust the AI vendor to certify its own product, just as a car buyer wouldn't let the seller certify the car — creating a structural opening the labs can't fill.
thesis🗣 guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)conviction: highAnthropicOpenAIAI securityindependent vendor
So what: Conflict-of-interest dynamics structurally protect independent oversight vendors from lab encroachment — the core bull case for standalone players.
“you're not going to trust the vendor of a product to tell you that this product is not going to mess your environment. You're going to want to have an independent party” ▶ 33:33
Onyx has a data advantage labs lack: enterprises let Onyx access historical agent-behavior data but won't give that data to Anthropic or OpenAI, because they fear those data-hungry companies will train on it.
contrarian🗣 guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)conviction: highAnthropicOpenAIOnyx Securitydata privacy
So what: Data-trust positioning is a defensible wedge against labs; the labs' training incentive is itself the barrier that keeps them out of oversight data.
“enterprises today are not willing to have anthropic or open AI give that historical data because they know these are very data companies that will want to train on that data.” ▶ 35:35
A multi-vendor model world (open-source for cost, specialists for tasks) makes it unrealistic for any single lab to provide uniform security across all the models an enterprise uses, favoring a vendor-agnostic security layer.
thesis🗣 guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)conviction: highopen-source modelsmulti-modelAI security
So what: Heterogeneous, multi-vendor model adoption is a structural tailwind for neutral cross-model security; bearish for single-lab lock-in security.
“we're heading for a world where there's a multitude of different vendors... it is be going to be unrealistic to expect all the vendors to provide the same level of security” ▶ 36:12
Model mistakes split into two types: 'jagged intelligence' silly mistakes (which labs are incentivized to and will fix) and a fast-growing category of semi-aware, semi-conscious agent behavior that diverges from the user's intent and is hard even for large vendors to solve.
framework🗣 guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)conviction: highAI alignmentfrontier modelsOnyx Security
So what: Onyx deliberately targets the durable, hard alignment problem (intent divergence), not transient bugs labs will absorb — defines where lasting third-party value sits.
“more I would say have a independent uh, you would even say semi-aware or semi-conscious h perspective on what should happen and that perspective might not always align with your perspective.”
Mythos: cheap vulnerability discovery & controlled rollout 4
The plummeting cost of AI-assisted vulnerability finding ('Mythos'-level automated vulnerability research) is the top fear of CISOs and engineering leaders, has already compromised vendors, and the market is not overreacting to it.
thesis🗣 host and guestconviction: highMythosvulnerability researchCISOAI security
So what: Automated vuln discovery is a step-change attack-surface risk; expect rising spend on patching, mitigating controls and AI-native defense across the security sector.
“automated vulnerability research looked like a a dream that would take 20 50 years to to happen... and suddenly it's coming all at once” ▶ 25:51
The pragmatic security response to Mythos is two-tier: fast quick-fixes (patching/mitigating controls) for immediate risk, then foundational controls (locked-down identity, firewall, endpoint detection) — and Onyx aims to be the foundational layer for the AI attack surface specifically.
framework🗣 guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)conviction: highOnyx SecuritypatchingidentityfirewallEDR
So what: Positions Onyx as a foundational 'must-have' (like EDR/firewall) for AI, not a point tool — a category-creation framing investors should track for durability.
