“for the AI attack surface that you now have... you also need a foundational security and that's kind of the role we play in that space.” ▶ 27:22
On Anthropic and OpenAI's phased/controlled rollouts of frontier offensive-capability models ('glass swing' and 'daybreak'), broad gradual access is preferable because a Chinese model reaching Mythos-level first would leave undefended companies with no recourse.
prediction🗣 guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)conviction: mediumAnthropicOpenAIChinese AI modelsMythos
So what: Geopolitical race dynamics make offensive-capability proliferation inevitable; investors should assume the defensive-tooling clock is already running.
“Now they're all vulnerable because you know there's a Chinese model that's mythus level and there's nothing they can do about it.” ▶ 28:38
Advice to all enterprises: assume Mythos-level vulnerability-finding models are coming regardless of rollout policy, and the only present move is investing in foundational controls that stop the downstream effects.
prediction🗣 guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)conviction: highMythosenterprise security
So what: Frames foundational AI-security spend as inevitable preparation — a demand-pull narrative supporting the whole defensive AI-security category.
“I would advise everyone to assume that these models are coming anyway. The only thing you can do right now is to invest in these foundational controls” ▶ 28:58
Enterprise risk posture & vendor concentration 4
Outright bans on AI tools have largely disappeared; even regulated financial firms now allow agents but restrict to a narrower, more granular set of approved tools.
data_point🗣 guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)conviction: highfinancial sectorenterprise AI adoption
So what: The 'ban it until safe' holdout cohort has collapsed — adoption is now universal and gated only by tool selection, expanding the addressable security market.
“Yeah, I hardly see it anymore.” ▶ 29:34
Enterprises that allow many different AI tools will do better than those betting on one vendor, because the model leadership landscape shifts fast — last year OpenAI was the safe bet, now Anthropic has better models and tools, and it could flip again next year.
contrarian🗣 guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)conviction: highOpenAIAnthropicvendor risk
So what: Warns against single-vendor lock-in; supports multi-model strategies and, by extension, cross-vendor security tooling — and flags model-leadership volatility as an investing risk.
“If you bet on OpenAI a year ago, that would have been the safest bet in the world, but suddenly Antropic has much better models and better tools” ▶ 29:59
Risk posture should scale with size: startups should let agents do everything (everything to gain, nothing to lose), while a JP Morgan with much to lose can rationally adopt AI more slowly and granularly — though JP Morgan is in fact adopting AI very quickly.
framework🗣 guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)conviction: mediumJP Morganenterprise AI adoption
So what: A reusable lens on differentiated adoption pace by firm size; the JP Morgan datapoint signals even the most conservative incumbents are moving fast.
“Where you're large, where JP Morgan, you have so much to lose... And by the way, I you know JP Morgan is adopting AI very quickly.” ▶ 30:28
Onyx's product roadmap rests on a bet that the two core primitives of 2026 AI — LLM foundation models and the LLM-decides-tool-calls agent pattern — have not changed in years, letting it stay lean while covering many applications, with a deliberately open mind on 2027 (AGI/ASI).
thesis🗣 guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)conviction: mediumLLMsAI agentsAGIASIOnyx Security
So what: The bull case depends on primitive stability through 2026; a new LLM or agent paradigm in 2027 is the stated key risk to the whole thesis.
“the two core pillars of how 2026 AI works have not changed in the last few years... for 2027, we're very open-minded” ▶ 31:19
Market size, interpretability bet & Israeli ecosystem 5
Bar Kogan sizes AI oversight/control as a $100B+ opening anchored to a future of $10-trillion AI companies, and frames Onyx's ambition as controlling advanced AI long-term, not merely enterprise security.
prediction🗣 guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)conviction: highOnyx SecurityAI governanceTAM
So what: A $100B+ TAM framing for AI control/oversight is the headline investable thesis for the AI-safety-as-a-business category.
“we think that's an opening that's you know a hundred billion plus opening for a really important company.” ▶ 20:56
Onyx is betting that mechanistic interpretability — understanding internal weights, activations and the mathematical structure of models — will be at least part of the long-term solution, and that models smarter than humans will be needed to crack interpretability itself.
thesis🗣 guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)conviction: mediummechanistic interpretabilityAI alignmentfrontier models
So what: A contested research bet (the industry is 'quite divided') — high upside if interpretability matures, but technical risk to the long-term moat if it doesn't.
“as we're starting to have models that are much smarter than us... we think that uh we'll be able to start cracking mechanistic capability much more effectively.” ▶ 22:45
A sub-100-person, 2-year-old startup is winning inbound from Fortune 10-20 companies purely because the pain is acute and daily, and security buyers (in the business of revenue preservation) prefer to back a promising early horse over doing nothing.
anecdote🗣 guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)conviction: highFortune 100Onyx Securityenterprise sales
So what: Acute-pain inbound from the largest enterprises is strong product-market-fit evidence for early-stage AI-security startups, compressing normal enterprise sales cycles.
“their pain is so strong that they're going to say... it's a problem that I have daily, so I'll give them a call.” ▶ 24:21
Israel is emerging as a credible frontier-AI and model-training hub (world models, AI infrastructure, chips), and Onyx's edge is a rare cyber-plus-AI DNA drawn from an Israeli intelligence unit working at the math/cyber intersection — co-founder Gil came from synthetic data and Nvidia.
thesis🗣 guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)conviction: mediumIsraelNvidia8200WizArmissynthetic data
So what: Talent-geography signal: Israel's adversarial/security bench is now training frontier models, a sourcing edge worth tracking for AI-security investing.
“one of the deepest adversarial thinking benches in the world is is the Israeli ecosystem 8200 whiz Armis” ▶ 37:07
Onyx is designing its product for an end-user base that will shift from humans to AI agents — optimizing today for human UX (not overwhelming with irrelevant info) while investing now in being convenient and token-efficient for agents to consume.
prediction🗣 guest (Maxim Bar Kogan)conviction: mediumAI agentssecurity teamsproduct design
So what: Forward signal that B2B software buyers/users will increasingly be agents, reshaping product design toward token-efficiency — a second-order theme across SaaS.
“as that audience becomes more agents than humans it will be important for us to evolve and to make it work really well for agents doing the work” ▶ 40:11

The Meb Faber Show 2 episode(s) · 68 insights

Most Investors Will Never Beat The Market53 min31 insights2026-06-05
▶ Watch on YouTube
Investing legend Charlie Ellis lays out a disciplined index-and-leave-it-alone playbook: most individuals destroy ~2% of annual return through trading mistakes, fees and taxes, and should hold low-cost diversified index funds for a 60-year horizon. He also recounts the structural history of US share buybacks and the asymmetric, low-cost 'great American investments' (Louisiana Purchase, Alaska, internet) that compounded into national wealth.
Index-and-leave-it-alone playbook · 5The arithmetic of return erosion · 3Behavioral overconfidence as the core risk · 3Share buybacks: history and corporate capital allocation · 5Social Security and retirement-claiming strategy · 3AI as the next great American investment · 3Asymmetric national investments as a framework · 3American equity culture and structural advantage · 3Concentration, sentiment and Buffett · 3
Index-and-leave-it-alone playbook 5
Charlie Ellis argues most investors should hold a low-cost diversified index fund because they don't know enough to pick individual stocks, and the fee drag on compounding is minimal.
thesis🗣 Charlie Ellisconviction: highCharlie EllisVanguardindex funds
So what: Reinforces the passive-allocation core for a long-horizon portfolio; active stock-picking is treated as a value-destroying option for the typical investor.
“you don't know enough to know which individual stocks to own, so you ought to do a diversified portfolio.”
Ellis frames a 25-year-old's investing horizon as 60 years (to age 85), and longer still if compounding for heirs, arguing the long horizon is what makes equity compounding decisive.
framework🗣 Charlie Ellisconviction: highCharlie MungerWarren BuffettDavid Swensen
So what: Anchors asset allocation to multi-decade horizons, favoring maximal equity exposure and discouraging horizon-shortening behavior.
“60 years is your long-term.”
Ellis's three rules for long-term success are: avoid the temptation to trade/improve, minimize taxes and fees, and leave the portfolio alone to compound.
framework🗣 Charlie Ellisconviction: highindex funds401k
So what: A simple operating discipline: behavioral restraint plus cost/tax minimization is treated as the dominant driver of realized returns.
“avoiding temptation to help and improve and make things work out better is the first and biggest secret about long-term investing success.”
Ellis advises avoiding taxes by simply holding rather than buying and selling, and sheltering equity exposure inside a 401k.
framework🗣 Charlie Ellisconviction: high401kindex funds
So what: Tax-deferred wrappers plus near-zero turnover protect the compounding rate; turnover is framed as a hidden tax drag.
“Avoid taxes by just hanging on. Don't buy and sell, just hang on.”
Ellis recommends Daniel Kahneman's 'Thinking, Fast and Slow' as the single best book for investors, tying investing success to understanding behavioral bias.
framework🗣 Charlie Ellisconviction: highDaniel KahnemanThinking Fast and Slowbehavioral economics
So what: Signals that the binding constraint on investor returns is psychology, not information or skill.
“I think it's the best book for investors ever written.”
The arithmetic of return erosion 3
Ellis claims the average investor loses about 2% of their return in an average year through mistakes (behavioral errors and mistimed trades).
data_point🗣 Charlie Ellisconviction: highindex funds
So what: Quantifies the behavioral cost; an investor who simply indexes and holds recaptures roughly 2%/year of return.
“The average investor in an average year loses 2% of their return.”
Ellis walks through a return-erosion stack: a 7% nominal expectation minus ~3% inflation leaves 4% real, minus 2% from mistakes leaves 2%, minus ~1% for an active manager leaves 'damn near nothing.'
framework🗣 Charlie Ellisconviction: highactive managementinflation
So what: Makes the cost of active management and trading vivid in real terms; the same fees that look small consume most of the real return.
“if I pay a active manager to run my portfolio, that's going to be another percent. I'm coming up with damn near nothing.”
Ellis uses ~7% nominal as a reasonable (if slightly optimistic) long-term equity return assumption and ~3% as a high-end inflation expectation.
data_point🗣 Charlie Ellisconviction: mediumequitiesinflation
So what: Provides a baseline real-return assumption (~4%) for long-term planning before costs.
“7% seems like maybe a little optimistic, but seems like a reasonable prospect for the longer term.”
Behavioral overconfidence as the core risk 3
Ellis cites that 80-90% of people rate themselves above average at various skills, with young males trading stocks among the most overconfident, leading them to act on misunderstood reality.
causal🗣 Charlie Ellisconviction: highbehavioral economicsretail traders
So what: Overconfidence concentrated in young male traders is a structural reason to expect retail underperformance and to fade crowd activity.
“usually it runs somewhere around 80, 85, even 90% in some cases, such as young males at trading stocks.”
Ellis argues investor overconfidence and mistakes are more pronounced in underdeveloped markets than in sophisticated ones like the US, UK, Canada and Germany.
thesis🗣 Charlie Ellisconviction: mediumemerging marketsUnited StatesUnited KingdomCanadaGermany
So what: Implies more behavioral mispricing (and potential active edge) in less-developed markets than in mature ones.
Ellis notes humans repeat the same speculative mistakes across centuries, citing the panic of 1720 as evidence that bubble behavior is durable and recurring.
thesis🗣 Charlie Ellisconviction: highpanic of 1720speculationWilliam Goetzmann
So what: Frames bubbles as a permanent feature; useful as a contrarian reminder near all-time highs.
“these humans keep doing the same dumb stuff over and over again”
Share buybacks: history and corporate capital allocation 5
Ellis says US share repurchases now exceed $1 trillion annually and have become a normal, board-driven part of sensible corporate management.
data_point🗣 Charlie Ellisconviction: highshare buybackscorporate finance
So what: Buybacks are a structural, durable demand source for equities, not an aberration; political claims they were recently illegal are factually wrong.
“Share repurchasing goes over a trillion dollars today.”
Ellis prefers companies return cash via buybacks rather than dividends because share-value accretion is more tax-efficient than dividend payouts over the long run.
thesis🗣 Charlie Ellisconviction: highshare buybacksdividendstaxes
So what: Favors buyback-heavy capital-return companies for taxable long-term holders; challenges the dividend-as-free-money mindset.
“I would rather people did more share buybacks than less dividends.”
Ellis pioneered the modern buyback thesis ~50 years ago, arguing post-WWII US firms had de-levered so much (1946-1965) that they were under-leveraged and should borrow to repurchase shares to restore an optimal balance sheet for equity returns.
framework🗣 Charlie Ellisconviction: highshare buybacksGoldman Sachscorporate leverage
So what: Capital-structure optimization (releveraging via buybacks) is the original rationale; relevant for screening firms with lazy balance sheets.
“You really ought to borrow money and repurchase shares to get her back to the right balance, to get long-term returns for investors.”
Ellis observes buyback culture is now spreading internationally to the UK, Japan, Korea and China, and that buybacks on average now outpace dividends in most years.
data_point🗣 Charlie Ellisconviction: mediumshare buybacksdividendsUnited KingdomJapanKoreaChina
So what: Rising buyback adoption abroad is a potential tailwind for international equity total returns and a shareholder-friendliness signal.
Ellis says some institutions and endowments still wrongly value dividend payouts more than equivalent stock-value accretion, which he calls inferior after tax over the long run.
contrarian🗣 Charlie Ellisconviction: mediumendowmentsdividendstaxes
So what: Identifies a persistent institutional bias toward income that long-term, tax-aware allocators can avoid.
Social Security and retirement-claiming strategy 3
Ellis says delaying Social Security to age 70.5 instead of claiming at 62 raises lifetime, inflation-adjusted benefits by roughly two-thirds (he cites a 76% increase).
data_point🗣 Charlie Ellisconviction: highSocial Securityretirement planning
So what: Delaying Social Security is a high-return, inflation-protected 'investment' that few capture; major lever for retirement income planning.
“the magnitude of increase in the benefits ... increases by 2/3.”
Ellis argues working longer lets people keep contributing to (rather than drawing down) their 401k while compounding both balances and the delayed Social Security benefit, materially improving retirement outcomes.
framework🗣 Charlie Ellisconviction: high401kSocial Securityretirement planning
So what: Combines longer work, continued tax-deferred contributions and delayed claiming as a stacked strategy versus retiring at 65 and claiming at 62.
Ellis notes Social Security was deliberately designed by FDR as a self-liquidating, self-funding program to insulate it from political reversal via general-revenue dependence.
anecdote🗣 Charlie Ellisconviction: highSocial SecurityFranklin Roosevelt
So what: Explains the program's durability; structural funding design shaped its political resilience.
AI as the next great American investment 3
Ellis names artificial intelligence as the most likely modern 'great American investment,' capable of transforming the economy but disproportionately benefiting the top 0.05% positioned at the top of the wave.
prediction🗣 Charlie Ellisconviction: mediumartificial intelligencelabor market
So what: Flags AI gains as highly concentrated; favors owning the capital/winners rather than expecting broad-based benefit.
“It also could be unbelievably important for the top one half of 1/10 of 1% who happen to be right on top of the whole wave.”
Ellis warns that AI-driven job displacement is more likely to happen faster than expected than slower, especially for entry-level employment.
prediction🗣 Charlie Ellisconviction: mediumartificial intelligencelabor marketentry-level employment
So what: Faster-than-consensus labor disruption is a macro risk for consumer/labor-sensitive sectors and a policy catalyst to watch.
“my fear, personally, is it's more likely to happen faster than expected rather than slower than projected.”
Ellis cites the internet as a government investment that cost under $1 million to develop yet generated civilian value vastly exceeding its military origins.
data_point🗣 Charlie Ellisconviction: mediuminternetgovernment R&D
So what: Illustrates the extreme convexity of foundational tech R&D; analogous asymmetric payoff framing applied to AI.
“It cost less than a million dollars to do all of the work that led to the internet.”
Asymmetric national investments as a framework 3
Ellis frames the Louisiana Purchase as a near-zero-money-down deal structured like a long-term margin loan, with credit not cashed for a dozen years, making it a spectacularly cheap leveraged real-estate acquisition.
framework🗣 Charlie Ellisconviction: highLouisiana PurchaseThomas JeffersonNapoleon
So what: Illustrates that financing structure and a motivated seller can make an asset wildly attractive regardless of headline price.
“basically, it was like a long-term margin loan. We didn't put up any money.”
Ellis describes the Alaska purchase ('Seward's icebox') as an even better bargain than Louisiana, bought cheaply from a motivated Tsar and later yielding oil, gold and fisheries windfalls.
anecdote🗣 Charlie Ellisconviction: highAlaska PurchaseWilliam Seward
So what: Case study in buying optionality cheap from a forced/motivated seller; value realized decades later via unforeseen resources.
“buying Alaska for a song”
Ellis notes national park land (e.g., Yellowstone) was cheap precisely because it was unsuitable for ranching or housing, making the public acquisition low-cost.
anecdote🗣 Charlie Ellisconviction: mediumnational parksYellowstone
So what: Reinforces the theme that the great American investments succeeded partly because acquisition cost was minimal.
“the land for the national parks, in most cases, was dirt cheap.”
American equity culture and structural advantage 3
Ellis argues the US leads the world in both entrepreneurship and the share of households that are equity owners, reflecting a distinctive risk-taking culture versus Europe (real estate/interest) and Japan (decades of flat stocks).
thesis🗣 hostconviction: highUnited StatesEuropeJapanequities
So what: Structural equity-ownership culture supports a persistent US equity-risk-premium argument and demand base.
“there's something in the American spirit that is the risk-taking gene.”
Ellis frames the founding of America as substantially driven by for-profit investors (Virginia Company, Dutch and Sweden companies, Hudson's Bay), not solely religious refugees.
framework🗣 hostconviction: mediumVirginia CompanyHudson's Bay Company
So what: Reframes US economic exceptionalism as rooted in a centuries-old investor/risk-capital DNA.
Ellis identifies universities as the most important engines of US economic progress through science and engineering research, citing Yale's need-blind admissions where ~40% of students attend on full scholarship.
thesis🗣 Charlie Ellisconviction: highYale UniversityuniversitiesR&D
So what: University research is framed as a core driver of long-run US productivity and innovation, underpinning equity returns.
“Universities are also largely the most important engines of economic progress because of the research that's done in science”
Concentration, sentiment and Buffett 3
Ellis recounts putting his firm's entire reserve fund into Berkshire Hathaway at ~$700/share in the early 1970s after Sandy Gottesman called it his 'forever' holding; it rose roughly 100x over 50 years.
anecdote🗣 Charlie Ellisconviction: highBerkshire HathawayWarren BuffettSandy GottesmanCharlie Munger
So what: A concentrated bet on a disciplined long-term compounder can dwarf diversified returns; the 'forever' holding period was central to the outcome.
“We're going to put it all in Berkshire Hathaway.”
Ellis flags a divergence between booming hard data (all-time-high markets, low unemployment, moderate inflation) and miserable consumer sentiment (e.g., University of Michigan survey), attributing it to phones, social media and political polarization.
causal🗣 hostconviction: mediumUniversity of Michigan sentimentsocial mediaconsumer sentiment
So what: Sentiment-vs-fundamentals divergence is a signal that soft data may be distorted by media/polarization rather than economic reality.
Ellis treats the persistent gap between wealthy and middle-class income shares as a real long-run structural problem for the US economy, distinct from short-term market strength.
thesis🗣 Charlie Ellisconviction: mediumincome inequalitymiddle class
So what: Inequality is flagged as a durable macro/political risk that could shape future policy and consumer demand.
“straightforward middle America is not earning as large a fraction of the pie would be right for the long long run”
The AI Bubble Might Be Exactly What We Need (William Goetzmann Explains)50 min37 insights2026-05-29
▶ Watch on YouTube
Yale finance historian William Goetzmann argues that today's AI/SpaceX-style tech mania is a normal, even productive, repeat of historical innovation bubbles (1720, dot-com): most booms do NOT end in catastrophic crashes, the corporate sector reliably adapts to shocks, and the bigger risk for investors is sitting in cash rather than staying in diversified global equities for the long run.
Bubble base rates and crash anxiety · 6AI / SpaceX mania as historical innovation cycle · 7Long-term equity and compounding as the base case · 4Portfolio construction by purpose and diversification · 7Behavioral finance, sentiment and volatility signals · 4Bubble taxonomy and historical analogs · 3Policy, structure and the long view · 3Finance as time-and-risk technology · 3
Bubble base rates and crash anxiety 6
After a stock market doubles in a single year (inflation-adjusted, across all world markets historically), it is roughly a 50/50 coin flip whether it doubles again versus gives back those gains the next year — not a reliable crash signal.
data_point🗣 William Goetzmann (guest, Yale finance professor)conviction: highstock marketsbubbles
So what: A one-year double is not by itself a reason to sell; investors front-running an assumed crash give up symmetric upside.
“it's a 50/50 shot whether you go up again the same amount or more or down again” ▶ 30:46
If an investor holds through 3-5 years after a market doubles, history shows they are more likely than not to be further ahead, so patience rather than profit-taking is the higher-expected-value move.
framework🗣 William Goetzmann (guest)conviction: highequity markets
So what: Argues against tactical exits after big run-ups; time horizon of 3-5 years tilts odds strongly toward equity holders.
“if you're willing to wait for 3 to 5 years, you're more than likely going to be continued to gain” ▶ 31:14
The scenario investors fear most — a market doubling and then losing all those gains — is statistically extremely rare, well under a 1% historical probability.
data_point🗣 William Goetzmann (guest)conviction: highbubblesequity markets
So what: Quantifies that catastrophic round-trips are tail events, so positioning entire portfolios around them is likely a costly mistake.
“doubling and then losing your profits, that's like really, really, really rare. It's not even a 1% chance” ▶ 31:39
True historical bubbles are so rare that an investor would struggle to name even eight of them — they are emotionally over-weighted precisely because they are scarce cautionary tales, not common events.
contrarian🗣 William Goetzmann (guest)conviction: hightech bubble1929 crash1720 crashtulip mania
So what: Counters media narrative that bubbles are everywhere; availability bias makes investors overestimate crash risk.
“I bet if you think really hard, you're not going to be able to think of eight bubbles in history” ▶ 31:58
A big boom is not always followed by a big crash; sometimes bubbles are simply 'right' and the elevated prices are validated by the technology delivering.
contrarian🗣 William Goetzmann (guest)conviction: highbubbles
So what: Reframes the boom-equals-impending-crash reflex; an AI boom could be a justified repricing rather than a setup for collapse.
“sometimes the bubbles are right. A big boom is not always followed by a big crash” ▶ 29:43
The most underappreciated risk is opportunity cost: keeping money on the sidelines guarantees zero real return, which is a more reliable wealth destroyer than the rare bubble crash.
thesis🗣 William Goetzmann (guest)conviction: highcashequity markets
So what: Frames cash-hoarding as the default loss; bias toward being invested over timing exits.
“if you just keep all your money on the sidelines, your money's not going to earn any money” ▶ 32:48
AI / SpaceX mania as historical innovation cycle 7
The 1720 London bubble was an explosion of venture-capital-style issuance funding speculative technologies (steam engines, flying machines), directly analogous to the dot-com and current AI/tech cycle — same playbook, larger scale (trillions vs millions).
framework🗣 William Goetzmann (guest)conviction: high1720 bubblesteam enginedot-comventure capitalLondon
So what: Gives a template for reading the AI cycle: expect both transformative winners and total busts within the same speculative wave.
“there was just an explosion of let's say venture capital issuance that some of the ideas like we've experienced in the dot period some of these things really transformed the British economy and then some of them were just a total bust” ▶ 21:20
Just as the steam engine looked absurd in 1720 but became transformative once mounted on a locomotive, today's speculative tech can look 'nutty' yet still reshape the economy — the winners come from non-obvious cross-disciplinary recombinations.
framework🗣 William Goetzmann (guest)conviction: highsteam enginerailroadstechnology
So what: Encourages tolerating apparent overvaluation of platform technologies; the locomotive-style application often arrives later than skeptics expect.
A $5 trillion SpaceX as the largest stock in the world would be 'surprising and a little nutty' but cannot be dismissed as impossible — markets have always been a little crazy and such valuations are within historical norms of mania.
prediction🗣 William Goetzmann (guest)conviction: mediumSpaceXElon Musk
So what: Don't reflexively short or dismiss extreme private-tech valuations; history shows extreme outcomes recur.
“I would say that it would be surprising and a little nutty, but I don't I wouldn't say if you know anything about markets and history is it's it's always been a little crazy” ▶ 25:50
SpaceX's valuation rests partly on US government contracts, and a dominant first-mover private firm in space could be exceptionally profitable as warfare and communication rules transform — satellite tech already proved decisive in the Ukraine war.
thesis🗣 William Goetzmann (guest)conviction: mediumSpaceXUS governmentsatellitesUkraine wardefense
So what: Space/defense-communication is a strategic-competition theme where private first-mover dominance may justify rich valuations; watch government revenue exposure.
“having a uh first mover or dominant position as a private firm might be incredibly profitable, valuable going forward in this period where we're in a transformation of rules of engagement in warfare” ▶ 26:50
Technological investment manias are ultimately driven by long-incubated cultural dreams (e.g., decades of science fiction from Asimov), not by incremental cost-cutting — money chases pre-existing aspirational narratives.
framework🗣 William Goetzmann (guest)conviction: mediumscience fictionIsaac AsimovElon Musktechnology
So what: Narrative-driven themes (Mars, AI) attract capital because the dream predates the product; useful lens for spotting where speculative money will flow.
“if Elon Musk has a dream of putting people on the moon, it's because Isaac Azimoff has prodded us back in the middle of the 20th century to think about doing this stuff” ▶ 25:05
The Mars-colony milestone (1 million people) attached to Musk's SpaceX compensation echoes 1720s speculation on the future value of Louisiana — promoters still monetize far-future promises using essentially the same playbook centuries apart.
anecdote🗣 host (Meb Faber)conviction: mediumSpaceXElon MuskMarsLouisiana1720 bubble
So what: Milestone-based equity awards on unproven far-future goals are a recurring promoter structure investors should price skeptically.
After the 1720 bubbles burst, capital markets needed roughly a century before speculative energy rebuilt enough to fund good ideas again — bursting a tech bubble can freeze risk-capital appetite for a very long time.
causal🗣 William Goetzmann (guest)conviction: medium1720 bubblecapital marketsventure capital
So what: A severe AI bust could durably impair risk-capital formation, not just reset prices — a long-tail second-order risk for innovation funding.
“Had to wait around for another century before the energy got sufficiently pumped up again to be able to go back to the capital markets for good ideas” ▶ 22:34
Long-term equity and compounding as the base case 4
A globally diversified equity portfolio held passively for years to decades has historically delivered real returns above inflation across many countries, making 'stay invested and forget it' the rational default for long-horizon savers.
thesis🗣 William Goetzmann (guest)conviction: highglobal equitiesinflation
So what: Reinforces buy-and-hold global diversification as the baseline against which any market-timing decision must be justified.
“as long as you're in the market, you're probably over the long term going to be have made some money in and above inflation” ▶ 28:27
Investing in 20th-century stock markets paid off well over ~125 years despite world wars and capitalist/anti-capitalist schisms, evidence that the corporate sector structurally adapts to even catastrophic shocks.
data_point🗣 William Goetzmann (guest)conviction: high20th centurystock marketscorporate sector
So what: Macro/geopolitical fear is a weak reason to abandon equities; corporate adaptability is the durable edge owners capture.
“if you were able to maintain a stock portfolio you did pretty well over the last 125 years” ▶ 44:20
Owning shares gives investors direct exposure to a decentralized 'invisible hand' of thousands of companies independently solving crises (COVID supply-chain breakdown, office/real-estate disruption), which is why equity markets recovered from those shocks.
framework🗣 William Goetzmann (guest)conviction: highCOVIDsupply chainsreal estateequities
So what: Frames equity ownership as a bet on aggregate corporate problem-solving; supports holding through crises rather than fleeing.
“you'll be connected to it directly if you own shares in the company that's responding to these” ▶ 45:11
Goetzmann's own most consequential investment was a passive decision decades ago to put his entire 401k/403b into the stock market and forget it, which compounded into a life-changing sum.
anecdote🗣 William Goetzmann (guest)conviction: high401k403bstock market
So what: Anecdotal endorsement of default-equity retirement allocation and the power of inattention plus compounding.
“I decided just to put it all in the stock market, then um 30 years later, lo and behold, it's done quite well” ▶ 47:54
Portfolio construction by purpose and diversification 7
Every allocation should be matched to an explicit time-horizon purpose: money needed soon (e.g., college tuition in 3 years) belongs in money-market/short-rate-insulated assets, not in volatile names like Nvidia.
framework🗣 William Goetzmann (guest)conviction: highNvidiamoney marketbonds
So what: Liability-matching framework: near-term cash needs dictate safe assets regardless of bullishness on equities.
“I can't just put all my money in Nvidia. I got to be prepared to weather a storm in the stock market because I need some cash to make those tuition payments” ▶ 35:02
Stock markets can endure rocky stretches of two-to-three years, so any capital needed within that window should sit in assets insulated from equity and long-term-bond rate shocks.
framework🗣 William Goetzmann (guest)conviction: highequitieslong-term bondsinterest rates
So what: Defines the practical danger zone (2-3 year drawdowns) for sequencing near-term liabilities into safe assets.
US-only investors are overexposed; the US does not always lead — in 2005 global equities materially outperformed US stocks — so periodic checks of global diversification via funds/ETFs are warranted.
contrarian🗣 William Goetzmann (guest)conviction: mediumUS equitiesglobal equitiesETFs
So what: Concrete historical counterexample to US exceptionalism complacency after a long US outperformance run; revisit international weight.
“2005, great year for the US stock market, way better year for the global equity markets” ▶ 36:30
The degree to which the US market co-moves with foreign markets is time-varying — sometimes highly correlated, sometimes not — so the diversification benefit of international stocks is conditional and must be reassessed, not assumed constant.
framework🗣 William Goetzmann (guest)conviction: mediumUS marketinternational marketscorrelation
So what: Diversification math shifts with regime; don't treat international allocation as a fixed free lunch.
Inflation and currency debasement are the investor's most persistent adversary over long arcs of history — currencies come and go, so capital must be deployed into something that preserves purchasing power.
thesis🗣 host (Meb Faber)conviction: highinflationcurrenciespurchasing power
So what: Frames the real long-run enemy as purchasing-power erosion, reinforcing the case for real assets/equities over idle cash.
“our seemingly biggest foe over time is often the purchasing power where you have to do something with your money or else it gets diluted” ▶ 33:36
Individual bonds (unlike rare multi-century perpetual bonds) mature, so a bond or money-market sleeve requires an explicit reinvestment/refresh plan rather than buy-and-forget.
framework🗣 William Goetzmann (guest)conviction: mediumbondsmoney market funds
So what: Reminds fixed-income holders that maturity-driven reinvestment risk requires active rollover planning.
Concentration can dominate a portfolio by accident: a single 'good stock' suggestion (Apple) compounded into a huge chunk of Goetzmann's diversified portfolio over decades, illustrating how winners silently overweight themselves.
anecdote🗣 William Goetzmann (guest)conviction: mediumApple
So what: Highlights that letting winners run produces large unintended concentration — a rebalancing-vs-let-it-ride tradeoff to monitor.
“now fast forward some decades. Apple's a huge chunk of the portfolio”
Behavioral finance, sentiment and volatility signals 4
Goetzmann's Yale center runs Robert Shiller's monthly investor-confidence survey (25+ years) and now uses machine-learning language models to separate the emotional (fear, anxiety, joy, disgust, happiness) versus rational drivers of investors' crash beliefs.
framework🗣 William Goetzmann (guest)conviction: highRobert ShillerYalemachine learningsentiment surveysbehavioral finance
So what: Points to a sentiment dataset/methodology for quantifying emotion-driven mispricing — an alpha source if emotional crash-fear diverges from fundamentals.
“we can parse out with language models the fear, the anxiety, the joy, the disgust, and the happiness” ▶ 41:02
Trimming equity exposure when the VIX roughly doubles (from ~18-20 to 30-40 during the Persian Gulf war) is a rational, not emotional, response — higher implied volatility genuinely means more risk for the same position.
causal🗣 William Goetzmann (guest)conviction: mediumVIXPersian Gulf waroptions market
So what: Distinguishes rule-based de-risking on VIX spikes from panic; the options market's volatility signal can justify reducing exposure.
“during the war, the VIX went way up to something like over 30 or 40, which means if the standard deviation that you're expecting doubles, it means you're taking more risk just by sitting in the market” ▶ 39:35
Unrelated emotional priming (e.g., hearing a story about an armed burglar) measurably raises people's stated probability of a stock market crash, proving sentiment can drive crash fear independent of any market signal like the VIX.
causal🗣 William Goetzmann (guest)conviction: highbehavioral financesentimentVIX
So what: Crash-fear is partly noise from ambient anxiety; contrarians can fade sentiment-driven selling not backed by real volatility.
“if I've told you a story about a burglar, is more likely to make people think there's going to be a stock market crash. It's not because VIX went up” ▶ 40:36
The lottery-like dream of a 10,000x transformative winner is innate to human psychology and is what reliably pushes people to take more risk than safety alone would warrant — the engine behind speculative manias.
framework🗣 William Goetzmann (guest)conviction: mediumbubblesbehavioral finance
So what: Explains the persistent demand for moonshot/lottery-payoff assets; durable structural bid under speculative tech.
“it's the kind of thing that's always gotten humans to take a little bit more risk than they should have if they just wanted to stay safe” ▶ 29:16
Bubble taxonomy and historical analogs 3
Goetzmann built an index of NFT prices and found the COVID-era NFT mania was the single biggest financial bubble in history, surpassing even Dutch tulip mania (1630s-40s) on the magnitude of price ascent from near-zero to millions.
data_point🗣 William Goetzmann (guest)conviction: highNFTstulip maniaCOVID
So what: Establishes a modern record-setting bubble benchmark for calibrating how extreme current digital/tech valuations could get.
“the NFT bubble just beat out the tulip mania bubble” ▶ 18:51
Bubbles arise specifically in markets where abstract claims (shares, bonds, promises) are traded on beliefs and expectations about the future, not in markets for physical goods — financialization of expectations is the precondition for manias.
framework🗣 William Goetzmann (guest)conviction: highfinancial marketsAmsterdamAntwerpLondon
So what: Identifies where bubble risk concentrates: highly financialized, expectation-driven assets (crypto, AI equities) versus tangible-goods markets.
“they happen in modern times at least in financial markets because people are trading on beliefs and expectations” ▶ 18:59
The 1720 Mississippi/Louisiana speculation in Paris produced roughly a 1,000% return over a year or two before collapsing — a concrete magnitude for how far a narrative-driven new-world bubble can run.
data_point🗣 William Goetzmann (guest)conviction: mediumLouisianaParis1720 bubble
So what: Historical yardstick for the upside-then-collapse arc of frontier/narrative bubbles relevant to speculative tech today.
Policy, structure and the long view 3
Goetzmann's pitch to policymakers is to expect the 21st century to rhyme with the war-torn, ideologically fractured 20th, yet trust that the corporate sector's demonstrated adaptability makes broad equity ownership the resilient long-term store of savings.
thesis🗣 William Goetzmann (guest)conviction: highcorporate sectorequitiespolicy
So what: Policy framing that geopolitical turmoil need not derail equity-based retirement savings; supports broadening equity access.
“I think that the 21st century might be a lot like the 20th century” ▶ 43:34
Forced/mandated long-term retirement saving like Australia's superannuation system is a powerful wealth-building mechanism, even though libertarian-leaning Americans resist the compulsory element.
thesis🗣 host (Meb Faber)conviction: mediumAustralian superannuationretirement
So what: Signals a policy direction (auto/forced retirement equity contributions) that channels structural flows into markets.
“I love the idea of some of the Australian superanuation funds, this kind of savings” ▶ 48:22
Markets continuously rotate out obsolete sectors (buggy whips, railroads) into new leaders, so index-level equity exposure self-renews — a reason long-term holders need not pick the next technology themselves.
framework🗣 host (Meb Faber)conviction: mediumequity indicesrailroads
So what: Supports broad-index over single-theme bets; the market culls losers and adds winners on the investor's behalf.
“none of us are putting most of our money in buggy whips and railroads anymore. The market evolves over time” ▶ 47:24
Finance as time-and-risk technology 3
The core technology of finance is contracting across time (moving value between present and future), with two prices embedded: interest for the use-value of deferred money and an additional charge for default risk — the basis for all stock and bond pricing.
framework🗣 William Goetzmann (guest)conviction: highinterest ratesriskstocksbonds
So what: Foundational decomposition: any security's price separates time-value from risk premium, useful for spotting mispriced risk.
“the basic technology of finance is time contracting through time” ▶ 9:46
Compound interest at credit-card-level rates (legal limit just under ~30%) grows to astronomical sums over long horizons — the same mechanism that turned an ancient Sumerian land-rent bill into billions of bushels of grain.
data_point🗣 William Goetzmann (guest)conviction: highcompound interestcredit cards
So what: Visceral reminder of compounding's non-linear power — destructive in debt, constructive in long-term equity holding.
“if you let your credit card bill compound for 80 years, if you were allowed to, you'd get up into a huge number” ▶ 6:51
Limited-liability, share-issuing, perpetual corporations existed by 1372 (the Bazacle mill company in Toulouse, paying dividends with a board and audited accounts, lasting until 1949 nationalization and re-privatized ~2010s) — the corporate form long predates 1600s Amsterdam.
data_point🗣 William Goetzmann (guest)conviction: highBazacle companyToulousejoint-stock companyAmsterdam
So what: Historical depth: durable dividend-paying corporate structures have survived 600+ years and multiple regime changes, underscoring corporate adaptability.
“it existed as an independent company from 1372 to 1949 when France nationalized it. But then about 10 years ago, it privatized it again” ▶ 14:04

Dwarkesh Podcast 1 episode(s) · 52 insights

What remains scarce after AGI? – Alex Imas and Phil Trammell76 min52 insights2026-06-04
▶ Watch on YouTube
Two AI economists (Imas of DeepMind/Chicago, Trammell of Epoch/Stanford) argue that whether labor share survives AGI hinges on (a) whether humans intrinsically value human-in-the-loop 'relational' goods and (b) whether demand for capital/compute keeps expanding via increasing variety rather than satiating. They contend negative-growth and 'messy middle' scenarios require improbable conditions, that capital ownership and indexing—not retraining—are the key levers for capturing AGI gains, and that whether AGI is monopolized (like social media) or commoditized (like electricity) decides where rents accrue.
What stays scarce after AGI — the relational sector · 5Labor share, capital share, and the Kaldor constancy · 5Increasing variety vs. satiation — the master variable · 5Elasticity of demand and the Jevons-paradox caveat · 2No white-collar bloodbath yet — current data · 4Why the recession / negative-growth scenario is improbable · 2The 'messy middle' and political economy of displacement · 4Taxation and redistribution mechanism design · 6Capital accumulation, satiation, and who ends up owning the future · 5Non-human preferences — AIs, firms, and von Neumann probes · 4Capturing AGI gains — indexing, housing, and developing countries · 6Commoditization vs. concentration — safety and political risk · 4
What stays scarce after AGI — the relational sector 5
After broad automation, value accrues to whatever stays scarce, and humans are naturally scarce, so the durable scarce category is the 'relational sector' — services/goods where a human being in the loop is itself part of the product's value.
framework🗣 Phil Trammellconviction: highrelational sectorAGIlabor share
So what: Investors hunting AGI-proof cash flows should look at businesses whose value depends on verified human involvement (live performance, in-person care, human-attested trust), not at commoditizable output.
≈ “What is scarce tells you where the value will accrue.” paraphrase
The relational thesis only holds if a human is 'not a horse' — i.e., humans must add value to the output itself (replacing them lowers output value), not merely be a substitutable input; if that intrinsic premium is weak or covers few sectors, labor share still collapses to zero.
causal🗣 Alex Imasconviction: mediumrelational sectorlabor share
So what: The bull case for human labor post-AGI rests on an empirical fact nobody has measured; treat 'humans stay valuable' theses as unproven optionality, not base case.
≈ “if you replace the human, the value of the output decreases” paraphrase
An incentive-compatible art-print experiment found human-made prints command a large premium over identical AI prints, and that premium collapses when supply rises from 1 to 500 units — while AI prints show no scarcity effect because AI output is already treated as a commodity.
data_point🗣 Alex Imasconviction: highAI-generated artwillingness to payscarcity premium
So what: Human premia are tied to perceived one-to-one connection and scarcity; scalable 'human-made' branding may not retain pricing power, while AI output is structurally a commodity with no scarcity rent.
“AI is already viewed as a commodity.” ▶ 17:49
The right reference class for relational jobs is not the ballerina but multi-task white-collar jobs (e.g., doctors) where most tasks are automatable except a human-delivered diagnosis/support task consumers will pay extra to keep human.
framework🗣 Alex Imasconviction: mediumdoctorstask-based modelrelational sector
So what: Screen service businesses by whether the residual human task carries willingness-to-pay; that residual, not the whole job, is the defensible margin.
We have essentially no data to identify which jobs are relational — there is no conjoint analysis of willingness-to-pay for keeping a human in the loop versus full automation — so all forecasts here are unanchored.
data_point🗣 Alex Imasconviction: highO*NETconjoint analysisconsumer demand elasticities
So what: Whoever first collects credible relational/elasticity data gains real edge; current macro-AI forecasts are speculation, so size positions accordingly.
≈ “I've been saying we need a Manhattan Project for data.” paraphrase
Labor share, capital share, and the Kaldor constancy 5
For centuries roughly 60% of the economy has been paid to labor as wages and 30-40% to capital, and this constancy held through the entire Industrial Revolution — a Kaldor fact that is surprising and central to whether AGI changes the split.
data_point🗣 Phil Trammellconviction: highlabor sharecapital shareKaldor facts
So what: Bets on a labor-share collapse are bets against centuries of empirical constancy; demand high evidence before pricing in a structural break.
≈ “For many hundreds of years, ~60% of the economy basically gets paid out to humans in wages” paraphrase ▶ 6:47
Claims that labor share has been falling for 20-30 years may be an accounting artifact — Atkinson's work shows that holding accounting methods constant, labor share hasn't fallen at all.
contrarian🗣 Alex Imasconviction: mediumlabor shareAtkinson
So what: The popular 'labor share is already declining' narrative may be measurement noise; don't extrapolate it into AGI scenarios.
≈ “if you keep the accounting constant over the years, labor share hasn't even fallen ever” paraphrase
On a network-adjusted basis (tracing the whole supply chain, not just the final step), nothing has yet been fully automated — US computer and electronic products have a stable network-adjusted capital share of only ~50%, because labor adds large value upstream.
data_point🗣 Phil Trammellconviction: highnetwork-adjusted factor sharescomputer and electronic productssupply chain
So what: Even highly 'automated' goods still embed ~50% labor up the chain; full capital-share=1 goods would be a genuine regime change worth watching for as a signal.
≈ “Computer and electronic products in the US have a very stable network-adjusted capital share of around 50%.” paraphrase
The coming qualitative shift is that some goods will have their network-adjusted capital share go to one (a fully automated supply chain with no point where a human is intrinsically wanted), but the net effect on aggregate capital share is ambiguous.
prediction🗣 Phil Trammellconviction: mediumnetwork-adjusted capital shareautomation
So what: Don't assume full automation of a sector mechanically lifts economy-wide capital share; satiation in those goods can offset it.
≈ “there will be at least some goods whose network-adjusted capital share goes to one” paraphrase
If fully automated 'everything else' satiates fast, quantity goes to infinity but marginal utility falls faster, so spending shifts to scarce human goods — meaning aggregate capital share could fall, not rise, after full automation.
causal🗣 Phil Trammellconviction: mediummarginal utilitysatiationcapital share
So what: Satiation dynamics, not just automation, drive where income lands; a sector being automated does not guarantee its owners capture more of the pie.
Increasing variety vs. satiation — the master variable 5
Whether labor share survives hinges on increasing variety: if new varieties of capital goods keep appearing fast enough that consumers never satiate, income keeps flowing to capital; if there is no comparable variety growth in human goods, labor share goes to zero.
framework🗣 Phil Trammellconviction: highincreasing varietysatiationlabor sharecapital
So what: The single most important question for AGI macro outcomes is the pace of new-variety creation in capital goods vs. satiation; track product-variety expansion as the leading indicator.
A 1400s Mongolian forecasting scarcity would have wrongly predicted satiation in 'horse-transport, yogurt and yurts' leaving all spending on singers — but variety expansion kept the singer share negligible; by analogy the speakers' central (uncertain) prediction is the human-intrinsic share stays small post-AGI.
thesis🗣 Phil Trammellconviction: mediumincreasing varietyhuman-intrinsic goods
So what: History favors variety expansion keeping human-only spending marginal; the base case is capital/automated goods dominate, with human-premium niches staying small.
≈ “the share spent on singers has stayed negligible” paraphrase
Chad Jones's result shows the share of the economy spent on transistors/computing has been decreasing even as transistor counts grew trillions-fold, because the marginal value of computation kept falling — 'every 18 months the value of computation halves.'
data_point🗣 Alex Imasconviction: highChad JonesMoore's lawtransistorscompute
So what: Historically compute spend shrank as a share; the AGI bet is precisely that this finally reverses — a regime change to watch in compute's share of GDP.
“every 18 months, the value of computation halves” ▶ 15:12
For the first time the historic pattern may be breaking: an H100 costs more to rent now than three years ago despite superior, more abundant compute, because smarter models raise the opportunity cost of compute — a sign demand for compute may not satiate.
data_point🗣 Alex Imasconviction: highH100Nvidiacomputeopportunity cost
So what: Rising GPU rental prices despite abundance is the concrete signal that compute demand is expanding faster than supply — bullish for compute owners and the case for rising capital share.
≈ “an H100 costs more to rent now than it did three years ago” paraphrase
Software is a good whose demand keeps rising as it gets cheaper, so the relevant question for compute is how many new uses are found per unit of compute — the 'ultimate question' for whether compute's share of the economy keeps climbing.
framework🗣 Alex Imasconviction: highsoftwarecomputeJevons paradox
So what: Monitor whether AI keeps unlocking new compute uses; sustained non-satiation is the structural bull thesis for compute and capital, satiation the bear thesis.
≈ “That is the ultimate question that we need to be looking at.” paraphrase
Elasticity of demand and the Jevons-paradox caveat 2
Jevons paradox (cheaper input raises total spend on it) only holds when demand is highly elastic; for inelastic goods like oil or insulin, making them cheap does not multiply consumption, so 'AI makes X cheap therefore we'll spend more on X' is often wrong.
framework🗣 Phil Trammellconviction: highJevons paradoxelasticity of demandoilinsulincoal
So what: Don't apply Jevons reflexively; the trade only works for elastic-demand sectors (software), making elasticity the screen for which 'AI-cheapens-it' bets actually grow revenue.
≈ “this only happens if the demand for something is highly elastic” paraphrase
Under the O-ring/O*NET task model, if AI automates 9 of 10 tasks and the product gets cheaper, sufficiently elastic demand can raise total volume enough to increase hiring of the remaining human task — which is why software-engineer demand has risen, not fallen.
causal🗣 Alex Imasconviction: mediumO-ring modelsoftware engineerselasticity of demand
So what: Automation can be net job-creating where demand is elastic; near-term, AI-exposed elastic sectors may see employment grow, not shrink.
No white-collar bloodbath yet — current data 4
Across the economy, including the most AI-exposed software engineering, there is currently no real evidence of mass AI-driven automation or unemployment — Yale's Budget Lab analysis shows you 'really have to squint to see anything happening.'
data_point🗣 Alex Imasconviction: highYale Budget Labsoftware engineeringunemployment
So what: The 'AI is already destroying jobs' narrative isn't yet in the macro data; fade trades premised on imminent labor collapse until the data turns.
≈ “you really have to squint to see anything happening” paraphrase
The only visible signal is junior developer hiring running modestly below trend (slower growth, not a level drop), while demand for senior software engineers has if anything increased.
data_point🗣 Alex Imasconviction: mediumjunior developerssenior software engineers
So what: AI exposure is hitting the bottom of the skill ladder first; the early labor signal is compressed entry-level hiring, not aggregate displacement.
“it's a bit below trend” ▶ 31:06
Reported AI layoffs may be coordination-driven theater: a narrative that not laying people off signals failure to adopt AI can cascade firms into layoffs that leave them worse off, just to be seen as 'using AI.'
contrarian🗣 Alex Imasconviction: mediumAI layoffsnarrativetoken counters
So what: Discount AI-layoff headlines as partly signaling/keeping-up-with-the-Joneses rather than genuine productivity displacement.
≈ “the firm might actually be worse off after the layoffs than before” paraphrase
Beyond the O-ring reliability barrier, demand for lawyers, accountants and engineers persists because buyers pay for accountability, ownership, regulatory backing and licensing — frictions that keep humans in the loop independent of capability.
causal🗣 Alex Imasconviction: mediumlawyersaccountantslicensingregulation
So what: Regulatory/liability moats, not skill, protect many professional jobs near-term — but the speakers view these as 'transitional,' so the moat erodes as institutions adapt.
≈ “There's a lot of regulatory layers that are also going to be keeping... humans in the loop” paraphrase ▶ 42:10
Why the recession / negative-growth scenario is improbable 2
The viral Citrini scenario (white-collar automation kills salaries, demand slumps, recession) is largely implausible: getting negative economic growth requires capital owners to hit a hard demand bound, refuse to consume more, AND refuse to invest the freed resources — conditions the speakers judge very unlikely.
contrarian🗣 Alex Imasconviction: highCitrininegative economic growthrecessiondata centers
So what: Fade the AI-driven-recession thesis; in a singularity-adjacent world the natural response is more data-center/fab investment, which sustains growth.
≈ “For abundance to generate negative economic growth, that's really hard to get.” paraphrase
Unlike the Great Depression (where the technological frontier did not expand), an AI scenario has an expanding frontier and genuine abundance, so demand-collapse dynamics that shrink the economy are far harder to trigger.
causal🗣 Alex Imasconviction: highGreat Depressiontechnological frontierabundance
So what: Expanding-frontier abundance is structurally different from a deflationary depression; don't map Depression-style demand-collapse models onto AGI.
≈ “Here, the technological frontier is expanding. You actually have abundance.” paraphrase ▶ 37:51
The 'messy middle' and political economy of displacement 4
The worst realistic scenario is the 'messy middle' (Molly Kinder): AI automates enough to displace workers but creates too little new wealth to fund a Pareto-improving payoff, with allocative and political frictions blocking targeted redistribution.
framework🗣 hostconviction: mediummessy middleMolly KinderPareto improvementredistribution
So what: The tail risk for markets is not collapse but a politically destabilizing slow grind; watch redistribution-policy fights as the channel for volatility.
Like 1920-1940 phone operators (automated over 20 years via a slow 'drip,' reabsorbed at lower salaries and underemployed per a QJE paper), the danger is not mass unemployment but workers drifting into lower-paying sectors — politically corrosive precisely because it's gradual.
anecdote🗣 Alex Imasconviction: mediumphone operatorsQJE paperunderemployment
So what: The displacement signature to watch is falling wages / downgrading, not headline unemployment; wage-mix data may matter more than the unemployment rate.
≈ “they got reabsorbed into the economy, but at lower salaries” paraphrase
Politics flips on small moves: per Andy Hall, a 2% rise in unemployment completely changes the political winds, and a fast 2-3% uptick could trigger a COVID-scale emergency fiscal response.
causal🗣 Alex Imasconviction: highAndy Hallunemploymentfiscal responseCOVID
So what: A 2-3% fast unemployment jump is the political tripwire for emergency stimulus/redistribution — a key macro-policy threshold to monitor.
≈ “If there's a 2% increase in unemployment, the political winds completely change.” paraphrase
The messy middle requires several improbable things to all hold (AI that automates entire white-collar jobs only piecemeal, while being only marginally cheaper than humans), and the speakers' model of intelligence implies that whatever can replace all software engineers can also automate accountants/analysts broadly — making the narrow, low-surplus case unlikely.
thesis🗣 Phil Trammellconviction: highsoftware engineerswhite-collar workintelligence
So what: If AI is good enough to fully replace one cognitive job it likely generalizes, producing large surplus — so the low-wealth-creation trap is improbable.
Taxation and redistribution mechanism design 6
Redistribution tools differ in implementation complexity and payoff timing, so the likely outcome is a layered stack — e.g., a negative income tax provides an immediate floor, while universal basic capital won't generate returns for months.
framework🗣 Alex Imasconviction: mediumnegative income taxuniversal basic capitalUBI
So what: Expect a portfolio of redistribution policies, not one; fast-acting income floors and slow-building capital schemes serve different phases of the transition.
UBI and negative income tax create a dangerous power-sharing arrangement: when people depend on a discretionary government check for basic needs (instead of labor income they own), whoever holds power can revoke it — whereas universal basic capital gives ownership/property rights and shareholder status.
thesis🗣 Alex Imasconviction: highUBInegative income taxuniversal basic capitalproperty rights
So what: Ownership-based redistribution (capital stakes) is structurally safer than dependency-based transfers; favors policy designs that put equity, not cash entitlements, in citizens' hands.
≈ “that feels like a power-sharing arrangement that's really dangerous” paraphrase
Universal basic capital's core flaw is targeting/indexing risk: you can't know which firms will win, so portfolios could be wrecked if, e.g., Anthropic goes to zero while some unknown robotics company captures everything.
causal🗣 hostconviction: mediumuniversal basic capitalAnthropicroboticsindexing
So what: The investability of AGI gains depends on being able to index broadly; concentrated, hard-to-predict winners are the central risk to capital-distribution schemes.
≈ “what if Anthropic goes to zero, but some random robotics company takes all this over?” paraphrase
Revenue source, tax base, and distribution should be separated: a broad-based tax (consumption/VAT or land) can fund the government buying equity (e.g., Anthropic shares) and distributing it — echoing David Autor's proposal and the privatize-Social-Security idea of handing everyone a basket of stocks.
framework🗣 Phil Trammellconviction: mediumconsumption taxVATDavid AutorSocial Securityland tax
So what: The cleanest AGI redistribution is tax-and-buy-equity; watch for VAT/consumption-tax-funded sovereign equity programs as a policy vector that would bid up broad equity ownership.
A wealth/capital tax risks having no stable equilibrium at a low rate — like the income tax it could start small (e.g., 0.5%, for war) and escalate, and could distort investment by diluting shareholders' stakes.
causal🗣 Alex Imasconviction: mediumwealth taxcapital taxincome tax
So what: Wealth-tax regimes tend to ratchet up; investors should weight escalation and investment-distortion risk when modeling post-AGI capital taxation.
≈ “there's no politically sustainable equilibrium at a 0.5% wealth tax” paraphrase
A Georgist (land) tax would not raise enough revenue for serious AGI redistribution, because land/housing's value comes mainly from proximity to other humans — a factor that won't be the main factor of production in the future.
thesis🗣 hostconviction: mediumGeorgist taxlandhousing
So what: Land-value taxation is too small a base for AGI-scale redistribution; housing-as-wealth is poorly positioned for the AI economy.
Capital accumulation, satiation, and who ends up owning the future 5
Most billionaire wealth is compounding stock, not consumption: Zuckerberg keeps Meta building data centers rather than paying himself dividends, revealing an almost 'Nick Landian' preference for accelerating capital — so the wealthiest already behave like non-satiating capital accumulators.
thesis🗣 hostconviction: highMark ZuckerbergMetadata centerscapital accumulation
So what: If the richest reinvest rather than consume, capital share rises endogenously; this favors capital owners and the compounding-reinvestment AI buildout over consumption-driven labor demand.
≈ “he would rather have his wealth compound and have Meta build more data centers” paraphrase ▶ 47:11
If even a few agents don't satiate in capital (driven by exploration, philanthropy, political/religious influence, or pure accumulation), rational behavior gives them the highest savings rate, so in the long run they hold most wealth and the overall capital share converges to their spending pattern — i.e., toward one.
framework🗣 Phil Trammellconviction: highsavings ratecapital sharesatiation
So what: Labor share's fate may be decided by a handful of non-satiating mega-accumulators; the existence of such agents tilts the long run toward capital, not labor.
≈ “the overall capital share will basically be the capital share of that person's spending, which is going to be one” paraphrase
Historically, dynastic capital didn't take over the economy because of 'dissipation shocks' — heirs squander wealth or foundations spend it down — but if people live forever or align trusts to perpetual accumulation, those shocks vanish and concentration could compound unchecked.
causal🗣 Phil Trammellconviction: mediumdissipation shocksdynastic wealthtrustslongevity
So what: Longevity and better wealth-perpetuation vehicles remove the historical brake on concentration; watch life-extension and perpetual-trust structures as drivers of extreme capital concentration.
≈ “They've given it to their kids who squandered it, or they put it in foundations which spent it.” paraphrase ▶ 57:25
If society satiates in capital, returns to capital fall, the rich consume more and invest less; conversely sustained high returns (e.g., data centers earning ~30%) signal continued non-satiation — so the returns-to-capital path is the general-equilibrium tell for which scenario we're in.
causal🗣 Alex Imasconviction: mediumreturns to capitaldata centerssatiation
So what: Track the realized return on AI capital (data centers): persistently high returns mean non-satiation and rising capital share; falling returns flag the satiation/high-labor-share branch.
Investment-specific technical change means the price of capital goods can fall faster than the capital stock grows (one robot becomes many robots while ballerina count is fixed), so standard one-good macro models that treat output as interchangeable between capital and consumption will mislead in this world.
framework🗣 Phil Trammellconviction: highinvestment-specific technical changerelative pricesmacro modelsreal interest rate
So what: Relative-price dynamics dominate: nominal/real interest rates and capital returns can look wildly different across robot-units vs. consumption-units; standard macro intuition about rates and growth breaks down.
≈ “The price of capital is falling relative to the price of consumption” paraphrase
Non-human preferences — AIs, firms, and von Neumann probes 4
Evolutionary selection among AI-containing firms will favor entities that grow and reproduce, which implies a preference for unsatiable accumulation of the relevant scarce resource (e.g., compute) — and such growth-maximizing agents will not prefer human-intrinsic goods, biasing future production away from the relational sector.
framework🗣 hostconviction: mediumAI firmsevolutioncomputepreferences
So what: Future demand may be set by growth-selected AI/firm agents that buy compute and capital, not human services — structurally bearish for the human-relational economy's share.
“It will probably favor firms or agents that grow.” ▶ 43:57
Whether AI 'beings' show up as raising or lowering labor share is partly an accounting choice: a self-owning von Neumann probe optimizing between making more probes vs. buying a ballerina has high marginal value for resources and near-zero value for human goods, but only final consumption and investment count in GDP.
framework🗣 Phil Trammellconviction: exploratoryvon Neumann probeGDPlabor shareAI welfare
So what: Post-AGI 'labor share' figures may be artifacts of how AI agents are accounted for; headline macro stats could mislead about who actually captures resources.
≈ “it just completely depends on how we're doing the accounting there” paraphrase
Human preference for genuine human connection over AI substitutes may strengthen, not fade, via natural selection: people with a moral aversion (Haidt framework) to offloading social interaction to AI are likelier to find mates and reproduce, selecting for stronger pro-human preference over generations.
thesis🗣 Alex Imasconviction: lowAI therapistJonathan Haidtnatural selectionDavid Reich
So what: If relational preference is selected-for, the human-intrinsic sector has a durable demand floor — but this is highly speculative and conditional on reproduction patterns holding.
Once AIs run production in 'neuralese' thousands of times faster, transaction costs and reliability concerns will make integrating any human into future production flows hard, even where a human has comparative advantage — so production will be organized around AI labor by default.
prediction🗣 Phil Trammellconviction: mediumneuralesecomparative advantagetransaction costsproduction flows
So what: Comparative advantage won't save human labor if integration frictions dominate; AI-native production stacks will exclude humans structurally, beyond mere cost.
≈ “there will be whole production flows organized for AI labor” paraphrase
Capturing AGI gains — indexing, housing, and developing countries 6
For both displaced workers and poor countries, buying the index of AGI (broad capital ownership) is a cleaner, more likely-to-succeed strategy than retraining or building local data centers — because labor income, not capital ownership, is what AGI undermines.
thesis🗣 hostconviction: highindexingretrainingdeveloping countriesAGI
So what: Position for AGI via diversified capital ownership rather than skills bets; policy that subsidizes citizen equity ownership beats jobs/retraining programs.
≈ “buying the index of AGI... is probably a much cleaner strategy and much more likely to succeed” paraphrase ▶ 1:09:25
The average person's capital is mostly a house, which is uniquely ill-suited to complement AI/robot production and won't be bid up by the rich — whereas AI wealth is increasingly in concentrated private companies (OpenAI, Anthropic) ordinary investors can't access.
thesis🗣 Alex Imasconviction: highhousingOpenAIAnthropicprivate companiescapital access
So what: Home equity is the wrong asset for the AI economy; access to AI-exposed (often private) capital is the real wealth lever, and a structural inequality driver.
≈ “Most of their capital is having a random house” paraphrase
The decisive macro question is whether AGI resembles electricity (downstream users capture the benefits, producers like ConEd hold little rent/power) or social media (ubiquitous but rents accrue to the platform) — this determines whether AGI gains can be indexed or are monopolized.
framework🗣 Alex Imasconviction: highelectricitysocial mediaConEdAGIrents
So what: The electricity-vs-social-media question is the master frame for AI equity strategy: commoditized AGI means buy-the-index works; platform-capture AGI means a few labs capture everything.
≈ “Is AI going to be like electricity or social media?” paraphrase
If open models stay only ~6-9 months behind the frontier (plus continual/online learning), then within months of AGI everyone gains access, commoditizing the labs and letting even Nigeria 'just buy the index' — making the recursive-self-improvement/continual-learning timeline a direct input to who captures AGI returns.
causal🗣 Alex Imasconviction: mediumopen modelsfrontiercontinual learningrecursive self-improvementNigeria
So what: The open-vs-closed-model gap is the swing variable for AI rent capture; track open-model lag and continual-learning progress as signals for lab pricing power.
≈ “in six months, everybody has access to this resource” paraphrase
Despite a recent uptick in privatized returns, well under 20% of the total market cap of non-tiny US companies is private, and frictions keeping firms (including OpenAI/Anthropic) private — disclosure requirements etc. — may themselves be reduced by AI, so the long trend toward easier indexing likely continues.
data_point🗣 Phil Trammellconviction: mediumprivate companiesmarket capOpenAIAnthropicIPO
So what: Indexing AI gains is less broken than headlines suggest, and the labs likely go public; the base case favors broad public-equity access to AI value over time.
≈ “well under 20% of the total market cap of non-tiny companies in the US is private” paraphrase ▶ 1:11:58
Developing countries should prioritize indexing into AI supply chains now (via sovereign wealth funds or citizen subsidies) over retraining, because their education systems are weak and AI could enable leapfrogging — as Nigeria leapfrogged into mobile banking ahead of Germany.
thesis🗣 Alex Imasconviction: mediumsovereign wealth fundsNigeriamobile bankingleapfroggingsupply chains
So what: Allocators advising emerging-market states should favor capital-market exposure to AI supply chains (HBM, fabs, ASML-type, labs) over domestic jobs programs.
Commoditization vs. concentration — safety and political risk 4
AI will more likely produce broad prosperity if its gains are as hard to capture as electrification's; commoditized frontier models diffuse benefits, whereas concentrated labs concentrate surplus narrowly.
thesis🗣 Phil Trammellconviction: highcommoditizationelectrificationfrontier models
So what: Bet on broad-based AI prosperity only in the commoditized branch; concentrated-lab dominance implies a few winners capture the surplus.
Commoditizing frontier AI carries a safety cost — a tech-race dynamic where no single lab has the buffer to slow down — but the speakers argue the trade-off is smaller than assumed because a leader-laggard gap is compatible with the leader being a widely-owned public company rather than fantastically rich private one.
thesis🗣 Phil Trammellconviction: mediumtech racefrontier companiesAI safetypublic company
So what: Safety-driven concentration and broad ownership aren't strictly opposed; widely-held public AI leaders could square distributed gains with a capability buffer.
Concentrated labs create a tangible political target for government expropriation — illustrated by the Defense Production Act threat against Anthropic — a risk that diffuses and is harder to exercise if many labs are at the frontier.
causal🗣 Alex Imasconviction: mediumDefense Production ActAnthropicgovernmentfrontier labs
So what: Single-lab dominance is a political/regulatory tail risk (nationalization, DPA-style action); concentration risk is a factor when valuing frontier AI equity.
≈ “We saw this with the Defense Production Act threat against Anthropic.” paraphrase
The negative AI narrative dominates not because it's more accurate but because loss of an existing thing is psychologically easier to convey than an as-yet-nonexistent utopia — a framing asymmetry that biases public and political sentiment.
causal🗣 Alex Imasconviction: mediumAI narrativepublic sentiment
So what: Persistent negative AI sentiment is partly a framing artifact, not a fundamentals signal; useful for gauging when pessimism is overdone in AI-exposed assets.
≈ “It's much easier for somebody to go on a podcast and say, 'These jobs that you like, they're going away'” paraphrase

What Bitcoin Did · Peter McCormack 2 episode(s) · 47 insights

Reality Is Radicalising People | Frank Wright157 min13 insights2026-05-29
▶ Watch on YouTube
On a Bitcoin podcast, polemicist Frank Wright frames Britain's decline as an 'omni-crisis' — and beneath the culture-war rhetoric sits a coherent UK macro-bear thesis: record sovereign debt, a fiscal 'doom spiral' as state dependency overtakes the tax base, capital and talent fleeing a punitive tax regime, and an eventual money-printer endgame that hard-money holders read as the structural bull case for Bitcoin and gold.
UK sovereign-fiscal risk & the 'doom spiral' · 5Capital & talent flight, tax and energy policy · 3Monetary debasement & the hard-asset / Bitcoin thesis · 3Social & political instability as a UK macro tail-risk · 2
UK sovereign-fiscal risk & the 'doom spiral' 5
UK public debt has reached ~£3 trillion with record borrowing and large unfunded liabilities, and no major party has a credible plan to break the trajectory.
data_point🗣 Peter McCormackconviction: mediumUnited KingdomUK giltssovereign debt
So what: A structurally bearish setup for gilts and sterling: rising issuance into an unfunded liability wall argues for higher term premium / steeper curve and a weaker GBP over time.
“We see the 3 trillion of debt. We know the unfunded liabilities. Everyone is experiencing the inflationary pressures of the printing press.” ▶ 2:05:14
Debt-interest now exceeds what the UK spends on education — a sign interest costs are crowding out productive spending.
data_point🗣 Frank Wrightconviction: mediumUnited Kingdomdebt interestfiscal policy
So what: Interest crowding-out is the classic late-stage sovereign signal; it limits fiscal room and raises the odds of either austerity (growth-negative) or monetization (inflationary) — both bearish for real gilt returns.
“We spend more on debt interest [than] we spend on education, which I think should be a crime.” ▶ 2:31:43
The number of people directly or indirectly dependent on the state will soon overtake the number of net taxpayers — a self-reinforcing fiscal 'doom spiral'.
framework🗣 Frank Wrightconviction: lowwelfare stateUnited Kingdomtax base
So what: If the dependency-to-productivity ratio is the binding constraint, the political path of least resistance is monetization — a regime that favours hard assets over nominal bonds and cash.
“Within a few years' time we are going to see the dependency overtake the productivity... That's an economic doom spiral.” ▶ 2:15:29
Whitehall factionalism and an intra-Labour standoff make meaningful fiscal or industrial reform politically impossible — the former cabinet secretary reportedly warned government would 'grind to a halt'.
causal🗣 Frank Wrightconviction: lowUK governmentLabour PartySimon CaseKeir Starmer
So what: Policy paralysis means no credible consolidation; it raises the probability that adjustment comes through the currency rather than the budget — another vote for short-GBP / long-hard-asset expressions.
“If you do anything significant to address the national crisis, 1/3 of the party will paralyze your government.” ▶ 2:07:12
Fiscal leadership lacks a coherent plan — an adviser who helped build 'the Reeves vision' privately conceded there wasn't one and 'there's no money'.
anecdote🗣 Frank Wrightconviction: lowRachel ReevesHM TreasuryLabour Party
So what: Second-hand and unverifiable, but it captures a market-relevant perception of low policy credibility around the Chancellor — the kind of confidence gap that shows up as a gilt/sterling risk premium.
“I sat down with Rachel Reeves to try and work out what it would be for the country... 'Well, there isn't one.'” ▶ 2:21:29
