Capital & talent flight, tax and energy policy 3
Punitive taxation is driving successful entrepreneurs to emigrate (e.g. to Switzerland) — capital and talent 'flow like water' to friendlier regimes.
thesis🗣 Frank Wrightconviction: mediumSwitzerlandUnited Kingdomwealth taxhigh earners
So what: Tax-driven emigration of high earners erodes the very tax base needed to fund the deficit — a feedback loop that pressures the fiscal math further and supports the capital-flight / weak-GBP thesis.
“Capital's like water, it flows where it flows. They will leave.” ▶ 2:18:39
Britain runs an energy crisis despite sitting on coal, North Sea oil & gas and abundant offshore wind/fishing resources — a self-inflicted policy failure.
causal🗣 Frank Wrightconviction: lowUK energy policyNorth Sea oil & gasenergy
So what: Domestic energy as a policy own-goal keeps input costs and import dependence high; it is a structural drag on UK competitiveness and a recurring macro-bear talking point.
≈ “We're in an energy crisis... we live on an island that's basically made out of coal, surrounded by oil and gas.” paraphrase ▶ 2:23:13
Over-regulation plus high tax destroys the entrepreneurial middle class while 'feeding the bankers' — households spend the month servicing debt.
thesis🗣 Peter McCormackconviction: lowUK middle classbanksconsumer debtsmall business
So what: A hollowed-out middle class and high debt-service burden imply weak domestic demand — a headwind for UK domestically-exposed equities and consumer credit.
≈ “We are destroying the middle class, there's no incentive to be an entrepreneur. We are basically just feeding the bankers.” paraphrase ▶ 2:18:44
Monetary debasement & the hard-asset / Bitcoin thesis 3
With no fiscal exit, every UK party will ultimately resort to 'the money printer', guaranteeing materially higher inflation.
prediction🗣 Peter McCormackconviction: mediumBank of EnglandinflationGBPmoney supply
So what: This is the core debasement trade: persistent monetization argues for real assets (gold, Bitcoin, scarce equity) over nominal cash and bonds — the explicit worldview underwriting this channel.
“The state only has one option... which is the money printer. And if they use the money printer... we're going to get more inflation, it's going to get a lot worse.” ▶ 2:18:59
Inflation is framed as a regressive wealth transfer — it hollows out ordinary households while benefiting asset-rich elites.
framework🗣 Peter McCormackconviction: mediuminflationwealth inequalityhard assets
So what: The 'inflation helps the asset-rich' framing is the retail on-ramp to the Bitcoin/scarce-asset thesis — useful as a sentiment read on why hard-money demand persists regardless of price.
“Inflation is the enemy of everybody apart from the super rich because the super rich benefit from inflation.” ▶ 9:00
Digitised/conditional welfare payments (a CBDC vector) are cast as a state control risk over dependent citizens.
contrarian🗣 Frank Wrightconviction: lowCBDCwelfare statefinancial surveillance
So what: Whether or not one shares the politics, CBDC-as-control is a durable narrative driving the censorship-resistance bid for Bitcoin and self-custody — relevant to crypto demand drivers.
≈ “Suddenly your benefits get digitized, then oh dear... no Macky D's for you. — CBDCs.” paraphrase ▶ 2:16:37
Social & political instability as a UK macro tail-risk 2
Economic hardship — not ideology — is radicalising the electorate and fuelling insurgent parties (Reform, 'Restore'), shifting the political Overton window.
thesis🗣 Frank Wrightconviction: mediumReform UKRestoreRupert LoweUK politics
So what: Rising political volatility and a credible populist challenge raise UK policy-regime uncertainty — a slow-burn risk premium for UK assets and a driver of safe-haven/hard-asset demand.
“It's reality that's radicalizing people... My life isn't working. How do I make my life work?” ▶ 0:00
Western politics has collapsed into short-termist 'machine politics' with no long-term strategic planning for economy, energy or industry.
framework🗣 Frank Wrightconviction: lowUK politicsindustrial policyenergy policy
So what: Absence of long-horizon planning is the bear's explanation for why structural decline persists across electoral cycles — it argues against mean-reversion bets on UK reform/productivity.
“You can't make a nation prosperous and secure on that frivolous time scale... politicians think about today, tomorrow, and the next election.” ▶ 33:59
AI, Bitcoin & The Rigged Economy | Tom Bilyeu86 min34 insights2026-06-03
▶ Watch on YouTube
Tom Bilyeu and Peter McCormack argue Western economies are in the late stage of Ray Dalio's big debt cycle, with deficit spending and money printing transferring wealth to asset holders and gutting the middle class; both treat Bitcoin and broader hard assets as the individual escape hatch, while a separate AI-labor disruption is poised to collapse company headcounts and reorder the jobs market faster than most expect.
Debt cycle & currency debasement as the master variable · 5UK macro decline & relative-wealth collapse · 5Bitcoin thesis & positioning · 5AI labor disruption & company restructuring · 7US-China conflict & geopolitical risk · 3Reform case studies: El Salvador, Argentina, Sweden · 3Individual strategy: assets, agency & optimism-paranoia · 3AI's long-horizon endgame: VR worlds & neural interfaces · 3
Debt cycle & currency debasement as the master variable 5
Western economies are at the terminal stage of Ray Dalio's long-term debt cycle, where every reserve-currency empire eventually prints its way to ruin, making fiscal discipline and balanced budgets the single most consequential macro variable.
thesis🗣 Tom Bilyeu (guest, Impact Theory founder)conviction: highRay DalioUS dollarUnited StatesUnited Kingdom
So what: Frames a structurally bearish view on fiat-denominated assets and a bias toward hard assets; watch deficit trajectories and reserve-currency stress as the dominant signal.
“Every empire in human history has failed the test of can you hold the one ring of being the reserve currency and not print your way to oblivion and everybody prints their way to oblivion.” ▶ 0:00
Money printing and deficit spending mechanically transfer wealth from dollar-holders to asset-holders, driving asset prices up versus a declining dollar — the explicit causal engine behind 'the rich get richer.'
causal🗣 Tom Bilyeuconviction: highUS dollarasset pricesinflation
So what: Reinforces the trade of owning scarce assets over holding cash; debasement is the persistent tailwind for equities, real estate, gold and Bitcoin.
“you force people that understand the economy to buy assets... the reason the rich are getting richer is because you're doing deficit spending and money printing which is causing the value of assets to go up certainly compared to the declining dollar.” ▶ 28:40
McCormack states he now earns more from asset inflation than from running his podcast, presented as direct evidence that the debasement trade dominates earned income for asset owners.
anecdote🗣 Peter McCormack (host)conviction: highBitcoinasset inflation
So what: A working operator confirming that holding appreciating assets outpaces active income — a case for prioritizing capital allocation over wage growth in a debasement regime.
“I make more money now through asset inflation than I do making a podcast.” ▶ 29:19
Inflation is best modeled as a deliberate, man-made invisible tax on dollar-holders rather than an organic rise in prices.
framework🗣 Tom Bilyeuconviction: highinflationUS dollar
So what: Investors should treat reported inflation as a policy choice and price in continued real-value erosion of cash holdings.
“when you print money, you devalue the dollar, which is just like taxing people that hold dollars.” ▶ 28:19
Populist political swings are a reliable downstream signal that the real economic pie is shrinking, not merely a cultural phenomenon.
framework🗣 Tom Bilyeuconviction: highpopulismUnited StatesUnited Kingdom
So what: Rising populism on both left and right can be read as a leading indicator of underlying real-income contraction and policy instability — a macro risk flag.
“a populist moment simply means people are watching the pie shrink, the pie is actually getting smaller.” ▶ 25:36
UK macro decline & relative-wealth collapse 5
The UK has become poorer than Mississippi on a per-state wealth basis, with Canada similarly trailing US states — a marker of severe relative decline in Anglosphere developed economies.
data_point🗣 Peter McCormack / Tom Bilyeuconviction: highUnited KingdomMississippiCanadaUnited States
So what: Signals structural underperformance of UK/Canadian assets and currencies versus US; supports underweighting GBP/CAD-denominated exposure.
“Dead last. We're poorer than Mississippi.” ▶ 44:07
GBP/USD has collapsed from roughly $2.5 in the early 2000s to about $1.30, illustrating long-run sterling debasement against the dollar.
data_point🗣 Peter McCormackconviction: mediumGBPUSD
So what: Long-term sterling weakness validates holding USD or hard assets over GBP cash for UK-based investors.
“It was $25 to the pound... It's now about $130 I think.” ▶ 43:24
The UK welfare bill now exceeds total income tax receipts and minimum-wage-driven wage compression is squeezing the economy, signaling an unsustainable fiscal structure under the current Labour government.
data_point🗣 Peter McCormackconviction: highUnited KingdomLabour Partywelfareminimum wage
So what: Fiscal math points to higher taxes, more borrowing or currency weakness ahead — bearish for UK gilts/GBP and a tailwind for capital flight.
“We have a welfare bill that's higher than our income tax at the moment.” ▶ 44:37
McCormack reveals his family has a 'Plan B' relocation option and frames the UK as potentially on an Argentina-style path of decades of high inflation or hyperinflation.
thesis🗣 Peter McCormackconviction: mediumUnited KingdomArgentinainflation
So what: Capital-flight and emigration risk among high-net-worth Britons; a tail scenario of UK currency/inflation crisis worth hedging.
“we could go the path of Argentina. could have decades of infl high inflation or hyperinflation.” ▶ 45:06
UK political fragmentation — seven prime ministers in nine years and insurgent parties (Reform, Restore, Green) threatening the Labour/Conservative duopoly — signals a breaking-down governance system.
data_point🗣 Peter McCormackconviction: highUnited KingdomReform PartyGreen PartyConservative PartyLabour Party
So what: Heightened UK political-risk premium; policy instability argues for caution on UK-specific assets.
“we've had seven prime ministers in nine years. Like it's breaking down.” ▶ 37:05
Bitcoin thesis & positioning 5
Bitcoin is Bilyeu's single largest position, held with a 10-20 year horizon on the simple thesis that the world becomes more digital and the next generation will grow up treating Bitcoin as a normal asset class.
thesis🗣 Tom Bilyeuconviction: highBitcoin
So what: A non-crypto-native macro investor sizing Bitcoin as top position validates the long-duration adoption thesis; default strategy is buy-and-hold through volatility.
“Well, it's my largest single position... I'm just like I'm hold this for 10 20 years unless something disrupts my thesis.” ▶ 50:29
Bitcoin currently trades as a store of wealth behaving like a tech stock, not yet as a functioning currency, with two parallel tracks: long-term holders and traders/treasury-leverage players.
framework🗣 Tom Bilyeuconviction: highBitcoinMichael Saylor
So what: Clarifies that Bitcoin's near-term price driver is risk-asset/tech correlation rather than monetary adoption; sets realistic expectations for the asset's behavior.
“right now Bitcoin is a store of wealth that's treated like a tech stock.” ▶ 52:05
Bilyeu characterizes Michael Saylor's strategy of using Bitcoin as 'a new era of stock' as gambling, distinguishing leveraged treasury plays from his own passive accumulation.
contrarian🗣 Tom Bilyeuconviction: mediumMichael SaylorMicroStrategyBitcoin
So what: A skeptical read on Bitcoin-treasury equity vehicles; flags that the leveraged/treasury layer carries materially higher risk than spot holding.
“with what Sailor's doing, treat it as uh a new era of stock. Um, what I call gambling.” ▶ 52:37
Bilyeu dismisses quantum computing as a near-term threat to Bitcoin, viewing encryption upgrades as a non-event for his thesis.
thesis🗣 Tom Bilyeuconviction: highBitcoinquantum computing
So what: Removes a common bear argument; quantum risk is being priced as manageable by sophisticated holders.
“I don't see any major hurdles with updating encryption for dealing with quantum. So that's sort of a non- entity for me.” ▶ 51:08
Bitcoin is framed as only 'a lifeboat in as much as assets are a lifeboat' — one of many asset classes to exit the fiat system rather than a near-term replacement for cash or currency.
framework🗣 Tom Bilyeuconviction: highBitcoingoldfiat
So what: Positions Bitcoin alongside gold and other hard assets in a debasement-hedge basket, not as a standalone currency bet.
“it is one of many asset classes that uh will help people get out of the fiat system.” ▶ 52:58
AI labor disruption & company restructuring 7
AI now lets a single non-technical operator build, in seven days, a full business operations system (budgets, bank feeds, invoicing, HR, contracts, AI agents) that previously required a 10-12 person team, 3-6 months and roughly GBP 500k-1m.
anecdote🗣 Peter McCormackconviction: highClaudeGitHubVercelSupabaseShopifyAI agents
So what: Concrete collapse of software-build cost and team size; bearish for traditional dev-agency labor and bullish for AI tooling platforms (Anthropic/Claude, Vercel, Supabase).
“I built the whole thing in seven days on my own. I can't code. Claude has written all the code and it's tested the code.” ▶ 56:05
Impact Theory cut its game-development headcount from ~100 contributors to 5-6 people while moving faster, at roughly 80-85% of prior human quality, by substituting AI for handcrafted art and code assets.
data_point🗣 Tom Bilyeuconviction: highImpact TheoryKaizenAIvideo game industry
So what: Real-world labor displacement ratio (~20:1) in creative production; bearish for headcount-heavy content/game studios and the artisan labor they employ.
“at one point we had a hundred people that were working on the game... Now, there's what, five or six of us, and we're getting more done by a lot faster.” ▶ 58:55
Bilyeu argues the structure of a small-to-medium company is changing: middle management largely disappears and every employee becomes an AI-empowered 'doer.'
prediction🗣 Tom Bilyeuconviction: highAImiddle managementcorporate structure
So what: Implies structural decline in white-collar middle-management roles; watch for sustained SMB headcount compression as a labor-market signal.
“everybody's a doer nobody is a middle manager... Everybody's expected to be empowered by AI.” ▶ 1:00:00
AI enabled Impact Theory to shrink its cost structure while growing top-line revenue and profitability simultaneously — reduced headcount with higher revenue.
data_point🗣 Tom Bilyeuconviction: highImpact TheoryAI
So what: Demonstrates AI-driven margin expansion at the firm level; supports a thesis of rising corporate margins and falling labor share as AI adoption spreads.
“we've reduced our headcount so dramatically and been able to at the same time make more just topline revenue is wild.” ▶ 1:00:27
The labor transition will be 'volatile,' destroying jobs before new ones arrive, and will brutally hit workers over ~35 who are trained for now-obsolete roles, even if net job creation eventually mirrors past tech revolutions.
prediction🗣 Tom Bilyeuconviction: mediumAIlabor market
So what: Flags a dislocation window where displacement leads creation; relevant for sectors with large mid-career workforces and for social/political instability risk.
“it is going to first destroy jobs before all the new ones come on board.” ▶ 1:00:58
Bilyeu observes adoption is bimodal: both young and old tend to resist AI, while a narrow mid-career band that masters it will 'steamroll ahead,' alongside a surge of solo entrepreneurs who no longer need employees.
framework🗣 Tom Bilyeuconviction: mediumAIentrepreneurshiplabor market
So what: Predicts a wave of zero-employee AI-native startups and a skills-based winner-take-most dynamic; bullish for solopreneur tooling and AI-leverage platforms.
“now I don't need employees. And so I'm going to start a business.” ▶ 1:01:49
Bilyeu runs an AI 'exec team' operating 24/7 for roughly USD 100/month, quantifying the near-zero marginal cost of always-on AI knowledge work.
data_point🗣 Tom Bilyeu / Peter McCormackconviction: highAI agents
So what: Sub-$100/month for continuous executive-grade output reframes labor cost curves; deeply deflationary for knowledge-work pricing.
“I've got a AI uh uh exact team that works 24/7 for like what is it? $100 a month” ▶ 57:31
US-China conflict & geopolitical risk 3
Citing Thucydides's Trap, Bilyeu puts the probability of kinetic US-China war at north of 70%, noting 12 of 16 historical rising-vs-declining-power collisions ended in war.
prediction🗣 Tom Bilyeuconviction: mediumUnited StatesChinaThucydides Trap
So what: A high stated probability of great-power conflict is a major tail risk for global markets, supply chains and defense/commodity positioning.
“12 out of 16 times when a rising superpower collides with a declining superpower they end up in war... the percentage is north of 70%.” ▶ 19:37
Bilyeu reads current US-China summit cordiality as PR/propaganda, with both sides using cooperation tactically to improve their position rather than signaling durable détente.
contrarian🗣 Tom Bilyeuconviction: mediumUnited StatesChina
So what: Cautions against pricing in lasting US-China thaw; trade and tariff truces may be temporary and reversible.
“They were saying nice things at the summit. Uh but that's all PR. It's propaganda.” ▶ 20:36
Bilyeu assesses China as in enough economic trouble that it would, at least short-term, cooperate with the US — implying both powers are negotiating from positions of domestic weakness.
thesis🗣 Tom Bilyeuconviction: mediumChinaUnited States
So what: Near-term US-China cooperation window may persist while China's economy is weak; relevant for trade-exposed and China-linked assets.
“I think China is in enough economic trouble right now that they would at least in the short term be willing to work with the US.” ▶ 20:45
Reform case studies: El Salvador, Argentina, Sweden 3
El Salvador (Bukele) and Argentina (Milei) are framed as the only two emerging success stories, where countries reached enough pain to elect leaders willing to attack corruption and stop endless money printing.
thesis🗣 Peter McCormackconviction: mediumEl SalvadorNayib BukeleArgentinaJavier Milei
So what: Identifies Argentina and El Salvador as reform-driven turnaround candidates; potential for re-rating of their assets/currencies if fiscal discipline holds.
“the only two remotely appearing as success stories appear to be what he's done in El Salvador... and in Argentina, what Malay is doing.” ▶ 18:09
Bilyeu argues Sweden is a free-market capitalist economy, not a socialist model: its corporate tax is lower than the US, social spending is comparable (Sweden ~24% vs US ~22% of GDP), and it privatized most services after its early-1990s banking collapse.
data_point🗣 Tom Bilyeuconviction: highSwedenUnited Statescorporate tax
So what: Reframes the 'Nordic socialism' narrative for policy-driven investors; the Swedish recovery is a case study in fiscal discipline and privatization driving turnaround.
“the corporate tax policy in Sweden is lower than in the US... we are free market capitalists. It was the only way for us to get out of the hell hole.” ▶ 41:43
In the US, the bottom 50% of earners pay only 3.4% of total income tax, a narrow tax base contrasted with Sweden's broad-based taxation of everyone.
data_point🗣 Tom Bilyeuconviction: highUnited Statestaxation
So what: Highlights US fiscal fragility from a concentrated tax base — relevant to debates on tax policy, deficits and revenue sustainability.
“In the US, the bottom 50% of people pay 3.4% of the tax. So, it's like comically low.”
Individual strategy: assets, agency & optimism-paranoia 3
Bilyeu's core actionable message is that the economy is knowingly rigged via debt and money printing, and the rational individual response is to own assets, retain agency and ensure family resilience regardless of the macro outcome.
framework🗣 Tom Bilyeuconviction: highassetsdebtinflation
So what: The repeated thesis: in a debasement regime, capital allocation into assets is the defensive play available to individuals — the central investable takeaway of the episode.
“I scream into a mic all day every day about the fact that the game is rigged. This is exactly how it's rigged and how you fix it while it's the way that it is. Invest like this.” ▶ 47:03
Bilyeu operates as 'optimistic but paranoid' — assuming a difficult decade or two for society while planning explicit family-level contingency (Plan B/C/D), treating preparedness as higher-utility than either doom or complacency.
framework🗣 Tom Bilyeuconviction: highrisk management
So what: A barbell mindset for tail-risk regimes: stay invested for upside while explicitly hedging downside scenarios including conflict and currency crisis.
“I am optimistic but paranoid... only the paranoid survive.” ▶ 46:06
Both hosts frame prolonged post-WWII prosperity (70-80 years) as the root cause of complacency, invoking 'good times create weak men' — implying the developed world is overdue for a hard reset.
framework🗣 Tom Bilyeu / Peter McCormackconviction: mediumdeveloped economies
So what: A cyclical-decline lens suggesting elevated probability of disruptive resets in Western economies — a backdrop argument for defensive, hard-asset positioning.
≈ “it's been too prosperous for too long. People think that prosperity is uh natural. It's an entitlement.” paraphrase
AI's long-horizon endgame: VR worlds & neural interfaces 3
Bilyeu forecasts four divergent human responses to AI — 'new Amish' tech-abstainers, cyborg/full-integration adopters, hard-mode Mars settlers, and people who live primarily in virtual worlds — predicting the VR-worlds path becomes profound within ~30 years (likely sooner).
prediction🗣 Tom Bilyeuconviction: mediumAIVRMarsNeuralink
So what: Maps long-horizon demand pools across VR, neural-interface and frontier tech; useful framing for very-long-duration thematic bets.
“you have what I call the new Amish... cyborg integration... go to Mars... they'll live in virtual worlds.” ▶ 1:03:18
Bilyeu states immersive lived-in virtual worlds require brain-computer interfaces (Neuralink-class), not VR glasses, and that the roadmap is already underway given existing augmentative brain implants.
prediction🗣 Tom Bilyeuconviction: mediumNeuralinkElon MuskVRbrain-computer interface
So what: Identifies neural interfaces, not headsets, as the gating technology for the 'real metaverse' — directing long-term metaverse investment toward BCI rather than VR hardware.
“that won't be interesting until you literally interface at the level of your biology... You need something like Neurolink.” ▶ 1:11:37
Bilyeu's studio Impact Theory is building a procedurally generated, Earth-sized open-world game 'Kaizen' launching December 2027, positioned as an early prototype of future livable virtual worlds.
data_point🗣 Tom Bilyeuconviction: highImpact TheoryKaizenvideo game industry
So what: A concrete product/timeline data point for tracking AI-enabled game production and the virtual-worlds thesis.
“our first video game, uh, coming out December of 2027, get ready, everybody, called Kaizen.”

Lenny's Podcast 1 episode(s) · 43 insights

The most rational take on AI you’ll hear this year | Benedict Evans80 min43 insights2026-05-31
▶ Watch on YouTube
Benedict Evans argues AI is as big as the internet/mobile but no bigger, and we're at a '1997' moment where adoption is wildly uneven and most use cases aren't built yet. His sharpest investor thesis: foundation models lack network effects and will become low-margin commodity infrastructure (like telecoms/utilities/cloud), so value accrues up-stack to applications and distribution-owners, not the model labs.
Model-layer value capture & pricing power · 7Where value accrues: apps, distribution & incumbents · 9Adoption pace & the 1997 framing · 6Jobs, labor & the automation fallacy · 6Task vs job & the consulting paradox · 4Market size, new value & the U-curve playbook · 4AGI definitions, hype & risk framing · 4Anti-AI backlash & societal risk · 3
Model-layer value capture & pricing power 7
Foundation models show no network effects and no radical product differentiation, so 3-6+ giant model providers should compete indefinitely, driving prices toward marginal cost and squeezing margins.
thesis🗣 Benedict Evansconviction: highOpenAIAnthropicfoundation models
So what: Bearish on standalone model-lab economics; favor businesses that capture value above the model rather than the labs themselves.
“the models don't seem to have network effects. So there doesn't seem to be a winner takes all effect... why would you have pricing power... it should end up looking more like cloud than it looks like Windows” ▶ 38:36
Models are likely to behave like AWS/cloud (commodity, customers indifferent to which one runs their software) rather than like Windows (lock-in giving the platform leverage up the stack).
framework🗣 Benedict Evansconviction: highAWSWindowsMicrosoftfoundation models
So what: If the cloud analogy holds, no model lab gets durable platform rents; value and lock-in live in the application layer.
The mobile/telecom industry is the cautionary analog for AI infra: ~$1T revenue, ~$200B/yr capex (15-20% of revenue), data consumption up ~1,500-2,000x since 2010, yet telco stocks went nowhere for 25 years because it's a low-margin commodity utility.
data_point🗣 Benedict Evansconviction: hightelecomsmobile industry
So what: Massive volume growth and indispensable infrastructure do not imply equity returns; AI capex buildout could mirror telco's poor shareholder outcomes.
“the stocks have gone nowhere in 25 years because it's an Xgrowth low margin commodity utility” ▶ 34:13
Sam Altman's vision of selling AI 'intelligence on a meter like water or electricity' misunderstands utility margin structure—utilities are low-margin and capture little of the value created downstream.
contrarian🗣 Benedict Evansconviction: highOpenAISam Altmanutilities
So what: Metered-intelligence framing signals commodity economics, not a high-margin toll; discount narratives that assume AI compute = high-margin annuity.
≈ “we're going to be selling AI intelligence on a meter like water or electricity and you look at this and think you know my dear sweet child you need me to explain the margin structure of the utility industry” paraphrase
Current extreme AI spending (e.g. one developer spending $1.5M/month on tokens) is a temporary disequilibrium analogous to a $50k mobile-data bill in 2010, not the steady-state—true pricing power must be judged at equilibrium.
framework🗣 Benedict Evansconviction: highAI tokensClaude Code
So what: Don't extrapolate today's token-revenue run-rates; model the steady-state where prices and margins normalize downward.
“that's like somebody getting like a 50 grand mobile data bill in 2010... that's temporary” ▶ 36:35
Whether the world ends up with 3-6 giant foundation models or hundreds of open/local models is still genuinely unresolved, and the labs may not capture the value even in the concentrated case.
thesis🗣 Benedict Evansconviction: mediumfoundation modelsopen modelslocal models
So what: Market structure of the model layer is unsettled; position sizing on any single lab should account for this binary uncertainty.
Whether model labs have pricing power is the under-asked question; most people simply assume today's situation will continue or that labs will of course be dominant.
contrarian🗣 Benedict Evansconviction: highfoundation modelsAI labs
So what: Consensus complacency on lab durability is a potential mispricing; the variant view (commoditization) is under-owned.
“I'm not sure how many people are asking whether model labs have pricing power” ▶ 59:43
Where value accrues: apps, distribution & incumbents 9
If AI requires thousands of differentiated applications, model labs can't build them all (just as Microsoft didn't), so value shifts to application builders—the 'wrappers/harnesses'—above the model.
thesis🗣 Benedict Evansconviction: highapplication layerfoundation modelsMicrosoft
So what: Tilt exposure toward the AI application layer and vertical software rather than the model providers.
≈ “I don't like seeing GPT wrappers. I do like harnesses” paraphrase
When the underlying product is a commodity, distribution becomes the decisive moat—favoring incumbents (Google/Meta/Apple) who can spray an 'adequate' model across existing surfaces over startups fighting for attention.
framework🗣 Benedict Evansconviction: highGoogleMetaAppleGeminidistribution
So what: Distribution-rich incumbents are structurally advantaged in AI rollout; weight that into competitive analysis of AI startups vs platforms.
“distribution of an adequate product when the field is basically commodity distribution on brand become a big deal” ▶ 45:33
The browser-wars pattern repeats: Microsoft used distribution to win browsers for 5-6 years but gained nothing because value sat further up the stack—implying winning the AI chatbot UX may likewise not confer durable advantage.
framework🗣 Benedict Evansconviction: mediumMicrosoftweb browsersGoogleGemini
So what: Even a distribution winner in chatbots may not monetize the win; watch where the actual value sits rather than UX share.
“Microsoft wins browsers for like five six years and it doesn't matter it doesn't get them anything” ▶ 44:53
Google and Meta are quietly competitive in consumer AI via distribution—Meta's Llama-based assistant polled between ChatGPT and Gemini in usage despite being written off in tech circles, because Meta sprayed it across its surfaces.
data_point🗣 Benedict Evansconviction: mediumMetaLlamaGoogleGeminiChatGPT
So what: Usage rankings driven by distribution defy tech-insider sentiment; don't write off platform incumbents in the consumer-AI race.
“meta was like behind it was up there between chat GPT and Gemini which if you're in tech you people have completely written it off” ▶ 45:21
The model is the 'dumb thing underneath'—a commodity that powers features—while the differentiated decisions about product, feature design and distribution sit at the app layer (e.g. Apple Intelligence on iOS vs Gemini on Android both possibly powered by Gemini but different products).
framework🗣 Benedict Evansconviction: highAppleApple IntelligenceGeminiAndroidiOS
So what: Differentiation—and pricing power—lives in product/distribution decisions, not the shared underlying model.
“the model is just like the dumb thing underneath... the model is the commodity that powers different decisions about what the feature should be” ▶ 47:18
Apple holds a distribution edge with ~1 billion devices capable of running AI on-edge, whereas Google's 'coming soon to our most powerful devices' means it won't run on most Android phones.
data_point🗣 Benedict Evansconviction: mediumAppleGoogleAndroidon-device AI
So what: On-device install base favors Apple for ubiquitous personal-AI deployment; a structural asset in the assistant race.
Mega-cap incumbents (Google, Apple, Meta, Amazon) look hard to disrupt with AI—mobile barely changed Google or Amazon and helped Meta—though one could 'drop the ball,' echoing how half the internet industry was unaffected by the mobile shift.
thesis🗣 Benedict Evansconviction: mediumGoogleAppleMetaAmazon
So what: AI may be a 'feature, not disruption' for several incumbents; their moats may persist through the platform shift.
“you can look at you know Google, Apple, Facebook, Amazon and say it's hard to see a problem for them really with all of this”
There is still no breakout consumer AI app because marginal token cost prevents the free-then-monetize playbook (free product to 50M users) that drove past consumer software waves.
causal🗣 Benedict Evansconviction: mediumconsumer AI appsAI tokens
So what: Until inference cost collapses, expect no viral free consumer-AI breakout; a gating factor for consumer-AI investment theses.
“we don't have breakout a consumer AI apps yet because I think because of marginal cost more than anything else so you can't make it free and get 50 million users” ▶ 1:15:18
Model labs building 'Claude for X / Claude for Y' vertical products resemble Excel's 'File > New' templates—though Evans concedes each such template-category can itself be a billion-dollar company.
framework🗣 Benedict Evansconviction: lowAnthropicClaudeOpenAIvertical AI
So what: Labs' vertical expansion may not foreclose third-party app opportunity; large standalone businesses can still form around each use case.
“which to me look very much like what you see if you hit file new in Excel” ▶ 35:03
Adoption pace & the 1997 framing 6
AI is at an internet-'1997' stage: very exciting, but most use cases aren't built yet and it's unclear how they'll work—adoption is bimodal, with most non-tech people using AI only every week or two.
framework🗣 Benedict Evansconviction: highAI adoptioninternet
So what: Sets expectations for a multi-year diffusion ramp; near-term enterprise transformation will be slower than headlines imply.
“if you're going to make the internet comparison it's like we're in 1997. Like it's very exciting. Most stuff kind of doesn't work yet” ▶ 3:23
Even among 13-18 year-olds, only ~15-20% are daily active AI users and ~20% weekly, while ~60% report not using it—adoption is far less universal than tech insiders assume.
data_point🗣 Benedict Evansconviction: highAI adoptionGen Z
So what: Penetration runway is large but real adoption lags hype; temper TAM-now assumptions for consumer AI.
“even if you look at like 13 to 18 year olds... it's still like kind of 15 20% of people are daily active users and another 20% are weekly active users and then the other 60%... are not using this” ▶ 8:54
AI adoption is faster than prior technologies only because it stands on existing infrastructure—ChatGPT reached ~900M weekly users because 900M people were already online, vs only 50-100M PCs existing when Netscape launched.
causal🗣 Benedict Evansconviction: highChatGPTOpenAINetscape
So what: Fast user growth reflects pre-built distribution, not faster enterprise transformation; deployment into workflows still takes years.
“of course chat GBT can get 900 million chat users because there's already 900 million people on the internet” ▶ 20:41
Enterprise transformation will take 3-10 years sector by sector because enterprise sales cycles run ~18 months and companies won't rip out SAP-class systems overnight—doomers who think firms buy AI then fire staff in two weeks misunderstand how the world works.
contrarian🗣 Benedict Evansconviction: highSAPenterprise softwareAI adoption
So what: Enterprise AI revenue and labor effects will be gradual; near-term enterprise-AI disruption theses are likely premature.
“no people aren't just going to tear out SAP and replace it with XY Z. Maybe in five in like three five 10 years yes” ▶ 21:58
Software development is already in the transformed moment ('before Claude Code and after Claude Code'), with developers going from writing all their code to ~0%, while most other professions are still puzzling out where AI fits.
thesis🗣 Benedict Evansconviction: highClaude Codesoftware development
So what: Coding is the leading indicator of AI's labor/productivity impact; other verticals lag and offer later-stage opportunity.
We have almost no reliable usage data on AI—the model labs publish no daily-active-user numbers and most data comes from academic economists backing figures out of BLS surveys or paid consultancy polls.
data_point🗣 Benedict Evansconviction: highOpenAIAnthropicBLSAI usage data
So what: The data vacuum makes AI penetration/impact claims unreliable; treat lab-cited adoption stats with skepticism.
≈ “We do not have a daily active user number for a forhat. This is crazy.” paraphrase ▶ 50:49
Jobs, labor & the automation fallacy 6
The most advanced AI companies—Anthropic and OpenAI—are increasing headcount, i.e. the firms most able to automate humans are adding many humans, undercutting the simple job-apocalypse narrative.
data_point🗣 Benedict Evansconviction: highAnthropicOpenAIlabor
So what: Real-world AI leaders' hiring behavior is a counter-signal to mass-displacement theses; watch headcount as evidence.
“the companies you would think would be least likely to add humans are adding many, many humans” ▶ 17:54
Accountant headcount rose through the entire 20th century and again into the 21st despite adding machines, mainframes, ERP and spreadsheets—historical evidence that automation tools grow rather than shrink a profession.
data_point🗣 Benedict Evansconviction: highaccountingspreadsheetsERPlabor
So what: Use prior-automation labor curves as base rate against linear job-loss forecasts for AI-exposed white-collar work.
“the number of people employed as accountants which went up right the way through the 20th century and has gone up again since the beginning of the 21st” ▶ 17:23
There is currently no clear consensus in the economic data that AI is reducing jobs—a slowdown in 18-24 year-old employment appears equally across degree/non-degree and AI-exposed/non-exposed fields, suggesting other causes.
data_point🗣 Benedict Evansconviction: mediumlabor marketyouth employment
So what: Don't attribute current youth-employment softness to AI; the displacement signal isn't yet in the data.
“there's no clear consensus that we're seeing an impact on jobs” ▶ 51:21
Scoring jobs by '% exposed to AI' (e.g. O*NET-based studies claiming 17% of a law partner's work is automatable) is the failed 'expert systems' fallacy—you cannot decompose a profession into automatable sub-tasks.
contrarian🗣 Benedict Evansconviction: highO*NETlaw firmslabor
So what: Task-exposure scores driving labor and consulting models are likely garbage; discount research built on them.
“You can't kind of look at a senior partner at a law firm and say, well, 17% of their work could be automated like this is horshit.” ▶ 1:03:35
You cannot predict which jobs AI will expose—e.g. personal trainers, long deemed safe, could be displaced by a phone-propped AI that builds and watches your workout, just as taxi drivers seemed un-automatable by the internet before Uber.
framework🗣 Benedict Evansconviction: highpersonal trainersUbertaxi driverslabor
So what: Exposure is non-intuitive; second-order disruptions emerge from adjacent tech, not direct automation—screen for the non-obvious.
“you can't predict which things are going to be exposed necessarily” ▶ 0:31
The unresolved structural risk is what happens to the apprentice/pyramid model of professional services if entry-level associate hiring (e.g. a law firm cutting from 100 to 50 hires) collapses.
thesis🗣 Benedict Evansconviction: mediumprofessional serviceslaw firmslabor
So what: Watch entry-level hiring in law/consulting/banking as the leading indicator of AI's labor restructuring and the pyramid economics it depends on.
“It's very unclear what... happens to the pyramid structure of professional services.” ▶ 1:07:22
Task vs job & the consulting paradox 4
The AI labs themselves are the biggest investors in consulting/professional-services/forward-deployed-engineer capacity—the opposite of the expectation that AI would eliminate consultants—because reimagining and rebuilding company workflows is itself a labor-intensive project.
contrarian🗣 Benedict Evansconviction: highOpenAIAnthropicAccentureBainBCGMcKinseyconsulting
So what: AI deployment creates services demand; consulting/integration and forward-deployed-engineering are a beneficiary, not a casualty.
“the most cutting edge AI labs are the ones most investing in these folks” ▶ 12:25
The key analytical question for any job is 'what's the task vs the job'—AI automates the deliverable (the code, the slide deck) but not the underlying judgment, context and politics that the client actually pays for (e.g. McKinsey's value is walking the enterprise, not the PowerPoint).
framework🗣 Benedict Evansconviction: highMcKinseyBainprofessional services
So what: A durable filter for which roles/companies AI commoditizes vs leaves intact—apply it across labor-exposure analysis.
“the PowerPoint is just like the task but that's not what you hired them for” ▶ 16:20
Price elasticity (the 'jevons paradox') means cheaper task-completion historically led to doing more for the same or more money, not fewer workers—e.g. Excel didn't shrink the ranks of junior investment bankers, and IDEs/libraries didn't reduce software engineer headcount.
framework🗣 Benedict Evansconviction: highExcelGoldman Sachsinvestment bankingsoftware development
So what: Cheaper AI labor likely expands output and demand rather than collapsing employment in those fields; bullish for AI-augmented service volume.
“before Excel junior investment bankers worked really long hours and now thanks to Excel Goldman's associates all work at lunchtime on Fridays. It's like well why is that not what happened?” ▶ 14:10
AI excels at what computers are bad at and is bad at what computers are good at—e.g. it nails image-based interior redecorating but is poor at precise information retrieval—inverting where prior automation added value.
framework🗣 Benedict Evansconviction: mediumgenerative AIimage generation
So what: Capability map for product builders: target fuzzy/creative tasks, not precision-retrieval, when picking AI use cases to monetize.
“AI is good at stuff that computers are bad at and bad at stuff that computers are good at” ▶ 1:09:45
Market size, new value & the U-curve playbook 4
The addressable opportunity keeps expanding (Andreessen's 'software eats the world'), so the era is producing many trillion-dollar companies and startups hitting $100M ARR in 5-6 months—AI extends this by automating large new swaths of the economy rather than just adding more users.
thesis🗣 Benedict Evansconviction: mediumMarc Andreessena16zsoftware
So what: TAM expansion supports outsized company valuations; the constraint shifts from user count to economic surface automated.
The old 'we've run out of people, the next platform can't be an order of magnitude bigger' model is wrong—growth now comes from branching into and automating new parts of the economy, not just adding the next billion users.
contrarian🗣 Benedict Evansconviction: mediumplatform shiftsTAM
So what: Reject saturation-based ceilings on AI TAM; the next leg is depth (economic automation), not breadth (users).
“you're just you know branching out and automating big big new ways of the economy” ▶ 31:28
The recorded-music revenue U-curve (down ~50% from 2000 to ~2015, recovered to ~75% of peak via streaming) shows new tech first does the old thing cheaper, then unlocks a genuinely new model ($15/month for all music) that Spotify built—the real AI value will come from the not-yet-imagined new question, not doing the old thing faster.
framework🗣 Benedict Evansconviction: highSpotifyrecorded musicstreamingUberAirbnb
So what: The biggest AI winners will be businesses that redefine the question (like Spotify), so hunt for new-model creators, not productivity tools.
“Spotify is not an online music store. It's something else.” ▶ 1:01:25
Disruption impact is heterogeneous and counterintuitive: Uber demolished and then grew the taxi market, but Airbnb's measured impact on hotels is marginal because business travelers (half of hotel demand) won't substitute—blanket 'X disrupts Y' theses overstate the effect.
contrarian🗣 Benedict Evansconviction: highUberAirbnbhotelstaxis
So what: Stress-test AI disruption claims against actual substitution behavior; incumbents in segments with hard switching costs may be far safer than narratives suggest.
“Airbnbs impact hotel hotels if you actually go and look at the numbers is pretty marginal” ▶ 1:05:25
AGI definitions, hype & risk framing 4
AGI is being redefined downward to mean 'can do a percentage of economically valuable work'—a bar an IBM mainframe met in 1975—so AGI/superintelligence terminology is a moving target ('AI is whatever machines can't do yet') with no fixed referent for investors.
framework🗣 Benedict Evansconviction: highAGIsuperintelligenceIBM
So what: Don't underwrite valuations on AGI/superintelligence milestones whose definitions shift; demand concrete capability metrics instead.
“AI is whatever machines can't do yet because once machines can do it. People say, well that's just software.” ▶ 27:29
Even if model progress hit a brick wall tomorrow, current AI is an enormously useful technology that will roll out over the next ~10 years—so the investment case does not depend on believing in AGI or superintelligence.
thesis🗣 Benedict Evansconviction: highAIAGI
So what: De-risk AI exposure from the AGI debate; the diffusion of today's capability is the base-case driver of value.
“even if like the model stopped it getting better tomorrow if this is it and we hit a brick wall tomorrow this is an incredibly useful technology that's going to change the world” ▶ 29:33
Dario Amodei's job-loss predictions deserve skepticism: running an AI lab gives authority on model trajectories, not on labor economics and comparative advantage, where his views carry no special weight.
contrarian🗣 Benedict Evansconviction: highDario AmodeiAnthropiclabor
So what: Weight lab-CEO labor forecasts as opinion, not expertise; avoid anchoring trades on 'Dario says' entry-level-job-apocalypse claims.
“I'm not particularly interested in opinions on theories of labor and market value and competitive comparative advantage” ▶ 18:31
Models still reliably hallucinate—push any model a little further on any question and you get confident falsehoods—a persistent limitation that doesn't negate usefulness but constrains precision-critical deployments.
data_point🗣 Benedict Evansconviction: highLLMshallucination
So what: Reliability ceiling caps near-term automation in high-stakes/precision domains (legal, finance); a real constraint on enterprise rollout.
“you push them, push them a little bit further, any question, and you'll still get like, Nope, that's not true.” ▶ 1:18:58
Anti-AI backlash & societal risk 3
Anti-AI sentiment is a fuzzy mix where only some grievances are real—data-center water use is essentially fake (US data centers consume ~0.017% of US water per a 2024 Livermore study) while electricity is a genuine but bounded issue (~5% of US energy, growing ~1 percentage point/year).
data_point🗣 Benedict Evansconviction: highdata centersenergywater
So what: Separates real from theatrical opposition for siting/regulatory risk; energy demand is the substantive variable for power/infra investors.
“US data center water consumption... came out at about 0.017% of US water consumption” ▶ 49:19
AI backlash resembles the social-media backlash but more compressed—a wide spread of grievances (slop, creative-job loss, deepfakes, energy) some genuinely real and some not—and it is politically potent regardless of whether the data supports it.
framework🗣 Benedict Evansconviction: mediumAI backlashsocial mediadeepfakes
So what: Expect sustained political/regulatory friction (data-center permitting, content rules) independent of facts; a real operational risk for AI rollouts.
Every technology wave brings new ways to ruin lives (e.g. the UK Post Office/Fujitsu Horizon scandal: 1970s-era software bugs led to wrongful prosecutions, bankruptcies and suicides), implying AI will produce analogous large-scale harms—deepfakes being the obvious near-term one.
anecdote🗣 Benedict Evansconviction: mediumFujitsuUK Post Officedeepfakes
So what: Systemic AI-harm episodes are near-certain and will drive liability and regulation; a tail risk for AI deployers and vendors.
“every wave of technology comes with ways that you can ruin people's lives either deliberately or by accident” ▶ 58:08

Capital Allocators · Ted Seides 1 episode(s) · 38 insights

Operator-Led Private Equity at Ethos - Erik Brooks58 min38 insights2026-06-01
▶ Watch on YouTube
Erik Brooks, co-founder of Ethos Capital, details an operator-led middle-market PE model: 16 former C-suite executives embedded across sourcing, diligence and value creation, a deliberate one-deal-a-year concentration strategy on ~$6B AUM, a proprietary AI-powered operating system, and a durability-first underwriting bar where 'how do I lose money' must be a sub-1% black-swan exercise. Identity Digital (top-level domain registries) is the worked example.
Operator-led PE as an information-quality edge · 4Concentration strategy: one deal a year on ~$6B AUM · 5Durability-first underwriting and risk · 4Identity Digital / DNS as the ideal business model · 4Value-creation engine: acceleration vectors and the FIT filter · 4Lessons learned in execution · 4Organizational design, talent and tech · 5GP-LP dynamics, alignment and exits · 5Sector thesis: durable digital-infrastructure models · 3
Operator-led PE as an information-quality edge 4
Ethos's core thesis is that PE is an information business ('our input is not glass or metal, it's information'), so embedding former operators who ask better management-meeting questions yields better data, better interpretation, and ultimately better returns over time.
framework🗣 guest, Erik Brooks (Ethos Capital co-founder)conviction: highEthos Capitalprivate equity
So what: Reframes value creation as a diligence/information edge rather than financial engineering; LPs should test whether 'operator-led' GPs actually change information quality vs. just add advisors.
“We don't make anything in our business... Our input to the process is not glass or metal. It's information. If the questions are better, then the information you're going to be getting is better.” ▶ 1:47
An operator (Fahad/'FOD') running a management meeting produces deeper, better answers than a PE analyst because peer-to-peer operating dialogue ('two people speaking Portuguese... and I speak Spanish') removes the interrogation dynamic management feels with PE buyers.
causal🗣 guest, Erik Brooksconviction: highEthos CapitalFahad (FOD)IBM
So what: Suggests durable edge comes from access to truer management signal; allocators can probe whether a GP's operators actually run meetings vs. observe.
“Even in the best scenarios, my asking questions is a little bit of an interrogation... When FOD would run the management meetings, it was a conversation.” ▶ 0:26
Not every operator can fill the operator-investor 'seat'; only those who are open, non-threatening and collaborative extract information, because a head-of-sales questioning another head-of-sales can read as antagonistic or competitive.
framework🗣 guest, Erik Brooksconviction: highEthos Capital
So what: Personality/temperament screening of operating partners is a hidden selection criterion that determines whether the operator-led model actually works.
Cross-industry operator backgrounds produce non-obvious reframings that drive measurable margin: an education-sector partner recast a healthcare administrative-services company as 'a school' (recruit, train, place), generating ideas worth ~500bps of margin in the base case.
anecdote🗣 guest, Erik Brooksconviction: highhealthcare serviceseducation
So what: Concrete evidence that diverse operating talent surfaces value-creation levers traditional deal teams miss; a tangible justification for the model's cost.
“They're a school. How is this a school? ... In our modeling, in our base case, that was 500 basis points of margin.” ▶ 34:08
Concentration strategy: one deal a year on ~$6B AUM 5
Ethos runs ~$6B AUM with a single deal team targeting roughly one deal per year and currently holds only four portfolio companies, deliberately concentrating firepower (16 operating partners) onto each holding.
data_point🗣 guest, Erik Brooksconviction: highEthos Capital
So what: An unusually concentrated GP structure; LPs get high engagement per company but accept high single-name risk, a distinct risk/return profile vs. diversified buyout funds.
“We have one deal team, which is a bit unusual in an organization that has almost $6 billion of capital under management. We're only looking to do about a deal a year.” ▶ 31:58
The math of concentration is the key constraint: with five deals in a fund, a single zero is severely damaging, so the underwriting bar for downside protection must be extreme.
framework🗣 guest, Erik Brooksconviction: highEthos Capital
So what: Explains why durability/loss-avoidance dominates their process; concentrated funds live or die on avoiding zeros, not maximizing upside.
“The negative would be if you get it wrong, having a zero and a fund of five, that math is slightly problematic.” ▶ 36:19
Ethos deep-dives on no more than six or seven companies a year and did zero deals in 2024, treating non-deployment as a feature; the freedom to not transact raises selectivity and decision quality.
data_point🗣 guest, Erik Brooksconviction: highEthos Capital
So what: Counter to deployment-pressure dynamics that plague many GPs; LPs should value patient capital cadence as a discipline signal.
“In 2024, we didn't do a deal. That's just fine... The calm and the patience that that brings to the organization elevates the decision-m makes us more selective.” ▶ 37:21
Ethos preserves the model across fund vintages by being strict on company characteristics but flexible on deal size (equity checks have ranged from ~$100M to $500M), letting it scale fund-to-fund without doubling headcount or deal size.
framework🗣 guest, Erik Brooksconviction: highEthos Capital
So what: Shows how a concentrated GP scales AUM without diluting strategy; variable check size plus co-investing LPs is the mechanism.
“the one place that we are flexible is in deal size. We've made equity investments of [a hundred] million. We've made equity investments of $500 million.” ▶ 38:48
Ethos relies on co-investing LPs to fund larger deals without growing the fund proportionally, allowing it to keep doing the same strategy across fund I, II, III.
data_point🗣 guest, Erik Brooksconviction: highEthos Capitallimited partners
So what: Co-invest appetite is structurally load-bearing for this model; LPs willing/able to co-invest get differentiated access and economics.
“Our limited partners are folks that like to co-invest.” ▶ 38:59
Durability-first underwriting and risk 4
Ethos's first underwriting filter is durability: the answer to 'how do I lose money in this deal?' must be a black-swan exercise with probability the speaker assigns under 1%; durability precedes any upside analysis.
framework🗣 guest, Erik Brooksconviction: highEthos Capital
So what: A loss-avoidance-first underwriting standard; the relevant due-diligence question for LPs is the downside narrative, not the upside model.
“My answer has to be some kind of black swanish intellectual exercise, the probability of which I would assign a single digit, but probably under 1%.” ▶ 47:15
Ethos targets business models with 'no expiration date' and explicitly avoids structurally declining industries (the 'yellow pages' test), favoring sectors with macro tailwinds rather than ones at risk of obsolescence.
framework🗣 guest, Erik Brooksconviction: highdigital infrastructureyellow pages
So what: Sector selection screens out terminal-decline risk up front; a useful frame for evaluating any long-hold buyout target's durability.
“we don't want to invest in a business that has an expiration date.” ▶ 15:33
Ethos deliberately under-levers acquisitions because it never wants a company's operating strategy dictated by its capital structure, a lesson Brooks says he learned from a prior mistake.
framework🗣 guest, Erik Brooksconviction: highEthos Capital
So what: Conservative leverage trades some IRR for operational flexibility and downside protection; differentiates them from max-leverage buyout shops.
“We're conservative in how we lever these businesses up front because we never want the operating strategy of a business to be dictated by the capital structure. I've made that mistake before.” ▶ 53:57
Brooks's formative Eastern Europe privatization experience (mid-late 1990s) taught that 7-8x returns came from circumstances, not genius, after a portfolio bought at 10 cents on the dollar rose to ~70-80 cents then collapsed to ~4 cents in the EM crisis.
anecdote🗣 guest, Erik Brooksconviction: highEastern EuropeRussiaUkraineKazakhstanemerging markets
So what: Origin of the firm's humility-about-luck and loss-aversion ethos; a reminder that high-multiple EM gains can fully reverse.
“your 70 cents on the dollar goes to 4 cents on the dollar. You recognize that it was not your genius. In fact, it was the circumstances.” ▶ 9:24
Identity Digital / DNS as the ideal business model 4
Identity Digital, the world's largest top-level-domain owner, exemplifies Ethos's ideal: ~60s% EBITDA margins, double-digit topline growth, 98% free-cash-flow conversion, and the highest customer-renewal probability Brooks has seen by vintage cohort — 'almost a mirror image' of VeriSign.
data_point🗣 guest, Erik Brooksconviction: highIdentity DigitalVeriSignDNSdomain name system
So what: Quantifies the financial profile Ethos seeks; VeriSign is offered as a public comp/benchmark for DNS-registry economics.
“The business has ebidal margins in the '60s is growing topline double digit has 98% free cash flow conversion very much like VeraSign for example almost a mirror image.” ▶ 44:26
Domain registration is mission-critical yet near-zero cost (~$30/year on average), producing structurally sticky demand — customers 'spend more on coffee, soda, and pens' than on the digital identity their business depends on.
framework🗣 guest, Erik Brooksconviction: highIdentity DigitalDNS
So what: The mission-critical-but-trivial-cost profile is a generalizable hunting ground for pricing power and retention in any portfolio.
“It costs on average for this company's domain names $30 a year. We spend more on coffee, soda, and pens individually than we do on our digital identities.” ▶ 44:00
The .com registry alone has ~173 million registrations, indicating the scale of the top-level-domain market Ethos targeted within its digital-infrastructure theme.
data_point🗣 guest, Erik Brooksconviction: mediumIdentity DigitalDNS.com
So what: Sizes the DNS registry opportunity; relevant context for VeriSign and registry-adjacent investments.
“About 173 million.com registrations.” ▶ 42:24
On Identity Digital, Ethos refinanced from a SOFR+625 unitranche (paying ~10-12%) into a securitized asset-backed loan at a fixed ~6.8%, saving roughly $50M a year, requiring three partners near-full-time to build bankruptcy-remote subsidiaries, backup-servicer agreements and rating-agency meetings.
data_point🗣 guest, Erik Brooksconviction: highIdentity Digitalsecuritizationrating agencies
So what: Concrete value-creation lever: capital-structure engineering (recurring-revenue securitization) can be worth tens of millions annually for high-FCF subscription assets.
“from a sofur plus 625 unit deal that we're paying 10 12 on into a securitized assetbacked loan that we're going to pay a fixed 6.8% and save $50 million a year.” ▶ 51:18
Value-creation engine: acceleration vectors and the FIT filter 4
Ethos requires a target to generate 20-30 candidate value-creation ideas ('acceleration vectors') across strategy, market and operations; if a company yields only four or five real ideas it is 'not an Ethos company' because there is no alpha to add.
framework🗣 guest, Erik Brooksconviction: highEthos Capital
So what: Idea-density is itself a deal screen — a usable test for whether an operationally-intensive GP can actually move a given asset.
“If we only come up with four or five ideas and it's not an ethos company, there's no alpha in our participating... It's got to have a lot of ideas.” ▶ 45:23
Ethos prioritizes ideas through a 'FIT' methodology — Feasibility, Impact, Timing — narrowing 20-30 raw ideas to a prioritized list of five to seven the firm actually pursues post-close.
framework🗣 guest, Erik Brooksconviction: highEthos Capital
So what: A reusable prioritization framework for operational value creation; the discipline avoids overwhelming management with low-yield initiatives.
“our fit methodology, which is feasibility, impact, and timing.” ▶ 49:14
Value-creation ideas are deliberately excluded from the base case, so any that succeed generate asymmetric upside on top of an underwriting that already clears on base-case math alone.
framework🗣 guest, Erik Brooksconviction: highEthos Capital
So what: Structures returns as 'free options' — base case must work without operational improvements, with upside layered on; a conservative underwriting posture for LPs.
“If any of them work and we hit our base case, then there's alpha in a system of hopefully asymmetric risk that we've created in the original base case.” ▶ 50:22
Eight Ethos partners worked the Identity Digital deal pre-close and ten partners actively participate post-close — pricing, channel expansion, M&A, cost structure, customer strategy — illustrating the manpower concentration the model enables.
data_point🗣 guest, Erik Brooksconviction: highIdentity DigitalEthos Capital
So what: Quantifies the operational intensity per holding; a benchmark for what 'hands-on' actually means in this model.
“Probably had eight of our partners on that deal. Once we own it, we have 10 of our partners actively participating.” ▶ 45:53
Lessons learned in execution 4
Ethos found front-end underwriting accurate but badly underestimated the difficulty of getting management teams to own and execute value-creation ideas while meeting quarterly EBITDA hurdles and day jobs.
anecdote🗣 guest, Erik Brooksconviction: highEthos Capital
So what: Implementation, not idea generation, is the binding constraint in operational PE; a caution for LPs underwriting value-creation-heavy theses.
“Our process of how we work with management teams to help them execute and implement these ideas... was overinflated in my mind.” ▶ 48:08
Ethos now staffs portfolio companies with added resources rather than expecting existing management to execute initiatives on top of their day jobs, after early efforts burned political capital on ideas that failed.
causal🗣 guest, Erik Brooksconviction: highEthos Capital
So what: Real value creation requires funding incremental capacity at the portfolio company; an operating-cost reality LPs should factor into return models.
“it's unrealistic to assume that someone's going to do their day job and all of these great things.” ▶ 48:59
A hard-won discipline is killing failing initiatives fast; operator partners instinctively 'will it to work,' so Ethos had to teach that walking away from a stuck idea is not failure — the failure is the sunk-cost persistence.
framework🗣 guest, Erik Brooksconviction: highEthos Capital
So what: Counterintuitive: the operator strength (relentless execution) is also a risk; disciplined stop-loss on initiatives preserves the alpha budget.
“you have people who are operators who will will it to work... The failure is continuing to spend time on something that you're never going to get to work.” ▶ 49:53
Real financial evidence of Ethos's value creation does not show up for two to three years, and the firm sometimes intentionally pulls EBITDA down near-term in service of longer-term value.
data_point🗣 guest, Erik Brooksconviction: mediumEthos Capital
So what: LPs should expect a multi-year J-curve on operational value creation and tolerate near-term margin dips; early marks understate the thesis.
“the real evidence of financial performance doesn't really show up for probably two or three years. Sometimes we pull Ebida dot down.” ▶ 53:47
Organizational design, talent and tech 5
Ethos inverts traditional PE talent design: instead of partners being 'a mile wide and an inch deep' (good at everything), it builds 'an inch wide and a mile deep' specialists, so no partner must master sourcing, diligence and value creation alike.
framework🗣 guest, Erik Brooksconviction: highEthos Capital
So what: A reusable org-design principle; lets a firm recruit deep functional operators who would never fit the generalist PE-partner ladder.
“We've done is created an organization of people that in some ways are an inch wide and a mile deep.” ▶ 30:26
Every one of Ethos's 16 operating partners is someone Brooks or his co-founder had known for at least a decade, with backgrounds spanning Disney, The Gap, Forbes, Google, Verizon and Brinks across functions (sales, finance, go-to-market, technology, HR, design).
data_point🗣 guest, Erik Brooksconviction: highEthos CapitalDisneyThe GapForbesGoogleVerizonBrinks
So what: Decade-long pre-existing relationships are the trust moat behind the model; hard for competitors to replicate quickly.
“It is Disney, The Gap, Forbes, Google, Verizon, Brinks, you name it, across the spectrum.” ▶ 32:59
Ethos built a proprietary AI-powered operating system feeding every email, Slack, calendar invite, note and file into a data lake and knowledge graph, giving 16 geographically dispersed partners real-time asynchronous visibility into every deal.
data_point🗣 guest, Erik Brooksconviction: highEthos CapitalAIknowledge graph
So what: Information infrastructure is the operational backbone that makes a 16-partner async model viable; a tech-enabled differentiation in PE workflow.
“Everything that we do at ethos feeds into our data lake then is run through our knowledge graph... Every email, Slack, calendar invite, every note, every file.” ▶ 35:36
Ethos deliberately overbuilt the organization at inception (full back office, deep partner bench) to grow into the next 5-15 years without restructuring, treating excess capacity as a strategic moat.
framework🗣 guest, Erik Brooksconviction: highEthos Capital
So what: Front-loading infrastructure trades early profitability for scalability and stability; relevant to evaluating GP institutionalization risk.
“By design, we have dramatically overbuilt for ethos... We overbuilt it on purpose to be able to grow into the next 5, 10, 15 years.” ▶ 39:51
Ethos learned it had to build a dedicated operating-support stack (a partner, Stephen Gold, leads operating executives, with principals/VPs/associates beneath) because senior operators needed far more execution support than a traditional investment team provides.
anecdote🗣 guest, Erik Brooksconviction: mediumEthos CapitalStephen Gold
So what: Operator-led models carry hidden infrastructure costs; the support layer is essential for the headline 16-partner engine to function.
“turns out you do need some more support.” ▶ 40:51
GP-LP dynamics, alignment and exits 5
In every Ethos deal the sellers have stayed on significantly, which Brooks expects to persist because sellers who only want the highest price won't value Ethos's operational contribution — a self-selecting screen for aligned counterparties.
causal🗣 guest, Erik Brooksconviction: highEthos Capital
So what: Winning deals on value-add rather than price implies Ethos avoids top-of-market auctions; a structural advantage if it holds, but caps the opportunity set.
“if somebody's selling a business and they just want the highest price, they're not going to value what we bring to the party in the same way.” ▶ 46:49
Ethos treats holding a company as an active, recurring buy decision: it exits only when the expected incremental rate of return from owning one more year falls below the base case, rejecting fixed hold periods.
framework🗣 guest, Erik Brooksconviction: highEthos Capital
So what: An evergreen-style hold discipline; LPs should understand exits are return-driven not calendar-driven, affecting fund liquidity timing.
“It is an active decision to own a company. It is not a passive decision... Exiting for us is driven by what do we think the incremental rate of return is on owning the business for another year.” ▶ 53:30
The Ethos partner-to-portfolio-counterpart relationship is a 'sacrosanct cone of silence' where only mutually agreed information flows out, deliberately engineered so management will share problems without fear of board-level repercussions.
framework🗣 guest, Erik Brooksconviction: highEthos Capital
So what: Trust architecture is the precondition for the information edge to materialize post-close; a non-obvious governance design choice.
“the relationship that the ethos partner has with their counterpart at the company is a sacrosanked cone of silence trust relationship.” ▶ 52:30
Brooks frames the competitive goal, citing mentor Royce Yudkoff, as wanting to 'play soccer on a field tilted towards the goal of my opponent' — building a structural edge rather than competing on price in an increasingly competitive PE market.
framework🗣 guest, Erik Brooksconviction: highEthos CapitalRoyce YudkoffABRY Partners
So what: Captures the strategic intent behind operator-led PE: manufacture structural advantage as entry multiples compress margins industry-wide.
“I want to play soccer on a field tilted towards the goal of my opponent.” ▶ 18:27
Brooks left ABRY Partners to found Ethos in 2019 specifically because PE had grown more competitive around 2015-16, with buyers paying ever-higher prices, eroding the edge of disciplined investing.
data_point🗣 guest, Erik Brooksconviction: highABRY PartnersEthos Capitalprivate equity
So what: Datapoint on mid-2010s PE multiple inflation as the catalyst for differentiated-edge strategies; context for why operator-led models proliferated.
“people were paying higher and higher prices being disciplined investors. That was uncomfortable.” ▶ 18:14
Sector thesis: durable digital-infrastructure models 3
Ethos's sector focus is macro-driven: financial services, supply chain, digital identity and infrastructure, and risk and resilience — areas with structural tailwinds where business models are accelerated rather than threatened by change.
thesis🗣 guest, Erik Brooksconviction: highfinancial servicessupply chaindigital identitydigital infrastructurecyber security
So what: Maps the specific verticals an operator-led PE firm believes offer durable, tailwind-backed buyout opportunities.
“Those were financial services, supply chain, digital identity and infrastructure, risk and resilience.” ▶ 27:16
Brooks generalizes media as 'technology-enabled content,' arguing media-like recurring-revenue characteristics (stable, predictable, recurring, high barriers) migrated into healthcare and business services as content digitized — the lens that lets a sector specialist expand its hunting ground.
framework🗣 guest, Erik Brooksconviction: highmediahealthcarebusiness services
So what: A reusable abstraction for spotting recurring-revenue business models outside their original industry; useful for sourcing durable assets.
“let's think about media as technology enabled content.” ▶ 14:59
Brooks flags that AI has fundamentally accelerated the pace of change across industries, raising the bar for what counts as a 'durable, no-expiration-date' business model relative to the prior decades of stable media/communications.
prediction🗣 guest, Erik Brooksconviction: mediumAI
So what: Even durability-focused investors now treat AI-driven disruption velocity as a live risk to the moats they underwrite; raises diligence bar on obsolescence.
“Giant air quotes based on where we are today given AI. The pace at which the game is changing in a lot of different areas has fundamentally changed.” ▶ 16:10

a16z Podcast 1 episode(s) · 30 insights

The New Rule for Picking AI Winners | The a16z Show33 min30 insights2026-05-29
▶ Watch on YouTube
An a16z growth investor (David) argues AI is not in a bubble because the sector is supply-constrained, not demand-constrained, with frontier labs (OpenAI + Anthropic) on track for ~$200B combined revenue run-rate by end of 2026 against <5% enterprise diffusion. The central new investing rule: back companies that are 'in the token path,' pick the leader in any space with strong founders, and accept that who captures value is now far harder to predict as company half-lives shorten.
Frontier-lab scale vs. near-zero diffusion · 6The new picking rule: token path + leader-in-space · 4Shrinking company half-lives and value-capture uncertainty · 4Token economics, China, and open source · 3Bigger, faster outcomes and concentration · 4Valuations, loss ratios, and the bubble debate · 6Public markets, indexes, and consumer upside · 3
Frontier-lab scale vs. near-zero diffusion 6
OpenAI and Anthropic are each adding more revenue per month than Meta, Google, or Microsoft, and their combined revenue run-rate could hit ~$200B by end of 2026.
data_point🗣 guest, a16z growth investor (David)conviction: highOpenAIAnthropicMetaGoogleMicrosoft
So what: Frontier-lab revenue is scaling faster than the hyperscalers' added revenue; watch for $200B run-rate as a milestone that validates AI infrastructure CapEx and private-lab valuations.
“Anthropic and OpenAI are adding more revenue per month than Meta, Google, or Microsoft.” ▶ 0:01
Actual diffusion of AI into the real economy is less than 5%, concentrated in coding and tech-forward firms, with most enterprise functions barely using the capability.
data_point🗣 guest, a16z growth investor (David)conviction: highenterprise softwarecoding
So what: The gap between current lab revenue and <5% diffusion implies a very large runway; treat enterprise AI adoption as early-innings, not late-cycle.
“actual diffusion of this technology into the real economy is tiny. It's like less than 5%.” ▶ 1:55
The upper bound on AI spend can be sized against Fortune 500 / S&P 500 collective profits of ~$2 trillion/year; two labs at $200B run-rate alone equals ~10% of that profit pool.
framework🗣 guest, a16z growth investor (David)conviction: mediumFortune 500S&P 500
So what: Framework for bounding total AI TAM: enterprises must fund AI from profit, so $2T profit pool sets the ceiling and forces either price hikes or labor restructuring.
“they generate like 2 trillion of profit per year at the collective” ▶ 2:50
The model companies are already adding more revenue than the entire public software universe combined, making prior SaaS market sizes look small.
data_point🗣 guest, a16z growth investor (David)conviction: highSaaSpublic software
So what: Implies relative reallocation away from legacy public software toward AI labs; bearish read-through for incumbent SaaS revenue capture.
“the model companies are adding more than the entire public software universe in terms of revenue added, you know, combined” ▶ 7:04
Cost pressure has already forced 'open source and local' to matter sooner than expected, because AI cost is hitting enterprise buyers in the face.
causal🗣 guest, a16z growth investor (David)conviction: mediumopen source AIlocal models
So what: Watch open-source and on-prem/local inference as cost-driven beneficiaries; budget pressure pulls demand toward cheaper alternatives faster than the consensus timeline.
“cost is going to hit us in the face and make them really important sooner than we thought” ▶ 3:32
The takeoff seen in coding is beginning in other white-collar functions like legal, and is expected to spread across enterprise functions and verticals over the next 12 months.
prediction🗣 guest, a16z growth investor (David)conviction: mediumcodinglegal techenterprise verticals
So what: Vertical AI applications in legal and other white-collar functions are the next adoption wave; an early entry point before diffusion broadens.
“it's starting to happen in legal” ▶ 4:01
The new picking rule: token path + leader-in-space 4
The number-one screen for backing an AI company now is whether it is 'in the token path' — directly in the flow of model inference rather than legacy-style software.
framework🗣 guest, a16z growth investor (David)conviction: highAI applicationsmodel companies
So what: Concrete investability filter: prioritize companies whose value is tied to token consumption; legacy-generation software outside the token path faces budget compression.
“right now, you have to be in the token path. Like that is the number one thing that we're looking to for our companies.” ▶ 12:54
The Khosla-derived philosophy is to back the leader in any space with strong founders and tailwinds; if the space works and you have the leader, great, and if it fails with the leader, 'no harm, no foul' — the only bad outcome is the space working and backing the wrong company.
framework🗣 guest, a16z growth investor (David)conviction: highKhosla Ventures
So what: Reframes early-stage risk: the error to avoid is picking the loser in a winning space, not betting on a space that fails; informs how to read a16z's concentrated AI bets.
“The the bad box of what I described is the space works out and we picked the wrong one.” ▶ 19:17
A low loss ratio is a red flag, not a badge of honor — a VC who has 'never lost money on a deal' is taking too little risk and behaving like a PE firm.
contrarian🗣 guest, a16z growth investor (David)conviction: highventure capitalprivate equity
So what: Investors evaluating GPs should be wary of suspiciously low loss ratios; in venture, appropriate loss rates signal genuine risk-taking and power-law upside.
“one of his uh big points of pride is he's never lost money on a deal. And we're like, that's not... a point of pride. Like that's a horrible data point.” ▶ 18:25
Model companies are 'legging their way up into the application layer' as their biggest lever to drive stickiness, after prior cycles oscillated between 'models are everything' and 'models are just APIs.'
thesis🗣 guest, a16z growth investor (David)conviction: mediumOpenAImodel companiesapplication companies
So what: Labs moving into apps raises competitive risk for AI application startups; assess whether a portfolio app can defend against the lab that supplies its tokens.
“now we're back in this moment where the model companies are kind of legging their way up into the application” ▶ 12:41
Shrinking company half-lives and value-capture uncertainty 4
40% of the companies on Forbes' AI 50 list dropped off from one year to the next, implying an incredibly short half-life for AI startups.
data_point🗣 host, LP / fund-of-funds investorconviction: highForbes AI 50
So what: Rapid turnover means today's leaders may not be tomorrow's; underwrite AI positions for high obsolescence risk and avoid over-anchoring on current leaders.
“40% of the companies that were on that list last year dropped off” ▶ 11:41
First movers no longer reliably capture the economic value of a market — as Google wasn't the first search engine and Facebook wasn't the first social network — and predicting AI winners has become much harder.
framework🗣 host, LP / fund-of-funds investorconviction: highGoogleFacebook
So what: Discount first-mover premium in AI; the value-capture winner may emerge later, favoring diversified exposure over concentrated early bets.
“Google wasn't the first search engine, Facebook wasn't the first social media site” ▶ 11:25
The single biggest unknowable driving AI value capture is the market structure of the model labs: a few frontier labs means higher token prices, while ~five frontier labs means lower token prices.
framework🗣 guest, a16z growth investor (David)conviction: highfrontier labstoken pricing
So what: Track the number of frontier labs as the key variable; lab concentration determines token pricing power, labor-restructuring pace, and where ecosystem value accrues.
