“If there's a couple at the frontier, token prices will probably be higher. If there are five at the frontier, token prices will probably be lower.” ▶ 13:52
There is a tremendous amount of inelasticity for frontier intelligence right now — buyers are voraciously demanding the absolute frontier rather than substituting cheaper prior-generation models.
data_point🗣 guest, a16z growth investor (David)conviction: highfrontier modelstoken demand
So what: Frontier demand inelasticity supports lab pricing power today; an eventual 'optimization phase' (substituting cheaper models) is the risk to watch and may arrive sooner than expected.
“there's a tremendous amount of inelasticity for frontier intelligence right now” ▶ 14:16
Token economics, China, and open source 3
Per-token cost for like-for-like capability is falling more than 10x year-over-year, yet aggregate dollar spend on frontier tokens is rising even faster because of demand.
data_point🗣 guest, a16z growth investor (David)conviction: hightoken pricingfrontier models
So what: Falling unit cost is not deflationary for the sector yet — volume growth swamps price declines; revenue keeps rising despite cheaper tokens.
“The sort of per token cost like for like is going down more than 10x year over year. But the appetite for tokens on the frontier is massively exceeding that in terms of dollars.” ▶ 16:42
Leading Chinese LLMs are roughly six months behind US frontier capability but about 10x cheaper, posing a classic innovator's-dilemma threat where an 80%-capability product at 10% of cost erodes the frontier over time.
thesis🗣 host, LP / fund-of-funds investorconviction: mediumChinaChinese LLMsUS AI labs
So what: Cheaper Chinese/next-tier models could capture share for tasks not needing the absolute frontier; a structural margin risk to US frontier labs over the decade.
“the leading LLMs in in China are probably six months behind where we are in the US in terms of the capability of their models, but they're 10x cheaper” ▶ 15:11
Distilling a big model costs on the order of 2% of its pre-training cost; if that remains possible, it bodes well for open source, and the big labs actively don't want their models distilled.
data_point🗣 guest, a16z growth investor (David)conviction: mediumopen source AImodel distillationfrontier labs
So what: Distillation economics (~2% of training cost) are the swing factor for open-source viability; labs' defenses against distillation determine whether open source stays competitive.
“it probably costs in the order of like 2% of the actual training cost pre-training cost of a model to distill it” ▶ 16:21
Bigger, faster outcomes and concentration 4
The threshold for a top-1% venture exit has roughly 10x'd in 24 months — from $10B (2020-2024) to $20B (early 2026) to $32B for closed exits — and could exceed $100B by September once OpenAI/Anthropic are counted.
data_point🗣 host, LP / fund-of-funds investorconviction: highventure capitalOpenAIAnthropic
So what: Exit magnitudes are exploding; LPs should expect returns concentrated in a handful of mega-outcomes, reshaping fund-sizing and ownership-target math.
≈ “between 2020 and 2024, top 1% exit started at $10 billion... it's now at $32 billion. So, we've 10xed... over the space of kind of 24 months” paraphrase ▶ 0:14
The combination of the largest AI companies may be larger than the entire Russell 2000, and the sum of all VC-backed IPOs over the last 6 years (~$1T) is likely smaller than any one of three expected large AI IPOs.
data_point🗣 guest, a16z growth investor (David)conviction: mediumRussell 2000VC-backed IPOs
So what: A few coming AI IPOs could individually dwarf six years of prior VC-backed IPO value combined; size public-market absorption and index effects accordingly.
“if you sum all of them up they're a little over a trillion dollars... that's probably going to be smaller than any of the three of the large IPOs uh that we expect to happen”
Companies like Wiz and Cursor went from nothing to $30B and potentially $60B in only 4-6 years, illustrating that the pace of value creation, not just the size of outcomes, is accelerating.
data_point🗣 host, LP / fund-of-funds investorconviction: highWizCursor
So what: Compressed value-creation timelines mean capital must be deployed and ownership locked in earlier; named comps (Wiz, Cursor) anchor the speed of the new cycle.
“Particularly something like Wiz and Cursor, you'd... like 4, 5, 6 years and... to get from nothing to well, 30 billion dollars and then potentially 60 billion dollars” ▶ 10:06
AI companies hit 'big company problems' very early — Cursor has billions in revenue while still tiny and young — forcing VCs to build large platforms (pricing, sales-scaling, international, channel) that founders now demand earlier in a company's life.
thesis🗣 guest, a16z growth investor (David)conviction: highCursorventure platforms
So what: Explains a16z's platform scale-up and why large established firms are winning early AI breakouts; founder preference for scaled platforms is a structural advantage over emerging managers.
“cursor as an example is you know, billions of dollars of revenue and they're very small and it's very early in their life” ▶ 21:20
Valuations, loss ratios, and the bubble debate 6
AI early-stage loss ratios are currently in single-digit percentages versus a historical ~60% loss ratio for early-stage funds, an unsustainable dynamic that mirrors 2021's 'peak emerging manager' zero-loss illusion — except now driven by established firms.
contrarian🗣 host, LP / fund-of-funds investorconviction: highventure capitalAI startups
So what: Today's abnormally low AI loss ratios will revert toward historical 60%; underwrite for mean reversion and rising failure rates as 'laws of gravity reassert.'
“when we look at our early stage funds, there's a 60% loss ratio... If I was looking at the loss ratio of the last couple of years in the AI space... it's probably single figures percentages... And like that's not sustainable.” ▶ 17:37
A UK VCA audience poll found 80% think AI valuations are too high and ~6% too low — read as roughly correct, since ~80% of AI companies are likely overvalued and a small undervalued subset will become the leaders worth multiples of today's price.
thesis🗣 host, LP / fund-of-funds investorconviction: mediumUK Venture Capital AssociationAI valuations
So what: Supports a diversified-basket approach to AI: most names overvalued, a few massively undervalued; LPs gain edge from breadth rather than single-company selection.
“80% said too high, about 6% said too low... 80% of companies probably are overvalued today... a small subset of those companies that are massively undervalued” ▶ 22:03
AI is not in a bubble now because it is supply-constrained (compute, memory, data centers, power) rather than demand-constrained — unlike classic bubbles defined by excess supply destroying economics.
contrarian🗣 guest, a16z growth investor (David)conviction: highdata centerscomputepower
So what: Reframes the bubble debate around supply scarcity; the bubble risk only materializes if supply catches up or a model-efficiency breakthrough causes oversupply.
“I feel pretty confident saying that we're not in a bubble right now. I'm less confident... that we won't be in a bubble 3 years from now.” ▶ 0:37
Data center capacity at scale cannot be obtained until late 2028 / early 2029, with US buildout running about a year behind schedule across the entire supply chain, partly due to TSMC's deliberate restraint.
data_point🗣 guest, a16z growth investor (David)conviction: highdata centersTSMCsemiconductorspower
So what: Multi-year supply scarcity through 2028-29 supports pricing and the no-bubble thesis; bullish for data-center supply-chain and power names, and a constraint on AI rollout pace.
“You can't get data center capacity at scale until late '28, early '29 right now” ▶ 24:49
If ~$5 trillion of CapEx is spent on buildout over four to five years, $1-2 trillion of revenue return is a reasonable expectation, and two labs alone reaching $200B run-rate this year should make that equation comfortable.
framework🗣 guest, a16z growth investor (David)conviction: mediumAI CapExOpenAIAnthropic
So what: Provides a sanity-check ratio for the AI-CapEx debate: $5T spend against $1-2T revenue; the labs' trajectory is the evidence cited to justify the spend.
“if we spend 5 trillion of CapEx... can you get one or two trillion dollars of revenue as a as a return on that?” ▶ 26:58
The main thing that could end supply scarcity and risk a bubble is massively smaller models from an algorithmic breakthrough — inspired by the human brain's far greater learning efficiency — making AI much less token-consumptive.
prediction🗣 guest, a16z growth investor (David)conviction: mediummodel efficiencyalgorithmic breakthrough
So what: A model-efficiency step change is the key tail risk to the supply-constrained bull case; it could flip the market to oversupply and compress infrastructure economics.
“the one thing that could shift that would be, you know, massively smaller models. You know, probably comes from like an algorithmic breakthrough of some sort.” ▶ 0:40
Public markets, indexes, and consumer upside 3
Excluding the data-center supply chain, almost no public companies are growing fast — the Mag Seven and software companies are all growing sub-30%, with Palantir nearly the only name growing ~70%.
data_point🗣 guest, a16z growth investor (David)conviction: highMagnificent SevenPalantirpublic software
So what: Scarcity of high-growth public names argues for bringing hyper-growth AI companies public; flags Palantir as an outlier and the data-center chain as the current growth pocket.
“the mag seven are all growing sub 30% at this point... Palantir is really the only one growing, you know, whatever 70%” ▶ 28:49
Bringing AI companies public while still in hypergrowth is good for investors, and index inclusion is likely — a needed counter to public-company count shrinking by half over 20 years.
thesis🗣 guest, a16z growth investor (David)conviction: mediumIPOsindex fundspublic markets
So what: Expect large AI IPOs and index inclusion to broaden retail/passive ownership; some rotation out of existing holdings to make room, but markets can absorb it.
“the number of public companies has shrunk in half... this is going to be a good shot in the arm to bring some very high-growth interesting stuff into the public markets” ▶ 28:29
The biggest AI outcomes will likely come from the consumer side via a shift in 'time spent,' reversing the prior decade in which big tech captured consumer attention and made competition extremely hard.
prediction🗣 guest, a16z growth investor (David)conviction: mediumconsumer AIbig techconsumer attention
So what: Watch consumer AI for the largest outcomes; a reallocation of consumer time/attention away from incumbent big-tech platforms is the thesis to track.
“we're going to see a shift in time spent, you know, consumer attention... which I think will probably create, you know, really extraordinary outcomes” ▶ 32:02
